summaries.display_title: 1920–1924: BIA Efforts to Repeal Acreage Limits in the Crow Act of 1920
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1924

This file from the Bureau of Indian Affairs Central Classified Files documents a sustained effort by Commissioner Charles H. Burke and Superintendent C. H. Asbury of the Crow Agency to repeal or modify the acreage limitation provisions of Section 2 of the Crow Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. L. 751, Public No. 239). That section prohibited the Secretary of the Interior from approving land conveyances to any person, company, or corporation already owning 640 acres of agricultural or 1,280 acres of grazing land on the Crow Reservation, with a maximum ceiling of 1,280 acres agricultural or 1,920 acres grazing, violations punishable by fines up to $5,000 or six months imprisonment.

The correspondence—between Burke, Senator Thomas J. Walsh of Montana, Senator Selden P. Spencer (Chairman, Senate Indian Affairs Committee), and Superintendent Asbury—reveals that by 1922–1923, approximately 1,200 to 1,400 deceased allotments aggregating at least 250,000 acres awaited disposition. Each Crow allottee held 800–900 acres; some heirs owned 1,500–2,000 acres. Land sales were failing dramatically: 55 tracts advertised yielded one bid; 100 tracts yielded 14–15 sales; 150 tracts yielded bids on only 30. Asbury argued the acreage cap prevented grouping dry grazing tracts with watered parcels, depressing prices and leaving heirs destitute. A Solicitor's opinion (August 17, 1920, by Charles D. Mahaffie) construed "at least" as "nevertheless," limiting purchases to 640 acres per transaction. A draft bill to repeal the restriction entirely was prepared and transmitted to both congressional Indian Affairs committees, with a parallel reference to the Blackfeet Reservation relief act of June 2, 1924 (43 Stat. L. 252). The Hardin Chamber of Commerce also sought to facilitate land company purchases. The file demonstrates how federal officials framed the removal of protective acreage caps—originally designed to prevent land monopolization—as beneficial to Indians, effectively facilitating consolidation of Crow lands into non-Indian ranching operations.

summaries.display_title: 1966: Joe Medicine Crow's Legal Handbook on Crow Laws and Treaties
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1825–1966

This is a comprehensive legal reference handbook compiled by Joe Medicine Crow (B.S., M.S.), Tribal Historian and Anthropologist, with legal research by Daniel S. Press, a Columbia Law School student serving as a VISTA worker on the Crow Reservation. Dated June 15, 1966, and endorsed by two successive Crow Tribal Council administrations (1964–1968), the booklet systematically documents the legal mechanisms through which the Crow Reservation was reduced from approximately 38,831,174 acres (1851 Treaty, 11 Stat. 749) to approximately 8,000,409 acres (1868 Treaty, 15 Stat. 649), then further diminished through the 1882 Act (22 Stat. 42) ceding the western portion, the 1891 Act (26 Stat. 989) ceding additional western lands for $940,000, the 1904 Act (33 Stat. 352) ceding approximately 1,150,000 acres in the north, and the 1937 Act (50 Stat. 884) eliminating the Hardin area. Railroad rights-of-way were granted to three companies between 1887 and 1893, including 5,084 acres to the Northern Pacific (22 Stat. 157) for $25,000.

The document traces allotment policy from the 1887 Dawes Act through the 1920 Crow Act (41 Stat. 751), which allotted the entire reservation—making it the largest allotted reservation nationally—with 82% (approximately 1.5 million acres) allotted. It details the progressive erosion of restrictions: the 1926 leasing amendment, the 1948 competent lessor act, the 1953 homestead repeal, and mineral rights provisions expiring in 1970. The handbook cites *Lone Wolf v. Hitchcock* (187 U.S. 553, 1904) to explain Congress's plenary power to abrogate treaties, and documents how each cession's compensation was controlled by the Secretary of Interior rather than the tribe. The Yellowtail Dam appropriation of $2,500,000 for 5,677.94 acres illustrates continuing dispossession into the modern era.

summaries.file_name: 1949 Joe Medicine Crow to Competency Commission, Feb 1949[61].pdf
summaries.display_title: 1949: Joe Medicine Crow's Statement Opposing Competency Classification
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1949–1949

This document is a formal statement by Joe Medicine Crow (Crow Agency, Montana) to the Competency Commission, dated February 1949, declaring that he is not a candidate for competency classification and requesting his name not be included on the list of nominees submitted to the Secretary of the Interior. Copies were sent to Senator Zales N. Ecton and Assistant Secretary of Interior William Warne, among others.

Medicine Crow provides a detailed critique of the competency classification process under the Crow Act. He defines competency as merely allowing an Indian to negotiate leases and receive rentals directly, rather than through the Agency office, arguing this does not constitute true self-sufficiency. He estimates approximately 96% of the Crow Reservation is leased to white lessees and contends that mass classification would destabilize the unit leasing system, depress rental rates, encourage haphazard leasing, and leave some allotments unleased and vulnerable to trespass. He warns that competency classification was being rushed indiscriminately—including the aged, alcoholic, illiterate, and vagrant—driven by a political clique motivated by personal animosity toward the Indian Service. Most critically, Medicine Crow warns that mass competency declarations would provide grounds for Montana counties and the Montana Taxpayers Association—which had already conducted preliminary surveys—to push toward taxation of Indian lands, ultimately leading to land loss through tax sales, forced dispositions of heirship lands, and erosion of federal treaty protections under a sympathetic Republican administration aligned with Senator Ecton's policies.

summaries.display_title: 1937–1938: Crow Tribal Council Meeting Minutes
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1937–1938

This document consists of minutes from multiple Crow Tribal Council meetings held between May 1937 and April 1938, forwarded by Superintendent Robert Yellowtail to the Commissioner of Indian Affairs. The records are from the Central Classified Files 1907–1939, Crow Agency, File 054, Record Group 75, National Archives, digitized via JSTOR from the University of Montana's Mansfield Library.

The minutes document extensive debates over grazing lease management on the Crow Reservation. Key issues include: the Secretary of the Interior's order canceling Miller Land and Livestock Company leases and redirecting them to M.H. Tschirgi at lower rates using contested powers of attorney; the Tribal Council's unanimous resolution (56–0, April 18, 1938) protesting this action and asserting that powers of attorney were obtained through misrepresentation; disputes over minimum per-acre rates (50¢ for mountain tribal land, 30¢ for Grapevine unit, 21¢ for Beauvais/Trask, 8–12¢ for lower areas); and the withdrawal of leasing privileges for noncompetent Indians by Washington. Regional Forester George Nyce and Superintendent Yellowtail administered leases under the Crow Act of June 4, 1920, and the Amendatory Act of May 26, 1926 (44 Stat. 656). Miller's Dana Cattle Company claimed $1,300,000 surplus and offered $34,000 checks; Tschirgi's Antler Sheep Company bid approximately $100,000 on 300,000 acres. The Council also addressed gold mining leases to Ebert & Company (837 acres approved September 1936, 629 acres March 1937), bentonite mining on 960 tribal acres, Campbell Farming Corporation farming leases on Units 1, 4, and 6 (~25,861 acres), a $17,787 per capita payment ($10 per enrolled member), and opposition to the Big Horn Dam. These records demonstrate systematic dispossession through federal administrative control over lease decisions, power-of-attorney manipulation, and suppression of tribal autonomy over allotted and tribal lands.

summaries.display_title: Undated: Federal Records Retrieval Form for Crow Reservation Files
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: undated

This document is an Optional Form 11 (Reference Request — Federal Records Centers), a standard General Services Administration form used to request retrieval of federal records stored at a National Archives and Records Administration (NARS) Federal Records Center. The form references Record Group No. 62 and pertains to records associated with the Crow Reservation, identifiable from the archival context as Central Classified Files (CCF) 1958–75, File 5497, Crow, Box 27. The critical notation on this form is that the box has been marked "MISSING" — defined on the form as "Neither record(s), information nor charge card found in container(s) specified." The searcher initialed "B" but no clear date of the search is legible due to poor OCR quality. The requester's name and agency address fields are largely illegible, though partial date fragments such as "3-4-2" and "///20" appear without full resolution.

The document's primary evidentiary significance lies in demonstrating that Bureau of Indian Affairs records directly relevant to Crow Reservation administration during the 1958–1975 period are missing from federal custody. The absence of both the records and any charge-out card suggests the materials were neither formally loaned nor officially destroyed, raising questions about the integrity of the archival record concerning Crow land management, allotment decisions, and federal policy implementation. Missing BIA records of this nature can obscure evidence of specific land transactions, lease arrangements, or administrative decisions that facilitated dispossession of Crow tribal and individual trust lands during a critical mid-twentieth-century period.

summaries.display_title: 1939–1940: House Records on H.R. 5477, Crow Reservation Benefit Bill
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1939–1940

This document comprises legislative and administrative records from RG 233 (Records of the U.S. House of Representatives, 76th Congress) relating to H.R. 5477, a bill for the benefit of the Indians of the Crow Reservation, Montana. The bill was introduced on March 31, 1939, by Representative James F. O'Connor of Montana's 2nd District and referred to the Committee on Indian Affairs chaired by Will Rogers. It was reported with amendments on February 29, 1940 (Report No. 1693).

The bill sought to amend Section 2 of the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), which imposed acreage limitations on land purchases within the Crow Reservation: no person owning 640 acres of agricultural land or 1,280 acres of grazing land could purchase additional allotted Indian land, and no purchaser could exceed 1,280 acres agricultural or 1,920 acres grazing. Acting Secretary of the Interior A.J. Wirtz, in his January 26, 1940 letter, explained that these caps, while protective against non-Indian cattle operators consolidating grazing districts, also prevented Crow allottees—many already holding allotments of up to 1,000 acres plus inherited lands—from consolidating scattered holdings into self-sustaining units. The Department recommended lifting acreage restrictions for Indians only, preserving them for non-Indians, and extending the bill to cover exchanges as well as sales. The letter also referenced Section 6 of the 1920 Act as amended by the Act of May 26, 1926 (44 Stat. 659), reserving tribal mineral rights for 50 years. The Crow Delegation endorsed the amended bill. This document reveals how federal acreage limitations, ostensibly protective, paradoxically constrained Indian land consolidation while the broader allotment framework continued facilitating non-Indian acquisition of reservation lands.

summaries.display_title: 1965–1977: AAIA Files on Crow Moratorium on Land Sales
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1965–1977

This document appears to be a bibliographic citation or catalog reference rather than a substantive primary source. It references materials from the Iliff McKay files, dated 1965–1977, held within the Association on American Indian Affairs (AAIA) collection at Princeton University's Mudd Library. The full collection is titled "The Association on American Indian Affairs: Publications, Programs, and Legal and Organizational Files, 1851–1983," specifically Organizational Files, Box 44, Folder 5. The citation was accessed through the Gale "Indigenous Peoples of North America" digital database on June 14, 2023, by a researcher affiliated with the University of Virginia (indicated by the "viva_uva" proxy identifier).

The document itself contains no substantive text beyond this citation metadata. The two pages appear to consist of a blank first page and the reference information on the second. No specific claims, acreages, dollar amounts, allotment numbers, legal statutes, or policy decisions are discernible from the available text. Despite the file being labeled "1976 Crow moratorium on land sales," the OCR capture provides no content related to the Crow Reservation, land sale moratoriums, or Bureau of Indian Affairs actions. The AAIA was a prominent advocacy organization involved in Native American land rights, and the McKay files likely contain correspondence or policy documents relevant to Crow land dispossession, but the actual document content is either missing from the OCR or was not captured in the digitization process. This severely limits analytical utility.

summaries.display_title: 1956–1960: BIA Files on Crow Tribe Land Purchase Program Efforts
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1956–1960

This file comprises Bureau of Indian Affairs internal memoranda, tribal resolutions, correspondence from the Billings Area Office, the Crow Agency superintendent, the Commissioner's office, and outside parties, documenting the Crow Tribe's protracted and largely unsuccessful efforts to establish a land purchase program to stem the alienation of allotted lands on the Crow Reservation in Montana.

The core legal mechanism is the Act of July 1, 1948 (62 Stat. 1214, Public Law 870), which authorized the Crow Tribe to purchase inherited trust lands for resale to individual members, and the Crow Allotment Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), under which approximately 2,001,736 acres were allotted in severalty at roughly 1,000 acres per member. By 1960, the reservation contained 1,330,562 acres of allotted land and 266,408 acres of tribal land. Resolution No. 176 (August 25, 1958) adopted a Declaration of Policies and Plan of Operation for tribal land purchase, but Resolution No. 189 (November 22, 1958) rescinded it unanimously, and the BIA immediately interpreted this as eliminating any tribal preference right in advertised sales. Resolution 61-05 (August 9, 1960) sought a $1,000,000 revolving credit loan for purchasing "key tracts" at an average price of $21.26 per acre (approximately 17,000 acres), but the BIA's Branch of Realty (IHLane, November 1, 1960) questioned the tribe's financial capacity—annual income under $100,000 against a $120,000 budget—and disparaged tribal management capability. Superintendent Clyde Hobbs noted that approximately 70% of trust land was leased under "competent leases" controlled by non-Indians, and that fee-patented lands were routinely sold to non-Indians through pre-arranged agreements before patents issued. Thomas D. Campbell's offer to sell his 50-year farming operation for $2,000,000 was similarly rebuffed. Chairman Edward P. Whiteman's January 1960 letter protested the accelerating transfer of land out of Indian ownership, calling Bureau policy "destructive." The documents collectively demonstrate how BIA bureaucratic obstruction, inadequate tribal funding, revolving credit fund unavailability, competent-lease exploitation, fee-patent alienation, and land speculation systematically dispossessed Crow allottees despite statutory authority for tribal land consolidation.

summaries.display_title: 1939–1940: Crow Tribal Council Minutes, Resolutions, and Correspondence
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1939–1940

These documents comprise minutes, resolutions, and correspondence from the Crow Tribal Council at Crow Agency, Montana, drawn from Bureau of Indian Affairs Central Classified Files (Record Group 75, File 69154, Crow Agency 054). The records span multiple council meetings from August 1939 through June 1940, with Superintendent Robert Yellowtail presiding and Chairman Charles Yarlott leading the tribal council.

Key actions include: a June 5, 1940 resolution reducing the Big Horn Placer Mining Corporation's gold royalty from 30% to 15% on approximately 1,466 acres along the Big Horn River (original lease dated July 28, 1937, approved by the Secretary of the Interior March 21, 1938), with a 12-month extension from September 1, 1940; a unanimous vote to repeal the Amendatory Act of June 20, 1936 (49 Stat. 244), which had transferred per capita distribution authority from the tribe to the Secretary of the Interior, overriding Section 11 of the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751); and extensive debate over Senator Wheeler's bill S-3732 proposing cancellation of irrigation assessments totaling $550,882.32 in unpaid O&M charges, with total irrigation construction expenditures of $2,231,012 including $820,921.69 in tribal funds used without consent. Attorney Tom Burke detailed Crow water rights under the Winters doctrine and the threat from Wyoming irrigation projects consuming Big Horn River water. The council also shifted from auction to sealed-bid leasing at 25 cents per acre minimum, approved the Massey Oil and Gas Company's drilling permit, and authorized $500 for garden seed and $1,000 for a sewing project. These records collectively document systematic dispossession through irrigation liens on allotments, mineral lease exploitation, and federal control over tribal funds.

summaries.display_title: 1920–1962: BIA Files on Enforcement of Crow Act Section 2 Acreage Limits
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1962

This collection of Bureau of Indian Affairs Central Classified Files documents the prolonged struggle over enforcement of Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), as amended by the Act of June 8, 1940 (54 Stat. 252), which limited non-Indian land acquisitions on the Crow Reservation to 640 acres of agricultural land or 1,280 acres of grazing land, with upper ceilings of 1,280 acres agricultural or 1,920 acres grazing. Conveyances exceeding these limits were declared void, with violators subject to misdemeanor penalties including fines up to $5,000 and six months imprisonment.

The documents reveal that these acreage limitations were systematically violated for decades. Between 1942 and 1955, the BIA conducted 21 supervised sales covering 517 tracts with no reference to statutory limitations in advertisements and no checking of buyers' prior holdings. Sales by fee-patented Indians were entirely unsupervised. The Commissioner suspended all Crow land sales in late 1955 upon learning of the violations. S. 55 and H.R. 439 (86th Congress, introduced January 1959 by Senator Murray and Representative Thomson) sought to repeal Section 2 and validate all illegal conveyances. The Crow Tribe, through Resolution No. 191 (May 2, 1959, passing 306-5), vigorously opposed repeal and demanded enforcement. The Department of Interior recommended against enactment. The Department of Justice declined criminal prosecution for pre-1955 violations but agreed to consider civil actions. On April 6, 1960, the Solicitor transmitted case information to Justice recommending suits to set aside void conveyances. A December 1959 Solicitor's memorandum regarding bidder Jo Annette Hammond (owning 1,435.08 acres grazing and 160 acres agricultural) overruled a 1957 Field Solicitor opinion, confirming that exceeding limits in either land class disqualified purchases of both classes. These records demonstrate that BIA negligence directly facilitated large-scale illegal land transfers from Crow allottees to non-Indians over three decades.

summaries.display_title: 1941–1942: Crow Tribal Council Proceedings and Wartime Resource Disputes
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1941–1942

This document comprises Bureau of Indian Affairs Central Classified Files (Record Group 75, File 69154, Part 3 [2 of 2]) from the Crow Agency, Montana, digitized from the University of Montana's Mansfield Library via JSTOR. It contains Crow Tribal Council proceedings, resolutions, correspondence, and telegrams spanning late 1941 through late 1942, documenting the tribe's struggles over control of their own funds and resources during World War II.

The records reveal systematic federal control over Crow tribal finances. The tribe's total available funds stood at $34,879.73, yet the Commissioner of Indian Affairs repeatedly denied per capita payments of $10–$17,879, insisting funds be directed toward "rehabilitation" cattle programs and reimbursable loan repayment. The Council voted unanimously in January 1942 to allocate $10,000 for Defense Bonds, $17,879 for per capita payment, and $7,000 for cattle purchases, but Washington rejected the per capita component. The $50,000 Crow Consolidated 4% Revolving Fund (authorized under the Act of June 4, 1920) had grown to $60,142, with $31,700 in outstanding loans and approximately 300 delinquent contracts. Individual cattle repayment obligations totaled 564 head, which the Commissioner threatened to collect and transfer to other reservations.

The documents expose how irrigation construction charges exceeding $2 million accrued against allotments, water rights were lost through inherited land sales to whites, and the tribe was compelled to pass resolutions merely to access their own money. Council members like Secretary Whitebear noted that post-1920 Crow children "neither are classed as whites or Indians" and had "no land or any income." The bureaucratic requirement that resolutions be submitted separately in triplicate—per Office Circular of August 15, 1938—further delayed tribal access to resources, effectively dispossessing the Crow of meaningful control over their reservation economy.

summaries.display_title: 1957–1961: BIA Correspondence on Crow Reservation Land Sale Acreage Limits
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1961

This file comprises Bureau of Indian Affairs correspondence, Solicitor's opinions, land sale advertisements, and inter-agency memoranda concerning enforcement of acreage limitations on Crow Reservation land sales under Section 2 of the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), as amended by the Act of June 8, 1940 (54 Stat. 252). The documents span from the Field Solicitor's May 23, 1957 opinion through the Solicitor's April 6, 1961 referral to the Department of Justice requesting civil action to void illegal conveyances.

Section 2 prohibited conveyances to any person owning 640+ acres of agricultural or 1,280+ acres of grazing land within the reservation, with a ceiling of 1,280 acres agricultural and 1,920 acres grazing. Violations carried penalties of $5,000 fine, six months imprisonment, and voided title. The record reveals that between 1940 and late 1955, the Bureau conducted 21 supervised sales (517 tracts) without enforcing these limitations. Commissioner Emmons suspended all sales in late 1955 upon learning of widespread violations. A specific case—Jo Annette Hammond's bid on 320 acres of Allotment 3171 (Claude Mapes Dawes)—was rejected because she already owned 1,435.08 acres of grazing land and 160 acres of agricultural land. The Solicitor's December 7, 1959 opinion overruled the Field Solicitor, holding that exceeding the ceiling in either category barred acquisition of both classes. The Faddis-Kennedy Cattle Company case exposed suspected dummy bidding by corporate secretary Callie F. Kennedy for 80 acres of Allotment 1552 (Beady Anderson). Assistant Secretary Roger Ernst's March 11, 1958 supplemental report to Congress withdrew support for S. 332, which would have repealed the acreage limitations. The file demonstrates decades of administrative negligence enabling non-Indian land consolidation on the Crow Reservation.

summaries.display_title: 1936–1938: Crow Tribal Council Minutes, Resolutions, and Petitions
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1936–1938

This document is a compilation of minutes from Crow Tribal Council meetings held between December 1936 and May 1937, along with associated resolutions, petitions, correspondence, and exhibits, drawn from Bureau of Indian Affairs Central Classified Files (Record Group 75, Crow Agency 054, File 69154 Part 1), housed at the National Archives and digitized via JSTOR from the University of Montana's Mansfield Library.

The minutes record deliberations by the Crow Tribal Council under Chairman Hartford Bearclaw and Superintendent Robert Yellowtail at Crow Agency, Montana. Key actions include: unanimous opposition to a proposed $15,000,000 Big Horn Canyon hydroelectric dam that would flood approximately 45 miles of canyon and destroy gold mining leases; approval of a three-acre school site near Mark Wolf's residence for a $158,000 Congressional appropriation ($40,000 for Crow Agency, $128,000 for Hardin); rejection of ten-year irrigated farm leases (33 opposed, 3 in favor); passage of a resolution requesting amendment to the Act of March 3, 1931 (46 Stat. 1494) regarding retroactive application to Indian custom adoptions in probate proceedings; authorization of Oliver Ebert's gold prospecting permit on tribal lands (Sections 13, 14, 23-26, Twp. 6 S., Rg. 88-89 E.); purchase of the Big Medicine allotment for $1,411 for tribal fair grounds; a grazing committee vote setting 50¢/acre minimum on tribal mountain areas with cattle-only restrictions; and a resolution seeking Congressional reimbursement of $2,386,157 in tribal funds used for irrigation construction without tribal consent. The Council also approved $3,000 from tribal funds for educational reimbursable college loans, authorized timber sales in the Pryor Mountains, and endorsed foundation livestock purchases from the tribal revolving fund.

The petition against the Big Horn Dam invokes the Fort Laramie Treaty of 1851, noting the original 38-million-acre reservation had been "carved down by cessions" demanded by Congress. The documents cite the Crow Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), Section 8 (requiring tribal consent for new irrigation), and Section 14 (allotment exchanges). A 1938 meeting record appended shows the Miller Land and Livestock Company forfeited bids on grazing units 52, 52A-C, 58, 58A-B, 59, 59A-C, and 24, with replacement bids ranging from 10¢ to 30¢/acre versus Miller's original 20-25¢ offers. Superintendent Yellowtail's speeches reveal the mechanisms of dispossession: competent/incompetent leasing distinctions, sub-leasing by white stockmen at multiples of Indian rental rates, fee patent sales leading to landlessness (citing Earl Howell's case), and the pending Indian Emancipation Commission threatening trust patent termination. The documents collectively demonstrate how federal administrative control over leasing, irrigation charges, oil royalties, and allotment management systematically extracted value from Crow lands while tribal members received minimal returns.

summaries.display_title: 1920: House Committee Hearings on Crow Reservation Allotment Legislation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1891–1920

These are published hearings before the House Committee on Indian Affairs, 66th Congress, held March 17 and 19, 1920, concerning Senate Bill 2890 and H.R. 9707, legislation to complete allotment of the Crow Reservation in Montana. Assistant Commissioner E. B. Meritt presented amendments agreed upon with a Crow delegation led by Russell White Bear and Frank Yarlott. Key amendments included: striking township boundary language in Section 1 to reserve the Big Horn and Pryor Mountains from allotment while opening better lands; vesting allotment title upon selection rather than Interior Department approval; limiting the 640-acre conveyance cap to "agricultural and irrigable land" while exempting grazing land; requiring allotment completion within eighteen months; reserving from tribal funds $100,000 for the agency boarding school, $50,000 for the hospital (capped at $4,000 annually), and $50,000 as a revolving fund for seed, machinery, and implements; and consolidating trust funds at 4 percent interest with remaining balances distributed per capita.

Extensive testimony addressed the Thomas D. Campbell lease, executed April 6, 1918, under authority of the Act of June 25, 1910 (36 Stat. 855) and the Act of August 15, 1894 (28 Stat. 305). The tribal council consented June 10, 1918, authorizing leases on up to 150,000 acres of tribal land at 7½–10 percent of crops for the first five years and 20 percent for the second five, with maintenance charges of $1 per acre. White Bear testified that Supervisor Frank Thackery pressured the council by invoking patriotism and war necessity, that individual allotments were taken without consent—citing William Brass, Deer Nose, and Bear Goes Into the Ground—and that Thackery and lease clerk Remick subsequently joined the Montana Farming Corporation. Meritt acknowledged approximately $1,500,000 in tribal-fund irrigation construction costs plus $500,000 in operation and maintenance, with over 30,000 irrigable acres lying idle before the lease. Superintendent C. H. Asbury estimated no more than five individual leases were made without allottee consent. The tribal herd inventory showed 15,982 animals valued at $824,335 as of December 31, 1919, against a total investment of approximately $419,008 in original stock. Population data showed decline from 1,941 in 1900 to 1,707 in 1919. The hearings document systematic mechanisms of dispossession: wartime duress in securing tribal consent, subleasing at 33⅓ percent while paying Indians 7½–10 percent, irrigation expenditures declared reimbursable without prior Indian agreement, fee patents issued to half-blood allottees under competency policy enabling rapid alienation, and administrative leasing of individual allotments over owners' objections.

summaries.display_title: 1963–1964: Legislative Struggle over S. 1757 to Validate Crow Land Conveyances
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1964

This file from RG 75, Bureau of Indian Affairs Central Classified Files, documents the legislative and administrative struggle over S. 1757 (88th Congress), a bill to validate, ratify, and confirm conveyances of Crow Indian allotted land made prior to October 18, 1957, in violation of acreage limitations in Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751). Section 2 prohibited sales to any person already owning 640 acres of agricultural or 1,280 acres of grazing land on the reservation, and capped acquisitions at 1,280 acres agricultural or 1,920 acres grazing, with violations punishable by fines up to $5,000 and imprisonment.

The Department of Interior's June 14, 1963 transmittal letter to the Senate President (signed by Assistant Secretary John A. Carver, Jr.) details systematic BIA failures: between 1946 and 1955, 21 supervised sales covering 517 tracts proceeded without any compliance checks. The Area Director claimed Washington told him the limitations were "not operative." The 1941 warning regarding Miller Land and Livestock Company violations was not followed up. The Department referred three violation cases to the Department of Justice on April 6, 1960; Justice took no action through 1962.

Attorney Bert W. Kronmiller (Hardin, Montana) wrote Commissioner Philleo Nash on January 15, 1963, proposing a compromise requiring purchasers to pay the difference between the purchase price and appraised market value. Crow Tribal Resolution No. 64-28 (April 11, 1964) formally objected to S. 1757, noting the Tribe received less than 24 hours' notice of the November 20, 1963 Senate hearing and was denied postponement. The resolution alleged "inadequate and unconscionable considerations" paid for allotted lands and that violations depressed reservation land prices. A competing petition from approximately 25 Crow members, forwarded by the law firm Hibbs, Sweeney & Colberg through Congressman James F. Battin, favored enactment. The documents collectively demonstrate how BIA negligence enabled large-scale non-Indian land accumulation on the Crow Reservation, and how proposed remedial legislation prioritized clearing non-Indian titles over compensating dispossessed Crow allottees.

summaries.display_title: 1935–1936: Crow Tribal Council Minutes on Land, Minerals, and Governance
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1935–1936

This document comprises the minutes and associated records of the Crow Tribal Council from January 1935 through September 1936, filed under Crow Agency Central Classified Files 1907–1939, File 69154, Record Group 75, National Archives. The records were forwarded by Superintendent Robert Yellowtail (Crow tribal member and Special Disbursing Agent) to the Commissioner of Indian Affairs in Washington, D.C.

The minutes document extensive discussions about land, mineral resources, and federal restrictions on Crow self-governance. Key topics include: the Crow Tribe's rejection of the Wheeler-Howard (Indian Reorganization) Act and its consequences—Assistant Commissioner William Zimmerman Jr. explicitly told the Council that federal funds for land purchase and loans were unavailable because the Tribe voted itself out of the Act; the heirship land crisis on approximately two million acres; per capita payments of $10.00 from tribal funds; oil lease disputes at Soap Creek (California Independent Oil Organization lease cancelled April 20, 1936, with $1,960 bonus owed and $7,000 cash deposit held) and Lodge Grass (4,740 acres in T6S R35E sought by Lodge Grass Oil Company); gold mining bids in Big Horn Canyon covering approximately 953 acres across eight units with royalty bids ranging from 16–30 percent; and a resolution to exclude Hardin from the reservation boundary because all land within the city was white-owned, citing the 1912 federal court decision (201 Fed. 191) holding that lands with extinguished Indian title were no longer "Indian country." The documents reference the Acts of June 4, 1920, May 26, 1926 (44 Stat. 658), and the competent Indian leasing system, revealing how sub-leasing by operators like Matt Tschirgi at $4–5 per acre disadvantaged allottees receiving only $0.03–0.04 per acre. Collectively, these records demonstrate systematic dispossession through heirship fragmentation, restrictive federal regulations, exploitative leasing, and the loss of tribal jurisdiction over white-owned lands within reservation boundaries.

summaries.display_title: 1929–1935: Crow Agency Superintendents' Annual Narrative and Statistical Reports
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1929–1935

This document is National Archives Microfilm Publication M1011, Roll 31, reproducing the Superintendents' Annual Narrative and Statistical Reports (SANSAR) from the Crow Agency jurisdiction in Montana for fiscal/calendar years 1929–1935, with partial Crow Creek reports (1910–21). The records were prepared by successive Crow Agency superintendents—Calvin H. Asbury (1929–1931), James H. Hyde (1932–1933), and Robert Yellowtail (1934–1935)—and submitted to the Commissioner of Indian Affairs.

The reports document a reservation of approximately 2,300,000 acres with a population growing from 1,947 (1929) to roughly 2,082 (1934), divided nearly equally between full-blood and mixed-blood Crow. Individual allotments averaged over 900 acres per person across 3,990 allotments totaling 1,726,345.57 acres; unallotted tribal mountain land comprised 551,237.59 acres valued at $1,600,000. The Act of June 4, 1920 completed allotment of all surveyed land. The Act of April 27, 1904 (33 Stat. 352) had opened the "ceded strip" north of the reservation, where 618,000 acres were sold in 1910, 1912, and 1917 for $813,212.28, $842,811.13, and an unstated sum respectively; canceled sales reverted land to Indian status, later allotted in 160-acre tracts to post-1920 children. The Act of May 26, 1926 (44 Stat. 658–661) created "competent Indian" status, allowing roughly one-third of allotted acreage to be leased without Agency supervision—a provision the reports repeatedly condemn as causing $62,500 annual loss to the Crows through below-market five-year advance leases manipulated by stockmen. Individual Indian Money balances ranged from $374,515.64 (1929) to $208,745.63 (1933). Grazing leases through the Agency generated approximately $219,223.50 annually, with competent leases doubling that figure; total lease income approached $438,000 per year. Tuition payments for Indian children in public schools rose from $14,037.60 (FY1928) to $20,336.80 (FY1930). Nearly all patents-in-fee resulted in immediate land loss through sale, mortgage default, or tax delinquency. The reports prove systematic dispossession through multiple legal mechanisms: cession agreements, fee patenting, competency classification, and inherited-land sales necessitated by fractionated heirship—while documenting the Agency's own recognition that these processes were impoverishing the Crow people.

summaries.file_name: 1947 The laws and treaties affecting the Crow Indians.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1947: BIA Legal Memorandum on Laws and Treaties Affecting the Crow Indians
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1825–1947

This document is a comprehensive legal memorandum prepared by Howard M. Gullickson, District Counsel, and Fred B. Woodard, Assistant Attorney, District No. 2, Billings, Montana, for the Bureau of Indian Affairs' Missouri River Basin Investigations (Hardin Unit), dated March 1947. It systematically traces the legal history of Crow Indian land dispossession from the first treaty of August 4, 1825 (7 Stat. 266) through legislation of 1946.

The memorandum documents successive territorial reductions: the 1851 Fort Laramie Treaty (11 Stat. 749) established the original Crow territory (Royce Map No. 517); the 1868 Fort Laramie Treaty reduced holdings to areas 635 and 619, relinquishing over three-fourths of treaty lands with "apparently" no consideration. The 1882 Act (22 Stat. 42) sold tract 619 for $30,000 annually over 25 years; 5,384 acres were sold for railroad right-of-way at $25,000 (22 Stat. 157). The 1891 Act (26 Stat. 989) ceded additional territory for $946,000. The 1904 Act (33 Stat. 352) ratified a cession for $1,150,000, with approximately $400,000 designated as an annuity fund at 4% interest. The 1875 executive order granting a 20-mile northern strip was revoked in 1876 without compensation. The Crow Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751) governed allotment, imposed irrigation liens as reimbursable charges, and limited land purchases to 640 agricultural or 1,280 grazing acres. The authors notably identify a one-mile-wide boundary discrepancy along the 107th meridian where Crow land may have been improperly excluded, recommending investigation. Six accompanying charts illustrate the progressive diminishment of the reservation.

summaries.file_name: Bradley From Individualism to Bureaucracy, Documents on the Crow Indians 1920-1945 mss by Chas Bradley 1974.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1974: Bradley Manuscript Summarizing Federal Records on the Crow, 1920–1945
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1945

This is an unpublished research manuscript by Charles Crane Bradley Jr. and Susanna Remple Bradley, completed in 1974 at Lodge Grass, Montana, for the Crow Human Research and Development Committee (a sub-group of the Tribal Culture Committee). It synthesizes and chronologically summarizes nearly every federal record on the Crow Indians stored in the National Archives (Bureau of Indian Affairs letters, Natural Resources Section, Judicial Section, and Cartographic Section) covering 1920 through the mid-1940s.

The manuscript is divided into four parts: Crow politics (1920–1940), reservation agriculture (farming and grazing leases), social institutions (liquor, health, education), and natural resource/land use problems (irrigation, oil, mining, wildlife). It documents the implementation of the Crow Act of June 4, 1920, which allotted the entire reservation except approximately 300,000 mountainous acres to individual Crows at 160 acres per family head, transferred 44,080.74 acres to Montana for schools at $5/acre ($220,403.70 total), imposed reimbursable irrigation charges, and reserved mineral rights tribally for 50 years. The manuscript traces how allotment destroyed tribal self-sufficiency: by 1943, 262,942 acres were non-Indian owned and 1,600 acres held fee patent within the reservation. It details the Crow rejection of the Indian Reorganization Act (Wheeler-Howard Act) in May 1935, which foreclosed land consolidation and federal funding mechanisms. It chronicles grazing lease politics involving major lessees—Tschirgi (Antler Sheep Co.), Snyder, Dana, Campbell Farming Corporation (30,000–69,000 acres), and the Big Horn Valley Development Company—documenting unit sizes, per-acre prices (6¢–50¢), collusion among the "Big Four," and the chaotic Miller Land and Livestock Co. episode. Superintendent tenures (Asbury 1918–1931, Hyde 1932–1933, Yellowtail 1934–1945) are detailed, including Robert Yellowtail's election by 501–199 vote and his significance as the first Crow superintendent. The manuscript proves systematic land dispossession through competent/incompetent classification, advance leasing practices, horse destruction (1926), tax campaigns against Crow property, and the structural inability of the Tribal Council—lacking constitution, bylaws, or fiscal authority—to prevent alienation.

summaries.display_title: 1926: House Committee Hearings on Amendments to the Crow Allotment Act
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1883–1926

This document is a published hearing transcript of the House Committee on Indian Affairs, 69th Congress, 1st Session, on H.R. 8185, held January 21 and February 4, 1926, chaired by Representative Scott Leavitt of Montana. The hearings concern proposed amendments to the Crow allotment act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751–757), which had allotted Crow Reservation lands in severalty under the framework of the General Allotment Act of February 8, 1887 (24 Stat. 388). A Crow delegation including tribal council president James Carpenter, Harry Whiteman, Russell White Bear, and Frank Yarlott, assisted by attorney Dennison Wheelock, testified alongside Commissioner Charles Burke and Assistant Commissioner Edgar B. Meritt.

The central disputes involved Section 8 (irrigation reimbursement) and Section 1 (leasing authority). On irrigation, the Crow delegation protested that the 1920 act imposed reimbursable charges—some assessed as high as $50 per acre—for irrigation systems built with tribal funds expended without tribal consent, causing incompetent allottees, particularly elderly women, to lose their land. Between $400,000 and $500,000 had been spent from the U.S. Treasury on these systems. The compromise distinguished three funding sources: tribal funds spent with tribal consent (charged off against the tribal fund), tribal funds spent without consent (remaining as individual land liens), and federal appropriations (also remaining as individual liens), with the Secretary of the Interior to ascertain and proclaim amounts. The act of August 1, 1914, making all Indian expenditures reimbursable, was referenced as a temporal boundary.

On leasing, the delegation sought unrestricted leasing authority for all allottees—competent and incompetent—to enable group leasing of contiguous family allotments (averaging 4,000–7,000 acres per family) for grazing, arguing the superintendent's arbitrary leasing yielded only 7–15 cents per acre on lands worth far more. The department insisted on restricting incompetent Indians (approximately 50–60% of the tribe). A compromise was reached: competent allottees could lease without restriction; adult incompetent Indians could lease "with the assistance of the superintendent"; minor children's allotment income would go to parents supporting them, but to the superintendent for all other minors. Section 18's $2,500 annual cap on delegation expenses (from a $10,000 tribal fund appropriation) was proposed for elimination. The Crows also ceded 1,150,000 acres in 1904, reducing the reservation from approximately 3,500,000 to 2,400,000 acres, yet grazing revenues remained stagnant at $40,000 annually until 1909. The document powerfully illustrates how reimbursable irrigation charges, arbitrary leasing by superintendents, and competency classifications operated as mechanisms of economic dispossession even after allotment, converting nominal Indian land ownership into encumbered, revenue-depleted holdings controlled by federal administrators.

summaries.display_title: 1919: BIA Correspondence on Crow Tribal Delegation to Washington
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1919–1919

This collection of Bureau of Indian Affairs correspondence documents the authorization and logistics of a Crow tribal delegation to Washington, D.C., to testify before the Senate Committee on Indian Affairs regarding proposed Crow legislation (referred to as "the Crow Bill," introduced by Senator Walsh). The documents include a Western Union telegram from Superintendent C. H. Asbury at Crow Agency, Montana (August 14, 1919); a reply telegram from Commissioner Cato Sells (August 15, 1919); a letter from Commissioner Sells to Senator Charles Curtis, Chairman of the Senate Committee on Indian Affairs (August 20, 1919); and a letter from Asbury to the Commissioner (July 18, 1919).

Six delegates were elected at a Crow council on August 11, 1919, to proceed to Washington for hearings around August 20. They were initially authorized railroad and sleeping car fare plus $5.00 per diem for subsistence and compensation, later increased to $10.00 per diem by the Senate Committee. Senator Curtis's August 14 letter named specific individuals he wanted invited: Lucy Old Horn and husband, Thomas Medicine Horse, Crooked Arms, Knows the Ground, Philip Dom Head, Minnie Reed Williams, Plenty Coups, Richard Wallace, Lies His Knee, Ben Spotted Horse, and Amy Scott—noting that some were half-breeds or quarter-bloods. George Hogan and Russell White Bear were also requested as special delegates. Asbury's July 18 letter reveals deep tribal divisions over the legislation and warns that the proposed bills would prolong trust status and increase unsettled estates rather than achieve genuine settlement of Crow land affairs, suggesting the legislation perpetuated dispossession rather than resolved it.

summaries.display_title: 1938–1939: Crow Tribal Council Meeting Minutes and Resolutions
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1938–1939

This document comprises Bureau of Indian Affairs Central Classified Files (Record Group 75, Crow Agency 054, File 69154, Part 2 [3 of 3]) from the Mansfield Library, University of Montana, digitized via JSTOR. It contains minutes of approximately twenty Crow Tribal Council meetings held between January 1938 and September 1939, along with associated resolutions, correspondence, and reports transmitted by Superintendent Robert Yellowtail to the Commissioner of Indian Affairs.

The minutes document sustained Crow resistance to multiple mechanisms of land dispossession. Key issues include: irrigation construction charges of approximately $4.33/acre (with accumulated interest raising liens to $8–$10/acre) imposed on allotted lands under the Act of June 4, 1920; the controversial use of "authority to grant grazing privileges" documents (characterized by tribal members as powers of attorney obtained through misrepresentation) to execute leases without individual allottee consent; disputes over grazing leases involving Matt Tschirgi, H.V. Wilcutt, and the Miller Land & Livestock Company; and proposed purchase of inherited allotments with tribal funds. The Council debated the Commissioner's draft bill and Senator Wheeler's bill (S. 3900/H.R. 5477) to amend Section 2 of the 1920 Crow Act's acreage limitations. Specific expenditures voted include $360 for 120 acres near a sulphur well (Township 3 South, Range 31 East), $500 for a buffalo corral, $253 for wells, $3,300 for WPA sponsorship, and $100,000 requested for road improvements. The delegation resolution sought $50,000 to match the tribal revolving fund and $160,000 for allotment resurveys. The documents reveal how irrigation liens, Secretarial leasing orders, and federal administrative control over incompetent allotments functioned as ongoing dispossession mechanisms despite the Act of May 26, 1926, which tribal members argued gave allottees leasing autonomy.

summaries.display_title: 1943–1948: Senate Records on Crow Tribal Governance Dispute and Yellowtail Dam Opposition
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1943–1948

This collection comprises correspondence, petitions, and authorization documents from U.S. Senate Records (RG 46), 80th Congress, Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs, relating to an intra-tribal governance dispute among the Crow Tribe of Montana and opposition to the proposed Yellowtail Dam.

The central document is a January 21, 1943 [likely 1948 based on context] letter from the Secretary of the Crow Tribal Executive Committee to Senator Arthur V. Watkins (Utah), asserting that delegates Robert Yellowtail, William Jennings, Minnie Reed Williams Enshaw, and Frank Hawk were chosen at an unauthorized council meeting on January 16, 1943 [1948] and lacked legal authority to represent the Crow Tribe. The letter states the Crow Tribal Executive Committee, created by resolution on approximately June 3, 1947, consisting of 13 members and a chairman, remained the legitimate governing body. Superintendent L.C. Lippert of Crow Agency had not recognized the dissident meeting or signed its resolutions. A February 6, 1943 [1948] reply from a Senate staff member to Dan Old Bull at Lodge Grass, Montana, acknowledged the protest.

The letter extensively discusses S-1317 (the Crow Leasing Bill), introduced by Senator Ecton, and its House amendment substituting H.R. 3200. The writer defends Section 2 of the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), which limited individual holdings on the Crow Reservation to 640 acres of farmland or 1,280 acres of grazing land—a protection attributed to Senator T.J. Walsh against stockmen exploitation. The original S-1317's Section 4 would have permitted sale of inherited allotments to non-Crows, effectively nullifying the 1920 acreage limitations.

Multiple petitions from allotted Crows of the Pryor District oppose the Yellowtail Dam, which would irrigate over 40,000 acres, and resist incorporation of tribal lands into Custer National Forest without Crow Tribal Council approval. These documents collectively demonstrate how federal administrative mechanisms—superintendent recognition of tribal delegations, leasing legislation, and dam construction—served as instruments of land dispossession, while internal governance disputes were exploited to advance policies favoring non-Indian stockmen and federal resource development. OCR quality is poor throughout, particularly on handwritten petition pages.

summaries.display_title: 1958: AAIA Memorandum on Congressional Bills Affecting Crow Forced Patents
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1958–1958

This document is a legislative and administrative memorandum (No. 3) from the General Counsel of the Association on American Indian Affairs (AAIA) to its Board of Directors, covering the period January 16–31, 1958. It reports on congressional activity before the House Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs and its Subcommittee on Indian Affairs, addressing multiple bills affecting tribal lands and rights.

The most significant item concerns S. 328, sponsored by Senator Murray (D., Mont.), which would amend Section 6 of a 1920 Act reserving mineral rights on allotted Crow Reservation land for tribal members. The 1920 Act provided that after fifty years (1970), mineral deposits would pass to individual allottees or heirs, but left ambiguous whether such rights would remain in trust and what happened when surface rights had been alienated. S. 328 as originally drafted would hold mineral title in trust for Indian owners even after surface alienation. However, at the Bureau of Indian Affairs' suggestion, an amendment was inserted allowing the Secretary of the Interior to determine—with or without application—that an Indian was "competent" and to issue a fee patent for mineral rights. Crow Tribal representatives testified before the Subcommittee supporting the bill's basic purpose but explicitly opposed this Bureau-sponsored amendment, characterizing it as "forced patenting." They argued the Bureau could unilaterally declare members competent, award fee simple title, and terminate federal trust responsibility without the individual's consent. The memorandum also covers H.R. 9617, providing compensation for 4,900 acres of Crow land within a reclamation project related to Yellowtail Dam, and H.R. 8544 (Congressman Metcalf, D., Mont.), restoring unsold ceded surplus lands to tribal ownership. The document demonstrates how administrative mechanisms embedded in otherwise protective legislation served as instruments of dispossession by enabling unilateral termination of trust status over Indian property.

summaries.display_title: 1922–1923: BIA Correspondence on Section 2 of the Crow Act and Reservation Land Sales
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1922–1923

This document consists of Bureau of Indian Affairs correspondence from the Crow Agency numerical correspondence file (RG 75, Box 10), comprising two letters from the Crow Agency Superintendent (C.E.A./C.H. Asbury) to the Commissioner of Indian Affairs and one reply from the Office of Indian Affairs. The first letter is dated approximately November 16, 1922; the second October 25, 1923; and the Washington reply November 12, 1923.

The letters concern Section 2 of the "Crow Bill," Public Law No. 239, approved June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. L., 751), which limited individual land purchases on the Crow Reservation to 2 sections of agricultural land or 5 sections of grazing land, voiding any conveyance exceeding those limits—even for fee patent land—and imposing criminal penalties on violators. Superintendent Asbury argued this restriction prevented the sale of deceased allotments, particularly dry grazing land with no water access. He reported approximately 1,200 to 1,400 deceased allotments aggregating at least 250,000 acres, with estates fractionated among numerous heirs. Of these, only about 300 were susceptible to partition. He detailed dismal sale results: 55 tracts advertised two years prior yielded one bid; 100 tracts the next year sold only 14–15; 150 tracts in 1923 drew bids on 60 with competition on only 3. Appraisements had been repeatedly reduced. Grazing land might fetch $6 to $10 per acre only if grouped into larger units. Asbury recommended repealing Section 2 entirely or exempting grazing land. The Washington office confirmed its intention to seek repeal legislation in the next congressional session, revealing coordinated federal efforts to remove restrictions protecting Crow land from concentrated non-Indian acquisition.

summaries.file_name: 1920 CCF 15343-20-013 Crow opp to Sec 2 Yellowtail.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1920: Crow Opposition to Section 2 of Pending Crow Bill
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1920

This file (CCF 15343-20-013) comprises Bureau of Indian Affairs correspondence and Western Union telegrams concerning the Crow bill then pending in Congress, specifically opposition among Crow Indians to Section 2 of the legislation. The principal documents include a telegram from Superintendent Asbury at Crow Agency, Montana, dated May 5, 1920, relaying that Crow Indians in council opposed a clause preventing the sale of leased land, arguing the House amendment would delay settlement of estates and receipt of funds for developing their own lands, effectively forcing land to remain idle. Asbury concurred with the Indians' position and asked whether the matter could be raised with Senate conferees. A separate letter dated February 19, 1920, from Commissioner Cato Sells to Robert Yellowtail of Wyola, Montana, advised that the Crow bill had passed the Senate and been favorably reported by the House Committee on Indian Affairs but faced objections on the Unanimous Consent Calendar. Sells urged Yellowtail to write Representative M. Clyde Kelly seeking favorable consideration and pledged the Office would do everything possible to secure passage.

The documents reveal tensions between federal legislative mechanisms restricting alienation of Crow allotted and leased lands and the practical economic consequences for individual Crow landholders—delayed estate settlements, inability to access funds, and idle agricultural land. Robert Yellowtail's involvement signals organized Crow advocacy regarding provisions governing their reservation lands. The OCR quality is poor throughout, with significant garbling on several pages, particularly the handwritten page 7.

summaries.file_name: 1947 To Grant Certain Rights to Crow Indians.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1947: Senate Hearing on S. 1317 — Patents-in-Fee and Leasing Rights for Crow and Other Tribes
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1889–1947

This document is a transcript of a hearing held on May 29, 1947, before the Subcommittee on Indian Affairs of the Senate Committee on Public Lands, Washington, D.C. The hearing addressed S. 1317 and numerous other Senate bills (S. 310, 311, 312, 313, 403, 486, 499, 500, 542, 608, 713, 714, 791, 860, 1315) concerning patent-in-fee issuances and leasing rights for Indians on multiple reservations, primarily the Crow Reservation in Montana but also Fort Peck, Pine Ridge, Rosebud, and Flathead reservations.

Robert Yellowtail, former Superintendent of the Crow Agency (1934–1945) and elected tribal delegate, testified extensively. He described 2,470 Crow Indians, of whom 2,152 were classified as restricted and only 318 as competent—a status unchanged since a competency commission operated briefly after the Act of June 4, 1924. He detailed 4,500 individual allotments, 75 percent in heirship status, with estates like Crosses The Water and Nice Knife involving over 150 heirs and common denominators in the trillions. The agency employed 100 persons at $225,075 annually to administer 108,802 acres and $600,000 in individual Indian money, costing $105 per Indian to manage $277 each. Yellowtail argued $43,065.40 in salaries (Forestry, Extension, lease clerks) could be eliminated. He cited the Crow Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), the Act of May 8, 1906 (34 Stat. 182), and the Lone Wolf v. Hitchcock (1903) Supreme Court decision as framing the legal landscape. The Rosebud Reservation lost 870,000 of 1,900,000 allotted acres through fee patents. Under Secretary Oscar L. Chapman filed adverse reports on virtually every patent-in-fee bill, citing land consolidation policies and allottee incompetence. The subcommittee (Senators Watkins, Ecton, Malone by proxy) overrode these objections, reporting most bills favorably. S. 1315 ratified helium contracts with the Navajo Tribe involving 7,800 acres in the Rattlesnake field, with $147,799 paid to the tribe and $166,594 to Continental Oil Company and Santa Fe Corporation. The document powerfully illustrates how bureaucratic classification of Indians as "incompetent," perpetuation of agency employment, and Interior Department opposition to fee patents functioned as mechanisms of continued land dispossession and paternalistic control.

summaries.display_title: 1931–1941: BIA Records on Crow Competency Classification and Land Control
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1931–1941

This file from the Bureau of Indian Affairs Central Classified Files (RG 75, CCF 8633-31-013) documents the Crow Tribe's competency classification system and its consequences for land control on the Crow Reservation in Montana. The documents span from the passage of the Act of March 3, 1931 (46 Stat. 1495, Public No. 801) through at least April 1941, encompassing correspondence among superintendents (C.H. Asbury, James H. Hyde, Robert Yellowtail), the Commissioner of Indian Affairs (C.J. Rhoads, John Collier), and First Assistant Secretary Jos. M. Dixon, as well as Crow tribal delegations.

Under the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), a commission classified all enrolled Crow Indians as competent or incompetent, with all minors classified incompetent. The Act of May 26, 1926 (44 Stat. 659) allowed competent Indians to lease their own allotments and those of minor children for up to five years without superintendent approval. The 1931 Act authorized reclassification from incompetent to competent via a three-person committee. In June 1932, Harry Whiteman and Alvin Morrison were elected as tribal representatives, with Superintendent Hyde as departmental representative. By December 1933, the committee reviewed approximately 58 applicants—including Claude Davis, Becker Littlelight, Perry Pretty Paint, and dozens of others—voting on each individually. However, in January 1934, Commissioner Collier halted all competency determinations pending policy revision.

Internal memoranda from the Forestry and Grazing Division (April 1937) reveal that the competency system enabled non-Indian stockmen to control reservation range through private leases with competent Indians, undermining coordinated grazing management. Competent Indians frequently collected five-year rentals in advance, then renegotiated leases prematurely, while children's rental income was often dissipated. The documents demonstrate how the competency framework—ostensibly promoting Indian self-management—actually facilitated external control of Crow lands and fragmented tribal resource governance. Thomas D. Campbell's wartime farming operations had destroyed allotment cornerstones, leaving many allottees unable even to locate their lands.

summaries.display_title: 1993: Big Horn County News Articles on Little Bighorn Battlefield and Crow-Cheyenne Boundary
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1891–1993

This document comprises three newspaper articles and an editorial from the *Big Horn County News*, dated November 10, 1993, written by John A. Young. The articles address events at the Little Bighorn Battlefield National Monument and a proposed Crow/Northern Cheyenne boundary settlement.

The first article reports that National Park Service superintendent Gerard Baker expelled the Custer Battlefield Preservation Committee from the battlefield visitors center, ending its use of a donation box that generated approximately $10,000 annually. Since 1985, the committee had raised over $100,000 and secured more than 1,800 acres of land between Last Stand Hill and the Reno Battlefield for eventual federal acquisition. The federally designated monument itself comprised only 640 acres at Last Stand Hill plus 160 acres at the Reno site. Baker also expressed concern about Crow Tribe plans to build a gambling casino, truck stop, and motel on tribal land adjacent to the battlefield. On October 31, 1993, the Park Service severed its 40-year relationship with the 2,800-member Custer Battlefield Historical & Museum Association, which planned to contribute $50,000 to the Preservation Committee and $7,500 for an archaeological survey.

The second article details a proposed settlement of a surveying error on the 107th Meridian boundary, originating in 1891 when smoke obscured Polaris. Senator Max Baucus announced that Crow Tribal Council Chair Clara Nomee had reached a tentative agreement involving a federal trust fund for the Crow Tribe, BLM coal deposit transfers, and state land exchanges involving an estimated 36,000–46,000 acres. The Crow Tribe sought up to $420 million. Northern Cheyenne representative William Walks Along objected, noting the Cheyenne-affected portion was 25 miles long and 0.8 miles wide. The settlement required presidential, congressional, and Interior Department approval. These documents collectively illustrate ongoing federal mechanisms—Park Service administrative authority, BLM land exchanges, and boundary adjudication—shaping tribal land control in Big Horn County.

summaries.file_name: 1925 Crow CCF 69634-25-312 lifting restrictions on homestead sales.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1929–1930: Legislative Debate over H.R. 9761 — Fee Patents for Crow, Blackfeet, and Fort Belknap Homestead Allotments
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1929–1930

This file (CCF 69634-25-312) documents the legislative and administrative debate over H.R. 9761, a bill introduced by Representative Scott Leavitt to authorize the Secretary of the Interior to issue patents in fee for homestead allotments on the Crow, Blackfeet, and Fort Belknap Reservations in Montana. The file spans from December 1929 through May 1930 and includes correspondence among Superintendent C. H. Asbury (Crow Agency), Superintendent Forrest R. Stone (Blackfeet Agency), Commissioner C. J. Rhoads, First Assistant Secretary J. M. Dixon, Representative Leavitt, and Crow allottee Robert Yellowtail.

The triggering case was Wilbur J. Scott, a one-quarter blood Crow Indian, who had already received patent in fee for approximately 260 acres and sought fee patent on his remaining 640-acre homestead (parcels in Sec. 18, T.4S., R.33E.; Sec. 34, T.2S., R.28E.; and Sec. 21, T.6S., R.32E.). Asbury noted the irrigated Big Horn land's value and cautioned against disposal. The Crow Allotment Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), the Blackfeet Act of June 30, 1919 (41 Stat. 3-16), the Act of June 2, 1924 (43 Stat. 252), and the Fort Belknap Act of March 3, 1921 (41 Stat. 1355-1358) are cited as governing inalienability provisions. Commissioner Rhoads's February 1930 memorandum to the Secretary recommended enactment. Stone vigorously opposed the bill, noting that several hundred Blackfeet fee-patent Indians had already lost their original allotments and retained only eighty-acre homesteads. The Blackfeet Tribal Business Council voted unanimously on May 6, 1930, to protest the bill, telegraphing Senators Walsh and Wheeler and Representative Leavitt. The document demonstrates how federal policy framed lifting restrictions as benefiting Indians in "urgent need of funds" while tribal governments and field superintendents recognized it as a mechanism accelerating permanent land loss.

summaries.display_title: 1963: Crow Tribal Council Resolutions on Section 2 Land Restrictions
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1955–1963

This document is a set of three Crow Tribal Council resolutions (Nos. 63-22A, 63-22B, and 63-22C) enacted unanimously on February 2, 1963, transmitted by the Billings Area Office of the Bureau of Indian Affairs to the Commissioner of Indian Affairs in Washington, D.C. The transmittal letter is signed by the Area Director, with a memorandum from Superintendent Otto K. Weaver of the Crow Agency recommending the resolutions be forwarded as expressions of tribal sentiment.

The resolutions concern the Department of the Interior's failure to enforce Section 2 (also referenced as Section 11) of the Crow Allotment Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), which prohibited non-Indians from purchasing Crow trust land. The Tribe alleges a conspiracy discovered in 1955 between white ranchers and the BIA, whereby trust land sales were approved to build "great land monopolies" in violation of the Act. Three entities are specifically named as illegal acquirers of "many thousands of acres": the Campbell Farming Corporation, the M. H. Tschirgi estate, and the Little Horn Land Company. The resolutions demand that the Justice Department institute civil actions for land restoration, condemn the BIA's complicity in attempting congressional legislation (six failed bills) to retroactively legalize the illegal sales, and note that the Attorney General had refused for three years to enforce restitution despite a 1960 request from the former Secretary of the Interior. The documents demonstrate active tribal resistance to dispossession and institutional collusion between the BIA and non-Indian ranching interests to circumvent statutory protections for Crow trust lands.

summaries.file_name: 1951 Billings Area Office fee patent legislation 1951.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1951–1953: Billings Area Office Records on Fee Patent Legislation and Non-Indian Exploitation of Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1951–1953

This file contains records from the Bureau of Indian Affairs Billings Area Office (RG 075, Central Decimal Subject Files, 1936–1958, Box 5) concerning fee patent legislation affecting Crow Reservation allottees and a 1953 congressional questionnaire about non-Indian exploitation of reservation resources.

The principal documents are two letters dated February 23, 1951, from Area Director Paul L. Fickinger to the Commissioner of Indian Affairs. The first concerns H.R. 9614, a bill to issue a fee patent to Percival H. Glenn, Crow Allottee No. 2365, for approximately 996.24 acres across multiple parcels in Sections 3, 4, 10, 33, and 34, Townships 1 S and 3 S, Range 27 E. Fickinger deems Glenn "completely competent" and "self-supporting" but recommends the bill be amended to completely sever Glenn's legal status as an Indian, removing all federal responsibility while preserving his equity in tribal corporate assets and inherited interests—though inherited land would convert from trust to fee status. The second letter addresses H.R. 2349, proposing fee patents for Mark and Estella Wolfe covering approximately 2,160 acres. Fickinger objects to alienating this sizeable block but again recommends that if patents issue, legislation should completely sever the Wolfes' Indian status. He warns that "the lands of the Indian people are fast getting away" and advocates that every fee patent carry full federal severance.

The 1953 questionnaire from the House Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs systematically probes non-Indian business exploitation of reservation resources—leases, lobbying, and influence on liquidation policy—demonstrating congressional awareness that fee patenting facilitated dispossession. Together these documents reveal how termination-era ideology embedded in individual fee patent bills served as a mechanism for alienating substantial Crow land holdings.

summaries.display_title: 1942–1951: BIA Records on Cash Sale of Crow Allotment — Edward Wilbur Spang Estate
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1942–1951

This file documents the cash sale of Crow Indian allotment lands, centering on Allotment No. 3526, the 934.36-acre allotment of Edward Wilbur Spang (deceased), located in T.4 S., R.38 E., P.M., Montana. The sole heir, Alphonso Spang (father, age 63, Northern Cheyenne enrollee), petitioned for sale on November 27, 1950, stating he wished to invest proceeds in land on the Northern Cheyenne Reservation. The land was appraised at $8,000. Superintendent L.C. Lippert of Crow Agency transmitted the petition through Area Director Paul L. Fickinger (Billings Area Office) to the Commissioner of Indian Affairs, who authorized the sale on March 20, 1951. Sealed bids were opened May 4, 1951; Joe E. Hope of Kirby, Montana, was the successful bidder at $10,110.00 ($10.82/acre), outbidding Oscar H. Blankenship ($5,048.20 on 587 acres) and W.B. Spear ($2,989.36 on 347.60 acres). Mineral rights were reserved to the Crow Tribe under the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751). The sale was conducted under the Act of March 15, 1934 (48 Stat. 647). A patent in fee was requested from the Bureau of Land Management pursuant to Secretarial Order No. 2252 (September 9, 1946). The file also references concurrent sales of allotments 3061 (James Biglake, 160 acres), 3097 (James Brown, 23.69 acres), and 3874 (Elizabeth Spotted Door, 163.03 acres). The documents demonstrate the systematic conversion of trust allotments to fee-patented land through competitive bidding, transferring nearly 1,000 acres of Crow reservation land to non-Indian purchasers.

summaries.display_title: 1919: Senate Hearing on S. 2890 — Allotment of Remaining Crow Reservation Lands
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1887–1920

This document is a printed hearing transcript before the Senate Committee on Indian Affairs, chaired by Senator Charles Curtis of Kansas, held September 3, 1919, regarding S. 2890, a bill to allot remaining Crow Reservation lands in Montana, distribute tribal funds, and address related matters. Key participants include Robert Yellowtail and other Crow delegates (Frank Yarlott, John Frost, Thomas Medicine Horse, Sits Down Spotted, Mark Wolfe, Hartford Bear Claw), Assistant Commissioner E.B. Meritt, and Superintendent Asbury. Senators Walsh of Montana, McNary, Nelson, Johnson, and McCormick were present.

The bill proposed allotting 160 acres to every enrolled member who died unallotted after December 31, 1905, and prorating remaining allotable lands equally by acreage among living members. It invoked the General Allotment Act of February 8, 1887 (24 Stat. 388) and proposed repealing portions of the Act of April 27, 1904 (33 Stat. 352) governing Crow trust funds and tribal herd disposition. Section 5 reserved all minerals—oil, gas, coal—for tribal benefit for 50 years, after which they would pass to individual allottees. Section 6 appropriated $50,000 for surveys, reimbursable from tribal funds. A three-person competency commission would classify members as competent (receiving fee patents) or incompetent (receiving trust patents). Homestead designations of up to 640 acres would remain inalienable for 25 years. Irrigation construction charges would attach as liens with proposed 10-year repayment (Curtis urged 20–25 years). Section 10 conferred Court of Claims jurisdiction over all tribal claims against the United States.

The hearing reveals the mechanisms of dispossession: competency determinations enabling fee patenting and immediate alienability; irrigation liens enforceable by foreclosure; pressure from the American Legion for soldier homesteading on Crow lands; and Curtis's explicit proposal to sell incompetent Indians' "surplus" lands on 20–25 year terms to settlers. Yellowtail's advocacy for tribal consent, elective patent types, and protection of water-power sites at the Big Horn River demonstrates Crow resistance to administrative control, while the committee's power to override tribal preferences—acknowledged openly—illustrates the fundamental asymmetry governing allotment-era dispossession.

summaries.display_title: c. 1920: Office of Indian Affairs Routing Slip — Crow File
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: undated

This document is a brief administrative routing slip or file cover sheet from the Office of Indian Affairs (a predecessor/alternate name for the Bureau of Indian Affairs). It contains minimal substantive content, consisting primarily of bureaucratic filing notations. The form includes fields for a file number, charge information, remarks, and cross-referencing to other file numbers, with a date field left incomplete (showing only "19" for the century prefix). Two handwritten file numbers are partially legible: what appears to be "8-16-23" or similar, and "6-326-242" or a close variant, though the OCR and handwriting make precise transcription uncertain.

The document's purpose was purely clerical — to track, route, or cross-reference files within the Office of Indian Affairs' record-keeping system. It is associated with the Crow Reservation based on its archival classification (42530-20-312 Crow), suggesting it relates to administrative actions concerning the Crow tribe. However, the slip itself contains no substantive information about land transactions, allotments, acreages, dollar amounts, legal mechanisms, or specific policy decisions. Its evidentiary value lies primarily in demonstrating the bureaucratic infrastructure through which federal Indian affairs records were managed, and in its potential to link to other substantive files through its cross-reference numbers. The fragmentary nature and lack of content severely limit its standalone analytical utility regarding land dispossession.

summaries.file_name: Ballensky, A.G. of Hardin, Montana - Investigation of loans to Indians by Ballensky Crow Agency (97-258 box 2).pdf
summaries.display_title: 1953: BIA Investigation of Unauthorized Loans to Crow Members by A.G. Ballensky of Hardin, Montana
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1953–1953

This document file comprises confidential correspondence, legal plats, and land maps from the Bureau of Indian Affairs relating to an investigation of unauthorized loans made by A.G. Ballensky of Hardin, Montana, to members of the Crow Tribe. The central document is a confidential letter dated May 14, 1953, from Superintendent L.C. Lippert of the Crow Indian Agency to Area Director Paul L. Fickinger at the Billings Area Office, forwarding multiple assignments of Indian income submitted by attorney Bert W. Kronmiller on Ballensky's behalf. The named Crow individuals who signed assignments were John Takes Gun, Marie Bull Chief, Louis Reed, James Brown, Virginia Little Light, Ralph Brown, Rose Plenty Good, Joseph Rock Above, Elizabeth Gun Shows, and Sarah Bull Chief. Kronmiller's April 23, 1953 letter reveals Ballensky advanced funds to these individuals based on their representations that they had obtained or were obtaining patents in fee to their allotted lands.

Lippert refused to recognize any assignments, noting the agency had not previously authorized such indebtedness. A May 26, 1953 letter to Area Counsel Harlow Pease details specific allotment parcels in Township 6 South, Range 31 East—including quarter-sections in Sections 11, 15, 16, and 23—all Indian trust lands under lease to Ballensky. Accompanying plats distinguish trust lands leased to Ballensky from lands he owned outright per county records. Lippert suspected Ballensky was attempting to force the agency to collect debts from Indian income, that patent-in-fee laws may have been intentionally violated, and that at least one assignment (John Takes Gun's) was signed under duress or intoxication. He recommended referral to the Department of Justice. The documents demonstrate a pattern of exploitative lending tied to anticipated fee patents, a classic mechanism of land dispossession on the Crow Reservation.

summaries.display_title: 1920–1960: BIA and Solicitor Records on Violations of Crow Act Section 2 Acreage Limitations
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1960

This file comprises inter-agency correspondence, legal opinions, memoranda, and press releases from the Bureau of Indian Affairs, the Office of the Solicitor, and the Office of the Secretary of the Interior concerning violations of the acreage limitations in Section 2 of the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 752), as amended by the Act of June 8, 1940 (54 Stat. 258), governing sales of Crow Reservation lands in Montana. The central issue is that for decades—particularly from 1942 through 1955—Bureau-supervised sales were conducted without enforcing the statutory ceilings (640 acres agricultural, 1,280 acres grazing for initial ownership; 1,280 agricultural and 1,920 grazing as absolute maximums), enabling non-Indian landowners to accumulate holdings in violation of the law. Commissioner Glenn L. Emmons suspended all Crow land sales in late 1955 upon discovering the violations.
