A May 23, 1957 Field Solicitor opinion by Harlow Pease construed "or" as "and" in the statute, permitting diversified operations but maintaining category-specific ceilings. The July 9, 1957 supervised sale of 58 tracts totaling 9,942.95 acres achieved bids on 50% of tracts. A December 7, 1959 Solicitor's opinion (G-59-1139.9) ruled Jo Annette Hammond—owning 1,435.08 acres grazing and 160 acres agricultural—ineligible to purchase any additional Crow land. On April 6, 1960, the Solicitor transmitted a recommendation to the Department of Justice to institute civil actions to void illegal conveyances. Assistant Secretary Roger Ernst's April 22, 1960 letter to Crow Tribal Council Chairman Edward F. Whiteman confirmed the Department sought judicial quiet title actions. The supplemental report to Congressman James A. Haley (March 11, 1958) withdrew support for S. 332, which would have validated illegal titles. The documents collectively demonstrate systematic administrative failure to enforce congressionally mandated protections against non-Indian land monopolization on the Crow Reservation over a 35-year period, with penalties of up to $5,000 and six months imprisonment going unenforced.

summaries.display_title: 1920–1929: Dispute over Erroneous Fee Patent Issuance to Barney and Mary One Goose — Crow Mineral Rights
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1929

This file from the Bureau of Indian Affairs Central Classified Files (Record Group 75, Crow Agency, File 15611-26-313) documents a dispute over mineral rights on Crow Reservation allotted land, centering on the erroneous issuance of fee patent No. 913115/913116 to Barney One Goose (allottee No. 1563) and Mary One Goose (allottee No. 1564). The patents, issued July 26, 1923, covered the N½ of Section 2 and the S½ SW¼ and NE¼ SW¼ of Section 20, Township 8 South, Range 36 East, Montana (120 acres), but omitted the mineral reservation clause required by Section 6 of the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751). That statute explicitly reserved all minerals, including coal, oil, and gas, for the benefit of the Crow Tribe in common.

Superintendent C.H. Asbury reported on March 24 and October 1, 1926, that the land had passed to a white purchaser named Chapman, who claimed mineral rights based on the defective patent and intended to open a coal mine near the railroad at Wyola. Asbury had issued informal prospecting permits and attempted to collect royalties for the tribe. The General Land Office confirmed the land was classified non-coal (April 30, 1918), but the Commissioner and Assistant Secretary John H. Edwards (January 21, 1928 letter to Senator John B. Kendrick) ruled that the statutory mineral reservation applied regardless of classification, and the patent's omission could not defeat the Act of Congress. The Department threatened litigation through the Department of Justice if Chapman resisted, while Chapman refused to surrender the patent for reissuance. The October 3, 1925 Solicitor's opinion established the broader principle that mineral reservations applied to all Crow and Blackfeet allotments under the 1920 acts, whether classified mineral or not, demonstrating how clerical errors in the General Land Office facilitated private appropriation of tribal mineral resources.

summaries.display_title: 1924: Crow Agency Correspondence on the Indian Citizenship Act
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1924–1924

This file consists of two pieces of correspondence between Superintendent C. H. Asbury of the Crow Agency in Montana and the Bureau of Indian Affairs in Washington, D.C., concerning the Indian Citizenship Act of 1924. Asbury's letter of June 9, 1924, requests a copy of the newly signed act granting citizenship to all Indians born in the United States, which President Coolidge signed on June 2, 1924. Assistant Commissioner E. B. Meritt responded on June 20, 1924, enclosing a copy of the act (filed under number 44727).

Asbury's letter is notable for its candid paternalism and anxiety about the political implications of Indian enfranchisement on the Crow Reservation. He warns that citizenship and voting rights would "open up a way for the Indian politician and grafter," claiming such figures exist "on most reservations" and would sell their influence to outside interests or candidates. He advocates state-level educational requirements for Indian voters, asserting that "many Indians are absolutely incapable of voting intelligently." He frames literacy tests as potential incentives for education rather than as tools of disenfranchisement.

The document reveals how federal field administrators viewed Indian citizenship not as a step toward self-determination but as a threat to their administrative control. Asbury's endorsement of educational voting restrictions parallels contemporaneous Jim Crow mechanisms used to suppress Black voting in the South, demonstrating that land dispossession and political marginalization operated in tandem through both federal policy and anticipated state legislation targeting newly enfranchised Indigenous peoples.

summaries.display_title: 1920–1957: Billings Area Office Report on Proposed Amendments to the Crow Allotment Act of 1920
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1957

This document is a compilation from the Bureau of Indian Affairs Central Classified Files (RG 75, Entry 12, Box 6) concerning proposals to amend the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), which governed allotment of Crow Tribe lands and imposed acreage limitations on conveyances. The core document is a report prepared by the Billings Area Office, authored by Area Director Percy E. Melis and transmitted to the Commissioner of Indian Affairs on October 16, 1957, for legislative hearings conducted October 18–19 by the Senate Subcommittee on Indian Affairs.

The report details that the Crow Reservation contained 2,282,764 acres, of which approximately 1,975,000 acres were allotted in roughly 3,800 allotments (approximately 10,000 separate tracts), with 266,763 acres remaining in tribal ownership. Between 1946 and 1955, supervised sales disposed of 111,315 acres across 517 tracts at an average appraised value of $10.27/acre, yielding $1,614,622 ($14.50/acre average)—significantly above appraisal but below comparable private sales averaging $13.15/acre for 31,518 acres. The report acknowledges a roughly 60% increase in land values during this period and notes the Field Solicitor flagged in fall 1955 that the Bureau failed to implement Section 2's acreage limitations during advertised trust land sales.

Senate Bill S. 3698 sought to repeal Section 2's acreage limitations and validate prior conveyances made in violation thereof. The Crow Tribal Council, chaired by Edward P. Whiteman, endorsed the bill after receiving assurances that mineral rights (oil and gas) would be retained by the Tribe. Senator James E. Murray pressed for expedited consideration. Attorneys for the Crow Reservation Association (representing non-Indian landowners) argued Section 2 was unconstitutional and urged passage. The document demonstrates how federal administrative inaction on statutory protections, combined with legislative repeal efforts, facilitated large-scale transfer of allotted Crow lands to non-Indian purchasers.

summaries.display_title: 1938–1941: Crow Tribal Council Minutes, Resolutions, and Correspondence
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1935–1941

This document is a multi-part compilation from Bureau of Indian Affairs Central Classified Files (Record Group 75, Crow Agency, File 69154, Part 2), digitized from the Mansfield Library at the University of Montana via JSTOR. It comprises minutes, resolutions, correspondence, and petitions from Crow Tribal Council meetings spanning 1938–1941, along with Office of Indian Affairs administrative responses.

The records document extensive Crow Tribal Council deliberations over oil, mineral, and grazing leases on the Crow Reservation. Key matters include: the Midland Empire Oil Company's Soap Creek tribal oil lease (560 acres of tribal oil land), which the Council repeatedly sought to cancel for non-performance; the Sun Ray Oil Company's attempted acquisition of Midland's interests, including a $10,000 partial payment on a refinery; William Lynde's request to re-drill the abandoned Lodge Grass Oil Structure under 12.5% royalty terms, distinguishing between pre-1920 allotments carrying individual mineral rights and post-Crow Act (June 4, 1920) allotments reserving minerals to the tribe for 50 years; H.M. Simmons' bentonite application covering 340 acres at $5 bonus and 10% per ton royalty; and the Big Horn Placer Gold Mining Corporation's request for a 15% royalty reduction.

The Council also addressed: cancellation of irrigation construction and operation/maintenance charges; repeal of the Amendatory Act of June 20, 1936 (49 Stat. 244); per capita payments of $10 from tribal funds ($20,031.79 available); $2,000 grazing fees from the buffalo pasture; $800 for buffalo corrals; $650 for fair purposes; $260 for portable irrigation equipment; $300 for Council office supplies; delegation expenses at $5–$8 per diem; and the Jurisdictional Act of July 3, 1926 (44 Stat. 807). Superintendent Robert Yellowtail and Assistant Commissioner J.C. McCaskill exchanged correspondence about procedural compliance with Office Circular of August 16, 1938. Council members including Russell White Bear, Max Big Man, Charles Yarlott, and Ties His Knees articulated frustrations over decades of unrealized mineral revenues, leasing authority disputes with the Forestry Department, and the inability of post-1920 Crow children to receive allotments or benefits. These records collectively demonstrate systematic dispossession through bureaucratic delay, unfavorable lease terms, unproductive mineral development, irrigation debt burdens, and federal control over tribal fund expenditures.

summaries.file_name: 1910 DIVISION OF LANDS AND FUNDS OF CROW INDIANS, ETC.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1910: Senate Committee Report on S. 6995 — Division of Crow Lands and Funds
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1904–1911

This document is a Senate committee report (Report to accompany S. 6995), submitted April 11, 1910, by Mr. Page from the Committee on Indian Affairs, recommending a substitute bill for the division of lands and funds of the Crow Indians in Montana. Senator Dixon introduced both the original bill (S. 3338) and the revised S. 6995, the latter reportedly at the instance of a Crow delegation that appeared before the committee.

The Crow Reservation then encompassed approximately 2,700,000 acres inhabited by roughly 1,700 Indians—about 1,600 acres per person. The bill's mechanism was to allot each tribal member a 160-acre inalienable, nontaxable homestead (with at least 40 irrigated acres where practicable) for a 25-year trust period, then distribute remaining lands in successive 160-acre selections designated "surplus land," which became taxable after three years and alienable under Secretary of the Interior supervision. Approximately 2,000,000 acres were expected to open to white settlement. The government had already spent $900,000 irrigating about 50,000 acres. Coal, oil, and gas rights were reserved tribally for 25 years, leasable in tracts up to 2,560 acres for 15-year terms. Tribal funds were to accumulate into a $1,000,000 trust fund at 4% interest. Sections 16 and 36 were granted to Montana for schools at $1.25/acre. The bill appropriated $170,000 for state land grants and agency reserves, plus $75,000 from tribal funds for surveys. It amended the April 27, 1904 Act ratifying an earlier Crow agreement. Individual allottees received only 50% of surplus land sale proceeds immediately; the remainder was held in segregated Treasury accounts. This legislation exemplifies systematic dispossession through allotment-and-surplus frameworks, converting collective tribal holdings into individually alienable parcels while channeling the vast majority of reservation acreage to non-Indian settlers.

summaries.file_name: 1908 T'E S;UIWtrEY ANI) ALLOTM1ENT OF INI)IAN LAND)S VITIl1IN THIELL1i11TS OF THE CROW INJ)IATN RESERVATION, M1ONTANA.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1908: Senate Hearings on Survey, Allotment, and Disposal of Surplus Crow Reservation Lands
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1882–1908

This document is Senate Document 445 of the 60th Congress, comprising hearings before the Senate Committee on Indian Affairs held April 1–4, 1908, on two bills: S. 2087 (incorporating a horse-breeding company on the Crow Reservation, Montana) and S. 2963 (surveying, allotting, and disposing of surplus Crow lands). The committee, chaired by Senator Moses B. Clapp, heard testimony from Commissioner Francis E. Leupp, Secretary of the Interior James R. Garfield, journalist and activist Helen Pierce Grey, Indian Rights Association secretary Matthew K. Sniffen, Agent Samuel G. Reynolds, and Chief Plenty Coos through interpreters.

Grey's extensive testimony alleged systematic dispossession of the approximately 1,700 Crow Indians on their roughly 1.8-million-acre reservation. She charged that grazing permits were fraudulently awarded—Charles M. Bair received sheep permits for 35,000 head on 400,000 unallotted acres at $7,000 over a competing $8,600 bid from T. J. Murphy of Helena. She alleged the F. M. Heinrich (Frank Henry) cattle operation, paying only $10,750 over eight years for permits originally arranged informally between the agent and lessee, actually ran 10,000–20,000 head while paying for roughly 3,000, with Agent Reynolds holding an undisclosed ownership interest through his registered X Diamond X brand. Carl Leiter drew $75/month as Government "boss herder" for a nonexistent tribal herd while acting as Heinrich's agent. E. L. Dana, the largest lessee, paid approximately $70,000 over five years and estimated Heinrich underpaid Indians by $25,000. Grey charged that approximately $40,000 in annual lease revenue never reached individual Indians, that $400,000–$500,000 of tribal funds spent on irrigation ditches produced little benefit, and that $30,000 annually appropriated for twenty-five years under the Act of April 11, 1882, for houses, seeds, and fences was largely unaccounted for on the ground.

Grey further alleged the Lincoln Townsite Company—linked to Burlington Railroad officials—acquired thirteen town sites under the Huntley irrigation project and dead allotments along the Big Horn River, grouped there by allotting agent John K. Rankin. She described inherited-land sales at the agency where orphan allotments, with Reynolds appointed guardian, passed to company affiliates at fractions of value. The Milliken family land near Big Timber, worth $22,000, was targeted for purchase at $9,000 by Thomas Ross of Billings after Reynolds allegedly coached the epileptic, near-incompetent Claude Milliken to sign papers secretly. Grey cited the Act of 1882 allotment provisions, a power of attorney executed during John E. Edwards's agency around 1901–1902 that altered treaty terms without tribal knowledge, and the resulting failure to pay the $1,150,000 treaty price for ceded lands.

Sniffen's testimony transmitted S. M. Brosius's July 23, 1907, memorandum to Commissioner Leupp cataloguing eleven categories of charges including misappropriation of inherited-land sale proceeds, Government farmers pasturing private stock and farming allotted land for profit, use of Government threshers for private benefit, irregular grazing permits, and conditions in the agency jail where men, women, and girls were confined together without fire or sanitation. Sniffen detailed his own January 24, 1908, arrest and expulsion from the reservation by Clerk Fred Miller under Reynolds's orders, the first such exclusion of an Indian Rights Association agent in twenty-five years. Secretary Garfield's December 13, 1907, letter declining reinvestigation was read into the record, as was the association's March 13, 1908, protest that the Department simultaneously demanded new evidence while preventing its collection.

Inspector Z. Lewis Dalby's investigation—ordered by Secretary Garfield at President Roosevelt's direction after Grey's April 1907 White House visit—was extensively criticized. Dalby, Garfield's former private secretary with no western experience, alienated the Indians at the first council by calling Joe Cooper "a God damn liar" and ordering him from the tent, then claimed to have tallied approximately 1,000 cattle including 250–300

summaries.display_title: 1935–1944: Crow Tribal Council Minutes and Resolutions
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1935–1944

This document is a Bureau of Indian Affairs Central Classified File (Record Group 75, File 69154, Part 3) from Crow Agency, Montana, classified under decimal code 054 (Tribal Council proceedings). It comprises minutes, summaries, resolutions, and transmittal letters spanning Crow Tribal Council meetings from January 1935 through August 1944, forwarded by Superintendent Robert Yellowtail to the Commissioner of Indian Affairs in Chicago.

The records document extensive debates over tribal economic self-determination and land use. Key subjects include: the revolving cattle program, under which 1,186 head were originally issued in 1934–35 with 542 head still owed to the government; authorization of $5,000 per year from tribal funds for cattle purchases over a 20-year program running to 1962; a $50,000 tribal reimbursable loan fund (accumulated to nearly $60,000) with $22,000 outstanding in approximately 250 loans; and Secretary Ickes's October 1942 letter conditioning a $10 per capita payment on tribal acceptance of a cattle and credit program. Specific resolutions authorized $1,500 for garden seeds, $250 for WPA sewing, $5,407.50 for 300 elk carcasses from Yellowstone Park, and $600 for seed potatoes. Oil leasing is prominent: the Carter Oil Company offered a $1,000 bonus for a direct lease at Mifflin Dome waiving advertising, approved unanimously on November 22, 1943; the Midland Oil Company received a time extension for Soap Creek; and the H. Lee Welsh Company's application was rejected. The Council also extended tribal attorney Charles J. Kappler's contract and debated the Shoshone claim recovery of $5,000,000. A liquor repeal resolution cited Section 24, Title 25 U.S.C.A. and the Crow Act of June 4, 1920, as amended May 26, 1926. The Act of June 20, 1936 (44 Stat. 244, Public No. 718, 74th Congress) and HR 2105 (revolving fund bill) are repeatedly invoked. The proceedings reveal tensions over BIA paternalism, reimbursable collections from individual lease incomes, and resistance to Zimmerman's August 7, 1942 circular mandating monthly budget disbursements. Collectively, these records demonstrate how federal administrative mechanisms—loan repayment enforcement, conditional per capita payments, lease advertising rules, and budgetary controls—constrained Crow economic autonomy and land use despite nominal tribal consent processes.

summaries.file_name: 1919 Crow CCF 94816-19-312 William Bends fee patent app.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1919–1920: Fee Patent Application and Denial for William Bends, Crow Allotment No. 1000
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1919–1920

This file documents the application and denial of a fee patent for William Bends, a full-blood Crow Indian, covering his Allotment No. 1000 on the Crow Reservation. The allotment consisted of 320 acres described as SE¼ NW¼ of Sec. 25, T. 2 S., R. 34 E.; NE¼ of Sec. 34; NE¼ NW¼ of Sec. 35; and SE¼ SE¼ of Sec. 27, T. 3 S., R. 34 E., Montana Meridian, valued at approximately $5,000. The allotment was made under the General Allotment Act of February 8, 1887 (24 Stat. L., 388), and the fee patent application invoked the Burke Act of May 8, 1906 (34 Stat. L., 182).

The superintendent's report, filed October 29, 1919, recommended against issuing the patent, stating Bends had "very little" business experience, had "not even farmed his own allotment," and that granting the patent would result in the land being "sold and money wasted." The superintendent noted Bends had been "urged to get patent for part of land by a white neighbor," revealing external pressure driving dispossession. The Office of Indian Affairs, through Chief Clerk C. F. Hauke, denied the application on December 13, 1919. S. G. Reynolds of Billings, Montana, a former Crow Agency official, wrote on January 15, 1920, supporting Bends but acknowledging the office's discretion. Bends was age 35, married, and described as "fairly" industrious. The file illustrates both the paternalistic trust mechanism restricting Indigenous land autonomy and the predatory interest of white neighbors seeking to facilitate alienation of allotted lands.

summaries.display_title: 1928: Crow Agency Correspondence on Proposed Land Exchange for Pearl Scott Loukes
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1928–1928

This file consists of correspondence between Superintendent C.H. Asbury of the Crow Agency in Montana and Commissioner of Indian Affairs Charles H. Burke regarding a proposed land exchange involving Mrs. Pearl Scott Loukes, a 1/4 Indian blood enrolled Crow member married to a white man. Asbury's letter of March 27, 1928, explains that Loukes holds approximately 600–700 acres of trust land scattered across three or four tracts, all located in remote areas unsuitable for farming and too far from market to be economically viable. An unnamed "large stockman"—a white man holding patented (fee) land throughout the reservation—offered to exchange consolidated, usable land for Loukes's scattered parcels, either acre-for-acre or on other mutually agreeable terms. Burke's reply of April 26, 1928, identifies the central legal problem: land conveyed to an Indian via restricted deed would likely remain subject to state taxation despite alienation restrictions, potentially causing Loukes to lose her new holdings through tax default. Burke authorized the exchange mechanism of a restricted deed to Loukes accompanied by proper abstracts and a fee patent to the white owner, but only if Asbury could certify Loukes was competent to maintain tax payments. The document illustrates how federal trust-to-fee conversion policies facilitated consolidation of reservation land into white ranchers' hands, using the administrative discretion of BIA superintendents and the structural vulnerability of scattered, restricted allotments to justify transfers that served settler stockmen's interests. OCR quality is poor but the substance is recoverable.

summaries.file_name: 1925 Crow Land Sale Bill (NAID 1923736) copy copy.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1925: BIA Public Notice of Sealed-Bid Auction of Crow Indian Allotment Lands
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1925–1928

This document is a public notice and land sale broadside issued by the Department of the Interior, United States Indian Service, Crow Agency, Montana, dated August 21, 1925, and signed by Superintendent C. H. Asbury. It announces a sealed-bid auction of inherited and "Non-Competent" Crow Indian allotment lands scheduled for October 21, 1925, at 2:00 p.m. The notice lists hundreds of individual allotments organized by district—Black Lodge (No. 1), Reno (No. 2), Lodge Grass (No. 3), Wyola (No. 4), Big Horn (No. 5), and Pryor (No. 7)—plus lands outside the present Crow Reservation. Each entry identifies the allottee by name and allotment number, legal land description (section, township, range), acreage (ranging from 17.36 to 640 acres), appraised value (from $100 to $8,360), and lease expiration dates (mostly November 30, 1928, or December 31, 1928).

The sale operates under regulations approved May 1, 1922, and the Crow Act of June 4, 1920. The notice quotes Section 2 of the Crow Act, which caps individual non-Indian ownership at 640 acres agricultural or 1,920 acres grazing land and declares violations a misdemeanor punishable by $5,000 fine or six months imprisonment. Section 8 is quoted establishing irrigation construction costs as reimbursable liens against the land regardless of ownership, estimated at $20–$50 per acre payable over twenty years, plus annual operation and maintenance charges of $1.50 per acre. Payment terms include a cash plan (10% with bid, 90% within thirty days) or deferred plan at 8% interest over three years. Mineral rights are reserved. The document demonstrates systematic, large-scale dispossession of Crow allotments through federally administered sales, converting trust-patent lands to fee-simple ownership and transferring them to non-Indian purchasers under bureaucratic procedures that, despite nominal protections, facilitated massive land loss across every district of the reservation.

summaries.file_name: 1956 Unpublished Hearing To Amend the Act of June 4, 1920, as Amended, Providing for Allotment of Lands of the Crow Tribe.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1956: Unpublished House Hearing on S. 3698 — Amendments to the Crow Allotment Act of 1920
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1956

This document is an unpublished 1956 hearing transcript from the House Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs, reproduced from the National Archives, concerning S. 3698 (with companion bill H.R. 11036 by Congressman Metcalf), which sought to amend the Crow Allotment Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), as previously amended by the Act of June 8, 1940 (54 Stat. 252). The hearing includes a read statement by Senator James E. Murray of Montana and remarks by Representatives Udall, Aspinall, and Metcalf.

The bill's purpose was to repeal the acreage limitation in Section 2 of the 1920 Crow Act restricting how much reservation land a non-Indian could own, and to validate, ratify, and confirm all prior conveyances made in violation of those limitations. Murray's statement reveals that for approximately thirty-five years, the acreage restrictions were continuously violated and ignored until an attorney in the Field Solicitor's office in Billings, Montana, discovered the situation, prompting the Commissioner of Indian Affairs to suspend all Crow Reservation land sales and order an investigation. Fee simple patents—rather than restricted fee patents—had been routinely issued, enabling large acreages to accumulate in individual white landowners' hands. The Crow Tribal Council adopted Resolution No. 77 on April 11, 1956, supporting the legislation, and reaffirmed it on July 14. The bill preserved tribal mineral rights, including oil and gas, under the fifty-year mineral reservation, and preserved Indians' rights to challenge conveyances for fraud or lack of consideration. The committee voted favorably. The bill passed the Senate June 11, 1956. This document demonstrates how decades of federal administrative negligence facilitated systematic non-Indian land accumulation on the Crow Reservation, which Congress then retroactively legalized rather than remedied.

summaries.file_name: Order of Disqualification of Judge Battin and transferring of Case to Hon. Russel E. Smith.p288.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1971: Order Disqualifying Judge Battin and Transferring Civil Case No. 891 — Montana District Court
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1971–1971

This document is a judicial order of disqualification and case transfer filed in the United States District Court for the District of Montana, Billings Division, in Civil No. 891. Dated August 9, 1971, and filed August 12, 1971, the order records Judge James F. Battin's recusal from the case due to "prior familiarity with the factual situation which gave rise to this case," transferring all further proceedings to Judge Russell E. Smith, who formally accepted the transfer.

The plaintiff is Phoebe Wilson Dillon, likely a Crow tribal member given the context of the collection. The defendants include multiple entities tied to land acquisition on or near the Crow Reservation: Antler Land Company of Wyola (a Montana corporation), Little Horn Land Company, Little Horn Land and Livestock Company, Union Bank and Trust Company as executor of the Estate of Bertha Tschirgi, Rex Hibbs as executor of the Estate of F. M. Tschirgi, Francis J. Murtha as trustee for the Trustees of Central States, Southeast and Southwest Areas Pension Fund, and the United States of America. The presence of the United States as a defendant suggests federal trust responsibility failures, while the corporate defendants—land and livestock companies operating near Wyola on the Crow Reservation—point to systematic acquisition of Indian lands by non-Indian corporate interests. Judge Battin's recusal for prior familiarity implies his involvement in earlier transactions or proceedings related to these land dealings, underscoring the entanglement of federal judicial actors in Crow land dispossession. No specific acreages or dollar amounts appear in this procedural document.

summaries.file_name: Dillon (Phoebe Wilson) v. Antler Land Co. of Wyola, 421 U.S. 992 (1975). Reply Brief (on Petition).pdf
summaries.display_title: 1975: Reply Brief — Dillon v. Antler Land Co. of Wyola, U.S. Supreme Court Certiorari Petition
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1975–1975

This document is a Reply Brief on Petition for Certiorari filed on March 20, 1975, in the case of Dillon (Phoebe Wilson) v. Antler Land Co. of Wyola, 421 U.S. 992 (1975), before the U.S. Supreme Court. The case involves a dispute between a Native American petitioner, Phoebe Wilson Dillon, and the Antler Land Company of Wyola, a locality situated on or near the Crow Reservation in Montana. The Supreme Court citation at 421 U.S. 992 indicates the Court ultimately denied certiorari, declining to hear the case.

Unfortunately, the OCR text for this document is entirely blank across all 27 pages, rendering substantive analysis of the legal arguments, specific claims, acreages, allotment numbers, dollar amounts, statutes invoked, or administrative procedures impossible. Only the bibliographic citation on page 27 is legible, sourced from The Making of Modern Law: U.S. Supreme Court Records and Briefs, 1832–1978, accessed through Gale.

Based on the case name alone, this litigation likely concerned land title or dispossession issues on the Crow Reservation, with the Antler Land Company of Wyola potentially claiming ownership of land originally allotted to Crow tribal members. The Supreme Court's denial of review effectively left the lower court ruling—presumably adverse to the Native American petitioner—intact, illustrating how procedural barriers at the highest judicial level could finalize the dispossession of Indian allotted lands. Without readable text, no further specifics can be confirmed.

summaries.display_title: 1971: Affidavit of R.F. Hibbs in Dillon v. Antler Land Company
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1955–1971

This is an affidavit by R.F. Hibbs, filed June 25, 1971, in the United States District Court for the District of Montana, Billings Division, in Case No. 891, *Phoebe Wilson Dillon v. Antler Land Company of Wyola, Little Horn Land Company, Antler Land Company, Little Horn Land and Livestock Company, Union Bank and Trust Company (Executor of the Estate of Bertha Tschirgi), Rex Hibbs (Executor of the Estate of F.M. Tschirgi), Francis J. Murtha (Trustee for Central States, Southeast and Southwest Areas Pension Fund), and the United States of America*. Hibbs, an attorney with Hibbs, Sweeney & Colberg of Billings, attests to his personal knowledge of the corporate operations and land transactions involving these entities on the North Half of Section 15, Township 9 South, Range 34 East, MPM, Big Horn County, Montana—land within or adjacent to the Crow Reservation.

Hibbs details a chain of title: M.H. Tschirgi, Bertha Tschirgi, and F.M. Tschirgi were principal stockholders and officers of Antler Land Company, dissolved in 1958. They surrendered their stock in exchange for a September 2, 1958 conveyance of the subject lands, then conveyed those lands to Little Horn Land Company on September 30, 1958, which in turn conveyed to Little Horn Land and Livestock Company on January 17, 1964. That company paid cash and issued stock, assuming Little Horn Land Company's obligations, and received a further conveyance January 7, 1967. Taxes were assessed by Big Horn County against successive titleholders and duly paid. The lands were used for livestock pasturing, fenced, and openly occupied. Hibbs notes that Louis Forsell, named in plaintiff's discovery, served as general counsel for the Crow Tribe. The affidavit establishes continuous non-Indian possession, tax payment, and mesne conveyance chains—evidence marshaled to defend against Dillon's claims, likely challenging the validity of these transfers of what may have been Crow allotment or reservation land.

summaries.display_title: 1967–1973: U.S. District Court Montana Archive Box 49, Civil Cases 885–891
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1967–1973

This document is a box label or inventory sheet for an archival container (Box 49) held among the records of the U.S. District Courts for Montana, accessioned under National Archives identifier 8NN-021-90-017. It indicates that the box contains civil cases numbered 885 through 891, spanning the fiscal year 2008 designation (FY08) for records management purposes, with the underlying case dates ranging from 1967 to 1973. The document itself is a single page serving as a finding aid or container list rather than a substantive legal record.

No specific claims, parties, dollar amounts, acreages, allotment numbers, or legal citations are present on this page. The OCR quality is poor, with numerous fragmentary characters and artifacts that obscure any additional text that may have appeared on the label. As a cover sheet, it provides no direct evidence of land dispossession, but its significance lies in identifying a set of federal civil cases from Montana during a period when litigation over Crow Reservation boundaries, allotment disputes, and tribal resource rights was active. Civil cases 885–891 from this era could potentially involve challenges to federal Indian policy, trespass actions, or disputes arising from the Crow Allotment Act or related statutes. The document's primary value is as a provenance marker directing researchers to the substantive case files contained within the box.

summaries.display_title: 1971: Plaintiff's Memorandum of Counsel Opposing Summary Judgment in Dillon v. Antler Land Company
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1971

This document is a memorandum of counsel filed October 4, 1971, in the U.S. District Court for the District of Montana, Billings Division, Cause No. 891, *Phoebe Wilson Dillon v. Antler Land Company of Wyola et al.* Plaintiff's attorney Thomas E. Towe of Towe, Neely & Ball submitted it opposing defendants' motion for summary judgment. Defendants included Antler Land Company, Little Horn Land Company, Little Horn Land and Livestock Company, Union Bank and Trust Company (executor of Bertha Tschirgi's estate), Rex Hibbs (executor of F.M. Tschirgi's estate), a pension fund trustee, and the United States.

Plaintiff Phoebe Wilson Dillon, an enrolled Crow tribal member, received Allotment No. 3636 containing 1,040 acres under the Crow Allotment Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751). Matt Tschirgi, president of Antler Land Company, induced her to apply for a fee patent by advancing $85 in 1949 and ultimately $3,029.06 before the patent issued September 19, 1955. Tschirgi contracted to purchase at $7.00/acre when the appraised value was $10.00/acre, resulting in $3,120 less than fair value. The memorandum argues the conveyance violated Section 2's acreage limitation (640 acres agricultural/1,280 grazing/1,920 maximum) and 25 U.S.C. §348(5), rendering the warranty deed absolutely void. Testimony from 1957 congressional hearings documented that one corporation alone had acquired 134,678 acres of former Crow allotments, while Antler Land Company controlled over 120,000 acres including leased land—over 40,000 deeded acres by 1958. The memorandum invokes 25 U.S.C. §345 for federal court jurisdiction, argues federal preemption bars state statutes of limitation and laches, and contends the BIA's failure to enforce Section 2 facilitated systematic dispossession through depressed bidding, manipulative leasing at 10-15 cents/acre versus commercial rates, and criminal inducement of trust-land conveyances under 25 U.S.C. §202.

summaries.file_name: Plantiff's Request for Admission of Facts and Genuineness of Documents and Interrogatories with attachments.p313-70.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1971: Plaintiff's Request for Admissions and Interrogatories in Dillon v. Antler Land Company
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1919–1971

This document is a Plaintiff's Request for Admission of Facts and Genuineness of Documents and Interrogatories filed October 4, 1971, in the U.S. District Court for the District of Montana, Billings Division, Case No. 891. The plaintiff is Phoebe Wilson Dillon, a Crow Indian allottee (Allotment No. 3636), represented by Towe, Neely & Ball of Billings. Defendants include Antler Land Company, Little Horn Land Company, Little Horn Land and Livestock Company, Union Bank and Trust Company (executor of Bertha Tschirgi's estate), Rex Hibbs (executor of F.M. Tschirgi's estate), Francis J. Murtha as Trustee for the Central States Pension Fund, and the United States of America.

The filing seeks admissions that Antler Land Company, controlled by M.H. Tschirgi and family, executed an agreement with Dillon and owned over 1,920 acres of grazing land within the Crow Reservation. Big Horn County assessed Antler Land Company 40,021.02 acres in 1958. By 1971, Little Horn Land and Livestock Company controlled over 120,000 acres in Big Horn County. Attached exhibits include a September 1958 quit claim deed from Antler Land Company to M.H. and Bertha Tschirgi and F.M. Tschirgi conveying thousands of acres; a September 1958 warranty deed from the Tschirgis to Little Horn Land Company; a 1964 warranty deed from Antler Land Company to Little Horn Land and Livestock Company reserving 6.25% mineral royalties; and a January 7, 1967 deed of trust securing an $1,800,000 loan from the Teamsters' Central States Pension Fund. A Bureau of Indian Affairs memorandum (July 17, 1963) and a 1964 Commissioner's memorandum confirm that Dillon's fee patent (No. 1154331, issued September 19, 1955) covering 1,040 acres was preceded by a May 21, 1955 agreement selling her land to Antler Land Company for $7,280—a transaction the BIA deemed void under Section 5 of the General Allotment Act of 1887 (25 U.S.C. 348) and potentially violative of Section 2 of the Crow Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751). Despite this finding, a 1967 Department of Justice letter closed the file without bringing suit, effectively abandoning Dillon's claim and enabling the Tschirgi family's massive land consolidation within the Crow Reservation.

summaries.file_name: Brief of Defendants Little Horn Land - Livestock Co.and Francis J. Murtha, as Trustee, etc.p175-246.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1971: Defendants' Brief, Little Horn Land & Livestock Co. and Murtha, in Dillon v. Antler Land Company
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1887–1970

This document is a legal brief filed in the U.S. District Court for the District of Montana, Billings Division, Cause No. 891, *Phoebe Wilson Dillon v. Antler Land Company et al.*, on behalf of defendants Little Horn Land and Livestock Company and Francis J. Murtha, as Trustee for the Central States, Southeast and Southwest Areas Pension Fund. The plaintiff, Phoebe Wilson Dillon, a Crow Indian allottee, sought to cancel all conveyances and return approximately 1,040 acres of Crow Reservation land to herself or to the United States in trust. She alleged that Antler Land Company of Wyola, Montana, fraudulently induced her to apply for a fee patent on February 25, 1955, misrepresenting the land's value as $7,280 when she claimed it exceeded $33,000. The government's official appraisal was $10,400.

The defendants' brief argues the patent in fee issued September 19, 1955, was valid under Section 349, Title 25 U.S.C., the Crow Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751) as amended by acts of 1926 (44 Stat. 658), 1931 (46 Stat. 1495), 1940 (54 Stat. 252), 1948 (62 Stat. 80), 1949 (63 Stat. 695), and 1953 (67 Stat. 42), and Montana's Enabling Act of 1889 (25 Stat. 676). Plaintiff was declared competent in April 1949, received trust patents in 1923–1924, and executed a warranty deed October 6, 1955, to Antler Land Company. Defendants assert that upon issuance of the fee patent, federal jurisdiction was extinguished, Montana state law governed all property rights, and plaintiff's claims were barred by Montana's five-year adverse possession statute, two-year fraud limitation, laches, estoppel by warranty deed, and defendants' status as bona fide purchasers. The brief extensively documents the Bureau of Indian Affairs' thorough investigation prior to patent issuance, including reports from the Credit Loan Examiner, Superintendent, and Realty Division, all recommending approval. The document reveals the systematic legal mechanisms—competency declarations, fee patents, and state law application—through which Crow allotted lands passed irreversibly from trust status to private non-Indian ownership.

summaries.display_title: 1958: House Subcommittee Executive Session Hearings on S. 328 and Related Indian Land Bills
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1905–1958

This document is a transcript of an executive (confidential) session of the House Subcommittee on Indian Affairs, held August 1, 1958, chaired by James A. Haley, with testimony from H. Rex Lee (Associate Commissioner of Indian Affairs) and Lewis A. Sigler (Assistant Legislative Counsel, Department of the Interior). The subcommittee considered nineteen bills in rapid succession, several directly bearing on Native American land and mineral rights.

Key measures include S. 328, amending the Crow Reservation allotment act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), as amended in 1926 (44 Stat. 658), which reserved tribal minerals for 50 years. The bill would authorize leasing under the 1938 Indian Mineral Leasing Act (52 Stat. 347) and clarify that minerals pass to individual allottees or heirs/devisees in 1970, held in trust unless the allottee received a fee patent—effectively creating a mechanism for involuntary competency determinations and fee patenting of mineral estates. The Crow Tribe opposed the bill; Congressman Anderson proposed striking involuntary competency language.

H.R. 11141/S. 3203 addressed the Wind River Reservation, where the 1953 Act (67 Stat. 592) extinguished Indian title to 161,500 acres for $1,009,500 (surface value only, ~$6.25/acre), shifting mineral leasing from competitive tribal leasing to noncompetitive public-domain leasing. Prior competitive leases on 4,910.76 acres had yielded $727,310 in bonuses; 117,473.75 acres were subsequently leased noncompetitively by BLM. The bill would revest mineral title in the Shoshone and Arapahoe Tribes.

S. 3754 authorized a 53,000-acre land exchange between the Navajo Tribe and the United States for the Glen Canyon Dam townsite (Page, Arizona), with equal acreage from McCracken Mesa public lands transferred to the tribe, minerals excluded. S. 3468 doubled Navajo-Hopi road authorization from $20 million to $40 million. S. 2922 authorized Red Lake Chippewa per capita timber payments. S. 2530 clarified Rocky Boy's Reservation land title for the Chippewa Cree Tribe over 45,000+ purchased acres. H.R. 9239 authorized approximately $2 million for irrigation on Cabazon, Augustine, and Torres-Martinez Reservations, creating recordable liens against Indian lands. S. 2592 amended 25 U.S.C. 81 to remove the requirement that tribal contracts be executed before a judge. The document collectively demonstrates multiple mechanisms of dispossession—below-value compensation, involuntary competency determinations, noncompetitive mineral leasing, and lien creation—alongside some remedial legislation.

summaries.file_name: Dillon (Phoebe Wilson) v. Antler Land Co. of Wyola, 421 U.S. 992 (1975). Petition.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1975: Petition for Certiorari, Dillon v. Antler Land Co. of Wyola, 421 U.S. 992
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1975–1975

This document is a petition for certiorari filed with the U.S. Supreme Court on February 3, 1975, in the case of Dillon (Phoebe Wilson) v. Antler Land Co. of Wyola, cited at 421 U.S. 992 (1975). The case involves a dispute between a Crow tribal member (Phoebe Wilson Dillon) and the Antler Land Company, based in Wyola, Montana—a community situated on or near the Crow Reservation. The involvement of a land company operating in Crow country strongly suggests the dispute concerned the alienation or control of allotted Indian land, a common mechanism of dispossession on the Crow Reservation where fee patents, leasing arrangements, and land sales transferred substantial acreage from individual Crow allottees to non-Indian ranching and agricultural interests.

Unfortunately, the OCR text for this document is entirely blank across all 81 pages, rendering it impossible to extract specific details regarding the legal claims, allotment numbers, acreages, dollar amounts, statutes invoked, or the lower court decisions being appealed. The only recoverable information comes from the bibliographic citation on the final page, sourced from The Making of Modern Law: U.S. Supreme Court Records and Briefs collection. The Supreme Court's disposition at 421 U.S. 992 indicates the petition was denied certiorari, meaning the lower court ruling—presumably adverse to the Crow petitioner—stood. This case exemplifies the legal barriers Crow individuals faced in attempting to recover or protect allotted lands from non-Indian commercial entities through the federal court system.

summaries.file_name: Brief of Defts. Little Horn Land - Livestock Co. - Francis J. Murtha re government files.p66-68.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1971: Defendants' Brief on Government Files, Little Horn Land & Livestock Co. and Murtha, in Dillon v. Antler Land Company
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1971–1971

This document consists of competing legal briefs filed in February 1971 in *Phoebe Wilson Dillon v. Antler Land Company et al.*, Cause No. 891, U.S. District Court for the District of Montana, Billings Division. The plaintiff, Phoebe Wilson Dillon, sued multiple corporate and individual defendants—Antler Land Company of Wyola, Little Horn Land Company, Little Horn Land and Livestock Company, Union Bank and Trust Company (executor of the estate of Bertha Tschirgi), Rex Hibbs (executor of the estate of F.M. Tschirgi), Francis J. Murtha as trustee for the Trustees of Central States, Southeast and Southwest Areas Pension Fund—and the United States of America.

The first brief, filed February 24, 1971, by the law firm Crowley, Kilbourne, Haughey, Hanson & Gallagher on behalf of Little Horn Land and Livestock Company and Francis J. Murtha, moved under Rule 34 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure and an Ex Parte Order of February 18, 1971, to compel the United States to deliver to the Clerk of Court all government files pertaining to the plaintiff and the lands at issue, maintained either at the Crow Indian Reservation or the Bureau of Indian Affairs office in Billings. The supporting affidavit of Cale Crowley asserted these records were subject to loss. The second brief, filed February 26, 1971, by U.S. Attorney Otis L. Packwood, opposed the motion, arguing Rule 34 did not authorize such relief and that the claim of potential document loss was an unsupported conclusion. The dispute over BIA and Crow Reservation records reveals that federal government files were central to adjudicating the plaintiff's land claims, and that parties feared destruction or loss of documentary evidence bearing on Native land title and dispossession.

summaries.display_title: 1916–1948: BIA Records on Joseph J. Pickett's Fee Patent Application, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1916–1948

This collection of Bureau of Indian Affairs records documents the prolonged effort by Joseph J. Pickett, Crow allottee No. 372, to obtain a patent in fee for his remaining 1,200 acres on the Crow Reservation, and the federal government's resistance to that effort. Pickett was originally allotted 961.20 acres under the Crow Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751). He received fee patents for 160 acres in 1916 and 161.20 acres in 1923, both of which he sold—spending the proceeds during Navy service in World War I and on personal rehabilitation, respectively. His remaining 640-acre homestead and 560 acres of inherited lands (from Kennuror, deceased, allottee No. 1097) were located in Township 8 South, Range 32 East, controlling key water on Rottengrass Creek across four grazing units.

Congress passed S. 484 directing fee patent issuance, but President Truman vetoed it on July 25, 1947, citing trust obligations to both Pickett and the Crow Tribe, noting that alienation would isolate surrounding Indian lands from water access. Under Secretary Oscar L. Chapman and Acting Commissioner William Zimmerman recommended the veto. Superintendent L.C. Lippert reported Pickett earned approximately $540 in civil service pension and $250 in lease rentals annually, with his wife receiving over $400 in lease income. Despite the veto, Pickett subsequently sold 320 acres of his allotment to Richard Alan Gregory for $3,051 ($9.53/acre, appraised at $2,560), approved in December 1948 with Senator Zales Ecton's intervention. The land was leased to R.T. Spear at 30 cents per acre. The file demonstrates how federal paternalism simultaneously constrained Indian autonomy while failing to prevent incremental dispossession through supervised sales.

summaries.file_name: 1974 Dillon v. Antler Land Co._ 507 F.2d 940.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1974: Ninth Circuit Opinion, Dillon v. Antler Land Company, 507 F.2d 940
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1974

This is a published opinion of the United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit, decided November 6, 1974, in *Dillon v. Antler Land Company*, 507 F.2d 940. The case involves Phoebe Wilson Dillon, a Crow Indian woman who received trust patents in 1923 and 1924 for 1,040 acres of grazing land on the Crow Reservation in Montana. In 1949, at age 34, she obtained competency status and began managing her own leases, raising income from 14.32 cents per acre to 50 cents per acre. On February 25, 1955, she applied for a fee patent, and on May 21, 1955, she agreed to sell the land to Antler Land Company for $7,280—a contract illegal under 25 U.S.C. § 348 because the land was still in trust. Matt Tschirgi, who controlled Antler, allegedly coached her to conceal his involvement. The BIA approved the fee patent on October 3, 1955. On October 6, 1955, Dillon conveyed the land by warranty deed, receiving only $3,711 in cash after deductions. The BIA had appraised the land at $10 per acre ($10,400 total), but Tschirgi refused to pay the $3 per acre difference he had promised. Antler already owned over 1,920 acres of Crow Reservation land, exceeding the acreage limitation in Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act of 1920 (41 Stat. 751). Dillon sued in 1970 seeking to void the deed and recover $33,000 in damages under the Federal Tort Claims Act and 25 U.S.C. § 185. The court affirmed summary judgment against her, holding Montana statutes of limitations barred her claims and that issuance of the fee patent extinguished federal trust obligations. The case powerfully illustrates how fee patenting, predatory purchasing by non-Indian corporations, and the extinguishment of federal fiduciary duties combined to dispossess individual Crow allottees of substantial acreage at grossly inadequate prices.

summaries.file_name: 1994 Crow Tribe of Indians v. Campbell Farming Corp._ 31 F.3d 768.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1994: Ninth Circuit Opinion, Crow Tribe of Indians v. Campbell Farming Corp., 31 F.3d 768
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1995

This is a published opinion of the United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit, *Crow Tribe of Indians v. Campbell Farming Corp.*, 31 F.3d 768 (9th Cir. 1994), argued January 5, 1994, and filed July 20, 1994, with certiorari denied March 20, 1995. Judge William C. Canby Jr. authored the opinion, joined by Judges Wright and Nelson. The Crow Tribe sued Campbell Farming Corporation (allegedly owning over 45,000 acres on the Reservation) and Robert Earl Holding and affiliated companies (allegedly owning 140,000 acres) for violating Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act of 1920, 41 Stat. 751, which prohibited the Secretary of the Interior from approving conveyances to any person or company already owning 640 acres of agricultural or 1,280 acres of grazing land on the Crow Reservation, and declared void any conveyance resulting in ownership exceeding 1,280 acres agricultural or 1,920 acres grazing, with criminal penalties of up to $5,000 fine and six months imprisonment. The Reservation encompassed roughly 2.3 million acres in southeastern Montana.

The court affirmed dismissal, holding the Tribe had no right of action under Section 2 because the statute referenced "any Crow Indian"—individual allottees, not the Tribe collectively. The decision effectively insulated massive non-Indian land accumulations from tribal challenge, demonstrating how allotment-era statutory language, designed to facilitate assimilation and individual land ownership, was later used to deny the Tribe standing to contest precisely the concentrated non-Indian land ownership the statute was meant to prevent—a stark illustration of how legal mechanisms of the allotment era perpetuated dispossession even when protective provisions existed on paper.

summaries.file_name: 1992 Crow Tribe of Indians v. Campbell Farming Corp._ 828 F. Supp. 1468.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1992: Federal District Court Opinion, Crow Tribe of Indians v. Campbell Farming Corp., 828 F. Supp. 1468
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1887–1992

This is a federal district court opinion, *Crow Tribe of Indians v. Campbell Farming Corp.*, 828 F. Supp. 1468 (D. Mont. 1992), decided July 15, 1992, by Judge Jack D. Shanstrom. The Crow Tribe sued Campbell Farming Corporation, Robert Earl Holding, Sinclair Oil Corporation (d/b/a Sunlight Ranch Company), Sun Valley Company, KMH Ranch Company, and 162 farmer/rancher intervenors, seeking declaratory judgment that defendants violated § 2 of the Crow Allotment Act of 1920 (41 Stat. 751), which prohibited conveyances of Crow allotted land to any person already owning 640 acres of agricultural or 1,280 acres of grazing land, or who would thereby exceed 1,280 agricultural or 1,920 grazing acres within the reservation. A 1977 Native American Rights Fund study identified 56 non-Crow persons collectively holding over 285,000 excess acres on the reservation. The Tribe sought divestiture, injunctive relief, and trespass damages affecting hundreds of thousands of acres and millions of dollars.

The court granted summary judgment to CFC and dismissed all claims on two threshold grounds. First, applying Montana's five-year statute of limitations (Mont. Code Ann. § 70-19-401), the court held that once fee patents issued under the Dawes Act and 25 U.S.C. § 349, the federal trust relationship terminated, subjecting the land to state law. The court rejected the Tribe's reliance on 28 U.S.C. § 2415 and *Oneida II*, distinguishing those cases as involving aboriginal or treaty title. Second, the court found the Tribe lacked Article III standing, characterizing its injuries as speculative and abstract. The decision effectively insulated decades of non-Indian land accumulation—facilitated by unenforced federal acreage restrictions—from legal challenge, demonstrating how allotment, fee patenting, and governmental non-enforcement permanently dispossessed the Crow of vast reservation lands.

summaries.display_title: 1916–1939: BIA Central Classified Files, Crow Reservation Fee Patent Receipts
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1916–1939

This document consists of archival box labels and folder dividers from Record Group 75 (Records of the Bureau of Indian Affairs), Central Classified Files, 1907–39, pertaining to the Crow Reservation. The pages identify the contents of at least four archival boxes (Nos. 99, 100, 101, and 102) organized under the decimal classification 312, which in the BIA's filing system designated "Patents" — specifically fee patent receipts. The boxes span patent receipts from 1916–312 through approximately 1939–312, referenced under Preliminary Inventory 163, Entry 121 (PI-163 E-121), with HM/UM 2001 notations indicating later accessioning or microfilm identifiers.

These are not substantive documents themselves but rather finding aids that confirm the existence of a substantial body of fee patent receipt records for the Crow Reservation spanning over two decades. Fee patents were the primary legal mechanism by which trust protections were removed from individual Indian allotments, converting them to alienable fee simple title under the Burke Act of 1906 and the broader Dawes Act framework. The volume of material — at least four boxes covering 1916 to the late 1930s — indicates a sustained and extensive program of issuing fee patents on Crow allotments, which historically resulted in massive land loss as fee-patented parcels were quickly sold or lost to tax foreclosure. The OCR quality is poor, with fragmented text and garbled characters across all pages, reflecting the nature of handwritten or typed box labels. These records would be essential for reconstructing the scale and pace of Crow land dispossession through the fee patent process.

summaries.file_name: First Reply of Defts. Little Horn Land and Livestock Co., and Francis Murth, as Trustee, to Plantiff's First Bried.p462-518.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1971: Defendants' First Reply Brief, Little Horn Land & Livestock Co. and Murtha, in Dillon v. Antler Land Company
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1868–1971

This document is the First Reply Brief filed by defendants Little Horn Land and Livestock Company and Francis Murtha (as Trustee) in *Phoebe Wilson Dillon v. Antler Land Company et al.*, Civil No. 891, U.S. District Court for the District of Montana, Billings Division, filed November 1, 1971. The brief was prepared by Crowley, Kilbourne, Haughey, Hanson & Gallagher of Billings, Montana.

Plaintiff Phoebe Wilson Dillon, a Crow Indian born in 1914, received trust patent allotments on October 10, 1923 and January 26, 1924. She was declared competent on April 12, 1949, applied for a fee patent on February 25, 1955 under the Act of May 8, 1906 (34 Stat. 182, 25 U.S.C. §349), and received a patent in fee simple on September 19, 1955 for approximately 1,040 acres within the Crow Reservation. On October 6, 1955, she conveyed the land by warranty deed to F.M. Tschirgi/Antler Land Company at approximately $7 per acre, against an official government appraisal of $10 per acre and her complaint's alleged valuation of $30,000 ($28/acre).

Defendants argue the Crow Act of 1920 incorporated the General Allotment Act's competency provisions (25 U.S.C. §349, §372), that the patent extinguished federal trust title and removed all alienation restrictions, and that plaintiff's claims are barred by state statutes of limitation. They invoke *United States v. Miller* (10th Cir., 1932, 57 F.2d 987), *Bond v. Sherburne Mercantile*, and *United States v. Budd* to argue no collateral attack on the patent is permissible. The brief reveals the systematic mechanism by which Crow allotments passed to white ranching interests—competency determinations, fee patents, and immediate conveyances at below-appraised values—while arguing this process was entirely lawful.

summaries.file_name: 1972 Dillon v. Antler Land Co._ 341 F. Supp. 734.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1972: Federal District Court Opinion, Dillon v. Antler Land Co., 341 F. Supp. 734
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1868–1972

This is a federal district court opinion, *Dillon v. Antler Land Co.*, 341 F. Supp. 734 (D. Mont. 1972), decided April 14, 1972, by Chief Judge Russell E. Smith. Plaintiff Phoebe Wilson Dillon, a Crow Indian, sued to recover 1,040 acres of Crow Reservation land she sold in October 1955 to Antler Land Company of Wyola (controlled by Matt Tschirgi) for $7,280 ($7.00/acre), when the land was appraised at $10,400 ($10.00/acre). She received only $3,711 in cash after deductions for prior advances and prepaid lease rentals. The land had been allotted to Dillon as a minor via trust patents issued in 1923–1924. She was declared competent in 1949 under the Crow Indian Allotment Act (41 Stat. 751, 1920) and applied for a fee patent in February 1955 at Tschirgi's insistence and instruction, including making false representations to the Indian agency. The court found the deed voidable for fraud and violation of 25 U.S.C. § 348 (General Allotment Act) and void under Section 2 of the Crow Act, which prohibited conveyances to persons owning more than 1,920 acres of grazing land on the reservation—Antler Land exceeded this limit. Nevertheless, the court granted summary judgment for defendants on adverse possession grounds under Montana law, holding that state statutes of limitations applied to fee-patented land of competent Indians per 25 U.S.C. § 349. The United States refused to intervene. This case powerfully illustrates how the fee patent system, predatory leasing schemes, and state adverse possession laws combined to dispossess Crow allottees even when federal law explicitly voided the underlying transactions.

summaries.display_title: 1956: House Report No. 2801 on S. 3698 to Repeal Crow Act Acreage Limitations and Validate Prior Conveyances
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1956

This document is House Report No. 2801 from the 84th Congress, 2nd Session, dated July 19, 1956, submitted by Mr. Engle from the Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs, recommending passage of S. 3698 (with companion bill H.R. 11036, introduced by Congressman Metcalf). The bill's purpose was to repeal the acreage limitation provisions in section 2 of the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), as amended by the Act of June 8, 1940 (54 Stat. 252), and to validate, ratify, and confirm all conveyances made in violation of those limitations.

The 1920 Crow Act had capped non-Indian acquisitions at 640 acres of agricultural or 1,280 acres of grazing land, with upper limits of 1,280 agricultural or 1,920 grazing acres, making violations punishable by fines up to $5,000 and six months' imprisonment. The report reveals that these restrictions were violated beginning in 1921 and continuously for 35 years, with the Bureau of Indian Affairs itself supervising sales in "complete disregard" of the law. Committee testimony estimated upward of 300,000 acres were conveyed in violation, with individual holdings reaching 30,000 acres. BIA representatives admitted they could offer "no explanation whatsoever" for the enforcement failure. The Crow Tribal Council, chaired by William Wall with secretary Philip Beaumont, passed resolutions on April 14, 1956, requesting the repeal. Assistant Secretary Fred G. Aandahl and Bureau of the Budget official Robert E. Merriam endorsed the bill. The report preserves mineral rights for allottees for 50 years. This document powerfully demonstrates how bureaucratic negligence—or complicity—facilitated massive land consolidation by non-Indians on the Crow Reservation, with retroactive congressional ratification effectively legalizing decades of illegal dispossession.

summaries.file_name: Affidavit of Phoebe Wilson Dillon with Attachments.374-82.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1971: Affidavit of Phoebe Wilson Dillon with Attachments in Dillon v. Antler Land Company
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1914–1971

This is a sworn affidavit filed by Phoebe Wilson Dillon in the United States District Court for the District of Montana, Billings Division, Cause No. 891, on October 4, 1971. Dillon, an enrolled Crow Tribe member born December 7, 1914, sued Antler Land Company, Little Horn Land Company, Little Horn Land and Livestock Company, Union Bank and Trust Company (executor of Bertha Tschirgi's estate), Rex Hibbs (executor of F.M. Tschirgi's estate), Francis J. Murtha as trustee, and the United States of America.

Dillon held Allotment No. 3636 comprising 1,040 acres under a Trust Patent and Homestead Allotment. She details how Matt Tschirgi leased her land at approximately 14.32 cents per acre ($149/year), far below market value. After her competency was approved April 12, 1949, she negotiated leases at $0.50/acre with the Reservation Ranchers Association and James Stanton. Tschirgi then manipulated her into applying for a fee patent, coaching her on application language, instructing her to conceal his involvement, and advancing loans totaling $3,029.06. On May 21, 1955, she signed a contract at $7.00/acre. The BIA appraised the land at $10.00/acre, but Tschirgi refused to pay the difference, deducted $540 for advance lease payments, and paid only $3,721 by check. The fee patent issued October 3, 1955, and she signed a warranty deed October 6, 1955, without having seen the appraisal. Superintendent L.C. Lippert offered no remedy. The affidavit invokes Section 2 of the Crow Indian Allotment Act of 1920 and demonstrates systematic collusion between Tschirgi and BIA personnel to dispossess Crow allottees at below-appraised values.

summaries.file_name: Affidavit of Cale Crowley re government files.p64-65.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1971: Affidavit of Cale Crowley Regarding Government Files in Dillon v. Antler Land Company
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1971–1971

This document is an affidavit filed February 24, 1971, in the United States District Court, District of Montana, Billings Division, in Cause No. 891, *Phoebe Wilson Dillon v. Antler Land Company, Little Horn Land Company, Little Horn Land and Livestock Company, Union Bank and Trust Company (Executor of the Estate of Bertha Tschirgi), Rex Hibbs (Executor of the Estate of F.M. Tschirgi), Francis J. Murtha (as Trustee for Trustees of Central States, Southeast and Southwest Areas Pension Fund), and the United States of America*. The affiant is Cale Crowley of the firm Crowley, Kilbourne, Haughey, attorney for defendants Little Horn Land and Livestock Company and Francis J. Murtha.

Crowley attests that pursuant to an Ex Parte Order dated February 18, 1971, he examined government files referenced in a prior motion and procured copies of pertinent papers. He notes that documents are missing from the files as currently maintained, making them "subject to loss," and argues that placing originals with the Clerk of Court would prevent further loss. He further recommends that counsel reference original files rather than cluttering the record with duplicate copies, preserving the "form, style, and content of originals."

The case involves multiple corporate land entities operating near Wyola, Montana—within or adjacent to the Crow Reservation—suggesting disputes over land title or dispossession. The affidavit is significant because it documents the incomplete and deteriorating state of government records pertaining to these lands, implying that evidence of original transactions or allotment transfers may have been lost from federal files, potentially obscuring the mechanisms of Native land alienation. The OCR is partially garbled, with some text out of sequence.

summaries.display_title: 1923: General Land Office Letter on Discrepancies in Crow Fee Patent Schedule
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1923–1923

This document is a letter from the General Land Office (GLO) to Commissioner of Indian Affairs Charles H. Burke, dated July 27, 1923, written by an Acting Assistant Commissioner. It concerns a fee patent schedule for Crow Indian allottees that had been transmitted to the GLO on June 15, 1923, and forwarded to the Department of the Interior on June 27, 1923. The letter identifies discrepancies between names on the fee patent schedule and the original allotment schedule, suspending action on five specific cases pending clarification. The cases are: allotment #48 (May Takesgun vs. Mary Takesgun); #751 (Emma Runsbetween vs. Sweat House Well Known Runsbetween); #1325 (Ashable Pease vs. Ashabale Pease); #1776 (Ada Prettyman vs. Old Medicine Tobacco Prettyman); and #2375 (Howard Clinton Heckenlively vs. Edward C. Heckenlively). The file is classified under CCF 61262-23-313 and bears GLO reference number 1095321 "K" CRH.

This document illuminates the bureaucratic machinery of Crow Reservation land dispossession through fee patenting—the mechanism by which trust protections were removed from allotments, enabling alienation and sale. The name discrepancies are revealing: several original allotment names reflect traditional Crow naming conventions (Sweat House Well Known Runsbetween, Old Medicine Tobacco Prettyman) that were anglicized on fee schedules, suggesting systematic erasure of Indigenous identity within the administrative process of converting trust land to fee simple ownership. The document demonstrates how the Indian Office under Commissioner Burke actively pursued fee patents for Crow allottees during the 1920s, a period of accelerated land loss on the Crow Reservation.

summaries.file_name: 1990 Crow Tribe of Montana v. United States, 789 F. Supp. 398.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1990: Federal District Court Opinion, Crow Tribe of Montana v. United States, 789 F. Supp. 398
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1868–1990

This is a federal district court opinion, *Crow Tribe of Montana v. United States*, 789 F. Supp. 398 (D.D.C. 1990), decided July 20, 1990, by Judge Norma Holloway Johnson. The Crow Tribe sued the United States and federal officials seeking declaratory judgment and a writ of mandamus to compel enforcement of Section 2 of the Crow Act of 1920, 41 Stat. 751. That provision prohibited any person, company, or corporation already owning 640 acres of agricultural land or 1,280 acres of grazing land within the Crow Reservation from acquiring additional land from individual Crow Indians, and capped acquisitions at 1,280 acres agricultural or 1,920 acres grazing. Violations rendered conveyances void and subjected grantees to fines up to $5,000 or six months imprisonment. The Tribe alleged the federal government had completely abdicated enforcement, citing a 1986 Department of Interior Inspector General audit report on "Purchases of Crow Indian Reservation Lands" as evidence. The court denied the government's motion to dismiss, finding the Tribe had standing because non-enforcement threatened the reservation's territorial integrity and contiguous land base. The court invoked the Second Treaty of Fort Laramie (1868), 15 Stat. 649, and the federal trust responsibility as potentially imposing special enforcement duties. The opinion explicitly acknowledged that allotment policy caused over half of Indian lands to pass from native ownership, creating checkerboard patterns that "interfered with tribal culture and society." This document proves that federal non-enforcement of acreage limitations directly facilitated large-scale non-Indian land consolidation within the Crow Reservation, constituting a form of ongoing dispossession through administrative inaction despite statutory protections.

summaries.display_title: 1970: Complaint Filed by Phoebe Wilson Dillon Against Antler Land Company et al.
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1851–1970

This is a federal complaint filed in the United States District Court for the District of Montana, Billings Division, on July 2, 1970, by Phoebe Wilson Dillon, a Crow tribal member, through attorney Thomas E. Towe of Billings. The plaintiff sues Antler Land Company of Wyola, Little Horn Land Company, Little Horn Land and Livestock Company, Antler Land Company, Union Bank and Trust Company (executor of Bertha Tschirgi's estate), Rex Hibbs (executor of F.M. Tschirgi's estate), Francis J. Murtha as trustee for the Trustees of Central States, Southeast and Southwest Areas Pension Fund (naming James R. Hoffa among the trustees), and the United States of America.

Dillon seeks to void the dispossession of Crow Allotment No. 3636—1,040 acres in Big Horn County (E½ Section 26, S½ Section 25, N½ and N½SE¼ Section 36, T4S R34E, MPM)—classified as grazing land under the Crow Allotment Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751). She alleges that on February 28, 1955, Antler Land Company and M.H. Tschirgi fraudulently induced her to contract for sale at $7,280 when the land's fair market value exceeded $33,000, paying her $100 cash initially and $1,375 total. She executed a warranty deed on October 6, 1955. The complaint invokes 25 U.S.C. §348(5) to declare the contract void, and argues all subsequent conveyances—to the Tschirgis (September 2, 1958), Little Horn Land Company (September 30, 1958), Antler Land Company (January 17, 1964), and Little Horn Land and Livestock Company (January 7, 1967)—are void because each holder exceeded the 1,980-acre non-Indian ownership limit under the 1920 Act. The second claim, under 28 U.S.C. §1346(b), alleges the United States breached its trust duty under 25 U.S.C. §185 and multiple Crow treaties (1851, 1868, 1882, 1904), noting the Billings Area BIA office recommended action but the government declined on June 12, 1970. The document powerfully illustrates how fraudulent undervaluation, fee patenting, and federal inaction operated as interlocking mechanisms of Indian land dispossession on the Crow Reservation.

summaries.file_name: Affidavit of F. F. Dubray, Property Clerk, B.I.A.p290-291.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1971: Affidavit of F.F. DuBray, BIA Property Clerk, in Dillon v. Antler Land Company
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1948–1971

This is a sworn affidavit by F. F. DuBray, filed August 30, 1971, in the United States District Court for the District of Montana, Billings Division, in *Phoebe Wilson Dillon v. Antler Land Company of Wyola* et al., Cause No. 891. Defendants include Antler Land Company, Little Horn Land Company, Little Horn Land and Livestock Company, Union Bank and Trust Company (executor of the Estate of Bertha Tschirgi), Rex Hibbs (executor of the Estate of F. M. Tschirgi), Francis J. Murtha as Trustee for the Central States, Southeast and Southwest Areas Pension Fund, and the United States of America. The affidavit was subscribed before Notary Public Mary Nagashima in Billings, Montana, on August 27, 1971.

DuBray details his career with the Bureau of Indian Affairs: Property Clerk at the Crow Indian Agency (1948–1950), Realty Officer at Crow Agency (January 1954–April 1956), Assistant Realty Officer at the Billings Area Office (July 1960), and Area Realty Officer there until his retirement on February 12, 1971. He describes three methods by which Crow Indian trust lands were conveyed. First, Indians petitioned to sell trust patent lands, the BIA advertised them for 30 days, and successful bidders could receive either a fee patent (issued by the Bureau of Land Management through the BIA) or a warranty deed. Second, after advertisement, a deed was issued directly from the Indian owner through the BIA, which stamped or wrote its approval on the deed. Third, Indians deemed competent could apply for fee patents in their own names. This affidavit documents the administrative machinery—interagency coordination between BIA and BLM, discretionary "best interest" determinations, and competency findings—that facilitated systematic alienation of Crow trust lands to non-Indian purchasers and corporate entities.

summaries.file_name: Dillon (Phoebe Wilson) v. Antler Land Co. of Wyola, 421 U.S. 992 (1975). Brief in Opposition (on Petition).pdf
summaries.display_title: 1975: Brief in Opposition to Petition for Certiorari, Dillon v. Antler Land Co. of Wyola, 421 U.S. 992
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1975–1975

This document is a Brief in Opposition filed on May 6, 1975, in the case of Dillon (Phoebe Wilson) v. Antler Land Co. of Wyola, 421 U.S. 992 (1975), submitted to the U.S. Supreme Court in response to a petition for certiorari. The case involves Phoebe Wilson Dillon and the Antler Land Company of Wyola, Montana—Wyola being a community located on or near the Crow Reservation in southeastern Montana. The involvement of a land company operating in Crow Reservation territory strongly suggests the dispute concerned the alienation of Crow allotted lands, a common mechanism of dispossession whereby individual Indian allotments were transferred to non-Indian purchasers or corporate entities through fee patents, sales, or other federally sanctioned processes.

Unfortunately, the OCR text for this document is almost entirely absent; pages 1 through 9 contain no readable content, and page 10 provides only the bibliographic citation from the Gale Making of Modern Law database. No substantive legal arguments, acreages, allotment numbers, dollar amounts, statutes, or specific claims can be extracted from the available text. The Supreme Court denied certiorari at 421 U.S. 992, meaning the lower court ruling—presumably adverse to the Indian claimant—stood. The case nonetheless documents the legal struggles of individual Crow allottees against corporate land interests and the failure of federal courts to provide relief against land dispossession on the Crow Reservation.

summaries.file_name: 1957 Sale of Crow Lands House hearings.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1957: House Subcommittee Hearings on Crow Indian Land Sales and Acreage Limitation Repeal Bills
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1868–1958

This document is the printed transcript of hearings held October 18–19, 1957, on the Crow Indian Reservation in Montana, before a Special Subcommittee on Indian Affairs of the House Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs, chaired by Representative James A. Haley (Florida). The hearings concerned H.R. 2381, H.R. 7255, and S. 332 (85th Congress), bills to repeal the acreage limitation in section 2 of the Crow Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), and to validate all prior conveyances made in violation of that provision. Section 2 prohibited the Secretary of the Interior from approving any sale of Crow land to a person already owning 640 acres of agricultural or 1,280 acres of grazing land on the reservation, and capped post-purchase holdings at 1,280 agricultural or 1,920 grazing acres, with violations declared void and punishable by a $5,000 fine and/or six months' imprisonment. The 1940 amendment (54 Stat. 252) exempted Indian-to-Indian transactions but left the non-Indian restriction intact.

Testimony established that from approximately 1946 through January 1956, when the Commissioner suspended all sales, roughly 111,815 acres of restricted Crow land were sold under Bureau supervision without any reference to section 2 in sale notices, resulting in "substantial" violations involving "thousands of acres." The Crow Reservation Association, representing 71 non-Indian members holding approximately 248,000 deeded acres, collected over $22,000 (at 10 cents per acre) to fund lobbying and legal efforts. Witnesses included ranchers (Faddis-Kennedy Cattle Co., owning approximately 6,700 pre-1920 acres plus 7,714 post-1952 acres; Vale Creek Ranch; Murphy Land & Cattle Co.), the Equitable Life Assurance Society (which had loaned $997,000 on reservation lands and frozen $390,000 in pending applications), the Billings and Big Horn County chambers of commerce, and attorneys. Robert Yellowtail, the sole surviving member of the 1919 Crow delegation and former reservation superintendent (1934–1945), testified that Senator Thomas J.
