Walsh personally drafted section 2 to prevent "cattle and sheep barons" from monopolizing leased grazing districts, and that Yellowtail himself had objected to it as likely unconstitutional. He acknowledged conducting land sales during his superintendency without enforcing section 2, accepting shared responsibility with the Department of the Interior and Congress.

The Crow Tribal Council had passed Resolution 77 on April 14, 1956, requesting repeal and validation, but rescinded it on April 20, 1957, by a vote of 169–100, and subsequently adopted Resolution 132 appropriating $15,000 in tribal funds to hire separate counsel to oppose the legislation and potentially litigate recovery of lands. Tribal attorney Bert Kronmiller argued that individual Crow allottees held voidable property rights under established equity doctrine (citing 42 C.J.S. 763), that restoration of consideration was not a prerequisite to voiding conveyances, and that the restriction remained sound policy. Opponents within the tribe, including the Mountain Crow faction represented by Eloise Pease and Donald Deernose, supported validation, contending the tribe should not spend tribal funds on litigation and that the Government bore responsibility for nonenforcement.

The document is critical evidence of how the Bureau of Indian Affairs' systematic failure to enforce congressionally mandated acreage limitations facilitated large-scale transfer of Crow allotted lands to non-Indian ownership. It reveals the interplay between federal trust obligations, administrative negligence, private capital interests (insurance companies, banks), and intra-tribal political divisions. The legal mechanisms at issue—the 1920 Crow Act, the 1887 General Allotment Act (24 Stat. 388), the 1906 Burke Act (34 Stat. 182), the 1940 amendment, supervised sales, fee patents, and restricted deeds—collectively constituted the apparatus through which individually allotted Crow lands passed out of Indian ownership, while the very statutory safeguard designed to slow that process was ignored for decades by the agency charged with enfor

summaries.display_title: 1921–1922: BIA Correspondence on Chief Plenty Coups's Complaints About Crow Reservation Land Management
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1921–1922

This file (CCF 64354-20-313) comprises correspondence between the Bureau of Indian Affairs, the Crow Agency superintendent Calvin H. Asbury, and Senator Thomas J. Walsh of Montana regarding complaints lodged by Crow Chief Plenty Coups (Plenty Coos) about multiple aspects of federal management of Crow Reservation lands. The documents include letters from Commissioner Charles H. Burke to Walsh (August 10, 1922), Asbury's report back to the Commissioner (August 22, 1922), Walsh's original inquiry (August 3, 1922), and Department of the Interior letters from Acting Secretary E. C. Finney to congressional committee chairmen Charles Curtis and Homer P. Snyder (October 1 and December 7, 1921) seeking legislative extensions for allotment work.

Plenty Coups complained about delays in allotment under the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), the Campbell farming leases (where Thomas Campbell personally assumed contracts from the defunct Montana Farming Corporation to avoid receivership), oil lease execution without tribal consultation, killing of his horses, and unauthorized hunters and fishermen on reservation lands. The allotment deadline of December 4, 1921 proved physically impossible to meet—eight townships required surveying, and the Department requested a two-year extension. Asbury's letter of August 1, 1921 noted approximately $280,000 owed on ceded strip lands sold under the 1904 agreement, with tribal funds critically low as grazing leases ended and the tribal herd was being liquidated. Burke dismissed Plenty Coups' complaints, and Asbury suggested "younger officious" Indians prompted the chief's letter. Oil leasing regulations approved December 10, 1921 nominally required Crow Council consent. The documents reveal systematic dispossession through delayed allotments, corporate lease transfers without meaningful tribal input, and depletion of tribal revenue sources funding reservation administration.

summaries.display_title: 1975: Supreme Court Certiorari Memorandum, Dillon v. Antler Land Co.
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1955–1975

This document is a Supreme Court certiorari memorandum prepared by a law clerk (initialed "JKW," dated 5-18-75) for the 1974 Term, case No. 74-961, *Dillon v. Antler Land Co.*, on petition for certiorari to the Ninth Circuit (panel of Chambers, Koelsch, McGovern). The clerk recommends denial.

The petitioner, an Indian woman, sought to quiet title to land originally allotted and patented to her on the Crow Reservation. The respondent allegedly induced petitioner to apply for a fee patent from the Department of the Interior, and while the application was pending, petitioner signed a contract to convey the land upon receipt of the patent. She conveyed the land to respondent in October 1955, three days after obtaining the fee patent. In 1970—fifteen years later—she sued, advancing three theories: (1) the pre-patent contract violated 25 U.S.C. §348, rendering both contract and conveyance void; (2) the conveyance violated the Crow Act's prohibition on conveying land to any person already owning 1,280 acres of grazing land within the Crow Reservation; and (3) respondent's fraudulent inducement of the patent application invalidated the fee patent itself.

The Ninth Circuit affirmed dismissal, holding that 25 U.S.C. §349 subjected the allottee to Montana's statute of limitations once a fee patent issued. The clerk analyzed *Ewert v. Bluejacket*, 259 U.S. 129, distinguishing it because restrictions here did not appear on the patent's face. The document illustrates how fee patenting and state limitations periods operated as mechanisms of dispossession, effectively barring Indian allottees from recovering land obtained through arguably coercive or fraudulent conveyances.

summaries.file_name: 1953 Eliminating Homestead Desgination in Crow Act.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1953: Senate Report on Eliminating Homestead Designation in the Crow Allotment Act
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1919–1953

This document is Senate Report No. 234 (83rd Congress, 1st Session), dated May 12, 1953, submitted by Senator Watkins from the Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs, recommending passage of H.R. 1244 without amendment. The bill proposed amending Section 13 of the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751, 756), which governed allotment and land tenure on the Crow Reservation in Montana.

Under the 1920 Act, every Crow tribal member was required to designate 640 acres as a homestead, which remained inalienable for twenty-five years or until the allottee's death. The existing law permitted sale of no more than 320 acres of the homestead, and prohibited selling more than one-half of irrigable or one-half of agricultural land. H.R. 1244 struck these acreage limitations entirely, allowing Indian owners to sell any homestead, irrigable, or agricultural land upon written application with approval of the Secretary of the Interior or his authorized representative.

The report justified the change by arguing that existing restrictions prevented Crow Indians from disposing of unused land and that parcels too small to attract bidders could not realize full value. It noted the "irregular pattern of land ownership" created by allotment and referenced 75 individual sale bills introduced during the 82nd Congress alone. The report also cited a parallel restriction on the Blackfeet Reservation under the Act of June 30, 1919 (41 Stat. 3, 16). Bureau of Indian Affairs representatives testified in favor. This legislation effectively dismantled the last protective barriers against alienation of Crow homestead lands, facilitating further dispossession under the guise of administrative efficiency and individual autonomy during the termination era.

summaries.file_name: Answer to Complaint by the United States.p48-49.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1970: U.S. Answer to Complaint, Dillon v. Antler Land Co.
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1970–1970

This document is the Answer to Complaint filed by the United States in *Phoebe Wilson Dillon v. Antler Land Company, et al.*, Civil No. 891 (also referenced as 69-547), in the United States District Court for the District of Montana, Billings Division, filed November 2, 1970. The answer was submitted by Otis L. Packwood, United States Attorney for the District of Montana.

The United States raises four affirmative defenses: lack of jurisdiction due to sovereign immunity; expiration of the statute of limitations under 28 U.S.C. § 2401; failure to present a prerequisite claim under the Federal Tort Claims Act, 28 U.S.C. § 2675; and failure to state a claim. On the merits, the government denies that the fair market value of the plaintiff's land was in excess of $33,000 and denies that the reasonable value was $33,000. The United States admits that Dillon had communicated to various federal representatives her contention that her deed to the Antler Land Company was invalid, but notes that while the Area Director of the Bureau of Indian Affairs recommended action to recover her land, only the Solicitor for the Department of the Interior held delegated authority to recommend such action to the Department of Justice. The government admits it has not brought an action to recover the lands for Dillon's benefit. This document demonstrates the bureaucratic and jurisdictional barriers that impeded Native landowners seeking federal assistance in recovering dispossessed allotment lands, with the BIA's own recommendation proving insufficient to compel legal action.

summaries.display_title: 1934: Crow Tribal Council Meeting Minutes, April 4, 1934
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1934

This document comprises the minutes of the Crow Tribal Council meeting held April 4, 1934, at Crow Agency, Montana, transmitted by Acting Superintendent Richard B. Millin to the Commissioner of Indian Affairs on April 6, 1934. The minutes are from the Central Classified Files (Record Group 75, National Archives), Crow Agency file 054/17240-34.

The council, attended by 135 men and 60 women, acted on several resolutions. First, it voted nearly unanimously for an immediate $10.00 per capita payment from tribal funds held in the U.S. Treasury, invoking Section II of the Crow Allotting Act of June 4, 1920, which mandated per capita distribution of consolidated tribal funds. Members testified that lease monies owed for three months had not been paid, that only one-tenth of the tribe was employed, and that rental income had dwindled to three and one-half cents per acre—evidence of systematic economic dispossession through depressed lease rates favoring white stockmen.

The council unanimously (142–0) endorsed a $158,000 congressional appropriation for School District 17-H, Big Horn County, noting the district had invested over $400,000 in facilities while carrying $155,000 in bonded debt, with one-third of enrolled students being Crow children who had received no proportional federal investment. The council voted 164–20 to support Robert Yellowtail's appointment as superintendent. Most significantly, it voted 109–0 to reject the Wheeler-Howard Bill (S. 2755/H.R. 7902), declaring the Crow were "not ready for self rule" and unwilling to surrender vested allotment rights. A separate resolution (72–0) opposed a bill amending the 1920 Crow Allotting Act to extend irrigable land leases to twenty years, with members citing concerns about long-term alienation of land use at inadequate rates.

summaries.file_name: Dillon v Antler Land Co. Civ. No. 891_cwm.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1972: Case File Index, Dillon v. Antler Land Co., U.S. District Court, Montana
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1972–1972

This document is the index to the case file for *Phoebe Wilson Dillon v. Antler Land Company et al.*, Civil No. 891, U.S. District Court for the District of Montana, Billings Division. The plaintiff-appellant, Phoebe Wilson Dillon, sued multiple defendants: Antler Land Company of Wyola, Little Horn Land Company, Little Horn Land and Livestock Company, Union Bank and Trust Company (executor of the estate of Bertha Tschirgi), Rex Hibbs (executor of the estate of F. M. Tschirgi), Francis J. Murtha as trustee for the Trustees of Central States, Southeast and Southwest Area Pension Fund, and the United States of America. The case involved motions for summary judgment and decrees quieting title, indicating a land ownership dispute. The geographic reference to Wyola, Montana, places the contested lands squarely within or adjacent to the Crow Reservation. Bureau of Indian Affairs involvement is confirmed by an affidavit from F. F. Dubray, identified as a BIA Property Clerk, and by an ex parte order granting counsel permission to examine official BIA files. Judge Battin disqualified himself, transferring the case to Judge Russell E. Smith. An opinion and order were issued, later corrected on August 17, 1972. The presence of BIA records, requests for admissions regarding land documents, and the United States as a defendant strongly suggest the case concerned the validity of transfers of Crow allotment lands to non-Indian corporate entities, illustrating how land companies and trust arrangements facilitated dispossession of individual Indian allottees. No specific acreages, allotment numbers, or dollar amounts appear in this index portion of the file.

summaries.file_name: Dillon (Phoebe Wilson) v. Antler Land Co. of Wyola, 421 U.S. 992 (1975). Brief in Opposition.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1975: Brief in Opposition, Dillon v. Antler Land Co., U.S. Supreme Court
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1975–1975

This document is a Brief in Opposition filed on March 4, 1975, in the case of Dillon (Phoebe Wilson) v. Antler Land Co. of Wyola, cited at 421 U.S. 992 (1975), before the United States Supreme Court. The case involves a dispute between a Native American party (Phoebe Wilson Dillon) and the Antler Land Company of Wyola, a locality situated on or near the Crow Reservation in Montana. The case likely concerns land title, allotment inheritance, or the validity of land transfers from Native American allottees to non-Indian entities, given the parties involved and the geographic context of Wyola, which lies within Crow Reservation boundaries.

Unfortunately, the OCR text for this document is entirely blank across all 50 pages, rendering substantive analysis of specific claims, legal arguments, statutory citations, acreages, dollar amounts, allotment numbers, or administrative procedures impossible. No content regarding the legal mechanisms invoked—whether the General Allotment Act, Crow Allotment Act of 1920, fee patent procedures, or Bureau of Indian Affairs trust responsibilities—can be extracted. The Supreme Court citation at 421 U.S. 992 indicates the Court likely denied certiorari, leaving lower court rulings intact. The case nonetheless documents the pattern of Native American land dispossession on the Crow Reservation through legal proceedings involving land companies acquiring former allotment lands. The complete absence of readable text severely limits the evidentiary value of this document as accessed.

summaries.display_title: 1972: Case Docket, Dillon v. Antler Land Co., U.S. District Court, Montana
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1972–1972

This document is the official List of Documents (case docket index) for Civil No. 891, Billings Division, U.S. District Court for the District of Montana, in the case of **Phoebe Wilson Dillon v. Antler Land Company of Wyola, Little Horn Land Company, Antler Land Company, Little Horn Land and Livestock Company, Union Bank and Trust Company (Executor of the Estate of Bertha Tschirgi), Rex Hibbs (Executor of the Estate of F.M. Tschirgi), Francis J. Murtha (Trustee for Trustees of Central States, Southeast and Southwest Area Pension Fund), and the United States of America**. The case was initially before Judge Battin, who disqualified himself, transferring it to Judge Russell E. Smith. An opinion and order dated April 13, 1972, was later amended, followed by a Judgment and Decree. Both plaintiff and defendants (excepting the U.S.) filed notices of appeal and cross-appeal.

The docket reveals significant procedural elements bearing on Native American land dispossession: an ex parte order granting defense counsel permission to examine official Bureau of Indian Affairs files; an affidavit by F.F. Dubray, BIA Property Clerk, concerning those records; and motions for summary judgment seeking to quiet title. The plaintiff, Phoebe Wilson Dillon—likely a Crow tribal member given the Wyola, Montana location on the Crow Reservation—challenged land transfers to corporate and individual defendants. The defendants' cross-complaint and motions to quiet title suggest they sought judicial confirmation of land acquisitions whose legitimacy the plaintiff contested. The involvement of the United States as defendant and the centrality of BIA records indicate that federal trust responsibilities and administrative procedures governing Indian allotment land sales were directly at issue.

summaries.file_name: CCF 4423-57-310 Crow Crow land sale, Crow Act.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1920–1959: BIA Billings Area Office File on Proposed Amendments to the Crow Allotment Act
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1959

This document is a multi-part Bureau of Indian Affairs file from the Billings Area Office concerning proposals to amend the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), which governed allotment and sale of Crow Reservation lands. The core report, dated October 16, 1957, details the history and scale of Crow land dispossession. The Crow Reservation contained 2,282,764 acres, of which approximately 3,800 allotments comprising roughly 1,975,000 acres were made to individual tribal members between about 1905 and 1933, with 266,763 acres remaining in tribal ownership and approximately 45,000 acres sold to Montana as school lands. Approximately 591,700 acres had trust restrictions released or were fee-patented by 1955, with 111,315 acres sold through 28 advertised government sales from 1946 to 1955 at an average appraised value of $10.27 per acre, yielding $1,614,622 at an average sale price of $14.50 per acre—while comparable private sales in 1954–1955 averaged $14.538 per acre. The document notes the Bureau failed to implement Section 2 of the 1920 Act in advertised sales. The Act of June 8, 1940 (54 Stat. 252) amended acreage limitations for intra-tribal sales. Correspondence from attorney J.F. Meglen (March 22, 1957) contested the proposed sale of allotments 1271 and 3085 to Clarence L. Westbrook, alleging the former superintendent reversed his own refusal just before retirement and that Westbrook may have been purchasing for an undisclosed third party. Additional correspondence concerns Walter O. Green Jr.'s appeal to purchase 1,064.89 acres of allotments 1131 and 1143 at $16,720. The file demonstrates systematic mechanisms of land alienation through fee patenting, advertised sales below market value, and administrative discretion that facilitated transfer of Crow lands to non-Indian ownership.

summaries.file_name: 1956 Unpublished hearing To Amend the Act of June 4, 1920, as Amended, Providing for Allotment of Lands of the Crow Tribe_2.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1956: House Subcommittee Hearing on Bills to Amend the Crow Allotment Act of 1920
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1868–1956

This document is an unpublished hearing transcript from the House Subcommittee on Indian Affairs, held July 13, 1956, chaired by James A. Haley, concerning S. 3698 and H.R. 11036, introduced by Representative Lee Metcalf of Montana. The bills sought to repeal the first paragraph of Section 2 of the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), as amended by the Act of June 8, 1940 (54 Stat. 252), which imposed acreage limitations on land conveyances from Crow Indians: no purchaser could acquire land if they already owned 640 acres of agricultural or 1,280 acres of grazing land, nor could they exceed 1,280 agricultural or 1,920 grazing acres total. Violations carried fines up to $5,000 and six months imprisonment.

The hearing reveals that these limitations were systematically violated for 35 years beginning in 1921, with approximately 400,000 acres sold on the Crow Reservation, of which an estimated 280,000 acres violated the statute. One landowner held as much as 30,000 acres. Three to four hundred non-Indian purchasers were affected. Associate Commissioner Rex Lee testified the Bureau discovered violations in September 1955 and suspended sales after five months of investigation, finding no explanation for non-enforcement. Sales occurred through supervised government auctions, fee patents issued administratively and by Congress, and restricted deeds approved by the Secretary of the Interior.

Attorney Tom Burke's memorandum traces Crow land rights from the 1868 treaty (15 Stat. 649), the 1880 treaty (I Kappler 195), and the 1882 ratifying act (22 Stat. 42), arguing Indians held equitable title while the U.S. held naked legal title. The Crow Tribal Council passed competing Resolutions No. 77 and 77-A on April 14, 1956, both favoring repeal, though subsequent telegrams from Chairman Edward Whiteman requested delay. A memorandum from Allen Old Horn opposed the legislation, noting 297,973 acres sold between 1950–1955 with 1,454,976 acres remaining, and requesting no further sales to non-Indians. The bill was ultimately passed over without prejudice. This document powerfully demonstrates how federal administrative negligence facilitated massive land concentration by non-Indians on the Crow Reservation despite explicit statutory protections.

summaries.display_title: 1920–1923: BIA Transmittal of Crow Reservation Allotment Schedules to Secretary of the Interior
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1923

This document is a multi-page transmittal letter and endorsement chain from the Bureau of Indian Affairs (Commissioner's Office) to the Secretary of the Interior, dated April 26, 1923, and approved June 7, 1923, concerning allotment schedules for the Crow Indian Reservation in Montana. The letter, signed by Assistant Commissioner F. M. Goodwin, submits schedules prepared by Charles E. Roblin, Special Allotting Agent, under the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), commonly known as the Crow Act. The schedules encompass 1,768 allotments covering 1,596,942.93 acres and include four categories: (1) prorata allotments to enrolled Crow tribal members who were living heads of families on June 4 or December 4, 1920, spanning pages 1–304; (2) reservations for "Agency Administration" and other governmental purposes, pages 306–310; (3) lands granted to the State of Montana under Section 15 of the Act, pages 311–313; and (4) lands to be patented in fee to Indians designated "competent," pages 314–520. The schedules incorporate a certified tribal roll of 1,768 names prepared by the Enrollment Commission, classifying each adult as "competent" or "incompetent"—a legal distinction that determined whether trust protections were retained or fee patents issued, effectively facilitating alienation. Section 6 reserved minerals, oil, gas, and coal for tribal benefit, while Section 13 imposed conditions on "homestead" designations. The General Land Office and Secretary approved the schedules with minor exceptions for conflicts. The document demonstrates the systematic bureaucratic machinery of dispossession: mass fee-patenting to "competent" Indians, state land grants, and administrative reservations that collectively dismembered the Crow Reservation. OCR quality is poor throughout, with significant character corruption.

summaries.display_title: 1957: Senate Report on Bill to Amend the Crow Allotment Act of 1920
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1957

This document is Senate Report No. 142 (85th Congress, 1st Session), dated March 14, 1957, submitted by Senator James E. Murray from the Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs, recommending passage of S. 332 to amend the Crow Allotment Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), as amended June 8, 1940 (54 Stat. 252). The bill's purpose was twofold: to repeal the acreage limitations in section 2 of the 1920 Act—which prohibited conveyances to persons owning 640+ acres of agricultural or 1,280+ acres of grazing land, and capped acquisitions at 1,280 acres agricultural or 1,920 acres grazing—and to validate, ratify, and confirm all conveyances made in violation of those limits since 1921.

The report reveals a remarkable 35-year pattern of systematic non-enforcement by the Bureau of Indian Affairs. Violations began in 1921 and continued until January 1956, when the Commissioner of Indian Affairs first learned of the problem and suspended all Crow Reservation land sales. The BIA's investigation disclosed "a substantial number of violations" involving "thousands of acres." Bureau representatives testified they could offer "no explanation whatsoever" for the enforcement failure. Sales occurred both through direct transactions by fee-patented Indians and through BIA-supervised sales conducted in "complete disregard for the statutory restrictions." The Crow Tribal Council adopted a resolution on April 14, 1956, requesting the legislation; Phillip Beaumont signed as Secretary. Assistant Secretary Hatfield Chilson and Budget Bureau Assistant Director Robert E. Merriam endorsed the bill. Mineral rights under allotted lands, reserved to the tribe for 50 years under section 6, were explicitly excluded from validation. This document demonstrates how bureaucratic negligence and assumed repeals facilitated massive non-Indian land consolidation on the Crow Reservation, with retroactive congressional ratification effectively legalizing decades of illegal dispossession.

summaries.file_name: Ex Parte Order re Granting permission of Counsel to examine official files of the B.I.A.p60.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1971: Ex Parte Order Granting Counsel Access to BIA Files, Dillon v. Antler Land Co.
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1971–1971

This document is an ex parte order issued by the United States District Court for the District of Montana, Billings Division, filed February 18, 1971, in Cause No. 891, *Phoebe Wilson Dillon v. Antler Land Company of Wyola et al.* The order was entered on oral motion of counsel for the defendants and directs the Bureau of Indian Affairs and the offices of the Solicitor of the United States to permit counsel for any party to examine official files and correspondence pertaining to the plaintiff and to the land at issue. If copies are needed, the parties are to reach mutual agreement on arrangements; failing that, the court would intervene. The clerk was John J. Parker, with a deputy clerk signing on his behalf.

The defendants include multiple Montana corporations—Antler Land Company of Wyola, Little Horn Land Company, Antler Land Company, and Little Horn Land and Livestock Company—along with Union Bank and Trust Company as executor of the Estate of Bertha Tschirgi, Rex Hibbs as executor of the Estate of F. M. Tschirgi, Francis J. Murtha as trustee for the Trustees of Central States, Southeast and Southwest Areas Pension Fund, and the United States of America. The involvement of BIA files, the Wyola locality (situated on the Crow Reservation), and the plaintiff's apparent Indian identity strongly suggest this litigation concerned dispossession of Crow allotment land, likely through transactions facilitated or overseen by the BIA. The order demonstrates that federal records were essential evidence and that access required judicial compulsion, underscoring the opacity of BIA administrative processes in land transfers affecting individual Indian allottees.

summaries.file_name: 1963 RATIFYING CERTAIN CONVEYANCES OF LAND ON THE CROW INDIAN RESERVATION.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1963: Senate Report on Bill to Ratify Unlawful Land Conveyances on the Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1963

This document is Senate Report No. 680 (88th Congress, 1st Session), dated December 4, 1963, submitted by Senator Jackson on behalf of Senator Church from the Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs, recommending passage of S. 1757, introduced by Senators Mansfield and Metcalf. The bill sought to validate, ratify, and confirm conveyances of Crow Indian Reservation land made prior to October 18, 1957, that violated acreage limitations in section 2 of the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751). That statute prohibited conveyances to any person already owning 640 acres of agricultural or 1,280 acres of grazing land on the reservation, or whose holdings would exceed 1,280 agricultural or 1,920 grazing acres, with violations punishable by $5,000 fines and six months imprisonment.

The report reveals systematic failure by the Bureau of Indian Affairs to enforce these limitations. Between 1946 and 1955, twenty-one supervised sales covering 517 tracts proceeded without any compliance checks, with the area director claiming Washington had told him the limitations were inoperative. The BIA could offer no satisfactory explanation. The Department acknowledged partial blame and noted that the Miller Land & Livestock Co. was flagged as early as 1941 for exceeding limits. The Department of Justice declined criminal prosecution for pre-1955 violations and delayed civil actions referred in April 1960. An accompanying letter from Assistant Secretary John A. Carver, Jr., dated June 14, 1963, recommended legislative ratification as the "most practical" remedy, arguing Indians had been paid and no individual was prejudiced—while simultaneously acknowledging the acreage caps depressed land values. The Crow Tribe initially supported ratification (resolution of April 14, 1956) but reversed its position and opposed the legislation. Mineral rights reserved to the tribe for fifty years under the 1920 Act were explicitly excluded from ratification. This document demonstrates how bureaucratic negligence and deliberate non-enforcement facilitated large-scale non-Indian land accumulation on the Crow Reservation, with Congress ultimately legalizing the dispossession retroactively.

summaries.file_name: 1953 Eliminating Homestead Designation in Crow Act.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1953: House Report on Eliminating Homestead Designation in the Crow Allotment Act
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1919–1953

This document is House Report No. 262 from the 83rd Congress, 1st Session, dated April 13, 1953, submitted by Mr. Miller of Nebraska from the Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs. It accompanies H.R. 1244, a bill to amend Section 13 of the Crow Allotment Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), which had required every Crow tribal member to designate 640 acres as an inalienable homestead for twenty-five years or until the allottee's death, with sales limited to no more than 320 acres and restricted to no more than one-half of irrigable or agricultural land, excluding home improvements.

The proposed amendment would eliminate these homestead designation restrictions entirely, permitting Indian owners of homestead, irrigable, or agricultural land on the Crow Reservation to sell such land upon written application with approval of the Secretary of the Interior. The committee justified this by arguing that existing restrictions prevented Crow Indians from disposing of unused land, that parcels limited to half-units were too small to attract bidders, and that allotments had created "an irregular pattern of land ownership." The report notes that during the 82nd Congress alone, 75 individual bills were introduced to authorize specific Crow land sales or fee patents. The committee also referenced similar restrictions on the Blackfeet Reservation under the Act of June 30, 1919 (41 Stat. 3, 16). A Bureau of Indian Affairs representative testified in support. This document demonstrates how Congress systematically dismantled protective inalienability provisions specifically designed to prevent dispossession, framing the removal of safeguards as administrative efficiency and Indian empowerment while facilitating large-scale land alienation from the Crow Reservation.

summaries.display_title: 1947–1950: BIA File on Fee Patenting and Sale of Robert E. Doyle's Crow Allotment
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1947–1950

This file documents the fee patenting and prospective sale of Robert E. Doyle's 640-acre Crow allotment (Allotment No. 1658) on the Crow Reservation in Big Horn County, Montana. The process began when S. 1407 was introduced in Congress and enacted as Private Law 222, 80th Congress, 2d Session, approved March 25, 1948, specifically authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to issue a fee patent to Doyle for lands in sections 28 and 29, Township 8 South, Range 37 East, Montana Principal Meridian. Doyle, a 1/4-degree Crow Indian classified as competent, had originally been allotted 800 acres; 160 acres had already been fee-patented in 1919. He also held a 1/2 inherited interest in 160 ceded-strip acres from his deceased son Elmer Lee Doyle (Allotment No. 3968). Doyle worked as a farmhand near Parkman, Wyoming, and leased his allotment for $1,500 over four years (1946–1950). A reimbursable loan balance of $167 was outstanding; he paid $132.50 on May 10, 1950, clearing the debt. Fee Patent No. 1129283 was issued June 15, 1950, transmitted by Acting Commissioner William Zimmerman Jr. to Superintendent L. C. Lippert. The superintendent's July 9, 1947 report noted the land lay on Owl Creek in the Wolf Mountains, was good grazing land used by Range Unit No. 62 permittees W. J. Moss and W. Stark—identified as the probable purchasers. The file reveals that special congressional legislation was required to circumvent standard trust protections, and that multiple Doyle family allotments in the same locality were simultaneously being fee-patented, effectively consolidating the area for non-Indian acquisition.

summaries.display_title: 1967–1973: National Archives Box Label, U.S. District Courts of Montana Civil Cases
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1967–1973

This document is a box label or container identification sheet for an archival records box held by the National Archives, pertaining to the U.S. District Courts of Montana. The box is identified as Box 50 within Record Group 21 (RG21), which is the standard National Archives record group for Records of District Courts of the United States. The accession number is 8NN-021-90-017, and the box covers fiscal year 2008 processing of civil cases filed between 1967 and 1973, specifically case numbers 891 through 893.

The document is extremely minimal — essentially a finding aid or shelf label rather than a substantive legal record. It contains no narrative text, no named parties, no acreages, dollar amounts, allotment numbers, or legal citations. It does not identify the specific nature of the civil cases contained within, though its inclusion in a collection related to Native American land dispossession and the Crow Reservation suggests that cases 891–893 may involve federal Indian policy disputes, land claims, or Bureau of Indian Affairs administrative actions litigated in the Montana federal district court during the late 1960s and early 1970s — a period of significant legal activity regarding tribal sovereignty, allotment policy, and reservation boundary disputes. The document's evidentiary value is limited to establishing the existence and archival location of potentially relevant civil case files. The OCR capture is clean but the source material itself is fragmentary, consisting only of the box label information.

summaries.file_name: 1948 To Provide for Enforcement of Penal Laws, Sale to Crow Tribe of Interests in Estates of Deceased, and Establishment of Commission To Determine Competency of Crow Indians.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1948: Senate Subcommittee Hearing on Crow Competency Commission, Estate Sales, and Penal Law Enforcement
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1948

This document is a transcript of a hearing held on February 6, 1948, before the Senate Subcommittee on Indian Affairs (Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs), presided over by Senator Hugh Butler, with Senators Ecton and Murray present. The hearing addressed three bills: S. 1435 (establishing a commission to determine competency of Crow Indians), S. 901 (sale to the Crow Tribe of interests in estates of deceased allottees), and S. 949 (enforcement of state penal laws on Indian reservations), along with discussion of H.R. 1049 (Indian liquor law repeal).

Robert Yellow Tail, elected Chairman of the Crow Tribal Council, delivered the principal testimony. He described how approximately 475 Crow members were placed on the competency list after 1920 legislation, most now deceased, while university-educated Crow members—including Joe Medicine Crow (Master's degree, University of California), William Jennings (graduate of two universities, Army Major, World War II veteran), and Yellow Tail's own daughter (California State Teachers College graduate)—remained classified as "incompetent." This classification prevented them from leasing their own land; Yellow Tail cited a specific case where a Crow woman was denied permission to plow 80 acres of her allotment for grain and alfalfa to maintain 100 head of cattle. The Crow Reservation encompassed 400,000 acres of tribal lands generating slightly over $20,000 annually. Yellow Tail served as Reservation Superintendent from 1934 to 1945.

The hearing revealed deep factional disputes over tribal governance—whether the General Tribal Council or a newly created 18-member Executive Business Committee legitimately represented the Crow people—with competing delegations present. Assistant Commissioner John Province acknowledged the Department lacked competency hearing action because granting competency would enable Indians to lease land freely, undermining conservation controls on trust land. This testimony powerfully documents how the "incompetency" classification functioned as a legal mechanism to maintain federal control over Indian land use, restricting property rights regardless of individual qualifications, while bureaucratic inertia and conservation rationales perpetuated dispossession even when existing statutes authorized competency determinations.

summaries.display_title: 1948–1974: AAIA Correspondence and Records on Crow Land Dispossession and Federal Indian Policy
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1948–1974

This archival file from the Association on American Indian Affairs (AAIA) comprises correspondence, newspaper clippings, memoranda, legal analyses, and political writings relating to the Crow (Absaroka) Tribe of Montana across roughly three decades. The documents address multiple dimensions of Crow land dispossession and federal Indian policy.

Central to the file is the illegal acquisition of Crow land by non-Indians in violation of the Crow Allotment Act of 1920 (41 Stat. 751), which imposed acreage limitations (1,280 acres grazing or 640 acres agricultural) on non-Indian purchases within the reservation. A 1962 Missouri River Basin Investigation report, obtained by Crow tribal member Roger Stops, found approximately 90% of leased Crow land—roughly 800,000 acres—was held under illegal "overlapping" leases violating a 1927 amendment (44 Stat. 658) that prohibited perpetual lease renewals. Average rental on illegally leased grazing land was 25 cents per acre versus 47 cents on supervised leases and 65 cents on non-Indian-owned land. The federal district court in United States v. Labbitt, 334 F. Supp. 665 (D. Mont. 1971), confirmed most competent leases were void.

Robert Yellowtail, former Crow superintendent (1934–1945), authored extensive political writings invoking the Northwest Ordinance of 1787, Lone Wolf v. Hitchcock (187 U.S. 553), Choate v. Trapp (224 U.S. 665), and Worcester v. Georgia (1832) to argue against federal condemnation of Crow treaty lands for the Yellowtail Dam project. His December 1956 appeal warned that condemnation under the Flood Control Act of 1944 (58 Stat. 887) and Public Works Appropriation Act threatened all Indian treaty rights. The Crow judgment fund of approximately $10,242,984.70, awarded by the Indian Claims Commission for 30 million acres ceded in 1868, became a major dispute between per capita distribution advocates and the Interior Department's insistence on programmatic use under the Act of June 20, 1936 (49 Stat. 1543). The Little Horn Indian Livestock Association's February 1959 letter opposing Senate Bill S.55 detailed how large non-Indian operators used overlapping leases, mutual non-competition agreements, and market suppression to maintain monopolistic control, with only about 50% of tracts offered for sale receiving bids prior to enforcement of Section II. The Crow Land and Livestock Association received AAIA grants of $2,000 (1973) and $1,500 (1974) to establish lease assistance services. The file collectively documents systematic land alienation through statutory violations tolerated by the Bureau of Indian Affairs, economic exploitation through below-market leasing, and persistent federal resistance to Crow self-determination in managing tribal resources.

summaries.file_name: 1940 CROW INDIANS, MONTANA, ELIMINATION OF THE ACRREAGE LIMITATION PROVISION, ACT OF JUNE 4, 1920.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1940: Senate Report on Eliminating Acreage Limitations in the Crow Allotment Act of 1920
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1940

This document is Senate Report No. 1527 (76th Congress, 3d Session), dated April 25, 1940, accompanying H.R. 5477, a bill to amend the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751) by eliminating acreage limitations on restricted land ownership for Crow Indians within the Crow Reservation, Montana. Senator Thomas of Oklahoma submitted the report on behalf of Senator Wheeler from the Senate Committee on Indian Affairs. The report incorporates House Report No. 1693 and a January 26, 1940, letter from Acting Secretary of the Interior A. J. Wirtz to House Indian Affairs Chairman Will Rogers.

The 1920 Act prohibited conveyance of Crow allotted lands to any person owning 640+ acres of agricultural land or 1,280+ acres of grazing land on the reservation, and barred acquisitions that would result in ownership exceeding 1,280 acres agricultural or 1,920 acres grazing. Violations constituted a misdemeanor punishable by up to $5,000 fine or six months imprisonment. Wirtz explained that because Crow allotments were large—some embracing 1,000 acres—and most allottees had inherited additional lands, many Indians had already reached the acreage ceiling and could not expand operations toward self-sufficiency. The bill exempted intra-tribal sales and exchanges from these limits while preserving acreage restrictions against non-Indians to prevent large cattle operators from controlling grazing districts. The legislation also addressed mineral reservations under Section 6 of the 1920 Act (as amended May 26, 1926, 44 Stat. 659), allowing allottees to retain mineral rights in lands sold to fellow tribal members. This document reveals how federal acreage caps, originally intended to prevent monopolization, paradoxically constrained Crow land consolidation efforts while the broader framework of allotment continued facilitating dispossession.

summaries.file_name: Request for Admission of Facts and Genuineness of Documents and Interrogatories (MT USDC Civil 891).pdf
summaries.display_title: 1971: Request for Admission of Facts and Interrogatories, Dillon v. Antler Land Co.
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1971

This document is a Request for Admission of Facts and Genuineness of Documents and Interrogatories filed March 6, 1971, in the U.S. District Court for the District of Montana, Billings Division, Cause No. 891, by the law firm Crowley, Kilbourne, Haughey, Hanson & Gallagher on behalf of defendants Little Horn Land and Livestock Company and Francis J. Murtha, as Trustee for Trustees of Central States, Southeast and Southwest Areas Pension Fund. The plaintiff is Phoebe Wilson Dillon, a Crow Indian (Allottee No. 3636), who received 1,040 acres under two trust patents (No. 1095321, October 10, 1923, and No. 930466, January 26, 1924) pursuant to the Crow Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), located in T.4S., R.34E., Montana.

The attached exhibits comprehensively document the dispossession process. Dillon applied for a patent in fee on February 25, 1955, stating she wished to sell land located too far from her home. On May 21, 1955—while the land was still in trust—she entered a contract with Antler Land Company (controlled by M.H. Tschirgi) to sell 1,040 acres for $7,280 ($7/acre), well below the August 23, 1955 appraised fair market value of $10,400 ($10/acre). Fee Patent No. 1154361 issued September 19, 1955, and on October 6, 1955, Dillon executed a warranty deed to Antler Land Company. The land subsequently passed through multiple Tschirgi family entities—Antler Land Company, individual Tschirgis, Little Horn Land Company, and Little Horn Land and Livestock Company—which secured a $1,800,000 loan from the Teamsters' Central States Pension Fund via a deed of trust on January 7, 1967.

The documents reveal that the pre-patent contract violated Section 5 of the General Allotment Act (25 U.S.C. §348) and that the Tschirgi holdings exceeded the 1,980-acre limitation under Section 2 of the Crow Act. Despite the BIA Area Director's September 11, 1963 recommendation urging suit, and attorney Harlow Pease's persistent advocacy, the Acting Associate Solicitor's August 11, 1964 memorandum demanded additional proof of fraud beyond statutory illegality, effectively stalling federal action. The defendants' interrogatories strategically sought to establish that Dillon knew of the conveyances, never occupied the land after 1955, never paid taxes, and delayed legal action—building a laches or statute of limitations defense. The file powerfully illustrates how BIA competency determinations, inadequate appraisals, and federal inaction facilitated the transfer of Crow trust land to non-Indian ranching interests at below-market prices.

summaries.display_title: 1948: House Committee Hearing on Fee Patent for Mabel Townsend Pretty On Top, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1948–1948

This document is a transcript of proceedings from the U.S. House of Representatives Committee on Public Lands, dated Tuesday, March 2, 1948, with Chairman Richard J. Welch presiding. The committee considered Senate Bill 400 (S. 400), an act authorizing the issuance of a patent in fee to Mabel Townsend Pretty On Top for 160 acres on the Crow Indian Reservation. Representative D'Ewart, Chairman of the Subcommittee on Indian Affairs, explained the bill, which had already passed the Senate and received a favorable recommendation from the subcommittee with amendments.

The Department of the Interior had objected to the bill, fearing the land would pass into private (non-Indian) hands without the Crow Tribe having an opportunity to reacquire it. To address this concern, the subcommittee adopted an amendment granting the Crow Tribe or any enrolled tribal member a 90-day preferential right to purchase the tract at the price offered by a prospective buyer willing and able to purchase. Additional technical amendments to line references were also adopted. The committee unanimously approved all amendments and voted to report S. 400 favorably.

The document demonstrates the fee patent mechanism as a vehicle for alienating trust land from the Crow Reservation, converting restricted Indian allotments into freely transferable private property. While the preferential purchase amendment nominally protected tribal interests, the underlying action facilitated potential dispossession by removing federal trust protections from the 160-acre parcel. The OCR quality is poor, with significant garbling throughout, particularly on pages 2 and 3.

summaries.display_title: 1949: House Subcommittee Hearing on Fee Patent for Francis Howe, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1949–1949

This document is a transcript of a hearing held on Wednesday, March 23, 1949, before the Subcommittee on Indian Affairs of the House Committee on Public Lands, chaired by Hon. Toby Morris. Members present included Representatives Morris, Bosone, Murdock, Bartlett, Doe, James, D'Ewart, Poulson, and Sanborn. The subject was H.R. 1030, a bill authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Francis Howe.

Representative D'Ewart introduced the bill, describing Howe as a self-supporting person engaged in the bakery business for many years who was able to manage his own affairs and did not depend on the Indian Bureau for assistance or guidance. D'Ewart stated that Howe did not use his Indian allotment and that revenue from leases was meager. Howe wished to sell the land, severing his interest in the reservation, to invest in a filling station business. Legislation was required because the land was part of his homestead allotment. The Department of the Interior report, signed by Secretary Oscar L. Chapman on March 17, 1949, stated no objection to enactment. The subcommittee unanimously voted to report the bill favorably to the full Committee on Public Lands.

The document illustrates the fee patent mechanism as a tool of Indian land dispossession: individual allottees were encouraged to sever reservation ties and alienate trust-protected land, often for modest commercial ventures, with both the Indian Bureau and Congress facilitating the removal of federal trust protections. The OCR quality is poor but the substance is largely recoverable.

summaries.display_title: 1949: House Committee Hearing on Fee Patent for Francis Howe, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1949–1949

This document is a transcript of a hearing before the House of Representatives Committee on Public Lands, held on April 1, 1949, at 9:51 a.m., with the Honorable J. Hardin Peterson presiding. The proceeding concerns H.R. 1030, a bill authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Francis Howe (rendered "Franois Hove" in the OCR) of Hardin, Montana, for lands allotted to him on the Crow Indian Reservation, Montana. The specific allotment numbers and acreages are referenced in the bill text but are not fully reproduced in the transcript as preserved here.

Mr. Morris informed the committee that in all the patent-in-fee cases under consideration that morning, there was no contest, that the applicants were entitled to the patents in his and the committee's unanimous judgment, and that the Department of the Interior had raised no objection. Mr. Morris moved that the bill be reported favorably, seconded by Mr. D'Ewart, and the motion carried by voice vote.

The document illustrates the routinized, expedited nature of fee patent issuance on the Crow Reservation by the late 1940s—multiple bills processed in rapid succession with no recorded opposition. Fee patents removed trust protections from allotted land, making it alienable and taxable, a well-documented mechanism of Native land dispossession. The perfunctory character of the hearing, with no testimony from the allottee or tribal representatives, underscores how federal legislative procedure facilitated the conversion of trust land to fee status with minimal scrutiny.

summaries.file_name: Crow Numerical Code 51 - Land taxation records.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1923–1925: BIA Circular and Correspondence on Taxation of Crow Reservation Indian Lands
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1923–1925

This file comprises a Bureau of Indian Affairs policy circular and related correspondence concerning the taxation of Indian property, centered on the Crow Reservation in Montana. The principal document is Circular No. 1849, issued February 3, 1923, by Commissioner Charles H. Burke, which systematically outlines federal rules governing taxation of allotted Indian lands and personal property. It establishes that trust-patent allotments are exempt during the 25-year trust period and any Executive Order extensions, citing the Acts of May 6, 1910 (35 Stats. 348) and December 30, 1916 (38 Stats. 865–866), along with numerous federal court decisions including *U.S. v. Thurston County* (143 Fed. 289), *Ward v. County of Love* (253 U.S. 17), and *Morrow v. U.S.* (243 Fed. 854). Lands sold on deferred payments remain non-taxable until fee patent issues.

Crow Superintendent C. H. Asbury's February 13, 1923 response reveals that Big Horn County was already taxing fee-patented allotments despite the unresolved legal question of whether such land was taxable within the original trust period. His May 1924 correspondence with Mrs. Hartford Frost and with County Assessor James Sullivan and Treasurer J. P. Ross in Hardin shows the county aggressively assessing personal property of Crow Indians, including range horses, and threatening seizure. Asbury's October 7, 1925 letter to Superintendent Coe at Dixon references the *Louis Topsseh* case in Missoula challenging tax sales of patented Indian land. Together, the documents demonstrate how fee patenting—often issued without the allottee's request via competency commissions—immediately exposed Crow Indians to county taxation and tax-sale dispossession, a mechanism Asbury himself acknowledged most Crow allottees could not resist because they lost their land before taxes even came due.

summaries.display_title: 1952: House Committee Hearing on Fee Patent for Agnes Stevens Fisher, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1952–1952

This document is a transcript of a hearing before the House Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs, held on March 25, 1952, with Hon. Toby Morris presiding. The committee considered H.R. 4218, a bill introduced by Representative D'Ewart authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Agnes Stevens Fisher for 640 acres of her homestead allotment on the Crow Indian Reservation in Montana. The bill was necessary because existing law prohibited a Crow Indian from selling more than 320 acres of homestead land, meaning special congressional legislation was required to allow disposal of the full 640-acre allotment.

The Bureau of Indian Affairs had raised an objection, prompting the subcommittee on Indian Affairs to add an amendment providing a 90-day preferential purchase right for the Crow Tribe or any enrolled tribal member to buy the land at the price offered by a prospective outside buyer. This amendment, drafted by former colleague Mr. Lake, had been used in similar bills and accepted by the Senate. Representative D'Ewart moved to report the bill favorably as amended; Representative Miller seconded, and the motion carried.

The document demonstrates a specific mechanism of Crow land dispossession: Congress individually overriding the 320-acre sale restriction—itself a protective measure—to enable fee patenting and alienation of a full 640-acre homestead allotment. The preferential purchase right offered nominal tribal protection but effectively facilitated transfer out of trust status, contributing to the erosion of the Crow Reservation land base through legislative action.

summaries.display_title: 1955–1957: Crow Tribe Opposition to Yellowtail Dam on the Big Horn River
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1955–1957

This collection comprises correspondence, radio addresses, legal memoranda, newspaper clippings, and advocacy documents centered on the Crow Tribe's opposition to the Yellowtail Dam on the Big Horn River, Crow Reservation, Montana. The principal author is Robert Yellowtail, a Crow tribal leader writing from Lodge Grass, Montana, with key correspondence involving the Association on American Indian Affairs (AAIA), its president Oliver La Farge, field director William Zimmerman Jr., executive director LaVerne Madigan, and general counsel Arthur Lazarus Jr.

The Bureau of Reclamation offered the Crow Tribe $1,500,000 for approximately 7,000 acres of tribal treaty lands needed for the dam site and 65-mile reservoir; the Tribe rejected this as inadequate. The Federal Power Commission estimated power costs at 10.5 mills per kilowatt hour as of March 1, 1955, which Yellowtail argued was prohibitively expensive compared to 2.5 mills from the proposed Lake DeSmet steam plant and 5.5 mills from existing Billings facilities. Congress appropriated $4 million for preliminary construction. The Crow Tribal Council voted 178-122 to grant right of entry after Secretary McKay threatened condemnation, then proposed two settlement plans: Plan 1 sought $5 million cash; Plan 2 sought $159,000 annual rental for 60 years with reversion. Senate Joint Resolution 135 authorized $5 million payment but President Eisenhower vetoed it in June 1956 as "extravagant." The government then filed condemnation proceedings in Federal District Court before Judge Pray in November 1956. AAIA filed an amicus curiae brief arguing condemnation was barred by the 1868 treaty and by Section 9 of Public Law 468 (79th Congress, July 28, 1946), which required prior tribal consent for construction on the reservation. Yellowtail invoked Worcester v. Georgia (6 Pet. 515), Choate v. Trapp (224 U.S. 665), Chippewa Indians v. United States (301 U.S. 358), Lone Wolf v. Hitchcock (187 U.S.), and Washington's Northwest Ordinance of 1787. The documents demonstrate systematic dispossession through legislative maneuvering, threatened condemnation, and circumvention of existing statutory protections requiring tribal consent.

summaries.display_title: 1946: House Hearing on Fee Patent Bill for Richard S. Fisher, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1945–1946

This document is a transcript of a hearing held on April 19, 1946, before the House Committee on Indian Affairs, chaired by Hon. Henry M. Jackson, concerning H.R. 4046, a bill introduced by Representative D'Ewart on September 12, 1945, authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Richard S. Fisher of Lodge Grass, Montana, for allotted trust lands on the Crow Indian Reservation totaling 970.19 acres. The lands are described across multiple sections in townships 7 and 8 south, ranges 36 and 37 east, Montana principal meridian, spanning sections 23, 24, 21, 26, and 30.

The Department of the Interior recommended passage on the grounds that Fisher was 27 years old, one-eighth degree Indian blood, married to a white woman with children of one-sixteenth Indian blood, and deemed competent to manage his own affairs. Fisher intended to sell the trust land and invest proceeds in a ranch outside the reservation in the Yellowstone Valley near his white father's holdings. The Crow Agency superintendent confirmed the sale of 970.19 acres would not affect reservation administration or future consolidation of Indian trust lands. The committee voted favorably on the bill, with Mr. White moving and Mr. Murdock seconding.

This document illustrates the fee patent mechanism as a vehicle for alienating Crow allotted lands, using blood quantum, competency determinations, and individual desire to leave the reservation as justifications for converting trust land to fee simple status, effectively removing acreage permanently from tribal trust holdings.

summaries.display_title: 1949: House Hearing on Fee Patent Bill for Jeannette Pearl Burns, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1949–1949

This document is a transcript of a hearing held on April 28, 1949, before the House of Representatives Committee on Public Lands, Subcommittee on Indian Affairs, chaired by Congressman Toby Morris. The hearing concerned H.R. 3886, a bill authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Jeannette Pearl Burns for 299.09 acres on the Crow Indian Reservation. Representative Wesley D'Ewart of Montana introduced the bill, explaining that Burns was employed by the State Department of Public Welfare at $1,800 per annum, supporting four children, and making payments on a small house near the reservation. The income from her trust land provided almost no help, and she sought to sell the land to pay off her house.

William Zimmerman, Acting Commissioner of the Bureau of Indian Affairs, confirmed D'Ewart's account but raised concerns that the land, located in the Wolf Mountain grazing area and entirely surrounded by other Indian land, could be disastrous if transferred to a non-Indian who might gain control of the broader area. The subcommittee adopted an amendment providing a six-month preference right for individual Indians or the Crow tribe to purchase the land before any outside sale. The bill was then reported favorably as amended.

The document illustrates how fee patents facilitated piecemeal alienation of Crow Reservation lands, even when framed as individual economic relief. Zimmerman's concern about non-Indian encroachment into contiguous grazing territory underscores the cumulative dispossession risk these individual transactions posed to tribal land bases. OCR quality is moderate, with some garbled names and words but the substance is clearly legible.

summaries.display_title: 1947: House Hearing on Fee Patent Bill for Mabel Townsend Pretty On Top, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1929–1947

This document is a transcript of a hearing before the House Subcommittee on Indian Affairs of the Committee on Public Lands, held Monday, April 7, 1947, chaired by Representative Wesley A. D'Ewart, concerning H.R. 1487, a bill to authorize issuance of a patent in fee to Mabel Townsend Pretty On Top, a Crow Indian. Acting Commissioner of Indian Affairs William Zimmerman Jr. testified on the case.

Pretty On Top's allotment originally comprised 320 acres in Section 13 and Section 14, Township 7 South, Range 37 East, on Wolf Mountain, approximately 37 miles from her home in the Little Bighorn River valley on the Crow Reservation. In 1929, a fee patent was issued for 160 acres (the southeast quarter of Section 14), which she subsequently sold. The remaining 160 acres (the southwest quarter of Section 13) was still held in trust. Her February 4, 1947 letter to D'Ewart explained she wished to sell the distant, inconvenient land and reinvest proceeds in deeded allotments with multiple heirs adjoining her ranch.

Zimmerman clarified that legislation was unnecessary because the Secretary of the Interior already possessed authority to approve the sale administratively upon her application, and could ensure proceeds were reinvested in adjacent land. He recommended no action on the bill. The subcommittee voted unanimously to pass over H.R. 1487 pending administrative resolution.

The document illustrates how the allotment system fragmented Crow landholdings, how fee patenting facilitated alienation of trust land, and how piecemeal legislative and administrative processes governed individual Indian land transactions, contributing to incremental dispossession.

summaries.display_title: 1947: House Hearing on Fee Patent Bill for Claude E. Milliken, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1946–1947

This document is a transcript of a hearing held on April 7, 1947, before the Subcommittee on Indian Affairs of the House Committee on Public Lands, chaired by Representative Wesley A. D'Ewart, concerning H.R. 1150, a bill to authorize the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Claude E. Milliken for 640 acres of land on the Crow Reservation. Milliken, a 66-year-old Crow Indian allottee whose wife was 75, had previously received patents in fee for 244.91 acres and sought to sell his remaining 640-acre homestead allotment, preferably to a non-Indian buyer, as he stated no Indians had the money to purchase it. His letters to D'Ewart (May 6, 1946, and March 26, 1947) expressed his desire to leave government wardship and become "full white man and taxpayer."

Acting Commissioner William Zimmerman, Jr., of the Bureau of Indian Affairs presented the Department of the Interior's unfavorable report, signed by Under Secretary Oscar L. Chapman. The report noted that 560 of the 640 acres lay within an Indian-owned grazing unit (with the remaining 80 acres in T. 5 S., R. 26 E.), and sale to a non-Indian would fragment the unit and burden administration of surrounding Indian lands. Existing law permitted fee patents on the Crow Reservation only when Indians retained at least 320 homestead acres. Milliken and his wife received $35.00 monthly in old-age assistance and $420.00 annually in lease rentals; selling would eliminate lease income and disqualify them from old-age benefits. Representative Lemke moved to table the bill indefinitely, which passed unanimously. The document illustrates the tension between individual Indian autonomy and federal paternalism, while revealing how allotment-era mechanisms facilitated piecemeal land loss from the Crow Reservation.

summaries.display_title: 1947: House Hearing on Fee Patent Bill for Henry Big Day and Heirs of Catherine Shield Chief, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1947–1947

This document is a transcript of a hearing before the House Committee on Public Lands, held Wednesday, March 19, 1947, concerning H.R. 2199, introduced by Representative D'Ewart. The bill authorized the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Henry Big Day and other heirs of Catherine Shield Chief, deceased, covering 120 acres on the Crow Indian Reservation in Montana. The land, allotted to Catherine Shield Chief under Allotment No. 236, is described as the West 1/2 of the NW 1/4 of Section 14, Township 3 South, Range 32 East, Montana Principal Meridian. Ownership was fractionated among 32 heirs, all of whom agreed to sell their interests to Ed Copath, a non-Indian who already held fee patent to between 5,000 and 6,000 acres of a surrounding 14,000-acre tract and leased the remainder from the Bureau of Indian Affairs on five-year leases. Copath had constructed granaries, corrals, barns, and bunkhouses on the allotment. Annual rental was 91 cents per acre for 60 acres of farmland and 15 cents per acre for 60 acres of grazing land. Transfer to Copath would occur either through land exchange appraised by BIA officials or for cash, at the Bureau's determination. The committee unanimously recommended favorable passage for the Consent Calendar. The document illustrates how fee patenting and fractionated heirship facilitated alienation of allotted Crow land to non-Indian ranchers, with the BIA acting as intermediary in consolidating large private holdings within reservation boundaries.

summaries.display_title: 1947: House Hearing on Fee Patent Bill for Erie F. Howe, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1947–1947

This document is a transcript of a hearing held on April 25, 1947, before the House of Representatives Committee on Public Lands, chaired by Richard J. Welch. The committee considered H.R. 2151, introduced by Representative D'Ewart, a bill authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Erie Fo. Howe (likely "Erie F. Howe" or "Erie Fox Howe"), a member of the Crow Tribe. The lands in question are located on the Crow Indian Reservation in Montana: the west half of Section 10 and the north half of the northwest quarter of Section 15, Township 5 South, Range 28 East, containing approximately 500 acres; and portions of Sections 21 and 22 (east half of the southwest quarter, north half of the southeast quarter, south half of the south half of the southeast quarter of Section 21, and the southwest quarter of Section 22), Township 8 South, Range 38 East, Montana Principal Meridian, containing 360 acres.

The Interior Department, through Acting Commissioner William Zimmerman, recommended an amendment requiring the land first be offered to the Crow Tribal Council, then to tribal members, before any outside sale. The committee adopted this amendment and reported the bill favorably. The OCR quality is poor, rendering some names and legal descriptions partially illegible. This document evidences the congressional mechanism of fee patenting—converting trust-held allotments to alienable fee simple title—which was a primary legal instrument of Crow Reservation land dispossession, enabling individual allottees to sell land out of tribal ownership.

summaries.file_name: Request for Admission of Facts and Genuineness of Documents and Interrogatories by Little Horn Land - Livestock Co. and Francis J. Murtha, Trustee.p69-77.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1971: Request for Admission of Facts in Dillon v. Little Horn Land & Livestock Co., Crow Allotment Dispute
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1923–1971

This document is a Request for Admission of Facts and Genuineness of Documents and Interrogatories filed March 6, 1971, in the United States District Court for the District of Montana, Billings Division, Cause No. 891. It was filed by defendants Little Horn Land and Livestock Company and Francis J. Murtha, as Trustee for Trustees of Central States, Southeast and Southwest Areas Pension Fund, against plaintiff Phoebe Wilson Dillon, a Crow allottee. The attorneys were Crowley, Kilbourne, Haughey, Hanson & Gallagher of Billings.

The filing traces the complete dispossession of Dillon's allotted Crow Reservation lands. Trust Patents 1095321 (October 10, 1923) and 930466 (January 26, 1924) originally allotted the lands to her. She applied for competency determination on December 31, 1948, received approval January 20, 1949, and was declared competent by Superintendent Lippert on April 12, 1949. She applied for Patent in Fee on February 25, 1955, receiving Patent in Fee No. 1154361 on September 19, 1955. On October 6, 1955, she conveyed the land by warranty deed to Antler Land Company. The land then passed through multiple conveyances—to M.H. Tschirgi, Bertha Tschirgi, and F.M. Tschirgi (September 2, 1958), to Little Horn Land Company (September 30, 1958), back to Antler Land Company (January 17, 1964), and finally to Little Horn Land and Livestock Company (January 7, 1967), which secured a $1,800,000 loan using these and other lands as collateral. Plaintiff alleged all conveyances were void under Section 2 of the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), which limited non-Indian ownership to 1,980 acres of patent fee land within the Crow Reservation. The defendants sought admissions that Dillon had never paid taxes, occupied, or used the land after October 6, 1955, constructing a laches and estoppel defense against her fraud claims.

summaries.display_title: 1947: House Subcommittee Hearing on Fee Patent Bill for Henry Big Day and Heirs of Catherine Shield Chief
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1947–1947

This document is a transcript of a hearing held on Saturday, March 15, 1947, before the Subcommittee on Indian Affairs of the House Committee on Public Lands, chaired by Honorable Wesley A. D'Ewart. The proceeding concerned H.R. 2199, a bill authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Henry Big Day and other heirs of Catherine Shield Chief, deceased, to certain lands on the Crow Indian Reservation in Montana. The OCR quality is poor, rendering portions of the title and some names partially garbled, but the substance is discernible.

The subcommittee had previously heard witnesses on February 28, 1947, at which time a Department of the Interior report had not yet been submitted. Despite the department's failure to provide its report even after fifteen days, the subcommittee proceeded to vote. Mr. Barrett moved that H.R. 2199 be reported out favorably to the full committee; Mr. Rockwell seconded. The motion carried unanimously with no opposition, and the session adjourned at 10:40 a.m.—only ten minutes after convening.

This document demonstrates a key mechanism of Crow Reservation land dispossession: the conversion of trust-status allotments into fee-patented land through congressional action, bypassing normal Bureau of Indian Affairs review. The subcommittee's willingness to advance the bill without the Department of the Interior's report underscores how procedural safeguards meant to protect Indian land tenure were routinely overridden, facilitating alienation of reservation lands from tribal ownership.

summaries.display_title: 1949: Senate Subcommittee Markup Session on S. 520, Sale of Crow Reservation Land
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1949

This document appears to be a fragmentary transcript of a congressional subcommittee markup session, likely from the Senate Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs (or its relevant subcommittee), dealing with S. 520, a bill concerning the sale of Crow Reservation land. The OCR quality is very poor, rendering much of the text on page 1 nearly illegible, but pages 2 and 3 are partially recoverable.

The key participants include Mr. Morris (apparently the presiding chairman), Mr. D'Ewart or Marshall (who seconds motions), and Congressman Lelaake (or a similar name, distorted by OCR). The substantive action recorded is the adoption of an amendment to S. 520 providing that when Crow Reservation land is offered for sale, the Crow tribe or any enrolled member shall have a 90-day preferential right to purchase the tract at the price offered by a prospective outside buyer who is willing and able to purchase. Following adoption of this amendment, the subcommittee voted to report S. 520 favorably, as amended, to the full committee as a public law measure. The session adjourned at 4:15.

The document is significant because it records the legislative mechanism by which Crow tribal land could be alienated through sale, with only a limited 90-day preferential purchase right serving as a safeguard. This procedural protection was often insufficient in practice, as tribal members and the tribe itself frequently lacked capital to match outside buyers, effectively facilitating continued dispossession of reservation lands under the guise of equitable process. The bill's favorable reporting indicates congressional momentum toward enabling further land sales from the Crow Reservation.

summaries.display_title: 1952: House Hearing on Fee Patent Bill for Erle E. Howe, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1952–1952

This document is a transcript of a hearing before the House Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs, held on Tuesday, April 22, 1952, in Washington, D.C., chaired by Honorable John R. Murdock. The committee considered H.R. 6556, a bill authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Erle E. Howe for the remainder of his allotment on the Crow Reservation, Montana, comprising 360 acres. A committee member (likely Mr. Stewart or a similar name, partially obscured by poor OCR) presented the bill, noting that the Department of the Interior had submitted a favorable report certifying that Howe was "well qualified to conduct his affairs without governmental supervision or protection" and that alienation of the land would not interfere with the Crow Indians' land-use program. No amendments were offered. The committee unanimously voted to report H.R. 6556 favorably and directed the chairman to place it on the calendar.

This document illustrates the fee patent mechanism of individual land dispossession on the Crow Reservation. By converting trust-status allotment land to fee simple title, the federal government removed restrictions against alienation, making the 360 acres subject to sale, taxation, and potential foreclosure. The perfunctory nature of the hearing—no debate, no testimony from Howe or tribal representatives, unanimous approval—demonstrates how routinized this process had become, effectively facilitating the transfer of tribal land out of Indian ownership through individualized congressional action rather than broad statutory mandate.

summaries.display_title: 1947: House Hearing on Fee Patent Bill for Able Townsend Pretty On Top, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1947–1947

This document is a transcript of a hearing held on June 18, 1947, before a Subcommittee of the House Committee on Public Lands, chaired by Representative Wesley A. D'Ewart, concerning H.R. 1487, a bill authorizing the issuance of a patent in fee to Able Townsend Pretty On Top, a Crow tribal member. The hearing took place in Room 14 of the New House Office Building in Washington, D.C.

Acting Secretary of the Interior Oscar L. Chapman submitted an unfavorable report on the bill, not because the request lacked merit, but because existing law already empowered the Secretary to issue a fee patent or arrange a supervised sale without additional legislation. A Mr. Critchfield, apparently a Bureau of Indian Affairs official, confirmed that Mrs. Pretty On Top's application was under active consideration and that such requests were "usually approved." Representative Barrett and Representative Murdock agreed to hold the bill for three weeks pending a favorable Bureau decision, with the understanding that the bill would be reported out if the Bureau failed to act.

Mrs. Pretty On Top's stated purpose was to sell her allotment on Wolf Mountain, located 37 miles from her home, and use the proceeds to purchase deceased Indians' allotments with multiple heirs near her present ranch, thereby consolidating her acreage. This case illustrates the fee patent mechanism under the General Allotment Act framework, whereby trust protections were removed to enable alienation of Indian land. It also reveals the fractionated heirship problem already complicating Crow allotments by 1947, and how individual land consolidation efforts paradoxically furthered the broader pattern of trust-to-fee conversion that facilitated dispossession.

summaries.display_title: 1948: House Committee Consideration of Senate Bill 400, Fee Patent for Mabel Townsend Pretty On Top
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1948–1948

This document is a transcript of proceedings from the U.S. House of Representatives Committee on Public Lands, dated Tuesday, March 2, 1948, with Chairman Richard J. Welch presiding. The committee considered Senate Bill 400, which authorized the issuance of a patent in fee to Mabel Townsend Pretty On Top for 160 acres on the Crow Indian Reservation. Representative D'Ewart, Chairman of the Subcommittee on Indian Affairs, explained that S. 400 had already passed the Senate and was favorably recommended by the subcommittee with amendments. The Department of the Interior had objected to the bill, fearing the land would pass into private (non-Indian) hands without the Crow Tribe having an opportunity to reacquire it. To address this concern, the subcommittee adopted an amendment granting the Crow Tribe or any enrolled tribal member a 90-day preferential right to purchase the tract at the price offered by a prospective buyer willing and able to purchase. Additional technical amendments to line references were adopted. The committee unanimously approved all amendments and voted to report S. 400 favorably.

The document illustrates the fee patent mechanism as a vehicle for alienating trust land from the Crow Reservation, converting restricted Indian allotments into freely transferable private property. Even with the protective amendment, the legislation facilitated potential dispossession by removing federal trust protections from 160 acres. The OCR quality is poor, with significant character degradation throughout, though the substantive content remains largely recoverable.
