summaries.display_title: 1949: House Hearing on Fee Patent Bills for Mark Wolfe, Estella Wolfe, and Lenora Farwell Fitzler, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1945–1949

This document is a transcript of a hearing held on Friday, June 3, 1949, before the Subcommittee on Indian Affairs of the House Committee on Public Lands, chaired by Representative Toby Morris. The hearing considered three bills introduced by Representative D'Ewart: H.R. 1031, a patent in fee for Mark Wolfe covering 640 acres; H.R. 1032, a patent in fee for Estella Wolfe covering 840 acres; and H.R. 3667, a patent in fee for Lenora Farwell Fitzler covering 1,000 acres — all on the Crow Reservation in Montana. The Bureau of Indian Affairs opposed the Wolfe patents because the lands along Corral Creek controlled water essential to remaining Indian lands. Mark Wolfe contested this in an April 9, 1949 letter, describing the land's location in section 32, township 5 south, range 38 east, and noting the Wolfes had previously sold approximately 2,000 acres, including 1,036 inherited acres sold in 1945. Acting Commissioner William Zimmerman testified that 95 percent of fee-patented lands were sold immediately after issuance and proceeds "frittered away." He reported that since January 1948, 65 patents had been issued on the Crow alone, and nationally in 1948, 501 patents in fee plus 170 restriction removals affected approximately 106,000 acres. Superintendent Critchfield warned that continued land loss would destroy Indian grazing capacity. H.R. 3667 (Fitzler) was reported favorably with an Indian preference amendment; the Wolfe bills' disposition is unclear. The document powerfully illustrates the systematic mechanism of fee patenting as a vehicle for Crow land dispossession, even as BIA officials acknowledged the pattern.

summaries.display_title: 1952: House Committee Markup on Fee Patent Bill for Lucy Yarlott, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1952–1952

This document is a transcript of a markup session of the U.S. House Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs, held on Tuesday, March 25, 1952, with Hon. Toby Morris presiding. The committee considered H.R. 4069, a bill introduced by Representative D'Ewart authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Lucy Yarlott, identified as a Crow Indian, for 169.04 acres of land on the Crow Reservation. The bill had been reported with amendments by the Subcommittee on Indian Affairs. The amendments, requested by the Bureau of Indian Affairs (which issued a favorable report), corrected the spelling of Yarlott's name in two places and added a critical provision: "prior disposition of the homestead lands to Lucy Yarlott is hereby ratified and confirmed." D'Ewart moved adoption of the amended bill by the full committee; the motion carried by voice vote, and the chairman was authorized to take legislative steps to ensure enactment.

The document illustrates the fee patent mechanism as a vehicle for converting trust-status allotment land into alienable private property, removing federal trust protections. The ratification clause suggests that a prior transfer of homestead lands to Yarlott may have occurred outside normal legal channels and required congressional validation. This process—individually legislated fee patents with Bureau endorsement—was a standard administrative pathway through which Crow allotted lands passed out of trust status, facilitating eventual dispossession through sale, taxation, or debt foreclosure once trust protections were lifted.

summaries.display_title: 1949: House Hearing on Fee Patent Bill for Leo Farwell Glenn, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1949–1949

This document is a transcript of a hearing before the House Committee on Public Lands, Subcommittee on Indian Affairs, held on Tuesday, August 2, 1949, in Washington, D.C. The Honorable J. Hardin Peterson, Chairman, presided over consideration of H.R. 3520, an act to authorize and direct the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Leo Farwell Glenn, identified as a Crow allottee, for certain lands on the Crow Reservation.

The hearing reveals that the Department of the Interior's report on the bill was unfavorable, yet the bill had already passed the Senate with a favorable Senate report. The subcommittee, led by Mr. Morris, had reported it unanimously without amendment. During the hearing, an amendment was adopted providing that when the described land was offered for sale, the Crow Tribe or any enrolled tribal member would have a 90-day preferential right to purchase the tract at the price offered by a prospective buyer willing and able to purchase. Mr. Lemke moved to report the bill, Mr. Morris seconded, and it passed by voice vote.

This document illustrates the fee-patenting mechanism by which individual allotments were converted from trust status to unrestricted fee simple ownership, a central instrument of Native American land dispossession. Despite Interior Department opposition, Congress overrode administrative judgment to remove trust protections, effectively enabling alienation of Crow tribal lands. The preferential purchase right amendment offered only a limited safeguard, requiring tribal members to match outside offers within 90 days rather than preventing sale outright. Specific acreages and allotment numbers are not legible in the transcript.

summaries.display_title: 1929–1930: BIA Correspondence on John T. Doyle's Petition to Remove Land Restrictions, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1929–1930

This file documents the effort of John T. Doyle, a Crow allottee of one-quarter blood, to obtain removal of restrictions on his homestead allotment on the Crow Reservation. The file contains Doyle's petition letter dated December 14, 1929, from Sheridan, Wyoming; a forwarding letter from Senator John B. Kendrick (Wyoming) to Commissioner of Indian Affairs Charles J. Rhoads dated January 3, 1930; and the Commissioner's reply dated approximately January 11, 1930, signed under the authority of E. B. Meritt, Assistant Commissioner.

Doyle described his allotment as approximately 630 acres in Sec. 17 and Sec. 18, Township 1 S., Range 37 East, P.M. Montana, including the NW¼, SE¼ of Sec. 18, Lots 1-2-3 of Sec. 18, and the N½ of the SW¼ of Sec. 17. He was 28 years old, a World War I veteran (18 months service, 12 overseas), married with two children, and had lived in Wyoming since 1916. He wished to sell the land to purchase a home in Sheridan and fund aviation training.

The Commissioner's reply cited Section 13 of the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. L., 751), which made Crow homesteads of 640 acres inalienable for twenty-five years or until the allottee's death. The statute permitted sale of no more than 320 acres—not exceeding one-half irrigable or one-half agricultural land—only upon written application approved by the Secretary of the Interior. The Commissioner explicitly stated the Secretary had no authority to remove restrictions from the entire homestead allotment; only a special act of Congress could accomplish that. Doyle was directed to file a partial-sale petition with the Superintendent of the Crow Agency.

The file illustrates how federal trust restrictions under the 1920 Crow Act simultaneously constrained individual Crow allottees' autonomy over their land while ostensibly protecting against wholesale dispossession, creating a bureaucratic gatekeeping mechanism that required congressional intervention for full alienation.

summaries.file_name: Metcalf Mansfield Bill re Crow Act.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1961–1962: Metcalf-Mansfield Bill to Ratify Illegal Land Transactions on the Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1961–1962

This is a newspaper article from *The Gazette* (likely the Billings Gazette) reporting on the Metcalf-Mansfield Bill, legislation introduced by Montana Senators Lee Metcalf and Mike Mansfield to ratify illegal land transactions on the Crow Indian Reservation. The article describes how as many as 400,000 acres of Crow land were held illegally by non-Indians, with most transactions occurring between 1946 and 1955. In October 1961, the U.S. Solicitor General issued an unofficial opinion that approximately 2,000 leases on Crow land held by non-Indians were illegal. In December 1961, Otto Weaver, Crow Reservation superintendent, confirmed the leases would need to be addressed.

The bill would validate illegal purchases made prior to 1957 while preserving mineral rights for original Indian owners or heirs. It referenced the Crow Act of 1920, which imposed statutory acreage limitations on Crow land purchases and outlawed overlapping leasing—the practice of yearly purchasing new five-year leases after voiding previous ones, creating perpetual leases. John A. Carver, Assistant Interior Secretary, recommended the legislation and admitted having no explanation for BIA allowing illegal sales. James F. Canan, BIA area director in Billings, called the leasing problem more serious than the illegal ownership controversy. Crow tribal official Posey Whiteman characterized the legislation as resulting from economic pressures by large landholders seeking to legalize their holdings. The OCR quality is poor, with significant fragmentation and garbled text throughout.

summaries.file_name: Supplement to Brief of Defts. Little Horn Land - Livestock Co. and Francis J. Murtha, Trustee, in support of Motions for Summary Judgement with attachments.p294-313.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1971: Defense Brief in Support of Summary Judgment, Dillon v. Little Horn Land & Livestock Co.
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1914–1971

This document is a Supplement to Brief of Defendants Little Horn Land & Livestock Company and Francis J. Murtha, Trustee, in support of Motions for Summary Judgment in Phoebe Wilson Dillon v. multiple defendants, Cause No. 891, U.S. District Court for the District of Montana, Billings Division, filed September 13, 1971, by Crowley, Kilbourne, Haughey, Hanson & Gallagher of Billings. The brief argues that the Supreme Court's decision in United States v. Powers (305 U.S. 527, 1939) controls the Dillon case, establishing that white owners of former Crow Reservation Indian lands hold vested fee simple titles immune from federal interference.

The brief traces the chain of title of M.H. Tschirgi and Antler Land Company, showing Tschirgi acquired approximately 2,700 acres from F.M. Heinrich (deed December 1, 1914) and additional acreage from the Millers (deed September 16, 1915), totaling over 3,000 acres of former Indian allotments patented in fee before the Crow Act of 1920. Post-Crow Act acquisitions included Patent No. 100402 covering 240 acres (June 14, 1927) with mineral fee reservations, 120 acres conveyed by Indian patentees to Tschirgi (July 27, 1927), and 480 acres conveyed by Charles, Frank, and Katie Yarlott directly to Antler Land Company (April 9, 1929, for $1,500). By February 1928, Tschirgi conveyed over 3,000 acres to Antler for $1.00. Attached deeds and patents—including patents to Bertha Tschirgi for 40-acre parcels with Crow Act mineral reservations—document acquisitions exceeding Section 2 acreage limitations of the Crow Act. The defendants contend that once fee patents issued under the Act of May 8, 1906 (34 Stat. 182), all property rights vested under Montana law, rendering Section 2 restrictions inapplicable and entitling defendants to summary judgment.

summaries.display_title: 1921–1925: BIA Correspondence on Land Partition and Allotment Disputes, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1921–1925

This collection of Bureau of Indian Affairs correspondence and legal documents from the Crow Reservation in Montana reveals multiple dimensions of land dispossession operating simultaneously. The core documents include a Petition for Partition of Inherited Indian Land filed under the Act of June 25, 1910 (36 Stat. L., 856), involving allotments of deceased allottees Cevera his Face (#1980), Strikes One Going in the House (#1981), and Blue Moccasin (#2010), who died September 2, 1908, October 21, 1913, and February 2, 1904, respectively. The allotments totaled approximately 519.81 acres, 174+ acres, and 160 acres, to be partitioned between heirs Pretty Coyote (1/2 interest) and Michael Blue Moccasin (1/2 interest). Chief Clerk correspondence dated August 1, 1921, urged Superintendent C.H. Asbury to consolidate scattered parcels.

A April 25, 1921 letter documents an heir named Yellow Face refusing a $1,310 bid on 135 acres (sale #1012, allotment #2021), with the superintendent characterizing the rejected offer as "fair and just"—revealing pressure on Indian landowners to accept government-endorsed valuations. Inquiries from attorney Albert Garvey (citing the Walsh Act for ex-servicemen), Burlington Railroad colonization agent L. Kuska, and prospective homesteaders demonstrate organized non-Indian interest in acquiring reservation land through deferred payment sales. A November 1925 letter from Crow Agency warns that systematic sale of streamside allotments was rendering waterless range land "practically valueless," with Indians proposing tribal fund purchases of water gaps—evidence that piecemeal allotment sales structurally dispossessed remaining Indian landholders by severing access to water.

summaries.display_title: 1947: House Hearing on Fee Patent Bill for Erle B. Howe, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1947–1947

This document is a transcript of a hearing held on April 7, 1947, before the Subcommittee on Indian Affairs of the House Committee on Public Lands, chaired by Representative Wesley A. D'Ewart. The hearing concerned H.R. 2151, a bill authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Erle B. Howe, a member of the Crow Tribe, for his allotment on the Crow Reservation. Howe, who had relocated to Los Angeles and secured employment as a warehouseman, sought to sell approximately 360 acres of grazing land and remove all restrictions on funds held to his credit. Character references from John C. Tillotson and insurance agent John C. Loomis attested to his competence.

Acting Commissioner William Zimmerman Jr. of the Bureau of Indian Affairs testified that the land was among the best grazing land on the Crow Reservation, situated in a predominantly Indian area. He noted the tension between an individual's right to dispose of his property and the collective interest of remaining Crow ranchers whose grazing operations would be disrupted by sale to non-Indians. Zimmerman referenced Order No. 520, which prohibited sale of Indian trust lands except in cases of dire distress, and confirmed that Howe held no other trust lands beyond his tribal membership interest. Representative Lemke moved to report H.R. 2151 favorably with a recommendation that the Crow tribal council or other Indians be given preference to purchase the land using tribal funds or Department loan funds. The motion carried unanimously. The case illustrates how fee patenting served as a mechanism of land dispossession, converting trust-protected allotments into alienable property, while administrative restrictions like Order No. 520 were circumvented through congressional legislation.

summaries.display_title: 1952: House Hearing on Fee Patent Bill for Jack Bravo, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1952–1952

This document is a transcript of a hearing before the House Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs, held on Tuesday, March 11, 1952, regarding H.R. 4678, a bill authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Jack Bravo, identified as a Crow Indian, for certain tracts of land on the Crow Indian Reservation in Montana. The bill was introduced by Representative D'Ewart and presided over by Chairman Clair Engle. Mr. Morris seconded the motion for favorable reporting. The committee approved the bill with no objections, and the brief proceeding concluded at 11:40 a.m.

The document does not specify acreages, allotment numbers, or dollar amounts for the land in question. The Department of the Interior had already reported favorably on the bill, and the subcommittee had previously approved it. The stated justification was that "Mr. Bravo is qualified to transact his own business," the standard competency determination used under fee patenting procedures rooted in the General Allotment Act of 1887 and subsequent administrative policies.

This record illustrates the fee patent mechanism as a vehicle for land dispossession on the Crow Reservation. Issuance of a fee patent removed trust protections, making the land subject to state taxation, sale, and alienation. Even when initiated by the allottee, fee patenting systematically transferred Indian land out of trust status, often resulting in eventual loss of the parcel. The perfunctory nature of the hearing—lasting minutes with no substantive debate—demonstrates how routinized this process had become by the termination era of the early 1950s.

summaries.display_title: 1948: House Subcommittee Hearing on S. 400, Fee Patent for Mabel Townsend Pretty On Top, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1948

This document is a transcript of a hearing held on January 23, 1948, before the House Committee on Public Lands, Subcommittee on Indian Affairs, chaired by Representative Wesley A. D'Ewart, concerning S. 400, a bill authorizing the issuance of a patent in fee to Mabel Townsend Pretty On Top for lands allotted to her on the Crow Reservation, Montana. The bill had already been reported favorably by the Senate Public Lands Committee.

The hearing record incorporates the Senate committee report explaining the legal necessity of the legislation. The Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), limited Crow Indian allottees to selling no more than 320 acres of homestead land, requiring Secretarial approval. Because Pretty On Top sought to sell all of her homestead allotment—exceeding the 320-acre statutory cap—special legislation was required. Acting Secretary of the Interior Oscar L. Chapman, in a letter dated June 10, 1947, to Senator Hugh Butler, recommended against enactment, arguing that existing administrative authority already permitted the Secretary to issue a fee patent or arrange supervised sale, making additional legislation unnecessary. Assistant Commissioner William Zimmerman characterized the case as "one of the most troublesome ones we have," and the subcommittee deferred action, adjourning at 12:15 p.m. without a vote.

The document illustrates how fee patenting served as a primary mechanism of Crow land dispossession, converting trust-protected allotments into alienable property. It also reveals tension between congressional and executive approaches to removing restrictions on Indian land, with Interior preferring discretionary administrative authority over statutory mandates that bypassed departmental oversight.

summaries.display_title: 1947: House Hearing on Fee Patent Bill for Joseph J. Pickett, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1916–1947

This document is a transcript of a hearing before the Subcommittee on Indian Affairs of the House Committee on Public Lands, held April 7, 1947, chaired by Representative Wesley A. D'Ewart, concerning H.R. 1217, a bill authorizing issuance of a fee patent to Joseph J. Pickett, a Crow tribal member. Acting Commissioner of Indian Affairs William Zimmerman Jr. testified in opposition. Pickett, approximately 54–55 years old with three children and a former Indian Service employee, sought fee simple title to his 640-acre original allotment on the Crow Reservation so he could sell part of it, purchase adjacent "dead Indian lands," and rehabilitate his ranch. He also held 560 acres of inherited land. Zimmerman noted that inherited land could be sold without congressional action, and that only 220 acres of the 640-acre allotment required legislative authorization due to the Crow Reservation's 320-acre limitation on original allotments. Pickett received only 22–25 cents per acre in grazing rental. Zimmerman revealed that Pickett had previously received fee patents on 160 acres (1916) and 161 acres (1923), both of which he sold. Senator Wheeler had earlier introduced S. 482 in 1945 for the same purpose, denied by the Chicago office of Indian Affairs. Pickett's letters express frustration with "Indian Office domination" and the paternalistic trust system preventing him from managing his own property. The subcommittee deferred action, directing Zimmerman to resolve the matter locally at the Crow Agency. The document illustrates how federal trust restrictions simultaneously constrained individual Native autonomy and created conditions—minimal rental income, inability to borrow or purchase land freely—that perpetuated economic dependency and facilitated dispossession when fee patents were eventually granted and land sold.

summaries.display_title: 1950: House Hearing on Fee Patent Bill for Guy C. Heckenlively, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1950–1950

This document is a transcript of a hearing held on Wednesday, May 10, 1950, before the Subcommittee on Indian Affairs of the House Committee on Public Lands, chaired by Representative Toby Morris. The hearing concerned H.R. 6963, introduced by Representative D'Ewart, authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Guy C. Heckenlively (rendered in the OCR as "Hickenlively") for land on the Crow Reservation in Montana. H.M. Critchfield, Chief of the Branch of Lands in the Bureau of Indian Affairs, testified that the Department of the Interior had no objection to the bill. Critchfield noted that the land in question was "surrounded on three sides by alienated lands," which he cited as reason to forgo inserting a clause granting Indians a right of first purchase. D'Ewart indicated the bill had been "cleared with the Indian Bureau" and referenced a companion bill, H.R. 7293, also receiving no departmental objection. Representative Marshall moved to report H.R. 6963 favorably to the full Committee on Public Lands; the motion passed unanimously. The hearing adjourned at 10:49 a.m.

The document illustrates the administrative and legislative mechanisms by which Crow Reservation trust lands were converted to fee simple ownership, effectively removing them from tribal control. The Bureau's acquiescence, the rationale that surrounding lands were already alienated, and the absence of any tribal voice in the proceedings demonstrate how federal patent-in-fee legislation facilitated incremental dispossession of reservation land, creating a checkerboard pattern of non-Indian ownership within reservation boundaries.

summaries.display_title: 1947–1948: House Subcommittee Hearing on Fee Patent for Mabel Townsend Pretty On Top (S. 400)
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1947–1948

This document is a congressional subcommittee hearing transcript concerning S. 400 (with companion bill H.R. 1497), legislation authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Mabel Townsend Pretty On Top for lands allotted to her on the Crow Reservation, Montana. The hearing involves Representatives D'Ewart (chairman), Lemke, Rockwell, and Jenison, with testimony from Acting Commissioner William Zimmerman and staff member Reeve. S. 400 was introduced January 27, 1947, and the Interior Department report from Acting Secretary Oscar L. Chapman, dated June 10, 1947, recommended against enactment, arguing existing law already permitted the Secretary to issue a fee patent or arrange a supervised sale without additional legislation.

The Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751) limited Crow allottees to selling no more than 320 acres of homestead land upon written application with Secretarial approval. S. 400 sought to circumvent this acreage cap so Pretty On Top could sell her entire homestead allotment. Zimmerman noted the allotment straddled a stream controlling a grazing unit, making its alienation harmful to surrounding Indian landholders, though the Crow Tribe had not arranged to purchase it. The subcommittee unanimously amended S. 400 to include a 90-day preferential purchase right for the tribe or individual tribal members before any outside sale, then reported it favorably as amended.

The document illustrates how individual fee-patenting legislation functioned as a mechanism of Crow Reservation land dispossession, bypassing protective acreage limitations in existing law and converting trust land to alienable status despite Interior Department opposition, with only a modest tribal preference right as a safeguard.

summaries.display_title: 1949: House Public Lands Committee Hearing on Fee Patent for Hodac C. Heckenlively (H.R. 1029)
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1949–1949

This document is a transcript of a hearing before the House of Representatives Committee on Public Lands, held on April 1, 1949, at 9:49 a.m., presided over by the Honorable J. Hardin Peterson. The proceeding concerns H.R. 1029, a bill authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Hodac C. Heckenlively (name partially garbled by OCR). The bill was sponsored by Representative D'Evart, who represented Montana, where the Crow Reservation is located, suggesting a connection to Crow allotted lands.

During the brief markup session, Mr. Morris moved to amend the bill by striking the word "vest" in Line 9 and inserting a corrected term. The amendment was seconded by Mr. D'Evart and approved. Mr. Morris then moved that the bill be reported favorably, noting it had been unanimously agreed to by the Subcommittee and that there was no objection from the Department of the Interior. The full committee approved the favorable report unanimously.

The document demonstrates the legislative mechanism by which individual fee patents were issued to remove trust protections from Indian allotments, enabling alienation of the land through sale or taxation. Fee patenting was a primary legal instrument of dispossession, converting restricted trust land into freely transferable private property. The speed and perfunctory nature of the proceeding—no substantive debate, no testimony from the allottee or tribal representatives—illustrates how routinized this dispossession process had become by mid-century. No acreage, dollar amounts, or allotment numbers are specified in the surviving text. OCR quality is poor, with some names and words partially illegible.

summaries.display_title: 1947: House Subcommittee Hearing on Fee Patent for B. M. Phelps, Crow Allottee (H.R. 1727)
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1947–1947

This document is a transcript of a hearing held on April 7, 1947, before the Subcommittee on Indian Affairs of the House Committee on Public Lands, chaired by Representative Wesley A. D'Ewart, concerning H.R. 1727, a bill authorizing issuance of a patent in fee to B. M. (Bud) Phelps, a one-quarter Crow Indian residing on the Crow Indian Reservation in Big Horn County, Montana. Phelps held Allotment No. 2174, approximately 1,000 acres known as the Lone Wolf Ranch. His affidavit, prepared by attorney L. S. Strahan of Lovell, Wyoming, stated the land was surrounded by patented lands and grazing leases controlled by larger operators including Tshirgi and C. A. Lewis, making independent livestock operations impractical. Phelps claimed he could sell for $15,000.

Acting Commissioner William Zimmerman Jr. of the Bureau of Indian Affairs opposed the bill, revealing that Phelps had been convicted of grand larceny for stealing 271 sheep and needed cash for his defense, suggesting the fee patent application was made under duress. Zimmerman submitted a signed statement from Phelps dated March 11, 1947, retracting his desire to sell. Zimmerman further disclosed that Lewis—whose attorney drafted the original affidavit—sought to purchase the allotment to consolidate his own holdings, and that the land controlled strategic water resources. The subcommittee passed the bill over without prejudice. The case illustrates how criminal prosecution, economic pressure, and neighboring non-Indian operators' land consolidation efforts combined to threaten Crow allotment retention, with the fee patent mechanism serving as the legal vehicle for potential dispossession.

summaries.display_title: 1962–1964: AAIA Legal Memoranda on Crow Tribe Land Problems and Exploitative Leasing
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1962–1964

This document is a letter dated February 5, 1964, from attorneys Arthur Lazarus, Jr. and Richard Schifter, General Counsel at the Association on American Indian Affairs (AAIA), to William Byler, AAIA Executive Director. The letter transmits two enclosures: a December 1962 legal memorandum concerning problems facing the Crow Tribe, and newly issued Department of the Interior regulations addressing exploitative leasing practices. The letter specifically describes how "competent" Crow Indians were induced into long-term leases on their lands structured so that advance rental payments kept them perpetually indebted and economically unable to cancel leases or negotiate better terms. The Department's new regulations were intended to halt this "insidious practice."

The accompanying memorandum (pages 2–10), though severely degraded by poor OCR, addresses multiple dimensions of Crow land dispossession. Discernible fragments reference approximately 13,900 acres, a requirement that purchasers own at least 640 acres of agricultural land, restrictions on sales within the reservation "whether held by trust," illegal land sales discovered in 1955 that the Bureau halted, a failed congressional bill to validate those illegal transactions, the Solicitor's opinion that such leases were "utterly void and non-enforceable" and cancellable at any time, and legal citations including 237 U.S. 74 (1915) and Cole, 263 U.S. 250 (1923). References to per capita distributions, tribal government deficiencies, and five-year lease terms appear throughout. The document demonstrates systematic economic dispossession through predatory leasing mechanisms targeting individual Crow allottees, with federal intervention arriving belatedly.

summaries.file_name: Crow RG 75 Crow Act, fee patents etc - (1).pdf
summaries.display_title: 1918–1927: Crow Agency Correspondence on Allotment Act Implementation and Fee Patents
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1918–1927

This archival file from the Crow Indian Agency (RG 75, Box 10) documents the implementation of the Crow Allotment Act of June 4, 1920 (Public No. 239, 41 Stat. 751), encompassing correspondence between Superintendent Calvin H. Asbury, the Commissioner of Indian Affairs, the Secretary of the Interior, and members of the Crow Enrollment and Competency Commission (Asbury, Robert Yellow Tail, and John Frost). The Act directed allotment of all Crow Reservation lands: 160 acres to heirs of members who died unallotted after December 31, 1905; 160 additional acres to heads of families; and prorated distribution of remaining allottable lands so every enrolled member living June 4, 1920, received an equal aggregate share. Each member was required to designate a 640-acre homestead, inalienable for 25 years.

A December 10, 1920 Solicitor's opinion by Charles D. Mahaffie held that fee patents could not issue until additional allotments were completed and homesteads designated, invalidating fee patent No. 763193 issued to Jasper Long Tail (allottee No. 762) and the approved order for Richard Wallace (allottee No. 460), both deemed violations of the Act. Competent Indians had to elect fee patents in writing; wives had to join applications for homestead patents. The Commission was paid $10/day from "Indian Moneys, Proceeds of Labor, Crow Indians." District councils at Wyola, Lodge Grass, Reno, Black Lodge, Big Horn, and Pryor elected delegates on June 28–30, 1920, who then elected Yellow Tail and Frost as tribal commissioners on July 2, 1920. Chief Plenty Coos urged that fee patents not be forced on Indians without consent. The documents reveal how allotment mechanisms, competency classifications, and fee patenting operated as instruments of land dispossession, while also showing Crow resistance through procedural engagement and explicit opposition to forced patenting and land sales.

summaries.display_title: 1948: House Subcommittee Hearing on Fee Patent for Claude Milliken, Crow Allottee (H.R. 1150)
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1948–1948

This document is a transcript of a congressional subcommittee hearing, likely from the House Committee on Public Lands or Indian Affairs, chaired by Representative Wesley D'Ewart, concerning H.R. 1150, a bill to authorize the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Claude Milliken, a Crow Indian allottee on the Crow Reservation in Montana. The hearing took place on or around April 1, 1948, with testimony from John R. Provinse, Assistant Commissioner of the Bureau of Indian Affairs.

Milliken, age 65–66, and his wife, age 75, retained a 640-acre homestead from his original allotment, having previously received patents in fee for 244.91 acres. Of the 640 acres, 560 lay within an Indian-owned grazing unit, with running water available in early summer; the remaining 80 acres were in T. 5 S., R. 26 E., in an adjacent township. The Department of the Interior's report was unfavorable, arguing that fee patenting would lead to sale to a non-Indian, fragmenting the grazing unit and burdening administration of surrounding Indian land. The couple received $35 per month in old-age assistance from Montana and $420 annually in lease rentals. Under existing Crow Reservation law, the Secretary could authorize fee patents only if the allottee retained at least 320 acres of homestead; disposal beyond that threshold required congressional action. Despite the Department's opposition, the subcommittee voted unanimously to report H.R. 1150 favorably with a Senate amendment. The document demonstrates how congressional override of BIA recommendations facilitated individual allotment alienation, fragmenting collectively managed Crow grazing units and advancing non-Indian land acquisition on the reservation.

summaries.display_title: 1947: House Subcommittee Hearing on Fee Patent for Joseph J. Pickett, Crow Allottee (H.R. 2127)
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1916–1947

This document is a transcript of a hearing held on July 18, 1947, before a Subcommittee of the House Committee on Public Lands, chaired by Representative Wesley A. D'Ewart, concerning H.R. 1217 (also referenced as H.R. 2127), an application by Joseph J. Pickett, a Crow tribal member, for issuance of a fee patent on his remaining trust lands. Bureau of Indian Affairs official Critchfield testified that Pickett was originally allotted 961 acres on the Crow Reservation. In 1916 he received a fee patent covering 180 acres, and in 1920 an additional 161 acres, totaling 320 acres in fee—both of which he subsequently sold, retaining nothing from the proceeds. He still held 640 acres in trust as his homestead and was now requesting fee patents on 560 acres of that homestead plus 960 acres of inherited land, totaling approximately 1,200 acres. Crow allottees faced a statutory limitation preventing disposal of more than 320 homestead acres even with departmental approval. Critchfield opposed the patent, noting the land lay in prime grazing territory the Bureau sought to keep intact for Crow livestock operations. A supporting letter from pastor C.A. Bentley of the Burgess Memorial Indian Baptist Church vouched for Pickett's competence. The subcommittee debated whether to impose right-of-first-refusal restrictions favoring the Crow Tribe and ultimately voted to hold the bill in abeyance for twenty days pending inquiry into tribal loan alternatives. The document illustrates the fee patent mechanism as a primary vehicle of Crow land dispossession: Pickett's prior fee patents led directly to alienation of 320 acres, and the Bureau explicitly acknowledged this pattern while simultaneously controlling whether Indians could access their own property.

summaries.display_title: 1951: House Interior Committee Hearing on Fee Patent for Joseph Pickett, Crow Allottee (H.R. 3638)
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1951–1951

This document is a transcript of a hearing held on Tuesday, July 10, 1951, before the full House Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs, chaired by Representative John R. Murdock. The committee considered H.R. 3638, a bill authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Joseph Pickett for lands on the Crow Indian Reservation in Montana. The lands described comprise the southwest quarter and the south half of the southeast quarter of section 13, the southeast quarter of section 14, and the north half of section 24, township 2 south, range 36 east, Montana principal meridian, totaling seven hundred and twenty acres. Subcommittee Chairman Morris reported that the subcommittee had unanimously approved the bill, noting a favorable report from the Department of the Interior stating that Pickett was "fully capable of directing his own affairs" and that there was no objection. The full committee voted unanimously to report the bill favorably, authorizing the Chairman to take necessary action for enactment. Representative D'Ewart seconded the motion.

This document illustrates the legal mechanism by which trust-status reservation land was converted to fee simple title, removing federal trust protections and making the land alienable and taxable. The patent-in-fee process was a primary instrument of Crow Reservation land dispossession, as fee-patented lands could subsequently be sold, mortgaged, or lost to tax forfeiture. The 720-acre parcel is substantial, and the perfunctory, unopposed nature of the proceedings—no amendments, no debate, unanimous approval—demonstrates how routinely Congress facilitated the transfer of Indian trust lands out of protected status.

summaries.display_title: 1948–1957: BIA Billings Area Office Statistics on Indian Trust Land Removed from Trust Status
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1948–1957

This document is a statistical table (Table VII) from the Bureau of Indian Affairs Billings Area Office, recording individual Indian trust lands removed from all trust status across seven reservations from 1948 to 1957. The table covers the Blackfeet, Crow, Flathead, Fort Belknap, Fort Peck, Northern Cheyenne, and Wind River agencies, tracking annual acreage figures for each.

The total acreage removed from trust status across all agencies over the decade is staggering: 1,082,263.83 acres. Annual totals range from approximately 34,689 acres to 379,403 acres, with peak years appearing in the early-to-mid 1950s—coinciding with the federal termination era and policies encouraging fee patenting and land alienation. The Crow Reservation shows substantial removals, with figures including 103,893 acres, 51,420 acres, and other large annual entries, though OCR quality makes precise year-by-year attribution difficult for some cells. Fort Peck and Flathead also show very large cumulative losses, with entries such as 363,106 acres and 214,219 acres respectively in certain years.

This table is critical evidence of systematic land dispossession through the administrative mechanism of removing trust protections from individual Indian allotments—effectively converting restricted trust land to fee simple status, which enabled sale, taxation, and foreclosure. The document quantifies the scale of dispossession during the termination era across the entire Billings area, demonstrating that trust removal was not incidental but a massive, sustained bureaucratic process affecting over one million acres in a single BIA administrative region within one decade.

summaries.display_title: 1947: House Subcommittee Hearing on Fee Patent for Hartford Bearclaw, Crow Allottee (H.R. 1483)
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1947–1947

This document is a transcript of a hearing held on April 7, 1947, before the Subcommittee on Indian Affairs of the House Committee on Public Lands, chaired by Representative Wesley A. D'Ewart, concerning H.R. 1483, a bill authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to issue a fee patent to Hartford (Comesfromabove) Bearclaw, a Crow Indian. William Zimmerman, Jr., Acting Commissioner of the Bureau of Indian Affairs, testified. Hartford Bearclaw, age 60, was the sole heir of Bearclaw, Crow Indian allottee numbered 1069, and had inherited thousands of acres on the Crow Reservation. The bill sought a fee patent for 80 acres so Bearclaw could sell the land and use the proceeds to purchase livestock and farm machinery to develop his remaining holdings. An unnamed attorney's letter read into the record argued that trust restrictions prevented Bearclaw from using his land as collateral, effectively barring him from obtaining bank loans. Zimmerman noted that the Secretary already had administrative authority to issue such patents but no application had been filed, and that Bearclaw was technically eligible for loans from tribal funds, a $5,000,000 federal revolving fund, and the Farm Home Administration. Representatives Lemke and Murdock pressed Zimmerman on the practical inability of Indian veterans and landowners to secure credit despite nominal eligibility. The subcommittee voted unanimously to report H.R. 1483 favorably, amending the land description from "south half" to "north half" in line 8. The document illustrates how trust restrictions functioned as mechanisms of economic dispossession—rendering large Indian landholdings effectively unusable as capital—while simultaneously creating pressure to alienate land through fee patents and sale.

summaries.display_title: 1987–1991: Federal Court Filings, Crow Tribe of Montana v. United States (Civil Action No. 87-2155)
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1982–1991

This FOIA release comprises federal court filings from *Crow Tribe of Montana v. United States of America*, Civil Action No. 87-2155, U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia, before Judge Norma Holloway Johnson. The documents include the original complaint (filed August 4, 1987), motions for a temporary restraining order, a memorandum opinion denying defendants' motion to dismiss (July 20, 1990), a motion for reconsideration, and related correspondence and exhibits. Attorney Charles Cervantes (later substituted by Thomas E. Towe) represented the Crow Tribe; DOJ attorney Silvia Sepulveda-Hambor represented the United States.

The complaint alleges that under the 1851 Fort Laramie Treaty the Crow held 38,000,000 acres, reduced by successive federal acts to approximately 2,000,000 acres. On July 2, 1982, Interior imposed GS-14 civil engineer Gale Loomis as "Tribal Executive Officer" with absolute control over tribal finances, media, contracts, and grants—effectively destroying tribal sovereignty. An April 1982 Inspector General audit of $2,400,690 in costs found only $10,753 in actual disallowances (.004%), yet Interior recommended full federal takeover. The Tribe claimed $10.3 million in inaccessible Treasury funds, potential economic development capitalization of $495,000,000, and sought $1.6 million in compensatory damages, $30 million for tortious interference, and $3.2 million in punitive damages. The TRO was denied August 11, 1987.

The second amended complaint focused on Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act of 1920 (41 Stat. 751), which voided conveyances exceeding 640 acres agricultural or 1,280 acres grazing land to any single buyer. The Tribe alleged complete federal non-enforcement despite an Interior Inspector General audit (March 3, 1986) documenting illegal purchases. The Court's July 20, 1990 opinion found the Tribe had standing, that sovereign immunity did not bar mandamus under 28 U.S.C. §1361, and that the Second Treaty of Fort Laramie's "bad men" clause and federal trust responsibility potentially limited prosecutorial discretion. Legal mechanisms invoked include the Indian Self-Determination and Education Act (25 U.S.C. §§450–450a), the Administrative Procedure Act (5 U.S.C. §706), P.L. 638 contracting authority, and the Fifth Amendment. The documents collectively demonstrate how federal administrative control, financial withholding, suspension of letters of credit, and non-enforcement of land-accumulation restrictions operated as mechanisms of ongoing dispossession of Crow resources and sovereignty.

summaries.file_name: Separate nswer of Defts. Little Horn Land Co., et al, to Request for Admissions of Fact.p433-37.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1971: Defendants' Answer to Request for Admissions, Dillon v. Antler Land Company et al.
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1955–1971

This document is a separate answer to a request for admissions of fact filed October 26, 1971, in the United States District Court for the District of Montana, Billings Division, Cause No. 891, *Phoebe Wilson Dillon v. Antler Land Company, Little Horn Land and Livestock Company, et al.* The answering defendants—Little Horn Land Company, Antler Land Company, Union Bank and Trust Company (executor of the estate of Bertha Tschirgi), and R. F. Hibbs (executor of the estate of F. M. Tschirgi)—were represented by Hibbs, Sweeney & Colberg of Billings, Montana.

The defendants admitted that Antler Land Company executed an agreement (Complaint Exhibit "A") bearing M. H. Tschirgi's genuine signature, and that on multiple dates in 1955 (February 25, May 21, September 19, October 3, and October 6), Antler Land Company owned in excess of 1,920 acres of grazing land within the Crow Indian Reservation. They further admitted that the Big Horn County Assessor assessed 40,021.02 acres to Antler Land Company for 1958, and that Little Horn Land and Livestock Company now controlled over 120,000 acres in Big Horn County through deeds or leases, fully surrounding the plaintiff's land. The corporation was wholly or nearly wholly owned by M. H. Tschirgi and his family, with Tschirgi serving as president. A patent (Defendants' Exhibit No. 10) was recorded October 14, 1955. These admissions demonstrate massive consolidation of former Crow Reservation lands by interconnected Tschirgi-controlled entities, evidencing systematic dispossession of individual Indian allottees like plaintiff Dillon through warranty deeds and corporate land accumulation far exceeding the 1,920-acre grazing threshold.

summaries.display_title: 1947: House Subcommittee Hearing on Fee Patent for Henry Big Day and Heirs of Catherine Shield Chief (H.R. 2199)
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1947

This document is a transcript of a hearing held February 28, 1947, before the House Subcommittee on Indian Affairs of the Committee on Public Lands, chaired by Representative Wesley A. D'Ewart, concerning H.R. 2199. The bill would authorize the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Henry Big Day and other heirs of Catherine Shield Chief, deceased, for 120 acres on the Crow Indian Reservation. Attorney Bert V. Kronmiller of Montana appeared on behalf of Ed Kopak, a non-Indian lessee who operated a 14,000-acre unit on the reservation and already owned 5,000–6,000 acres in fee within it. Kopak had built granaries, corrals, barns, and bunkhouses on the allotment and sought fee title to protect those improvements. He paid 75 cents per acre annually for 60 acres of farmland and 15 cents per acre for 60 acres of grazing land. Henry Big Day held a one-half interest, Jennie Smart Enemy one-fourth, and numerous minor heirs the remaining one-fourth, some owning as little as 1/384th. Kronmiller noted a 1943 fee patent application to the local Indian Office had been ignored. Lawrence N. Stevens of the Bureau of Indian Affairs cited Order No. 420 (August 12, 1933), codified at 25 CFR 241.12a, which restricted sales of trust land except in cases of distress or emergency. Herbert J. Slaughter of the Solicitor's Office invoked the Crow Act of 1920, which required designation of 640-acre homesteads and prohibited disposal of more than half without legislation. The subcommittee voted unanimously to report the bill favorably. The document illustrates how fractionated heirship, bureaucratic inaction, and non-Indian lessee pressure combined to facilitate alienation of Crow trust land.

summaries.display_title: 1947: House Subcommittee Hearing on Fee Patent for Spencer Burgess Doyle, Crow Allottee (H.R. 1148)
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1945–1947

This document is a transcript of a hearing held on April 7, 1947, before the Subcommittee on Indian Affairs of the House Committee on Public Lands, chaired by Representative Wesley A. D'Ewart, concerning H.R. 1148, a bill authorizing issuance of a patent in fee to Spencer Burgess Doyle for his allotment on the Crow Indian Reservation in Montana. Doyle, a military veteran residing in Boise, Idaho, had been seeking the patent for several years, having previously had H.R. 6436 introduced in an earlier Congress without success. His letters to D'Ewart express frustration at bureaucratic delays and his desire to sell the allotment to fund a business venture, arguing he would never return to the reservation.

William Zimmerman Jr., Acting Commissioner of the Bureau of Indian Affairs, testified that Doyle appeared competent to manage his own affairs and that the Bureau saw no objection to the bill's enactment. However, Zimmerman raised a broader policy question: under existing law, even after selling his allotment, Doyle could return to the reservation and claim federal gratuity services including hospital care and relief. Zimmerman suggested this case illustrated the need for general legislation—referenced alongside H.R. 2165, Representative Case's bill to "emancipate" Indian veterans—requiring individuals who sever tribal connections to waive future federal benefits. Representative Lemke proposed a blanket statutory provision rather than case-by-case conditions. The subcommittee voted unanimously to report H.R. 1148 favorably.

The document reveals how fee patenting facilitated permanent alienation of Crow allotment land while federal officials simultaneously sought to terminate ongoing trust obligations, linking individual land dispossession to broader emancipation and termination-era policy development.

summaries.display_title: 1949: House Subcommittee Hearing on Fee Patent for Howard C. Heckenlively, Crow Allottee (H.R. 1029)
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1949–1949

This document is a transcript of a hearing before the House Committee on Public Lands, Subcommittee on Indian Affairs, held on Wednesday, March 23, 1949, presided over by Representative Toby Morris. The OCR quality is poor but the substance is largely recoverable. The hearing concerns H.R. 1029, a bill authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Howard C. Heckenlively for his homestead allotment on the Crow Reservation. Representative D'Ewart introduced the bill, explaining that Heckenlively possessed a small percentage of Indian blood, had never lived on the reservation, worked as a switchman in the railroad yards at Livingston, Montana, and was self-supporting. The allotment comprised 320 acres and yielded little or no income from leases. Heckenlively had a prospective purchaser, and the proceeds would fund his retirement from railroad employment. The Department of the Interior report, signed by Oscar L. Chapman, Undersecretary of the Interior, raised no objection to enactment but recommended a technical amendment changing "west" to "north" in line 9 of the bill to correct the land description. The subcommittee adopted the amendment and voted to report H.R. 1029 favorably to the full committee. Members present included Morris, Murdock, Baring, Bosone, Lemke, D'Ewart, Poulson, and Sanborn. The document illustrates the fee patent mechanism as a vehicle for alienating trust-held Crow Reservation land, converting restricted Indian allotments into freely saleable private property, thereby facilitating permanent dispossession of tribal land base through individual congressional action.

summaries.display_title: 1950: House Public Lands Committee Hearing on Fee Patent for Anson Harold Pease, Crow Allottee (S. 2510)
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1950–1950

This document is a transcript of a brief hearing before the House Committee on Public Lands, held Wednesday, June 14, 1950, in the New House Office Building, Washington, D.C., with Chairman J. Hardin Petersen presiding. The committee considered S. 2510, a Senate bill to authorize and direct the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Anson Harold Pease, identified as a Crow allottee, for certain lands. The bill had already passed the Senate with an amendment. Representative Barrett moved to report the bill out favorably, seconded by Representative Murdock, and the motion carried by voice vote. The Chairman was authorized to take all parliamentary steps to facilitate passage.

The document is significant as evidence of the fee-patenting mechanism used to remove trust protections from individual Indian allotments. A patent in fee converted trust land—held by the federal government for the benefit of the allottee—into unrestricted private ownership, making it alienable and subject to state taxation, which historically led to rapid land loss among Native allottees. That Congress acted through a specific private bill for a single Crow allottee suggests either the standard administrative process was insufficient or particular circumstances required legislative action. The OCR quality is poor, with numerous garbled characters, but the essential content—bill number, allottee name, tribal affiliation, committee action, and date—is recoverable. No specific acreages, dollar amounts, or allotment numbers appear in the surviving text.

summaries.display_title: 1920–1967: DOJ Litigation Case File on Crow Trust Land Conveyance, Dillon v. Antler Land Company
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1967

This file (DOJ Litigation Case File 90-2-5-355) documents the federal government's deliberation over—and ultimate refusal to pursue—litigation to recover approximately 1,040 acres of Crow Indian trust land conveyed by Phoebe Wilson Dillon (Crow Allottee No. 3636) to the Antler Land Company, a Montana corporation controlled by the Tschirgi family. The land, described as portions of Sections 25, 26, and 36, T. 4 S., R. 34 E., Montana Meridian, was held under two trust patents (No. 920401, issued October 10, 1923, and No. 930466, issued January 26, 1924) pursuant to the Crow Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751). On February 25, 1955, Dillon applied for an unrestricted fee patent, stating she wished to sell. While the application was pending and the land still in trust, she contracted with Antler Land Company on May 21, 1955, for $7,280. The fee patent (No. 1154361) issued September 19, 1955; the warranty deed followed October 6, 1955. Antler subsequently quitclaimed to the Tschirgi family members, who conveyed to Little Horn Land Company on September 30, 1958.

The proposed action invoked Section 5 of the General Allotment Act (25 U.S.C. § 348), which renders void any contract touching allotted land before expiration of the trust period, and Section 2 of the 1920 Crow Act, which prohibited conveyances to persons owning more than 640 acres of agricultural or 1,280 acres of grazing land within the reservation. Attorney Harlow Pease argued the contract was "absolutely null and void" under the statute. However, the Interior Solicitor's office, citing *Andreas v. Henderson* (160 F. Supp. 252) and *United States v. Waller* (243 U.S. 452), concluded the case presented poor facts for a test case because the fee patent had issued before the deed, and no fraud by Antler in procuring the application could be demonstrated. A 1955 report documented the Tschirgis held "thousands of acres purchased in violation" of Section 2, and a 1960 compilation identified over 250,000 acres of potentially illegal conveyances on the Crow Reservation involving Antler Land Company, Campbell Farming Corporation, and the Scott interests. Despite four years of bureaucratic delay (1963–1967), the Department of the Interior ultimately declined to recommend litigation. On November 20, 1967, the DOJ closed both the Dillon file and the general file (90-2-11-6748) on Crow conveyance violations, deferring only to possible challenges in Yellowtail Dam condemnation proceedings. The file powerfully demonstrates how legal technicalities, institutional inertia, and inter-agency disagreement combined to ratify massive land dispossession on the Crow Reservation.

summaries.display_title: 1947: House Public Lands Committee Hearing on Fee Patents for the Doyle Family, Crow Allottees (S. 394–399)
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1947–1947

This document is a transcript of a hearing before the House Committee on Public Lands, held June 23, 1947, presided over by Chairman Richard J. Welchman, concerning five Senate bills (S. 394, S. 395, S. 396, S. 397, and S. 399) authorizing the issuance of patents in fee to members of the Doyle family, all Crow Indian allottees on the Crow Reservation in Big Horn County, Montana. The bills, which passed the Senate on June 16, 1947, covered Raymond Wesley Doyle, Richard Jay Doyle (deceased, with patent directed to his sole devisee Kathleen Doyle Harris), Thurlow Grey Doyle, Lawrence Stanley Doyle, and Gladys May Doyle. Their allotments aggregated 4,953.43 acres across multiple sections in townships 8 and 9 south, ranges 30, 31, 36, and 37 east, Montana principal meridian.

Representative D'Ewart read from Secretary of the Interior J. A. Krug's report, which explained that the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751) restricted Crow allottees from selling more than one-half of their homestead (320 acres), necessitating special congressional legislation to authorize disposal of entire allotments. The Secretary's report stated each allottee was "qualified to conduct his affairs without governmental supervision or protection." The committee voted unanimously to recommend all five bills favorably, with Representative Russell seconding the motion. This document demonstrates how fee patents—removing trust protections and enabling alienation of nearly 5,000 acres of Crow Reservation land—were expedited through Congress with minimal deliberation, effectively facilitating permanent dispossession under the guise of individual competency determinations.

summaries.display_title: 1947–1948: House Subcommittee Hearing on Fee Patent for Clarence M. Scott, Crow Allottee (S. 401)
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1947–1948

This document is a congressional subcommittee hearing transcript concerning S. 401, a bill authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Clarence M. Scott of Billings, Montana, for 440 acres of allotted homestead land on the Crow Indian Reservation. The hearing was chaired by Representative D'Ewart, with Assistant Commissioner William Zimmerman and Representative Rockwell participating. The bill was unanimously reported favorably to the full committee.

The lands at issue comprise the south half of section 16 and the north half of the northwest quarter and the northwest quarter of the northeast quarter of section 21, township 6 south, range 32 east, Montana principal meridian — totaling 440 acres out of an original 640-acre homestead allotment. Under existing law (41 Stat. 751, 756), the Secretary could only authorize sale of up to half the homestead lands at his discretion, necessitating special legislation to dispose of the remaining acreage. Acting Secretary Oscar L. Chapman's report of November 3, 1947, recommended enactment with an amendment requiring Scott's written application, noting that "simple justice would appear to indicate that a patent in fee should not be forced on an Indian who does not want one," since fee patenting would subject the land to taxation, removing trust protections without the owner's consent.

The document demonstrates how individual legislative acts facilitated Crow allotment alienation beyond statutory limits, converting trust land to fee status and exposing it to taxation and potential loss — a piecemeal but systematic mechanism of dispossession.

summaries.display_title: 1964: Billings Gazette Coverage of Crow Reservation Land Title Legislation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1964–1964

This document is a newspaper page from *The Billings Gazette*, dated Sunday, January 12, 1964. The OCR quality is extremely poor, rendering much of the text fragmentary and illegible. The page contains a column by Dick Gilluly titled "Federal Beat" with the subheading "Crow Land Title" and a secondary heading referencing "Law Compromise?" The article appears to discuss pending federal legislation concerning the Crow Reservation land title dispute, likely the 1964 Crow Act (Public Law 88-290). Fragmentary passages reference the Crow Tribe's approval requirements, Justice Department officials wanting to "give the land back," a Senate bill that "passed the Senate recently," and "compromise" language suggesting contested negotiations over land title resolution. A reference to a committee that cannot act "unless the Crow Tribe approves" indicates tribal consent provisions. Mentions of an "Agency to investigate the land situation" likely refer to Bureau of Indian Affairs involvement. The phrase "constitutional property rights" appears in a partially legible segment, and references to individuals purchasing land and the "difference between what he paid" suggest discussion of non-Indian purchasers' equitable claims on ceded or disputed Crow lands.

Despite severe OCR degradation, the document proves that Crow land title legislation was actively debated in Congress in early 1964, that compromise between tribal sovereignty claims and non-Indian property interests was central to the dispute, and that both the Justice Department and BIA were involved in adjudicating competing claims to Crow Reservation lands. Specific dollar amounts, acreages, and legal citations are not recoverable from the corrupted text.

summaries.display_title: 1895–1982: Eloise Whitebear Pease Collection on Crow Allotment and Land Protection Cases
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1895–1982

This collection comprises multiple document types—BIA compliance forms, allotment and estate records, trust patents, deeds, research contracts, research reports, affidavits, probate transcripts, and correspondence—assembled by Eloise Whitebear Pease as part of her work on Crow Reservation land protection cases. Two principal matters are documented.

The first concerns Harold Male Bear (Allottee #4062), sole heir to Allotment #3486 (Janette Ridesbear), covering approximately 593.31–753.31 acres in Sections 34–35, T.1S., R.38E., Big Horn County, Montana. Pease's August 31, 1979 letter to George S. Jennings, BIA Billings Area Office, documents that Peabody Coal Company drilled 17 core holes on Male Bear's trust land circa 1965 without compensation, while Shell Oil paid $50 per hole to Indian landowners and $100 per hole to non-Indian surface owners. No payment records existed at Crow Agency for Male Bear. The case was developed under Public Law 89-505 (28 U.S.C. § 2415), the federal statute of limitations for claims. Trust patents (#920259 and #960578) dated 1923 and 1925 reserved minerals for the Crow Tribe under the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751).

The second matter involves the Alice N. Whiteman/John Hayden estate—7,323.91 acres of trust land appraised at $338,421 in 1979. The State of Montana, through attorney Bert W. Kronmiller, demanded the BIA issue a fee patent, arguing Hayden was not Indian. Pease conducted archival research in Seattle (March 1981) and under a $5,000 Buy-Indian contract (Purchase Order 2C50-0100397, July 1982, 25 USC 47) to establish Hayden's Cherokee-Choctaw heritage. Multiple affidavits from Robert S. Yellowtail Sr., Ray Bear Don't Walk Sr., and others confirmed Hayden's adoption by Plays With Himself under Crow custom. These documents collectively reveal systematic dispossession through uncompensated resource extraction on trust allotments and state attempts to escheat substantial trust holdings through challenges to Indian identity.

summaries.display_title: 1958: Billings Gazette Clipping on Crow Reservation Land Issues
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1958–1958

This document is a newspaper clipping from *The Billings Gazette*, published Wednesday, August 27, 1958, page 10, sourced from Newspapers.com and downloaded on February 16, 2024. Unfortunately, the document is essentially a metadata stub: it contains only the newspaper's masthead information, publication date, page number, URL, and copyright notice, with no substantive article text reproduced in the OCR transcription. The actual content of the page — which, given the collection's focus on the Crow Reservation, federal Indian policy, and Bureau of Indian Affairs records, likely pertained to matters of Native American land dispossession or related policy developments in Montana — is entirely absent from the extracted text.

No specific claims, actions, decisions, names, dollar amounts, acreages, allotment numbers, or legal citations can be identified from the available transcription. The document's placement within an archival collection concerning Crow Reservation land dispossession suggests it may have reported on mid-twentieth-century federal policy actions, BIA administrative decisions, or legislative developments affecting Crow tribal lands during a period of active termination-era policy. However, without the article text itself, no analytical conclusions can be drawn. The OCR failure or incomplete digitization renders this item fragmentary and of no independent evidentiary value in its current form. The original newspaper page would need to be consulted directly to recover its substantive content.

summaries.display_title: 1962: Billings Gazette Clipping on Crow Reservation Land Issues
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1962

This document is a newspaper clipping from *The Billings Gazette*, published Friday, August 17, 1962, page 15, sourced from Newspapers.com and downloaded on February 16, 2024. Unfortunately, the document text provided contains only the bibliographic metadata—the masthead, date, page number, and copyright notice—with no substantive article content reproduced. The OCR capture or digital extraction appears to have failed to include the actual body text of whatever article or articles appeared on page 15 of that edition.

Given the collection context concerning Native American land dispossession, the Crow Reservation, federal Indian policy, and Bureau of Indian Affairs records, it is reasonable to infer that the clipping likely pertains to Crow tribal affairs, land transactions, or federal policy developments affecting the Crow Reservation in south-central Montana during the early 1960s—a period marked by termination-era policies, ongoing allotment sales, and resource extraction pressures on tribal lands. However, without the actual article text, no specific claims, names, acreages, dollar amounts, allotment numbers, legal citations, or policy mechanisms can be identified or analyzed. The document as provided proves nothing substantive about land dispossession beyond establishing that a potentially relevant newspaper source from this date and publication exists in the archival collection. The full page image would need to be consulted to extract meaningful content.

summaries.display_title: 1960: Billings Gazette Report on Northern Cheyenne Tribe Land Purchase Program
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1960–1960

This is a newspaper article from the *Billings Gazette*, dated Friday, February 5, 1960, reporting on the Northern Cheyenne Tribe's efforts to develop a land purchase program to reacquire allotted lands on their reservation. The article identifies total allotted land on the Northern Cheyenne Reservation at 200,000 acres, valued at $2–$3 million at current grazing land prices. The tribe had already borrowed $50,000 from the BIA's revolving credit fund the previous summer and was requesting an additional $500,000 loan. Milton A. Johnson, assistant area director of the BIA Billings area office, noted that grazing income was approximately 40 cents per acre annually—insufficient to service loan repayment and interest. Tribal attorney Bert W. Kronmiller of Hardin and BIA personnel advised the tribe to budget supplemental tribal income to cover shortfalls and create surplus for further purchases.

Senator Mike Mansfield intervened with Commissioner Glenn Emmons and Assistant Secretary of Interior Roger C. Ernst, noting the revolving fund was fully obligated and requesting a moratorium on Indian land sales in Montana until Congress could increase funding. A sale of 3,734 acres of Northern Cheyenne land was cancelled at the tribe's request pending adoption of a satisfactory program. BIA policy permitted the tribe to meet high bids on sealed-bid sales, negotiate directly with allottees, or bid at open auction. Key participants included tribal chairman John Woodenlegs, vice chairman William Hollowbreast, and land board members Grover Wolfvoice, Albert Tallbow, Melvin Wounded Eye, and Charles Whitedirt. The document demonstrates how inadequate federal loan funding and low land productivity structurally impeded tribal land consolidation, perpetuating dispossession through allotment sales.

summaries.display_title: 1959–1983: Eloise Whitebear Pease Collection on Crow Reservation Eastern Boundary Survey Error and Land Loss
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1959–1983

This collection comprises Bureau of Indian Affairs memoranda, legal opinions, tribal resolutions, appraisal reports, cadastral survey computations, and congressional legislative analysis documenting the Crow Tribe's loss of approximately 36,164.47 acres along the eastern boundary of the Crow Reservation due to an erroneous 1891 General Land Office survey. The Treaty of May 7, 1868 (15 Stat. 649, 650) fixed the east boundary at the 107th meridian of west longitude, but the approved 1892 survey placed the boundary at approximately 107° 01.2' west, creating a strip roughly 50 miles long and one mile wide that was excluded from the reservation. BLM calculations in T. 5 S., R. 35 E., secs. 33–34 confirmed the discrepancy. A June 16, 1959 BIA Billings Area Office memorandum initiated the investigation following Arthur Garrigus's visit; the Commissioner's November 5, 1959 reply stated the BIA was "without administrative authority to change the boundary or to reimburse the Crow Tribe." Field Solicitor A.E. Bielefeld's August 6, 1959 opinion cited controlling survey law and noted patents had been issued to non-Indians and allotments made to Northern Cheyenne within the contested strip. Crow Tribal Resolution 63-25B (February 1963) placed oil, gas, and mineral lease funds in escrow pending title determination. A 1981 cadastral resurvey broke the area into four parcels: 11,317.20 acres south of the Northern Cheyenne Reservation, 12,964.11 acres jointly shared, 2,468.63 acres north of the Northern Cheyenne, and 9,414.53 acres in the ceded area. The Minerals Management Service estimated total coal and oil/gas value at approximately $97,000,000 as of October 1982, with coal bonus value of $33,335,000 and coal royalty of $79,850,000. Eight congressional bills between 1952 and 1971 failed due to Department of Justice opposition citing the Indian Claims Commission Act's five-year statute of limitations (25 U.S.C.A. 70k) and arguments the Crow Tribe was overcompensated in Docket No. 54 (284 F.2d 361, 1960), which awarded $30,530,764.03. The documents collectively prove systematic dispossession through survey error, bureaucratic refusal to correct the boundary, and legal obstacles that prevented compensation for decades.

summaries.display_title: 1910–1962: Crow Tribal Land Purchase and Sales Program — Resolutions, Statutes, and BIA Policy
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1910–1962

This document is a comprehensive program brochure for the Crow Tribal Land Purchase and Sales Program, from the Eloise Whitebear Pease Collection. It compiles Crow Tribal Council resolutions, federal statutes, and Bureau of Indian Affairs policy memoranda governing the tribe's effort to reacquire and consolidate lands within the Crow Indian Reservation at Crow Agency, Montana.

The centerpiece is Resolution 62-12, which programmed the Crow Tribe's Indian Claims Commission judgment of $10,242,984.70, allocating $1,000,000 for land purchases (with a request for an additional $1,000,000 BIA loan), $1,000,000 for economic development, $275,000 for tribal credit expansion, $1,000,000 for competent lease relief, $200,000 for education, $120,000 for a tribal headquarters building, and $100,000 for law and order. Per capita payments of $100 and $50 were authorized for enrolled members. Resolution 62-05 (August 11, 1961, signed by Chairman John B. Cummins and Secretary Arlis Whiteman, recommended by Superintendent Otto K. Weaver) established the Declaration of Policies and Plan of Operations, creating a six-member Resources Committee elected by district (Pryor, Black Lodge, Lodge Grass, St. Xavier, Reno, Wyola) with authority over purchases and sales. Congressional authorities cited include the Act of June 25, 1910 (36 Stat. 855), Act of June 8, 1940 (54 Stat. 252), and Act of July 1, 1948 (62 Stat. 1214). Commissioner Philleo Nash's December 4, 1961 memorandum established ten criteria restricting alienation of Indian lands. The document reveals the tribe's systematic response to decades of land dispossession through fractionated heirship, competent leases to non-members, and fee patenting, attempting to reconsolidate a fragmented land base using judgment funds.

summaries.display_title: 1962: Billings Gazette Report on Illegal Leasing of Crow Reservation Lands
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1927–1962

This is a newspaper article from the *Billings Gazette* (circulation 30,141 daily, 41,460 Sunday), dated October 18, 1962, written by Dick Gilluly. It reports on a Missouri River Basin Investigation (MRBI) unit report from the Bureau of Indian Affairs office in Billings, which found that approximately 90 percent of Crow Reservation land leased to non-Indians—roughly 800,000 acres—was probably leased illegally. The MRBI report, furnished to the newspaper by Roger Stops, a Crow Indian of Crow Agency, had not been officially released and was described by MRBI director Walter F. Fuhriman as a "first draft" prepared for internal comment.

The article identifies the legal basis for illegality as a 1927 amendment to the Crow Allotment Act, which prohibits overlapping leasing—the practice of voiding a prior lease and purchasing a new yearly lease, effectively creating perpetual leases that prevent Indians from bargaining for better terms. Most Crow land was leased under the Crow Competency Act, giving individual Indians full authority over their allotted land with minimal BIA supervision. Average grazing rental on illegal leases was approximately 25 cents per acre in March 1962, compared with 47 cents on federally supervised leases and 65 cents for non-Indian-owned land. U.S. Attorney Moody Brickett of Butte sought to bring a test case to invalidate all illegal leases. The Crow Tribe allocated $1 million from its $8 million judgment fund for competent lease purchases. The document powerfully demonstrates how "competency" legislation, combined with economic coercion and BIA administrative neglect, facilitated systematic below-market dispossession of Crow land resources.

summaries.display_title: 1959–1987: Pease Collection — Crow Reservation Land Dispossession, Boundary Disputes, and Section 2 Enforcement
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1959–1987

This collection from the Eloise Whitebear Pease Collection comprises Bureau of Indian Affairs correspondence, Crow Tribal Council resolutions, Native American Rights Fund memoranda, Department of Interior and Justice reports, and proposed legislation spanning nearly three decades of Crow Reservation land dispossession issues. The documents center on two major legal controversies: the 107th Meridian boundary dispute and enforcement of Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act of 1920 (41 Stat. 751).

The 107th Meridian documents reveal that an 1891 government survey erroneously placed the Crow Reservation's eastern boundary approximately 0.9 miles west of the true 107th meridian, effectively stripping 29,760 acres from the reservation along a 53-mile strip. Of this, 15,360 acres were absorbed into the Northern Cheyenne Reservation (10,840 allotted, 4,520 tribal), and 14,400 acres were treated as public domain (14,080 patented to non-Indians, 320 vacant). The Department of Interior's 1966 report to Chairman Aspinall acknowledged the error but recommended compensation at 1891 values plus 4% interest rather than present-day value. The Bureau of the Budget and Department of Justice both recommended against legislative relief, citing the Indian Claims Commission Act's five-year filing bar (25 U.S.C. §70k). James Watt's 1987 letters to Chairman Richard Real Bird discuss ongoing Claims Court litigation and statute of limitations problems. Escrow funds of $33,866.56 from disputed-strip leasing remained frozen as of 1971.

On Section 2, a June 1977 NARF memorandum by Jeanne Whiteing and Daniel Israel details efforts since January 1974 to compel the United States to enforce acreage limitations (1,280 agricultural/1,920 grazing acres for non-Indians). Interior recommended litigation to Justice on February 20, 1976, but Justice delayed. S. 55 (86th Congress) proposed repealing Section 2 and validating all prior violations. A 1982 BIA letter from Superintendent Ed Hall to Chairman Donald Stewart threatened processing 105 fee patent applications covering 18,244 acres unless the Tribe secured purchase funds by April 10, 1982. The Crow Tribal Council responded by redirecting $14,007.13 in claim settlement funds and $393,107.90 in oil and gas bid revenues toward land purchases. These documents collectively demonstrate systematic federal complicity in Crow land loss through erroneous surveys, non-enforcement of protective statutes, administrative pressure for fee patenting, and bureaucratic obstruction of remedial legislation.

summaries.display_title: 1967: Billings Gazette Clipping on Crow Reservation Land Issues
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1967

This document is a newspaper clipping from *The Billings Gazette*, published Tuesday, July 25, 1967, page 5, sourced from Newspapers.com and downloaded on February 16, 2024.
