summaries.display_title: 1978–1981: BIA Statute of Limitations Claims Development on Crow Reservation — Pease Research Contracts
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1978–1981

This collection documents the Bureau of Indian Affairs' implementation of 28 U.S.C. §2415 Statute of Limitations case development on the Crow Reservation and other Montana reservations, primarily through "Buy Indian" contracts (25 U.S.C. §47) awarded to Eloise W. Pease, an enrolled Crow tribal member. The core document is Contract No. C50C14205100, dated February 28, 1979, a $15,000 fixed-price agreement between the BIA Billings Area Office and Pease to research tort and contract claims accruing before July 18, 1966, that the United States could file on behalf of the Crow Tribe before the April 1, 1980 (later extended to April 1, 1981) statutory deadline. The contract was modified twice: Modification No. 1 (August 30, 1979) added $2,715 and extended performance through August 20, 1979; Modification No. 2 (October 18, 1979) added $2,975 for estate-repayment claims research, bringing the total to $20,690.

Pease's July 30, 1979 progress report identifies 19 potential cases including: CB&Q Railroad right-of-way violations (free transportation promise broken, unauthorized highway grant to Big Horn County); mineral rights in Ceded Area school lands (citing P.L. 85-628, 72 Stat. 575); will contest cases potentially restoring 840 acres to heirs of Pup Plays, Bull Weasel, Clara Whitehip, and John Fights Well Known; and estate claims where Montana collected Old Age Assistance and Disability repayments from deceased Crow members' IIM accounts—approximately 107 cases identified in Big Horn County lien records. An August 25, 1980 purchase order (C50-0100609, $7,500) funded easement/right-of-way research on utility and transmission lines crossing trust land without valid easements. Parallel contracts for the Chippewa Cree Tribe ($18,000, Contract C50C14205088) and Fort Belknap Indian Community ($18,000) followed identical structures. The documents collectively reveal systematic failures to secure or compensate for rights-of-way, unauthorized state collection from Indian estates, and the rushed, underfunded federal effort to identify decades of dispossession before statutory deadlines expired.

summaries.display_title: 1903–1979: Pease Collection — BIA Memoranda and Records on Crow Reservation Land Dispossession
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1903–1979

This collection comprises Bureau of Indian Affairs memoranda, legal research notes, intake forms, land sale records, and statutory materials documenting multiple dimensions of Crow Reservation land dispossession and rights protection efforts. The documents originate from the BIA Billings Area Office, Crow Agency, and researcher Eloise Whitebear Pease, spanning from an 1903 Office of Indian Affairs letter authorizing the American Baptist Home Mission Society to select 160 acres for missionary purposes at Lodge Grass, through 1979 contracts with Montana Legal Services Association to discover claims under 28 U.S.C. 2415 (Statute of Limitations).

A June 21, 1976 memorandum (Rights Issue 202005) identifies critical deficiencies in land conveyance acts (1906, 1907, 1910, 1932, 1940, 1948), noting that individuals cannot take land in trust unless they already own trust land, and that the tribe has no authority to buy fee land—structural barriers to land consolidation. A detailed 1930 sale file documents the sale of three Crow allotments (Nos. 1091, 1092, 1080—Mountain Sheep, Looks at the Fox Sheep, Big Ox) totaling approximately 320 acres to Big Horn County for an airport at $8/acre ($1,600, $640, $320 respectively), with competitive bidding waived. The Superintendent acknowledged the land was worth more but recommended sale. A rights protection inventory lists over 35 individual claims including trespass on ceded land, invalid rights-of-way, delinquent O&M charges, inadequate drill site restoration, the Crow Tribe v. Mohasco Industries breach of contract, and destruction of a cemetery on Allotment #1495 (Whitearm). The 1926 Crow Act amendments and Section 2 acreage limitation enforcement (Resolution of January 19, 1974, Issue 202002) are also documented. OCR quality is poor on handwritten pages, limiting full extraction of claim details.

summaries.display_title: 1976–1979: Crow Reservation Statute of Limitations Claims Identification — Pease Research Files
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1976–1979

This collection documents the Crow Reservation Statute of Limitations claims identification process under 28 U.S.C. §2415 (Public Law 89-505), compiled primarily by Eloise Whitebear Pease as tribal researcher under Contract No. C5051420 5100. The core document is Pease's March 31, 1979 letter to George S. Jennings, Chief of the Division of Resource Development at the BIA Billings Area Office, transmitting 14 initial information sheets identifying potential claims. Accompanying BIA memoranda from February 1, 1979 and April 24, 1979 establish deadlines of July 1 and August 1, 1979 for referring valid claims to the U.S. Attorney and Justice Department.

The claims encompass multiple categories of dispossession. The abandoned Big Horn Southern Railroad (later C.B.&Q., then Burlington Northern) right-of-way, which became Montana State Highway #48, crossed at least 89 trust allotments across multiple townships without proper consent; Pease's research indicated only 16% of Crow Reservation roads had proper easements. Specific forced patent cases include SL C52 202 015 (Fredrick Geisdorff, Allotment 2336, S½NE¼ and SE¼NW¼, Sec. 19, T.9S., R.34E., a minor who died before patent issuance) and SL C52 202 008 (Josephine Terrell Collins, Allotment 3137, Sec. 14, T.3S., R.33E.). The Ash Creek oil field case (SL C52 202 005) alleges McDermott illegally assigned a lease to Tenneco/Shell Oil, with 22 producing wells at 260 barrels initial production, revenues not corresponding to reported output. George Pease Sr. (enrolled #1323) received forced patents on 320 acres. The 107th Meridian boundary dispute involved approximately 53 miles of erroneously surveyed land, with estimated damages of $3 million. Additional claims address pipeline trespasses in the Ceded area, cemetery destruction at Allotment #1495, and violations of the Crow Act of June 4, 1920's acreage limitations. The documents collectively demonstrate systematic failures of federal trust responsibility across railroad rights-of-way, mineral leasing, forced fee patenting, and boundary surveying.

Montana and the Crow Reservation were directly affected by such policies. summaries.display_title: 1934–1945: Pease Collection — Crow Reservation Land Rights, Wheeler Correspondence, and Senate Bill 475
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1934–1945

This collection comprises several related documents concerning Crow Reservation land rights: a printed broadside/flyer urging Crow Indian landowners to attend a council meeting, correspondence between George W. Hogan at Crow Agency and Senator Burton K. Wheeler's office, and a formal report from the Secretary of the Interior to Senator Joseph C. O'Mahoney's Committee on Indian Affairs regarding Senate Bill 475.

The Interior Department report, the most substantive document, concerns Bullshows (Crow allottee No. 1852) and Horse Bullshows (Crow allottee No. 1853), husband and wife aged 72 and 73, who sought patents in fee to their allotted lands. Bullshows held 360 acres (NE¼, NW¼, NW¼SW¼, sec. 13, T. 6 S., R. 27 E., Montana principal meridian), and Horse Bullshows held 80 acres (S½ SE¼, sec. 12, same township and range), near the Pryor Mountains. The lands were appraised at $5 per acre with annual rental income equivalent to 5 percent of value. The Secretary recommended against enactment, citing section 13 of the Crow Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), which required allottees to retain half their 610-acre homestead and prohibited disposal of more than 320 acres. The Secretary argued fee patents would impair the elderly couple's security, as they depended on lease income.

The broadside, likely from the 1940s, reveals grassroots Crow opposition to dam construction in Big Horn Canyon and outright land sales, urging landowners to vote "NO" on sales and rights of entry—documenting organized resistance to dispossession mechanisms. OCR quality is poor throughout, particularly on pages 1, 3, and 6.

summaries.display_title: 1917–1919: Correspondence and Legislation on Proposed Allotment and Opening of the Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1917–1919

This collection comprises correspondence, legislative texts, council minutes, and speeches relating to proposed legislation to allot and open the Crow Reservation in Montana during 1917–1919. The central document is S. 195 (66th Congress, 1st Session), introduced by Senator Walsh of Montana on May 20, 1919, authorizing the Crow Tribe to submit claims to the Court of Claims. Related bills include S. 3256 (introduced December 17, 1917) and the Nugent bill, both proposing pro rata allotment of all reservation lands outside the "Flat Iron" district between the Big Horn and Little Horn Rivers, with surplus lands opened to settlement.

Superintendent C. H. Asbury's detailed reports to the Commissioner of Indian Affairs document approximately 1,500,000 acres on the reservation, with roughly 380,000 already allotted, 64,000 acres designated as state school sections, and approximately 600 acres per capita remaining for distribution. About 130 fee patents had been issued covering 26,000 acres, with 86 more pending, totaling roughly 55,000 acres. Annual lease income was approximately $220,000–$250,000, with projected future income of $600,000. Asbury noted the 640-acre purchase limit in Section 2 would depress grazing land sales below $10 per acre, and recommended school land compensation exceed $2.50, suggesting $5 per acre against Montana's $10 minimum.

The August 11, 1919 general council at Crow Agency, attended by 153 tribal members with Robert Yellow Tail as chairman, adopted pro rata allotment including the Flat Iron, reserved mineral rights on Wolf, Big Horn, and Pryor Mountains, and elected district delegates. Chief Plenty Coos's speeches reveal deep divisions: older Indians opposed opening the reservation, while fee-patent Indians favored immediate division. Assistant Commissioner E. B. Meritt's August 7, 1919 remarks pressured the tribe toward legislation, warning Congress would eventually open the reservation regardless. Senator John B. Kendrick's letter to Asbury endorsed allotment but opposed allowing Indians to sell homesteads. The documents collectively reveal the administrative machinery—allotment schedules, fee patents, competency determinations, lease policies, and legislative pressure—driving Crow land dispossession despite significant tribal opposition. OCR quality is poor on several handwritten pages (10–13, 43–51), rendering them largely illegible.

summaries.display_title: 1933–1975: Pease Collection — BIA Reports, Land Sales, and Crow Competent Leasing Policy
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1933–1975

This collection comprises multiple document types from the Eloise Whitebear Pease Collection: Bureau of Indian Affairs memoranda, a BIA planning study, Congressional Record excerpts, land sale bid abstracts, a federal administrative order, and a BIA informational report on land restoration policy.

The central analytical document is the February 23, 1973 Planning Support Group report, "Effects of Proposed Changes in Crow Competent Leasing Practices," prepared for the Crow Indian Tribe. It demonstrates how the Crow Act of June 4, 1920, and subsequent acts of 1926, 1948, and 1949 enabled a leasing mechanism whereby non-Indian lessees annually cancelled and renewed five-year competent leases, keeping land tied up at discounted rates. In 1970, 1,762 of 1,877 competent leases (94%) were cancelled and renewed. Office leases averaged $1.65/acre on 415,467 acres ($849,538 total), while competent leases averaged only $0.76/acre on 918,412 acres ($703,215 total)—an annual loss of $817,000 ($0.89/acre). The study proposed a $2,000,000 loan fund at 7% interest to sustain landowners through lease expiration, projecting returns of $1.14/acre during payback. MRBI Report No. 170 (September 1963) provided supporting analysis.

The January 20, 1959 bid abstracts document allotted Indian land sales to non-Indian purchasers including Clark McGarry, Floyd Slattery, Claude A. Lewis Jr., and others, with numerous tracts receiving "No Bid." Commissioner John Collier's August 12, 1933 Order No. 420 halted further sales of Indian allotments due to economic conditions. The October 11, 1963 Congressional Record excerpt records Senate debate on S. 1049 (fractionated heirship legislation), where Senator Burdick's amendment requiring tribal funds before land sales was rejected. Together, these documents demonstrate systematic economic dispossession through below-market leasing practices and inadequate protections against land alienation on the Crow Reservation.

summaries.display_title: 1950–1954: BIA Records on Sale of Crow Indian Allotted Lands — Billings Area Office
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1950–1954

This collection comprises Bureau of Indian Affairs internal memoranda, correspondence, and bid abstracts from the Billings Area Office and Crow Indian Agency (RG 75, Box 2) concerning the sale of Crow Indian allotted lands during the early 1950s. The documents reveal a systematic process of converting trust allotments to fee patents through supervised public sales, authorized by the Commissioner of Indian Affairs.

A central episode involves R. B. Fraser, Jr., whose high bids on ten tracts totaling 2,340.94 acres at $33,651.62 in the October 26, 1954 Crow land sale were contested. Field Solicitor Harlow Pease's November 4, 1954 memorandum to Area Director John M. Cooper documented twelve years of Fraser livestock trespasses on Crow grazing units (Units 18, 19, 20, 20A, 22, 23), with $2,506.00 in accumulated unpaid penalties, citing 25 USC 179. Pease recommended rejecting Fraser's bids, noting the elder Fraser used his son's name as a "device" to circumvent his persona non grata status. However, M. A. Johnson's November 5 memo to Cooper found no regulatory authority to reject bids based on pending trespass litigation.

A December 8, 1952 abstract lists twenty accepted bids on allotments of deceased Crow allottees, with bidders including Antler Land Co., Vale Creek Ranch, and individual non-Indian ranchers. Superintendent Lippert's July 10, 1950 letter reveals approximately 110 ceded-portion allotments previously advertised received no bids, as lessees could lease land cheaper than paying taxes. The documents collectively demonstrate how federal sale mechanisms, combined with non-Indian rancher dominance of bidding, facilitated large-scale alienation of Crow trust lands.

summaries.display_title: 1958: Interior Department Solicitor Opinion on Crow Tribal Resolution and Section 2 Enforcement
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1958–1958

This document is a legal memorandum from the Field Solicitor of the U.S. Department of the Interior, Office of the Solicitor, Billings, Montana, dated April 9, 1958, addressed to the Area Director, Bureau of Indian Affairs, Billings, Montana. It analyzes Crow Tribal Resolution No. 155, which appropriated $1,000 for "payment of expenses and court costs" related to enforcement of "Section 2" — likely Section 2 of the Crow allotment or reservation statute — on behalf of individual Crow allottees, heirs, or devisees of individually owned Crow land.

The memorandum clarifies that the resolution does not constitute an employment contract with an attorney nor bind the Tribe to pay attorney fees, but rather makes a limited appropriation for litigation expenses. The Field Solicitor distinguishes this special appropriation from the general Kronmiller attorney contract with the Tribe, advising that any agreement must be executed as a separate special contract with formalities required under 25 U.S.C. § 81, the federal statute governing contracts with Indian tribes. The Solicitor cites Montana Revised Codes of 1947, §§ 93-8601 and 93-8613, and *In Re Baxter's Estate*, 22 P.2d 182, 94 Mont. 257, regarding attorney fee obligations.

The document reveals that individual Crow landowners needed to bring plenary title actions to protect their allotted lands, and that the Tribe recognized an indirect collective interest in defending individual allotments from dispossession — significant enough to fund litigation costs. The OCR quality is poor, with substantial garbling on pages 1 and 3, though the legal substance remains largely recoverable.

summaries.display_title: 1959: Hardin Tribune-Herald Coverage of Crow Indian Land Sale and Crow Act Dispute
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1917–1959

This is a page from the *Hardin Tribune-Herald*, a Big Horn County, Montana newspaper dated Thursday, January 29, 1959. It contains two parallel accounts of a Crow Indian land sale conducted on January 20, 1959: one from the office of Crow Reservation Superintendent Clyde Hobbs, and one from the Crow Leaseholders Association.

According to Hobbs, 77 tracts totaling 26,301.66 acres of Crow Indian land were offered for sale. Ninety-one bids were received; 22 tracts received no bids. Acceptable bids covered 12,009.2 acres, yielding $195,895.51 at an average of $16.31 per acre. The highest per-acre bid was $51.15 on a 395.05-acre combination farm-grazing tract; one straight farm tract sold at $126 per acre; the highest grazing bid was $42.50 per acre. Hobbs declared the sale "successful."

The Leaseholders Association's independent survey challenged this characterization. They noted that of 57 grazing parcels, 22 received no bids and only 14 exceeded $10 per acre; the average grazing price was $8.54 per acre. They explicitly blamed Section 2 of the Act of June 4, 1920 (the Crow Act) for depressing competition, stating that many association members who would have submitted higher bids were "precluded from doing so" by its provisions. The association reported that Crow Indians themselves expressed disappointment at the lack of competitive bidding. This document demonstrates how federal statutory mechanisms governing Crow land sales suppressed market competition, resulting in below-market dispositions of Indian land—a structural mechanism of dispossession operating through ostensibly lawful administrative sales.

summaries.display_title: 1962: Billings Gazette Coverage of Crow Act — Pease Collection Clippings

The document appears to relate to the 1962 Crow Act based on its archival file name ("1962 Crow Act Pease Billings Gazette-1.pdf"), which likely refers to Public Law 87-649 or related legislation concerning the Crow Reservation in Montana. The 1962 Crow Act was a significant piece of federal legislation that addressed per capita distribution of Crow tribal funds, land sales, and other matters affecting the Crow (Apsáalooke) people's land base and resources. However, without readable article text, no specific claims, dollar amounts, acreages, allotment numbers, legal citations, or named individuals can be extracted from this document. Researchers would need to consult the original newspaper images to recover the article content.

summaries.file_name: Crow RG 75 Important Crow Act, land sales, competency.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1921–1956: BIA Records on Violations of Section 2 of the Crow Act — Land Sales and Competency Files
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1947–1967 (archival box dates), with documents spanning 1921–1956

This collection from RG 75, Billings Area Office (8NS-075-97-316, Box 5), comprises Bureau of Indian Affairs internal memoranda, tribal resolutions, solicitor opinions, and correspondence documenting systematic violations of Section 2 of the Crow Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), which limited non-Indian land acquisition on the Crow Reservation to 1,280 acres agricultural or 1,920 acres grazing land, with violations punishable by $5,000 fine or six months imprisonment.

A confidential October 27, 1955 tabulation by Area Director J. M. Cooper to Associate Commissioner H. Rex Lee identifies over two dozen non-Indian landholders exceeding statutory limits, including: Antler Land Company/M.H. & F.M. Tschirigi (34,195.75 acres), Campbell Farming Corporation (14,762.53 acres), Henman-Kelly (33,303.28 acres), Warren & Sons (22,505.60 acres), Homer A. Scott's combined properties (14,051.69 acres), and P.K. Ranch (8,862.28 acres). Cooper's November 10, 1955 letter reveals the Antler Land Company held 100 tracts, with restrictions released on 68 via fee patents, and that 60% of land sales over the preceding four years went to large operators already in violation. The Area Realty Officer's October 4, 1955 memo confirms the acreage limitation provision had been "disregarded" since the Area Office's establishment.

The documents reveal BIA officials treated the discovery as confidential, fearing market disruption, while seeking remedial legislation. Senator Mansfield introduced S.3698 in April 1956 to repeal the acreage limitations. The Crow Tribal Council unanimously passed a resolution on April 14, 1956, authorizing its attorney to promote passage. Acting Assistant Solicitor William V. Koetler confirmed Section 2 had never been repealed. Extensive solicitor opinions by Harlow Pease address how "competent leases" by entities like the Antler Land Company were used to block supervised sales, creating perpetual leaseholds that further dispossessed Indian landowners.

summaries.file_name: Pease Collection Box 24a 2415 forced fee, 1948 act.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1979: Interior Solicitor Memorandum on Forced Fee Patents and the 1948 Act — Crow Reservation Claims
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1887–1979

This document is a multi-part collection centered on a February 16, 1979 memorandum from Hans Walker, Jr., Acting Associate Solicitor for Indian Affairs, distributed via a March 5, 1979 cover memo from E. L. Meredith, Field Solicitor in Billings, to the Associate Solicitor and Area Director for Indian Affairs. The core legal analysis concerns whether the Act of May 14, 1948 (25 U.S.C. § 483) authorized the Bureau of Indian Affairs to sell inherited trust allotments on Indian Reorganization Act reservations without the consent of all beneficial heirs. The memorandum concludes that between 1948 and 1958, Bureau personnel erroneously interpreted the 1948 Act as reinstating the 1910 Act (25 U.S.C. § 372), permitting heirship sales where one heir was deemed incompetent without obtaining all heirs' consent. The Minneapolis Area Office alone identified at least 490 such unauthorized sales, with estimates of upwards of 2,000 on Minnesota Chippewa reservations. The attached March 7, 1978 Twin Cities Field Solicitor memorandum details specific examples: Fond du Lac Allotment #14B (46 heirs, only 17 consented), Fond du Lac #1d (41 heirs, 31 consented, 2 objected in writing), and Leech Lake #15 (5 heirs, none consented, entire proceeds applied to an Old Age Assistance claim). The collection also includes Crow Reservation allotment records for Allotment No. 121 (Showscoos, 560.27 acres under Act of 2-8-87), Allotment No. 3137 (Josephine Collins/Terrel, 200 acres, Fee Patent No. 913127 issued July 26, 1923, lost to Big Horn County tax deed in 1937), and Allotment No. 1609 (Looks at the Lodge, 160 acres, NW¼ Sec. 10, T.7S., R.36E., transferred to Cedric Not Afraid by order approved 12-9-52). A 1949 easement deed conveys right-of-way across Grey Dog's allotment (S/2 SE/4 Sec. 13, T.6S., R.35E.) to Big Horn County Electric Cooperative for $100. The urgency of the review was driven by the April 1, 1980 statute of limitations (28 U.S.C. § 2415). This collection powerfully documents systematic, nationwide dispossession of Indian allotted lands through unauthorized Bureau sales lacking legally required consent, with specific Crow Reservation examples illustrating forced fee patents, tax forfeiture, and non-consensual transfers of inherited interests.

summaries.display_title: 1964: Billings Gazette Column on Crow Reservation Land Title Legislation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1964–1964

This document is a newspaper page from *The Billings Gazette*, dated Sunday, January 12, 1964. The OCR quality is extremely poor, rendering much of the text fragmentary and illegible. The page contains a column by Dick Gilluly titled "Crow Land Title — Law Compromise?" under the "Federal Beat" heading, alongside unrelated advertisements and news items.

From the recoverable fragments, the article discusses pending federal legislation concerning Crow Reservation land titles. Key references include the Crow Tribe's approval requirement before a committee can act, Justice Department officials who "want to give the land back," and mention of legal action. The article references a figure named "Pease" who "cites several instances" and notes that a compromise "has never been made." A related bill apparently "passed the Senate recently," and a figure named "Haley" is expected to give scrutiny if a resolution passes. The article references an Agency investigation into the land situation and discusses "constitutional property rights" and the difference between what an owner paid and some other valuation standard.

Despite severe OCR degradation, the document proves that as of early 1964, Crow land title disputes remained unresolved at the federal level, involving competing claims between the tribe and non-Indian landholders, with the Justice Department, congressional committees, and the Bureau of Indian Affairs all engaged. The reference to compromise legislation and tribal consent requirements reflects the ongoing tension between federal dispossession mechanisms and tribal sovereignty over reservation lands. Specific acreages, dollar amounts, and legal citations are not recoverable from the corrupted text.

summaries.display_title: 1987: Crow Tribal Members' Memo Opposing Proposed Crow Tribal Tax Code
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1987–1987

This document is a political memo dated March 16, 1987, addressed "TO ALL ENROLLED CROW INDIANS" and authored by "CROW TRIBAL MEMBERS WORKING TO SAVE THE CROW RESERVATION FOR ITS PEOPLE," preserved in the Wtteeear (likely Whitebear) Pease Collection. Its purpose is to mobilize Crow tribal members to attend an upcoming April Council meeting and vote against a proposed Crow Tribal Tax Code. The OCR quality is poor, with significant garbling on pages 1 and 2, though pages 3 and 4 are largely legible.

The memo argues that adopting a tribal tax code—specifically a $1.00 per acre levy on all leased land held by Crow Indians and non-Indians alike—would constitute the "greatest major step toward total termination of the trust responsibility of the federal government" over approximately 2,000,000 acres of trust land on the Crow Reservation. It invokes House Concurrent Resolution 108 (1954, 83rd Congress) as still operative law, noting that the Crow Tribe was previously excluded from termination largely through the efforts of Senator Lee Metcalf of Montana. The memo draws a causal chain linking earlier assertions of tribal regulatory authority—selling fishing licenses, establishing the Crow Tribal Hunting and Fishing Management Commission—to the loss of treaty-protected resources, including the bed of the Big Horn River and mountain lands. It warns that penalties under Sections 192 and 193 of the proposed code include $5,000 fines and one year in the Crow jail, and that the code provides no services in return for taxation. The document reveals internal Crow political divisions over sovereignty strategies, illustrating how tribal self-governance mechanisms could paradoxically be perceived as facilitating federal termination and state-driven land dispossession through tax liability on trust allotments.

summaries.display_title: 1956: Billings Gazette Clipping on Crow Reservation Section 2 Land Issues
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1956

This document is a newspaper clipping from *The Billings Gazette*, published Thursday, January 19, 1956, page 5, sourced from Newspapers.com and downloaded on February 16, 2024. The archival record is essentially a metadata stub — it contains only the newspaper masthead information, publication date, page number, and copyright notice, with no substantive article text reproduced in the OCR transcription. Given the date and the Billings, Montana, location, the article likely pertained to mid-1950s federal Indian policy developments affecting the Crow Reservation or other Montana tribal lands. This period coincided with the federal termination era, Public Law 280, and ongoing pressures on tribal land bases through surplus land sales, leasing arrangements, and allotment inheritance fractionation. Billings served as a regional hub for BIA administrative activity and was the nearest major newspaper covering Crow Agency affairs. However, without the actual article text, no specific claims, acreages, dollar amounts, legal citations, names, or decisions can be identified. The document in its current form proves nothing substantive about land dispossession beyond confirming that the *Gazette* published relevant coverage on this date. The OCR failure or incomplete digitization significantly limits the record's analytical value.

summaries.display_title: 1995: Billings Gazette Report on Supreme Court Refusal to Hear Crow Tribe Section 2 Land Appeal
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1995

This document is a newspaper article from *The Billings Gazette*, dated March 30, 1995, written by Lorna Thackeray, reporting on the U.S. Supreme Court's refusal to hear the Crow Tribe's appeal in a land dispute over non-Indian ownership limits on the Crow Indian Reservation. The case centered on Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act of 1920, which the tribe argued limited private non-Indian holdings on the approximately 2-million-acre reservation to 1,280 acres of farmland or 1,920 acres of grazing land. These limitations were never enforced by the federal government.

In 1991, the Crow Tribe filed its own lawsuit naming two of the largest non-Indian landowners as defendants, including Robert Earl Holdings, alleging they held property in excess of the Section 2 acreage limits. U.S. District Judge Jack Shanstrom in Billings dismissed the case on jurisdictional grounds, ruling that Section 2 referenced only individual tribal members, not the tribe itself, and thus established no particular tribal rights. The Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals upheld this dismissal in July 1994. Attorney Michael E. Webster represented the landowner defendant.

The article notes that hearings were held at Crow Agency in 1957 concerning proposed legislation to eliminate the acreage limitations, but no legislation passed, leaving the issue unresolved for another 38 years. This document demonstrates how the non-enforcement of statutory protections against land monopolization, combined with narrow judicial interpretations of tribal standing, effectively facilitated ongoing non-Indian land accumulation on the Crow Reservation despite explicit congressional limits.

summaries.file_name: Metcalf Mansfield Bill re Crow Act.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1961–1962: Metcalf-Mansfield Bill to Ratify Illegal Non-Indian Land Acquisitions on Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1961–1962

This is a newspaper article from *The Gazette* (likely the Billings Gazette) reporting on a bill introduced by Montana Senators Lee Metcalf and Mike Mansfield to ratify illegal land transactions on the Crow Indian Reservation. The OCR quality is poor, with significant fragmentation and garbled text, but key details are recoverable.

The Metcalf-Mansfield bill would legalize deeds on as many as 400,000 acres held illegally by non-Indians, most of which were acquired between 1946 and 1955. The bill would validate only illegal purchases made prior to 1957 and would preserve mineral rights for the original Indian owners or their heirs. The legislation was supported by Assistant Interior Secretary John A. Carver, whose letter—read into the Congressional Record by Metcalf—stated that "legislative action is the most practical and desirable method of clearing the land titles." Carver admitted he had "no explanation for BIA allowing illegal sales of Crow land." The Crow Act of 1920 imposed statutory acreage limitations on non-Indian purchases of Crow land; the U.S. Solicitor General issued an unofficial opinion in October 1961 finding approximately 2,000 leases on Crow land held by non-Indians were illegal. In December 1961, Crow Reservation Superintendent Otto Weaver confirmed the leases would need to be addressed. Crow tribal official Posey Whiteman characterized the legislation as resulting from "economic influences" and pressure from large landholders seeking to legalize their holdings. BIA Area Director James F. Canan in Billings acknowledged overlapping leasing created perpetual leases but claimed he was "not convinced any serious inequities have arisen out of the illegal ownership." The document demonstrates systematic federal complicity in dispossessing Crow landowners through BIA administrative failures and subsequent legislative ratification of illegal transactions.

summaries.display_title: 1967: Letter to Editor on Crow Tribe's Position on NPS Recreational Area Proposal
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1904–1967

This document is a newspaper page from *The Hardin Tribune-Herald*, Thursday, December 28, 1967, containing multiple letters to the editor and local columns. The OCR quality is very poor, rendering much of the text fragmentary and garbled, but key content is discernible. The most substantive piece is a letter from DeWitt Dillon (Pryor, Montana) addressing the Crow Tribe's position on a proposed National Park Service (NPS) recreational area on Crow Tribal lands. Dillon recounts that the Crow Tribal Council, at a May 1967 meeting, rejected the proposed agreement on several grounds: the proposal was "too vague and ambiguous"; the Tribe would have been "encroaching on the bargaining" position regarding its own lands; and the NPS would have gained administrative control of access to adjacent Tribal areas. Dillon credits former Tribal Chairman John Wilson, Sr., and Council staff for ensuring districts received copies of the Ernst and Ernst Report before deliberation. He references the Bureau of Indian Affairs' history of "maladministration" regarding the Crow Tribe and notes rumors that the Interior Department had already committed Crow Tribal lands to the recreational program without proper Council consent. Dillon invokes the Tribe's treaty obligations, noting promises "sealed under a sanction of mutual trust in God" dating to original treaty-making, and references 1904 as a prior instance when similar land issues arose—likely the 1904 Crow cession act. The letter also criticizes the proposed purchase of the Antler Ranch as "preposterously asinine" and detrimental to individual Crow Indians. This document evidences ongoing federal pressure to appropriate Crow lands for recreational development, BIA complicity, and Crow resistance through formal Council resolutions.

summaries.display_title: 1933–1950: BIA Correspondence on Crow Reservation Land Alienation Restrictions
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1933–1950

This archival collection from the Crow Indian Agency (RG 75, Bureau of Indian Affairs) contains several distinct documents addressing land alienation restrictions on the Crow Reservation in Montana. The core documents are two letters dated July 29, 1937, from Acting Secretary of the Interior Charles West to Senator Elmer Thomas, Chairman of the Senate Committee on Indian Affairs, reporting on S.2511 and S.2512. S.2511 would have prohibited conveyance of restricted Crow land to anyone other than Crow tribal members except in emergencies. S.2512 sought to amend Section 2 of the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751-752), governing Crow allotment and tribal fund distribution. West recommended against enacting S.2512, noting the Crow had voted to exclude themselves from the Indian Reorganization Act of June 18, 1934 (48 Stat. 984), and that Department Order No. 420 (approved August 14, 1933, signed by Commissioner John Collier and Secretary Harold L. Ickes) already precluded sales of restricted allotments except in emergencies.

A lengthy August 10, 1934 memorandum to the Secretary of the Interior catalogs dozens of "opened" reservations—including the Crow under the Act of April 27, 1904 (33 Stat. 352)—recommending temporary withdrawal of surplus lands from disposition under IRA Section 3 to prevent non-Indian entry before tribal organizations could decide on permanent restoration.

A 1949 letter from Robert Yellowtail to Commissioner John R. Nichols passionately opposes Senator Malone's S.2723 to abolish the BIA, arguing federal protection remains essential against dispossession through taxation, political chicanery, and economic exploitation. A 1950 letter from the Crow Agency superintendent clarifies that proposed homestead restriction legislation would not authorize general fee patent issuance, noting the Bureau had been "very slow to issue fee patents." Together these documents demonstrate the persistent tension between protecting Crow land from alienation and pressures for fee patenting and state jurisdiction.

summaries.display_title: 1990: Letters to the Editor on Redlining and Discriminatory Lending Against Crow Tribal Members
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1990–1990

This document is a newspaper opinion page from *The Billings Gazette*, published June 24, 1990, containing two letters to the editor addressing discriminatory lending practices ("redlining") against Native Americans on Montana reservations. The primary letter, authored by Warren D. Smith of Pryor, Montana, a Crow rancher, details systematic exclusion of Crow tribal members from bank lending. Smith argues that banks refuse loans to Crow borrowers for land, cattle, or equipment because the bankers themselves own substantial Crow land and view Indian ranchers as economic competitors. He cites pretextual excuses offered by banks, including claims that the "BIA Indian Financing Act is too complicated" and that reservation residents fall outside "primary service areas."

Critically, Smith invokes Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act, which he states limits non-Crow land ownership on the reservation to 1,920 acres, and alleges that bankers, ranchers, and attorneys hold acreage "far in excess" of this legal cap. He charges that the BIA—the federal trustee obligated to protect Indian interests—along with local governments and banks, permits these illegal holdings to persist. Smith also identifies "Citizens Rights Organization" (C.R.O.) as a hate group established by wealthy non-Indian interests to foment internal tribal conflict and maintain Indian economic powerlessness. A rebuttal letter from William R. Chamberlin of Consumer Credit Counselors of Eastern Montana defends First Interstate Bank of Colstrip against similar Northern Cheyenne redlining allegations. Together, the letters document the intersection of discriminatory credit practices, illegal non-Indian land accumulation beyond statutory limits, and BIA trustee failure as mechanisms of ongoing Crow land dispossession.

summaries.display_title: 1962: Billings Gazette on Non-Indian Acquisition of Crow Reservation Land in Violation of the Crow Act
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1962

This is a newspaper article from *The Billings Gazette*, published Saturday, August 18, 1962, by reporter Dick Gilluly, covering a dispute over non-Indian acquisition and leasing of Crow Reservation land in violation of federal law. The article reports that Billings attorney Harlow Pease, formerly BIA area counsel and Interior Department solicitor's employee, claimed he repeatedly informed Bureau of Indian Affairs officials that they were ignoring provisions of the Crow Act of 1920, specifically Section 2, which forbids purchase of additional Crow land by persons or companies already owning 640 acres of agricultural land or 1,280 acres of grazing land within the reservation boundary. A 1927 amendment further outlawed "overlapping leasing"—the yearly purchase of five-year leases effectively creating perpetual leases. BIA officials told Pease the law had been amended to revoke these provisions, which Pease flatly denied after checking the U.S. Code. BIA area realty officer Robert Seitz stated approximately 70–73 percent of the reservation was owned by Indians, but 50 to 60 percent was leased mostly to non-Indians, with some fee patents issued. Attorney General Robert F. Kennedy was reportedly interested in the legal status of Crow lands operated by non-Indians. The Justice Department had been sitting on two cases since 1960 awaiting BIA referral. A 1957 House subcommittee hearing on the reservation heard testimony from attorney John M. Dietrich that the law was ignored because the U.S. Code Annotated omitted reference to Section 2. Assistant Secretary Roger Ernst acknowledged in a March 1960 letter that "no adequate explanation of the circumstances is available." The document demonstrates systematic administrative neglect enabling large-scale dispossession through illegal purchases and perpetual leasing arrangements, with BIA officials falsely claiming governing statutes had been amended.

summaries.display_title: 1962: Great Falls Tribune on Potential Justice Department Action Over Crow Act Violations
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1962

This is a newspaper article from the *Great Falls Tribune*, published August 21, 1962, reporting on potential U.S. Justice Department action regarding violations of the Crow Act of 1920 on the Crow Reservation in Montana. The article draws on statements from multiple officials, including U.S. Attorney Moody Brickett of Butte, BIA realty officer Robert Seitz, Crow Tribal attorney Bert W. Kronmiller of Hardin, and Harlow Pease, a former BIA area counsel in Billings.

The Crow Act of 1920 prohibited any individual or company—including Indians—from purchasing Crow land if they already owned 640 acres of agricultural land or 1,280 acres of grazing land within the reservation. The article identifies two categories of alleged violations: outright purchases exceeding these acreage caps, and "overlapping leasing," whereby five-year leases were purchased annually from individual Indians, effectively creating perpetual leases that circumvented the Act's protections. One specific case cited by Kronmiller involved a single rancher who owned 12,180 acres of Crow land. Seitz acknowledged that approximately 70 percent of the reservation was in private hands, with 50–60 percent leased by non-Indians or Indians and 27 percent owned outright by non-Indians—estimated at more than one million acres. BIA officials in Billings claimed their realty officers believed the Act had been amended, while Pease stated he had repeatedly warned BIA officials of their neglect. Attorney General Robert F. Kennedy was reportedly interested in the situation, though Brickett indicated no complaints had yet been filed. The document demonstrates how BIA administrative negligence and misinterpretation of law facilitated massive land dispossession on the Crow Reservation.

summaries.display_title: 1959: Hardin Tribune-Herald on Sale of 26,000 Acres of Crow Indian Land
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1917–1959

This is a page from the *Hardin Tribune-Herald*, a newspaper in Hardin, Big Horn County, Montana, dated Thursday, January 29, 1959. The page contains multiple articles, but the most significant for land dispossession are two adjacent reports on a sale of Crow Indian land conducted through the Crow Agency office under Superintendent Clyde Hobbs.

On January 20, 1959, 77 parcels totaling 26,301.66 acres of Crow Indian land were offered for sale—57 grazing tracts and 21 farm-pasture tracts. The Crow Agency reported 91 bids received, with acceptable bids on 12,009.2 acres (54.55% sale rate), generating $195,895.51 at an average of $16.31 per acre. The highest grazing bid was $42.50/acre; one farm tract sold at $126/acre. No bids were received on 22 tracts.

The Crow Leaseholders Association conducted its own survey and challenged the sale's success. It reported grazing lands averaged only $8.54/acre, with only 14 of 57 grazing parcels receiving bids above $10/acre. Farm-pasture lands (2,580.10 acres) averaged $41.38/acre. Critically, the Association blamed Section 2 of the Act of June 4, 1920 (the Crow Act) for suppressing competition, stating that many members who would have submitted higher bids were "precluded from doing so" by its provisions. Indians themselves reportedly expressed disappointment at the lack of competitive bidding. This document demonstrates how federal statutory mechanisms governing Crow land sales depressed prices, facilitating dispossession at below-market values while nominally following legal procedures.

summaries.display_title: 1964: Billings Gazette on Crow Indian Land Title Validation Bill Pending in Congress
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1964

This is a newspaper article by Dick Gilluly, staff writer for *The Billings Gazette*, published January 12, 1964, analyzing the Crow Indian land title validation bill then pending in Congress. The article examines the legislative and legal complexities surrounding non-Indian ownership of land on the Crow Reservation in Montana.

The piece centers on a bill introduced by Senators Lee Metcalf and Mike Mansfield that passed the Senate and awaited action in Representative James A. Haley's House Indian Affairs Subcommittee. The bill sought to validate titles held by non-Indian ranchers who had acquired Crow Reservation land in violation of the Crow Act of 1920, which prohibited non-Indian ownership beyond certain acreage limitations. The article notes that the law "was repeatedly violated" and that "many ranchers own Crow land thousands of acres in excess of the limitation," creating widespread clouded titles that prevented ranchers from borrowing against their deeds.

The article raises the critical question of whether Indians received fair market value for their land, with Billings attorney Harlow Pease and Justice Department officials citing specific instances where they definitively did not. A comprehensive study of all transactions had never been conducted. Gilluly outlines a potential compromise: validating titles where fair prices were paid, while requiring non-Indian owners to pay the difference on undervalued purchases. Some lawyers argued that any validation would deprive Crows of constitutional property rights. The Crow Tribe was reportedly considering a new resolution opposing the bill. The document demonstrates systematic dispossession through statutory violations that went unchecked, with subsequent legislative efforts aimed at legalizing those violations rather than restoring Indian land rights.

summaries.display_title: 1915–1921: BIA Administrative Records on Allotment of the Crow Reservation Under the Crow Act of 1920
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1915–1921

This archival file from the Crow Indian Agency (RG 75, Box 24, Denver) documents the administrative machinery behind the Crow Act of June 4, 1920 (Public No. 239, 66th Congress, S-2890; 41 Stat. 571), which mandated the complete allotment of the Crow Reservation in Montana. The collection includes correspondence from Superintendent Calvin H. Asbury, instructions from the Office of Indian Affairs, communications with Secretary of the Interior John Barton Payne, congressional committee logistics, and field reports from the Crow Enrollment and Competency Commission and Special Allotting Agent Charles E. Roblin.

The Act provided for closing tribal rolls, allotting 160 acres to heirs of members who died unallotted after December 31, 1905, additional 160-acre allotments to new heads of families, and pro-rating all remaining allottable tribal lands equally among enrolled members. Approximately 2,004,955 acres of surveyed land were at issue, with roughly 1,396,000 acres of tribal land in the diminished reservation. Sections 16 and 36 were granted to Montana for school purposes at $5 per acre—estimated at 80,000–85,000 acres. Minerals were reserved to the tribe for fifty years. Each member was to designate 640 acres as an inalienable homestead, and no purchaser could acquire more than 640 acres. Irrigation construction charges were to be reimbursed over twenty years. Asbury noted in August 1919 that isolated tracts of 40–80 acres left after allotment should be sold, and by August 1921 he warned that the 18-month statutory deadline for completing allotments was physically impossible, estimating four to five years were needed. Multiple unsurveyed townships required new surveys before allotment could proceed. The documents collectively reveal how the Act converted collective Crow landholding into individualized parcels, creating the legal infrastructure for eventual alienation through fee patents and sales to non-Indians, while railroad companies like the Burlington Route actively sought information about opening reservation land to white settlement.

summaries.display_title: 1962: Billings Gazette on BIA Report Finding Widespread Illegal Leasing of Crow Reservation Land
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1962–1962

This is a newspaper article from the *Billings Gazette* (circulation 30,141 daily, 41,460 Sunday), dated October 18, 1962, written by Dick Gilluly. It reports on a Bureau of Indian Affairs Missouri River Basin Investigation (MRBI) unit report finding that approximately 90 percent of Crow Reservation land leased to non-Indians—roughly 800,000 acres—was probably leased illegally. The MRBI report, furnished to the newspaper by Roger Stops, a Crow Indian of Crow Agency, had not been officially released and was described by MRBI director Walter F. Fuhriman as a "first draft" prepared for internal comment.

The illegal leasing was found to violate a 1927 amendment to the Crow Allotment Act, which prohibited overlapping leasing—the practice whereby lessees voided prior-year leases and purchased new ones annually, creating perpetual leases that prevented Indians from bargaining for better prices. Most Crow land was leased under the Crow Competency Act, which gave individual Indians full authority over their allotted land with minimal BIA supervision. Average grazing rental on illegal leases was approximately 25 cents per acre in March 1962, compared with 47 cents on federally supervised leases and 65 cents for non-Indian-owned Crow land. U.S. Attorney Moody Brickett of Butte sought to bring a test case to invalidate all illegal leases. The Crow Tribe allocated $1 million from its $8 million judgment fund for competent lease purchases, potentially centralizing future leasing through the tribe. The document demonstrates how "competency" legislation, ostensibly granting Indian autonomy, functioned as a mechanism of economic dispossession by removing federal oversight and enabling systematic below-market extraction by non-Indian ranchers.

summaries.display_title: 1959: Meeting Notes and Memoranda on Proposed Repeal of Section 2 of the Crow Act of 1920
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1902–1990

This document collection comprises meeting notes, analytical memoranda, and legislative texts related to the Crow Reservation land dispossession debate, centering on the proposed repeal of Section 2 of the June 4, 1920 Act (41 Stat. 751), as amended by the June 8, 1940 Act (54 Stat. 252). A substitute bill was introduced January 9, 1959, by Senators Murray, Mansfield, and McGee (86th Congress, 1st Session, referred to the Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs), which would repeal Section 2's acreage limitations on conveyances and validate prior conveyances made in violation of those limits, while preserving mineral rights and access easements.

The analytical memorandum, likely authored by Joe Medicine Crow or a close associate, argues strenuously against transferring trust period extension authority from the President to Congress. It identifies nine groups of Crow allotments with trust periods expiring on staggered dates through 1973, including Ceded Strip allotments recently extended to 1964 and 1920 Act allotments expiring in 1973. The proposed deal—extending all trust periods to 1990 in exchange for repealing Section 11—is characterized as a trap that would ultimately give Congress termination power over the reservation. The document invokes the Lone Wolf Decision of 1903 (referenced as 1902) as evidence of congressional hostility toward Indian interests. Robert Yellowtail and Meglen are named as key political actors. OCR quality is poor throughout, with pages 1–2 and 5–6 largely illegible. The document powerfully illustrates how legislative mechanisms were deployed to facilitate alienation of Crow lands under the guise of administrative reform.

summaries.display_title: 1950–1955: Documents on Federal and State Taxation of Joseph Medicine Crow and Restricted Crow Allottees
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1950–1955

This collection of documents centers on the federal and state taxation of Crow tribal member Joseph Medicine Crow and other restricted Indians, revealing how taxation functioned as a mechanism of economic dispossession. The core documents include: a February 8, 1951 letter from the Superintendent of Crow Indian Agency transmitting guidance on federal income tax liability for income from restricted trust property; a February 20, 1954 letter from Big Horn County Attorney C. W. Jones demanding $99.05 in back personal property taxes from Medicine Crow for 1953; a November 15, 1954 letter from County Treasurer Mildred Ewing demanding $82.09 in 1954 personal property taxes with threat of seizure and sale; and Medicine Crow's own letters of protest (November 30, 1953 and January 11, 1955) arguing that assessments were selectively imposed on specific families—Carson Yellowtail, his sons, nephews, Arial Whiteman, and Robert and Tom Yellowtail—while other Indians with more property were untouched.

The legal framework invoked includes the General Allotment Act of February 8, 1887 (24 Stat. 388), the Act of April 11, 1882 (22 Stat. 42), and Section 2 of the Act of July 1, 1948 (62 Stat. 1214). The 1951 letter references *Taunah et al. v. H. C. Jones, Collector of Internal Revenue* (Western District of Oklahoma, March 21, 1950), initially holding Indians not liable, later reversed on appeal. A Bureau of Indian Affairs letter from Acting Commissioner W. Barton Greenwood also cites *Capeman v. United States* (1952). The documents collectively demonstrate that both federal income and county personal property taxation were imposed on trust-status Indian property despite statutory exemptions, with county authorities threatening seizure—effectively converting trust protections into instruments of dispossession. OCR quality is poor throughout, particularly on pages 1–2.

summaries.display_title: 1887–1957: Joseph Medicine Crow Papers on Crow Reservation Land Dispossession and Federal Legislation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1887–1957

This collection from the Joseph Medicine Crow papers comprises multiple document types: federal legislative correspondence, tribal resolutions, legal briefs, policy memoranda, competency classification records, and advocacy materials, all centering on Crow Reservation land dispossession in Montana.

The core legislative documents include a March 11, 1957 letter from Assistant Secretary Hatfield Chilson to Senator James E. Murray recommending enactment of S. 332, which would repeal acreage limitations in Section 2 of the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), as amended by the Act of June 8, 1940 (54 Stat. 252). That 1920 provision prohibited conveyances to persons owning 640+ acres of agricultural or 1,280+ acres of grazing land on the reservation, with violations punishable by $5,000 fines or six months imprisonment. The Department acknowledged these limits went unenforced for 35 years, with "substantial" violations from 1921 onward—some through Bureau-supervised sales. The bill validated all prior illegal conveyances while preserving mineral rights for allottees.

A February 5, 1952 statement by Joe Medicine Crow, addressed to the Competency Commission, systematically critiques mass competency classification under the 1920 Crow Act, documenting that of 70 unit permits modified to independent leases, 68% failed to increase rental rates. Accompanying lists enumerate hundreds of Crow allottees classified as "Competent," with allotment numbers ranging from 26 to 3651, the majority marked deceased—evidencing generational land loss. A 1955 letter from Wilkinson, Boyden, Cragun & Barker details the Crow claim (Docket No. 54) before the Indian Claims Commission for unconscionable compensation on 30+ million acres ceded under the 1868 Treaty. A January 1939 tribal resolution proposes using tribal funds to repurchase fractionated heirship lands, noting 800,000 of 1,016,585 allotted acres were already in heirship status. Senator Hugh Butler's Congressional Record remarks reveal explicit designs to tax and alienate Indian lands. Together, these documents demonstrate dispossession through allotment under the General Allotment Act of 1887, competency classifications, unenforced protective statutes, Bureau-supervised sales at deflated appraisals ($5/acre on 89% of tracts), and legislative maneuvering to remove restrictions on alienation.

summaries.file_name: Laura Lee Collection Thomas Sloan letter.pdf
summaries.display_title: c. 1937–1938: Thomas Sloan Letter on Crow Land Policy and John Collier's Indian New Deal
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: undated

This document is a lengthy letter from Thomas Sloan ("Tom S.") to an unnamed recipient associated with the Laura Lee Collection, likely written in the late 1930s based on references to the Seventy-Fifth Congress (1937–1938) and John Collier's tenure as Commissioner of Indian Affairs. The OCR quality is poor, with substantial garbling on the first two pages, but the letter's substance is largely recoverable from pages 3–6.

Sloan, an Omaha Indian attorney, discusses multiple legal battles over Indian allotment rights. He references his petition for rehearing before the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals in an allotment case involving Palm Springs Mission Indians, where a two-judge majority ruled against him in nine pages while a dissenting opinion favorable to his position ran thirty-three pages. He cites the Mission Indian Act of January 12, 1891, and the special act of March 2, 1917, as establishing allotment obligations. He invokes *Cramer v. United States*, 261 U.S. 219; *Knight v. Lane*, 228 U.S. 11; *United States v. Powers*; *Raymond Bear Hill*, 52 Land Decisions 488; his own early cases at 95 Fed. 193 and 118 Fed. 293 (decided July 1899); and Solicitor Margold's opinion at 55 Land Decisions 303–4 affirming that allotment conclusively transfers occupancy rights. He describes winning will cases for Omaha and Pawnee allottees, receiving fees of $2,000 and $500 respectively. He references Senate hearings on S. 1424 and S. 2589. Sloan condemns Commissioners Burke and Merritt and especially Collier for destroying Indian rights, arguing the Bureau perverted supervisory power over leasing to benefit white renters over Indian allottees, and that lands were being taken because they became valuable as winter resorts—directly evidencing dispossession through administrative manipulation rather than lawful process.

summaries.file_name: Murray Crow James Stanton and leading.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1928–1937: Senator Murray Correspondence on Crow Allotment Leasing Dispute Involving Jennie C. Smith
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1928–1937

This collection comprises correspondence between U.S. Senator James E. Murray (Montana), the Office of Indian Affairs (Commissioner John Collier and Assistant Commissioner William Zimmerman), Crow Agency Superintendent Robert Yellowtail, and Crow tribal member Mrs. Jennie C. Smith of Pryor, Montana, regarding a protracted leasing dispute over Smith's 360-acre allotment on the Crow Reservation. The key analytical document is a January 22, 1937 memorandum from James W. Stanton, Forest Supervisor at Crow Agency, to the Superintendent, detailing the full history of the case.

Smith was granted an unrestricted leasing privilege on August 1, 1928, authorizing her to lease her lands free of governmental supervision. The E.L. Dana Livestock Company, financed through the Regional Finance Corporation, controlled the range unit surrounding her land beginning in 1930 (previously held by the Frank L. Heinrich Company). Smith consistently refused Dana's lease offers, demanding back rent from April 30, 1930. Dana offered a three-year lease (December 1, 1934–November 30, 1937) at 50¢/acre for 160 acres and 15¢/acre for 200 acres, totaling $110/year, with a $275 check covering two and a half years and $55 due June 1, 1937—$330 total. Smith separately leased to John Shupack at $72/year ($36 received for six months before Shupack abandoned the lease). Surrounding land rented at 7¢–15¢/acre; trespass value was calculated at 10¢/acre ($270 for the period). Over seven years Smith received only $36 total—approximately 1-3/7¢ per acre annually.

Stanton recommended Smith fence her land, as her unfenced allotment lay within Dana's range unit, making trespass prevention impossible. The Commissioner's office concluded no further federal assistance could be extended, effectively placing the burden on an individual Indian allottee against a corporate ranching operation backed by federal financing—illustrating how unrestricted leasing privileges, while nominally empowering, left allottees without institutional support to enforce their rights against well-capitalized lessees controlling surrounding range units.

summaries.display_title: 1952–1955: Senator Murray Papers on Harvey Driftwood's Fee Patent Application for Crow Allotment
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1952–1955

This collection of documents from the James E. Murray Papers (Collection 91, Series 11, 83rd Congress) at the University of Montana's Mansfield Library traces the effort of Harvey Driftwood, a 33-year-old Crow Indian allottee (Allotment No. 3191), to obtain a fee patent for 80 acres of his allotment on the Crow Reservation in Montana, described as the SW¼ NW¼ and NW¼ SW¼ of Section 21, Township 8 South, Range 35 East. On November 26, 1952, Driftwood filed a fee patent application through the Crow Indian Agency. The land included 32 irrigated acres and 48 grazing acres, valued at $3,000, leased to Carl W. Gross of Wyola, Montana, for $80 annually. The Crow Agency superintendent denied the application on March 26, 1953, citing Section 13 of the Crow Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751, 756), which prohibited sale of more than one-half of an allottee's irrigable land. Since all 35 irrigable acres fell within the 80-acre tract, the restriction applied. Robert Yellowtail of Lodge Grass then wrote Senator Murray, who introduced S. 1718 on April 22, 1953. However, Assistant Secretary F.E. Wormser issued an adverse report on January 14, 1954, recommending against enactment because Public Law 49 (83rd Congress, 67 Stat. 42) had already removed the irrigable-land restriction, giving the Secretary of Interior existing authority to approve the patent administratively. The Billings Area Director had approved Driftwood's application on November 9, rendering the bill unnecessary. The documents reveal how overlapping statutory restrictions—designed ostensibly to protect Indian land—created bureaucratic obstacles that channeled allottees toward fee patenting and ultimate alienation of irrigable reservation land.

summaries.display_title: 1948–1949: Senate Bill to Issue Fee Patent to Leo Farwell Glenn, Crow Allottee
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1948–1949

This collection from the James E. Murray papers (Collection No. 91, Mansfield Library, University of Montana) documents the legislative effort to issue a patent in fee to Leo Farwell Glenn, Crow allottee No. 3234, for approximately 880 acres on the Crow Reservation in Montana. The core document is S. 520, introduced January 17, 1949, in the 81st Congress, 1st Session, by Senator Joseph C. O'Mahoney (Wyoming) and Senator James E. Murray (Montana), directing the Secretary of the Interior to issue Glenn a fee patent for lands in sections 26, 27, and 34, Township 3 South, Range 27 East, P.M., Montana.

The Department of the Interior issued an adverse report dated March 14, 1949, signed by Under Secretary Oscar L. Chapman, recommending against enactment. The report cited the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), which prohibited Crow allottees from selling more than one-half of their homestead, necessitating special legislation. Superintendent Lippert at Crow Agency opposed the patent because Glenn's land lay in an area used predominantly by Indians, and family members were actively farming there. The Interior Department warned that issuing patents without competency determinations jeopardized Indian welfare and violated federal trust obligations. Despite the adverse report, the bill ultimately passed as Private Law No. 241, signed by the President on August 24, 1949. A Western Union telegram from Glenn to Robert Yellowtail, prominent Crow leader, requested his advocacy. The file illustrates how individual fee patents circumvented protective restrictions, converting trust land to alienable status and facilitating dispossession despite agency-level opposition.

summaries.file_name: Murray Crow patent in fee Anson Pease 1949.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1949–1950: Senate Bill to Issue Fee Patent to Anson Harold Pease, Crow Allottee
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1949–1950

This collection documents the legislative process behind S. 2510, introduced by Senator James E. Murray on August 30, 1949, to authorize the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Anson Harold Pease, Crow allottee No. 1322, for 649.55 acres of his homestead allotment on the Crow Reservation in Montana. The lands are described across sections 11, 14, and 15 of Township 1 South, Range 27 East, principal meridian, Montana. The bill was necessary because Section 13 of the Crow Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751) prohibited a Crow allottee from selling more than one-half of his homestead, or 320 acres. A patent in fee would remove trust restrictions on the entire allotment, enabling its sale to non-Indians.

The file includes the Crow Agency superintendent's letter of August 22, 1949, confirming Pease's competency and off-reservation residence, while noting that fee patenting would alienate trust land in a predominantly trust-status area and interfere with future consolidation of Indian lands. Acting Secretary Oscar L. Chapman's October 19, 1949 report recommended enactment with an amendment granting the Crow Tribe or tribal members a preferential purchase period. The Senate committee report (No. 1501, April 18, 1950) adopted a modified 60-day right of first refusal with notice requirements through the Crow Agency superintendent. The documents illustrate how individual fee patenting served as a mechanism of land dispossession, converting restricted trust allotments into alienable property despite acknowledged harm to tribal land consolidation efforts.

summaries.file_name: Murray Crow patent in fee Anson Pease 1952.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1950–1952: Senate Bill to Issue Fee Patent to Walter Anson Pease, Crow Allottee
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1950–1952

This collection documents the legislative process by which Senator James E. Murray of Montana sought to authorize a patent in fee for Walter Anson Pease, a Crow allottee, covering Crow Allotment No. 3452 on the Crow Reservation, Montana, comprising 983.72 acres. The documents include S. 2573 (82nd Congress, 2nd Session), introduced January 31, 1952; Senate Report No. 1458 (Calendar No. 1386), reported April 16, 1952; the Department of the Interior's favorable report dated April 7, 1952, signed by Assistant Secretary Dale E. Doty; correspondence between Murray, Pease, and the Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs; and Pease's original 1950 request letter. A prior bill, S. 3389, introduced in the 81st Congress, had expired without enactment.

The legislation was necessary because the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751) restricted Crow allottees from selling more than one-half of their homestead, or 320 acres. Pease had already conveyed 40 acres to another Indian. The Interior Department certified Pease was "qualified to conduct his affairs without governmental supervision," noting he lived off-reservation in Billings, was a bricklayer and Marine veteran, and was one-quarter Crow. The legal description spans portions of sections 1, 2, 11, and 12, T. 1 S., R. 27 E. The documents illustrate how fee patents functioned as a dispossession mechanism: by removing trust protections, they enabled alienation of nearly 984 acres of Crow Reservation land, with the federal government itself recommending removal of restrictions and confirming that alienation would "not adversely affect other Indian interests."

summaries.file_name: Murray Crow patent in fee Frederick Knows Gun 195.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1952–1953: Senate Bill to Issue Fee Patent to Frederick P. Knows Gun, Crow Allottee
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1952–1953

This collection of documents from the James E. Murray papers (University of Montana Archives, Collection No. 91, Series 11, 83rd Congress) traces the legislative and administrative history of S. 367, a bill introduced by Senator Murray on January 13, 1953, authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to issue a patent in fee to Frederick P. Knows Gun for approximately 320 acres on the Crow Indian Reservation in Montana—specifically the east half of Section 17, Township 7 South, Range 38 East, Montana principal meridian. The land constituted part of Knows Gun's homestead allotment.

The Department of the Interior issued an adverse report, transmitted by Assistant Secretary Orme Lewis, recommending the bill not be enacted. The Department's reasoning was that legislation was unnecessary because the second proviso of Section 13 of the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751, 756) already permitted the sale of up to 320 acres of a homestead allotment on the Crow Reservation. Knows Gun, described as a 38-year-old Crow Indian widower with seven minor children, owned approximately 2,349 acres of trust land and derived his sole income from lease rentals on trust property. He had been advised of the administrative pathway by the Area Director at Billings on July 10, 1952. Senator Murray informed Knows Gun on April 22, 1953, that the Senate would not act on the bill and suggested he apply directly to the Crow Agency superintendent. The file reveals how fee patenting—a primary mechanism of Indian land dispossession—could proceed through routine administrative channels without congressional action, potentially converting trust-protected land held by an economically vulnerable allottee into alienable property.

summaries.file_name: Murray E Posey Whiteman and leasing 1955.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1955: Senator Murray Correspondence on Crow Reservation Grazing Land Leases
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1955–1955

This document is a series of correspondence from the James E. Murray papers (Collection No. 91, Series I, Box 273) at the University of Montana's Mansfield Library, involving Senator James E. Murray, the Bureau of Indian Affairs Commissioner, and Edward P. Whiteman, Sr., a member of the Crow Tribal Council from Pryor, Montana. The letters, dated between February 3 and February 18, 1955, concern the leasing of Crow Reservation tribal grazing lands and the question of whether previous (non-Indian) permittees should receive preference rights to meet high bids on grazing units.

The BIA's February 18 response to Murray explains the regulatory framework under 25 CFR 71.13, noting that grazing permits on the Crow Reservation were set to expire November 30, 1955. The BIA clarified that the federal government does not itself extend preference to permittees; rather, the Crow Tribal Council, by resolution adopted July 26, 1950, had granted previous permittees the privilege of meeting high bids while giving Indian preference precedence. Whiteman's letters (February 14 and earlier) argue that the Tribal Council should have full discretion over leasing decisions, that Indian-owned land should be put into Indian use, and that existing BIA policy effectively disregarded Crow wishes by favoring incumbent non-Indian lessees. He cites the 1949 Code of Federal Regulations, paragraphs 71.9–71.12, to support tribal authority. The documents reveal how federal administrative procedures for grazing permits on tribal lands structurally disadvantaged Indian livestock operators despite nominal tribal consent mechanisms, perpetuating non-Indian control over Crow tribal range lands.

summaries.display_title: 1941–1947: R. W. Bonko Fee Patent Request and Crow Allotment Control Case
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1941–1947

This collection of correspondence documents the case of R. W. Bonko, a 1/8 Crow Indian and World War II veteran (Army Air Corps, December 16, 1941 – October 16, 1945, including eleven months overseas and seven months as a prisoner of war), who sought control of his 1,038-acre allotment in grazing unit #24 on the Crow Reservation. The documents include Bonko's April 3, 1947 letter to Senator James E. Murray, a March 11, 1947 letter from Crow Agency Superintendent L. C. Lippert, Murray's April 8 memorandum to the Office of Indian Affairs, and Commissioner William Zimmerman's April 14, 1947 reply. The materials are from the James E. Murray manuscripts (Collection 91, Series I, Box 272) at the University of Montana's Mansfield Library.

Bonko received only $176.62 annually from his allotment, which the agency estimated could support 41 head of cattle year-round and included 180 irrigable acres. The entire allotment was locked into a five-year grazing permit issued December 1, 1945, awarded through competitive bidding with consent of 51 percent of unit landholders. Commissioner Zimmerman's office disapproved fee patent applications when issuance would interfere with range land management, noting that fee patents would merely subject lands to taxation. Certificates of competency had been discontinued. While regulations permitted allotment withdrawal for personal use during the permit's life, Bonko could not withdraw land for leasing or third-party use, effectively preventing a returning veteran without capital from utilizing his own property. The case illustrates how collective grazing unit administration and bureaucratic obstacles functioned as mechanisms of continued dispossession, trapping individual allottees in minimal rental returns while denying them operational control.

summaries.display_title: 1957: Senate Bill to Convey Fort Peck Reservation Land to Theresa Brost
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1908–1957

This collection from the James E. Murray Papers (Collection No. 91, Series II, 85th Congress) at the University of Montana's Mansfield Library documents the failed attempt by Senator James E. Murray to pass S. 327 in the 85th Congress (introduced January 7, 1957), directing the Secretary of the Interior to convey by quitclaim deed, without consideration, 440 acres on the Fort Peck Indian Reservation to Theresa Brost of Nashua, Montana. The land—E½, E½NW¼, NE¼SW¼, Section 25, Township 29 North, Range 41 East—comprised homestead entries of Nicholas A. Brost (Great Falls 054270, allowed January 21, 1916) and Charles A. Brost (Great Falls 055278, allowed April 25, 1917), opened under the Act of May 30, 1908 (35 Stat. 555). The entries were cancelled June 10, 1940 for nonpayment; only a 20 percent initial deposit and one token payment (October 20, 1927) were ever made. The Secretary's withdrawal order of September 19, 1934 returned such undisposed lands to tribal status. On February 4, 1956, the United States obtained a permanent injunction against the Brosts for trespass and damages. The Fort Peck Tribal Executive Board (Assiniboine and Sioux Tribes), through Resolution #92-57-8 adopted unanimously August 8, 1957 by Chairman Dolly C. Akers, formally protested the bill, citing a March 1952 General Council resolution forbidding tribal land sales. Both the Secretary of the Interior (July 19, 1957) and the Bureau of the Budget (Robert E. Merriam, July 16, 1957) recommended against enactment. Attorney Leonard H. Langen's August 19, 1955 letter reveals the Bureau of Indian Affairs had already leased the land to George Bud Edwards in April 1954, and that the Brosts occupied the land nearly 40 years paying neither taxes nor rent. The file powerfully illustrates how ceded reservation lands, once opened for homesteading, reverted to tribal ownership upon entry cancellation, and how congressional private relief bills could not override tribal sovereignty and federal trust obligations.

summaries.file_name: Murray James Stanton as candidate for Supt 1945.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1945: Senator Murray Recommendation of James W. Stanton for Crow Agency Superintendent
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1945–1945

This document consists of two pieces of correspondence from the archival collection of U.S. Senator James E. Murray (Montana). The first is a letter dated March 17, 1945, from the Office of Indian Affairs (Chicago office) to Senator Murray, responding to his March 2, 1945, recommendation of James W. Stanton, a Range Supervisor at the Crow Agency, for the superintendency of the Crow Indian Agency. The letter, signed on behalf of the Commissioner for Assistant Secretary Oscar L. Chapman, explains that a recommendation for filling the position had already been made before Murray's letter arrived, following the resignation of Superintendent Robert Yellowtail. While acknowledging Stanton's "very good record" in forestry and grazing work, the office deemed him lacking in administrative experience for the superintendent role.

The second letter, dated March 20, 1945, is from Senator Murray to attorney Thomas C. Colton of Billings, Montana, relaying this outcome and noting that the selected candidate was a Mr. Warren O'Hara, formerly employed at the Crow Agency and then working in the Chicago Office of Indian Affairs.

The document illuminates the political dynamics surrounding BIA appointments at the Crow Reservation. Robert Yellowtail's resignation as superintendent—he was notably one of the first Native American superintendents—marked a significant transition in Crow Agency leadership. The selection process, influenced by senatorial patronage and internal BIA personnel decisions, demonstrates how federal administrative control over reservation governance operated, with non-Native bureaucrats replacing Indigenous leadership and outside political actors vying to place favored candidates in positions directly overseeing tribal affairs and land management.

summaries.file_name: Murray Jennie Smith and white ranchers.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1944: Jennie Smith Correspondence on Crow Indian Grazing Land Access and White Ranchers
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1944–1944

This collection consists of correspondence between U.S. Senator James E.
