Murray of Montana, Mrs. Jennie Smith (a Crow Indian of Pryor, Montana), and federal officials including Assistant Commissioner of Indian Affairs William Zimmerman and Commissioner John Collier, housed in the James E. Murray Papers (Collection 911, Series I, Box 271) at the University of Montana's Mansfield Library. The documents date from April–May 1944 and concern Smith's inability to secure adequate grazing land for her livestock on the Crow Reservation.

Senator Murray wrote to Collier on April 20, 1944, describing Smith as a "ward" in "distress through lack of adequate grazing lands," warning she might have to sell her stock and "become another propertyless Indian." Murray followed up on April 29, 1944, protesting to the Office of Indian Affairs. Zimmerman's reply of May 13, 1944 (file 17199-44, Forestry & Grazing division) reveals the structural mechanism of dispossession: Smith held a "leasing privilege to lease her lands without Agency supervision" and had leased all her lands to "livestock operators" on the reservation—likely white ranchers—"without reserving any of the lands for her own livestock." Zimmerman concluded she had no "proper cause for complaint," though he suggested she negotiate grazing rights on lands controlled by her lessees.

The documents illustrate how the federal leasing system enabled Crow allottees to be effectively dispossessed of usable grazing land even while retaining nominal ownership, with the BIA declining to intervene on behalf of Indian landholders whose allotments were entirely committed to outside operators. Several handwritten pages from Smith are largely illegible due to poor OCR quality.

summaries.file_name: Murray Mark Wolf patent in fee 1942.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1942–1944: Mark Wolf Denied Fee Patent Application for Crow Reservation Allotment
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1942–1944

This collection of correspondence documents the denied application of Mark Wolf, a Crow tribal member and retired Indian Service employee, for a patent in fee to a portion of his inherited allotment on the Crow Reservation in Montana. The file, drawn from the James E. Murray papers (Collection 91, Series I) at the University of Montana's Mansfield Library, spans from May 1942 through at least September 1944 and involves Senator Murray's office, attorney Charles C. Guinn of Hardin, Montana, and the Office of Indian Affairs (file numbers 2537-42, 23042-42, 36738-42).

Wolf sought a patent in fee for approximately 1,038 acres of inherited grazing land situated on the edge of the reservation, claiming offers of approximately $10,000 for the tract. Guinn argued the land yielded only $155 annually in rent, while the Office of Indian Affairs reported $270 per annum and noted Wolf received an $80 monthly pension. Assistant Commissioner William Zimmerman Jr. and Assistant to the Commissioner Walter V. Woehlke repeatedly denied the application, citing departmental policy to keep land in Indian ownership, noting that "issuance of patents in fee is usually followed by sale of the land or its loss through delinquent taxes," and finding no "extreme and pressing emergency." The Superintendent at Crow Agency submitted supplemental reports but the Office maintained its denial through at least September 1942. This file illustrates the tension between individual Indian landholders seeking fee patents to monetize allotments and federal policy designed to prevent further land loss—while simultaneously demonstrating how trust restrictions left elderly allottees dependent on inadequate lease income controlled through the agency office.

summaries.file_name: Murray Minnie K. Reed forced fee claim 1958.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1958: Mary K. Reed Crow Allotment and Forced Fee Patent Inquiry
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1905–1958

This file from the James E. Murray papers (Collection No. 91, Series I, Box 274) documents the case of Mary K. Reed, a Crow Indian allottee, and her inquiries to Senator Murray regarding her allotment and financial hardship. The core documents are letters dated June 18, 1958, from Superintendent Clyde W. Hobbs of the Crow Indian Agency responding to Senator Murray's referral of Reed's correspondence.

Records show that on March 20, 1905, an unnumbered trust patent was issued to Mary K. Reed for Allotment No. 34, covering SW¼SW¼, Section 24, Township 4 South, Range 32 East, and NE¼NE¼, Section 13, Township 6 South, Range 31 East—totaling 160.0 acres. On August 9, 1916, fee patent No. 541610 was issued covering all of this property. On March 16, 1917, the patent was delivered to Mrs. Nellie Reed Lobdell per Reed's signed authorization dated November 10, 1916. The Bureau explicitly noted that once land entered fee status, it relinquished jurisdiction entirely.

By 1931, Reed wrote Superintendent C.H. Asbury noting approximately $850 in delinquent Big Horn County taxes against the fee-patented land. By 1958, Reed was receiving Old Age Assistance, which was reduced from $56.00 to $28.00 (and separately from $43.00 to $13.00) due to increased lease income, per Big Horn County Welfare Department notices signed by caseworker Dorothy Egnew. The file starkly illustrates how forced fee patenting exposed allottees to tax liability and eventual impoverishment, while the BIA disclaimed further responsibility once trust status was removed.

summaries.display_title: 1957: Christmas Card from Minnie R. Williams to Senator Murray, Crow Agency
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1957–1957

This document is a Christmas card sent by Minnie R. Williams to U.S. Senator James E. Murray of Montana, dated Christmas 1957. It is held in the James E. Murray Manuscript Collection (Collection No. 91, Series I, Box 274) at the Mansfield Library, University of Montana Archives. The card originates from the Crow Indian Reservation, specifically referencing the Crow Indian Agency.

Beyond seasonal greetings, Williams uses the card to signal her intent to write Senator Murray in greater detail after the holidays about "certain features of the Crow Indians" that she believes "the Congress should know as heretofore unknown." She references recent radio comments by Congressman Lee Metcalf regarding "the setup of the Indian bureau department," affirming his remarks as "all true as far as the address went." This situates the document within the broader mid-1950s political context of federal Indian policy reform, the termination era, and congressional scrutiny of Bureau of Indian Affairs operations on reservations including the Crow.

The OCR quality is poor, with fragmented text on several pages, making portions difficult to reconstruct. No specific acreages, dollar amounts, allotment numbers, or legal citations appear. Nevertheless, the document is significant as evidence that Crow Reservation residents were actively engaging Montana's congressional delegation about BIA administrative practices and potential abuses, and that constituent pressure from Indigenous communities and their advocates contributed to congressional awareness of conditions on the Crow Reservation during the termination policy period.

summaries.file_name: Murray Minnie Williams on women in Crow politics.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1956: Minnie R. Williams Correspondence on Women in Crow Politics and Tribal Payment Veto
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1956–1956

This correspondence, held in the James E. Murray Papers (Collection No. 91, Series I, Box 273) at the University of Montana's Mansfield Library, consists of letters exchanged between Minnie R. Williams, a Crow Indian woman at Crow Agency, Montana, and U.S. Senators James E. Murray (Chairman, Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs) and Mike Mansfield during July 1956. The documents address two principal subjects: the $5 million payment vetoed by the President for Crow tribal land on the Big Horn River related to the Yellowtail Dam hydroelectric project, and the unprecedented appointment of Crow Indian women to tribal council committees under new Chairman Edward P. Whiteman.

Williams corrects her earlier misunderstanding that Murray and Mansfield introduced a House resolution to override the presidential veto, which Murray clarifies was not attempted due to insufficient votes. Williams provides the Crow perspective on the Yellowtail project, noting that the proposed taking area of no less than 7,000 acres was valued at $5 million by consulting engineer Berry Dibble, and that the Crow people believed this undervalued their tribal mountain lands. She references nearly 50 years of resistance to land cessions, stating Crow Indians had historically been treated "like jokers in all our land deals." Murray indicated he had asked Interior Secretary Seaton to discuss Yellowtail matters. The documents demonstrate ongoing federal pressure to acquire Crow reservation lands for reclamation projects at contested valuations, while also documenting a significant moment in Crow women's political inclusion.

summaries.file_name: Murray N. Cheyenne land sales and a Fort Peck fee patent.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1937–1959: Senator Murray Correspondence on Land Sales at Fort Peck and Northern Cheyenne Reservations
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1937–1959

This folder from the James E. Murray Papers (Collection 91, Series 1, Box 271) at the University of Montana contains correspondence between U.S. Senator James E. Murray of Montana and various constituents, Bureau of Indian Affairs officials, and fellow legislators regarding Indian land sales and allotment dispositions on the Fort Peck and Northern Cheyenne Reservations, spanning 1937 to approximately 1959.

The earliest documents concern Janice Black (also known as Violet Ivy Taylor/Violet Ivey), Fort Peck allottee No. 2085, who held 326.16 acres plus an additional 40-acre tract. In 1937, Assistant Commissioner William Zimmerman Jr. reported that a deed had been submitted conveying 40 acres of her allotment to Louis James Sayers, a Turtle Mountain Indian allottee. The superintendent proposed Black trade 160 acres of grazing land for 40 acres under the Wiota Irrigation Project, acknowledging that 160 acres of grazing land was insufficient for subsistence.

By 1958–1959, the focus shifts to a proposed sale of approximately 25,000 acres (roughly 150 tracts) of allotted land on the Fort Peck Reservation. Superintendent David P. Weston proceeded with advertising the sale despite the Montana congressional delegation's request for a moratorium, explicitly stating the sale would go forward "without consideration of the desires of the Fort Peck tribal board." Carl L. Pearson and J.P. Markley raised concerns about the Bureau reversing its earlier recognition of "key tracts" on the Northern Cheyenne Reservation. Murray's Interior Committee had issued a questionnaire on April 17, 1958, investigating Indian land disposition over the preceding decade. These documents demonstrate systematic Bureau disregard for tribal governance and individual Indian welfare in facilitating land alienation.

summaries.display_title: 1955: Alice Yarlott Other Medicine Fee Patent Telegrams, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1955–1955

This folder from the James E. Murray papers (Collection 91, Box 273, University of Montana Archives) contains a series of Western Union telegrams from December 1955 documenting the efforts of Alice Yarlott Other Medicine, a Crow Indian allottee (Allotment No. 3652) from Hardin, Montana, to obtain a patent in fee on her trust property. Yarlott Other Medicine, who identified herself as a competent Crow Indian and registered nurse employed by the U.S. Public Health Service, wrote to Senator Murray on approximately December 10, 1955, complaining that her patent-in-fee application had been held up for three and a half months at the Crow Agency office, despite the land already having been classified and appraised by the Soil and Moisture Branch of the Indian Service. She asserted she was competent to handle the property herself and requested Murray's assistance.

Senator Murray responded on December 12, 1955, by wiring J.M. Cooper, Area Director of the Bureau of Indian Affairs in Billings, Montana, urging expedition of the appraisal. Murray also telegraphed Yarlott Other Medicine that the Indian Office cited lack of personnel for appraisal delays. Cooper replied by December 15, confirming that a competency investigation was underway and that her late-September application was preceded by numerous others. The exchange reveals how bureaucratic bottlenecks—understaffing and redundant appraisal requirements—functioned as mechanisms delaying the conversion of trust land to fee simple status, effectively prolonging federal control over individual Crow allotments even when allottees demonstrated clear competency and actively sought autonomy over their own property.

summaries.file_name: Murray Real Bird fee patent 1955. Kronmiller contract.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1954–1955: Crow Allotment Land Sales and Bert Kronmiller Tribal Attorney Contract
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1954–1955

This document is a folder from the James E. Murray Papers (Collection No. 91, Series I, Box 273) at the University of Montana's Mansfield Library, containing correspondence between U.S. Senator James E. Murray (D-Montana), Bureau of Indian Affairs officials, and Billings attorney J. F. Meglen regarding Crow Reservation land sales and the Crow Tribe's attorney contract with Bert Kronmiller.

The central transaction involves a supervised sale of the allotment of Otter Stays In Water, Crow Allottee No. 601, to Leone and Clyde J. Dygert. James Real Bird, a Crow Indian, brought this matter to Murray's attention. The heirs—Lucy Wallace, Laura Alice Wallace, Anna Plenty Good, and Ernest Gets Down Often—were selling to Leona Dygert of Hardin, Montana. The Superintendent's sale report reached the Billings Area Office on December 16, 1954; the sale was approved and a fee patent request sent to the Bureau of Land Management on December 20, 1954. Patent No. 1149722 was issued on approximately February 11, 1955. A separate transaction involved the allotment of Good Now, deceased Crow Allottee No. 1879, where fee patent No. 1147283 was issued to Jay Fitzgerald on October 11, 1954.

A January 3, 1955 letter from Murray aide Richard L. Callaghan to Meglen discusses Crow Tribal Council members seeking termination of Bert Kronmiller's tribal attorney contract, noting the contract allowed termination by the Secretary of the Interior with tribal approval. The documents collectively illustrate the bureaucratic machinery converting trust allotments into fee-patented land alienable to non-Indians, with congressional intervention expediting the process.

summaries.display_title: 1958–1959: BIA Correspondence on Crow Reservation Land Sales and Section 2 Acreage Limits
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1958–1959

This folder from the James E. Murray papers (Collection 91, Box 274, University of Montana) contains Bureau of Indian Affairs correspondence, constituent letters, petitions, and policy documents concerning land sales, trust status, and statutory interpretation on the Crow Indian Reservation in Montana. The central legal issue is the interplay between Section 2 of the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), which imposed acreage limitations (640 acres agricultural, 1,280–1,920 acres grazing) on land purchases within the reservation, and the Act of June 8, 1940 (54 Stat. 252), which authorized the Secretary of the Interior to approve sales to Crow tribal members exceeding those limits "for the purpose of consolidating the restricted land holdings" with title held in trust.

Specific cases include George C. Lande Jr. of Pryor, Montana, who owned 1,960 acres (265 agricultural, 1,695 grazing) and sought to purchase 160 additional grazing acres in trust status, appealing the Billings Area Director's requirement that he take fee simple title. Other transactions involved Corn Woman allotment No. 1922 (160 acres, negotiated sale to Orville Stovall Jr., Crow allottee No. 3700), Martha Lincoln allotment No. 1869 (supervised sale to John Snyder), sales to Helen Pease Wolf and Walter O. Green of Lodge Grass, and Florence Ileta Dailey. Commissioner H. Rex Lee, approved by Assistant Secretary Roger Ernst on May 12, 1959, instructed the Billings Area Director to adopt a "liberal construction" favoring Crow Indians' land consolidation and enlargement needs, citing House Report No. 1693 (76th Congress, 3d Session).

A separate case documents the denial of a patent in fee to Paul (Pius) Crookedarm for allotment No. 3157 (180 acres of Mary Crookedarm's allotment), with the BIA citing his limited education, poor negotiating ability, and lack of self-sufficiency. A petition signed by 438 Crow members requested repeal of Section 2's acreage restrictions, extension of the trust period to 1990, and validation of prior conveyances violating acreage limits. Crow Tribal Council Chairman Edward P. Whiteman opposed repeal of Section 2 (S. 55 and H.R. 439, 86th Congress), arguing it would sanction illegal land transfers and constitute an unconstitutional taking of Indian property, citing Heckman v. U.S. (224 U.S. 413) and Ewert v. Bluejacket (259 U.S. 129). Senator Murray urged Secretary Seaton to delay a January 20, 1959 land sale at Crow Agency because acreage limitations foreclosed competitive bidding, depressing prices paid to Indian sellers. The documents collectively reveal how federal statutory frameworks, BIA competency determinations, and trust-versus-fee-patent decisions operated as mechanisms of land dispossession, while Crow members were internally divided over whether acreage restrictions protected or harmed their interests.

summaries.file_name: Murray Section 2, Rep Nat comm, fee patent 1957.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1957: Crow Reservation Land Sales, Fee Patents, and NCAI Resolutions
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1949–1957

This collection from the James E. Murray papers (Series I, Collection No. 91, University of Montana Archives) comprises correspondence, telegrams, NCAI resolutions, and Bureau of Indian Affairs communications concerning land sales, leasing, and fee patents on the Crow Reservation in Montana during 1957. The documents reveal multiple intersecting mechanisms of land dispossession.

Commissioner Glenn L. Emmons wrote Senator Murray on March 3, 1953 [likely 1957 based on context], refusing to delay a pending Crow Reservation land sale despite Murray's office requesting postponement until Congress could act on legislation removing acreage limitations. The BIA justified proceeding by claiming sales occurred only at Indian owners' requests and required appraised-value bids. A November 15, 1957 telegram from Charles A. Murray to Clem R. Cormier confirmed a protest was filed against further land sales.

Thomas D. Campbell of Campbell Farming Corporation, Hardin, Montana, sought to retain preference rights on leases covering trust allotments of "incompetent" Crow Indians, claiming 40 years of operations and rentals averaging $7.00/acre. The BIA's Deputy Commissioner noted that preference-right provisions were removed from regulations amended January 1, 1949, though special authority was granted in 1952. Campbell controlled one-third of the land outright and leased competent Indians' land directly.

NCAI Resolutions 38 and 39 (October 28–November 1, 1957, Claremore, Oklahoma) opposed H.R. 2381 and S. 332, which would remove Section 2 acreage limitations from the 1920 Crow Allotment Act (41 Stat. 751) and validate illegal titles exceeding 640 agricultural/1,280 grazing acres. A fee patent (No. 1173693) was issued July 26, 1957 on Allotment 958 (Yellowblackbird), while Philomena Five Crooked Arm's fee patent application on Allotment 612 (Charges Five Times, 460 acres) was denied by the Superintendent as incompetent, channeling her land toward supervised public sale instead.

summaries.file_name: Murray Section 2, fee patent applications, tribal council on all or nothing 1956.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1956–1957: Crow Reservation Fee Patent Policy, Antler Land Company, and Tribal Council Governance
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1957

This collection from the James E. Murray papers (University of Montana Archives, Collection No. 91, Box 273) comprises correspondence, memoranda, and policy statements relating to Crow Reservation land dispossession, fee patent policy, and tribal governance during the 1950s. The central documents include a detailed status report on lands held by the Antler Land Company (including M.H. Tschirgi, Bertha Tschirgi, and F.M. Tschirgi), totaling 42,565.91 acres acquired through various mechanisms—5,870.27 acres via pre-1920 fee patents, 18,611.60 acres through post-1920 patents transferred to Antler Land Company or the Tschirgis, 1,860 acres purchased at government sales, and 5,368.61 acres through sales by non-competent Indians approved under the Act of March 1, 1907 (34 Stat. 1018). The report acknowledges that approximately 50% of acquisitions violated the acreage limitations of Section 2 of the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), which capped individual holdings at 1,280 acres agricultural or 1,920 acres grazing land. A pending bill sought to validate these questionable titles.

Chairman Edward P. Whiteman's February 18, 1957 statement to Commissioner Emmons and his March 23, 1957 letter detail the Crow Tribal Council's opposition to the Bureau's "all-or-nothing" fee patent policy, which forced Indians accepting fee patents on any parcel to accept patents on all holdings, exposing them to immediate taxation and eventual forced sales. The documents reference H.R. 2381, S. 332, S.J. Res. 12, the 1940 amendment (54 Stat. 252), and specific cases including the blocked sale of 1,002.27 acres (Allotments 1271 and 3085) to Clarence Westbrook (Allottee No. 3808) and Mary Ann Bear Below's purchase of Allotment No. 921. Commissioner Emmons's office restricted tribal delegation travel to Washington, prompting protests about Bureau obstruction of tribal self-governance. Together these documents demonstrate systematic land alienation through fee patenting, tax foreclosure, and corporate accumulation in violation of statutory protections.

summaries.display_title: 1949–1950: Juanita Spang Denny Fee Patent Application for Crow Reservation Allotment
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1949–1950

This collection of correspondence documents the efforts of Mrs. Juanita Spang Denny, a Crow allottee residing at Lame Deer and Lodge Grass, Montana, to obtain a patent in fee for her allotment No. 3527 on the Crow Reservation, comprising 648.23 acres. The records are housed in the James E. Murray papers at the University of Montana's Mansfield Library (Collection No. 91, Series I, Box 272) and consist of letters between Senator Murray, the Bureau of Indian Affairs, Assistant Commissioner William Zimmerman Jr., Commissioner John Nichols, Superintendent L.C. Lippert at Crow Agency, and Mrs. Denny herself.

Mrs. Denny first applied in fall 1949. Superintendent Lippert denied the application on January 25, 1949, stating the allotment lay in a predominantly trust-status area and that fee patenting would defeat BIA policy of "blocking up Indian land for administrative purposes." Zimmerman concurred, citing the land's appraised value of $5 per acre (leased at only $0.25 per acre per annum) and arguing that selling would undermine her economic security. Critically, the BIA noted that the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751) prohibited a Crow allottee from selling more than one-half of homestead land, or 320 acres, meaning special legislation would be required for full alienation. Mrs. Denny resubmitted her application in April 1950 with Lippert's signature, hoping to purchase two neighboring allotments for a home. The documents reveal how BIA administrative discretion, paternalistic judgments about Indian competency, and statutory restrictions functioned together to prevent individual Crow allottees from exercising control over their own lands. OCR quality is poor throughout, with significant character-level corruption.

summaries.display_title: 1947–1948: Senate Bill to Authorize Sale of George Peters Crow Reservation Allotment
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1947–1948

This collection of documents from the James E. Murray papers (University of Montana Archives, Collection No. 91) traces the legislative effort to authorize the sale of 840 acres of Crow Reservation allotment land belonging to George Peters (Crow allottee No. 1292) of Wyola, Montana. The land comprised the N½N½ and N½N½S½N½ of section 29 and all of section 20, Township 4 South, Range 37 East, Montana principal meridian.

Senator Murray introduced S. 1385 on June 5, 1947, initially seeking a fee patent for Peters. The Department of the Interior, through Under Secretary Oscar L. Chapman, issued a favorable report on October 22, 1947, but recommended significant amendments. The Interior Department noted that the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751) required Crow Indians to retain one-half of their homestead lands (320 acres), making additional legislation necessary to permit sale of the full 840 acres. Critically, the Department opposed fee patenting to prevent sale to non-Indians, since the land lay in a "predominantly Indian-owned" area where non-Indian ownership would "hinder the administration of Indian lands" and "interfere with future land consolidation." Instead, Interior recommended restricting the sale to another Crow tribal member—specifically George Redfield, Peters's nephew—under existing regulations, with the land remaining non-taxable. The bill stalled in the Senate Subcommittee on Indian Affairs under Chairman Arthur V. Watkins and failed to pass the 80th Congress. Murray's July 8, 1948 letter to Peters acknowledged the failure, blaming a committee oversight. The documents reveal how federal trust restrictions and the 1920 Act's homestead retention requirements constrained individual Crow allottees' ability to dispose of their own lands, while the Interior Department simultaneously used consolidation policy to limit alienation options.

summaries.display_title: 1954–1955: Fee Patent Applications on Blackfeet and Crow Reservations
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1954–1955

This collection from the James E. Murray Papers (University of Montana Archives, Collection 91, Box 273) comprises fee patent application correspondence involving two separate cases on the Blackfeet and Crow Reservations during 1955, processed through Senator Murray's office.

The first case involves John J. Galbreath, Blackfeet Allottee No. 1809, seeking fee patents on lands acquired from multiple allottees: Minnie Dustybull (No. 1723), Gunwomanfornothing Mountainchief (No. 214), John Killsacrosstheway (No. 1958), and George Westwolf (No. 1102). Attorney J.J. McCaffery Jr. of Butte aggressively lobbied Senator Murray's administrative assistant Charles Murray to expedite these patents, explicitly noting the lands were "attractive to the big Oil Companies." McCaffery's October 7, 1955 letter alleged "skullduggery" in reserving minerals to the Tribe, calling surface-only patents "valueless." The Blackfeet Agency required Galbreath to settle $215.59 in indebtedness before processing, invoking the Act of May 8, 1906 (34 Stat. 182). One patent covered approximately 200 acres.

The second case involves Agnes Cummins Lobdell, Crow Allottee No. 3164, applying for fee patent on 1,040 acres (SE Sec. 11, SW Sec. 12, T.6S., R.36E.) valued at $12,000, with mineral rights reserved to the Crow Tribe. Superintendent Lippert denied the application February 25, 1955, finding Lobdell—a 46-year-old, three-quarter blood widow with a dependent grandchild—entirely dependent on $520 annual lease rental income. A third case involves Lawrence Flatmouth seeking fee patent on 120 acres of Crow Allotment No. 2119. These documents reveal how attorneys and political intermediaries pressured the BIA to convert trust lands to fee status, facilitating alienation despite agency determinations that patenting would leave allottees landless and destitute.

summaries.display_title: 1954–1955: Crow Allottee Fee Patent Applications and Supervised Land Sales
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1954–1955

This folder from the James E. Murray Papers (Collection 91, Series 1) at the University of Montana contains correspondence between U.S. Senator James E. Murray of Montana, the Bureau of Indian Affairs (BIA), the Bureau of Land Management, and Crow tribal members regarding fee patent applications, supervised land sales, and allotment dispositions on the Crow Reservation in 1954–1955.

Key cases include: Rose Bends (Crow Allottee No. 1001), whose petition to sell 80 acres (SW¼SE¼, SE¼SW¼, Sec. 27, T.3S., R.34E.) was denied by Acting Superintendent B.C. Schrock on May 17, 1955, because her husband William Bends had already sold 280 acres for $3,262 and had an additional 241.25-acre supervised sale pending, with total family proceeds exceeding $4,000. Pete Old Bull of Lodge Grass was denied a supervised sale by the Billings Area Director J.M. Cooper, who characterized him as dependent on his elderly mother and insufficiently self-supporting. Lester Jefferson sought a fee patent on inherited land from his father's Allotment No. 799; his application was denied because his expenditure plan was deemed unsound, despite his family holding approximately 19,283 acres under competent leases paid through November 1959. The Susan Farwell Glenn estate (Crow Allotment No. 285A) involved two supervised sales—239.99 acres to R. Rex Roberts for $10,027 and 480 acres to Robert B. Fraser, Jr. for $15,880—with patents 1149984, 1149983, 1149473, and 1149474 issued in early 1955. A February 1955 letter from Richard Callaghan criticized the BIA's "all or nothing" patenting policy forcing applicants to patent entire allotments. These documents demonstrate how BIA paternalism—through competency determinations, expenditure plan reviews, and supervised sale requirements—controlled Crow allottees' ability to access or dispose of their own land.

summaries.file_name: Murray fee patent applications and Kronmiller contract.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1953–1963: Crow and Fort Peck Fee Patent Applications and Kronmiller Attorney Contract Dispute
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1953–1963

This collection from the James E. Murray Papers (Collection No. 91, Series 1, Box 272, Mansfield Library, University of Montana) comprises correspondence, telegrams, and memoranda documenting Senator Murray's office facilitating fee patent applications for Native American allotments on the Crow and Fort Peck Reservations, along with a detailed conflict-of-interest complaint regarding attorney Bert Kronmiller's appointment as Crow Tribal counsel.

The fee patent correspondence involves multiple allottees: Blake Whitemanruns (Crow Allotment No. 1267), Edward Schenderline (Crow No. 1424), Kate W. Simons (Fort Peck No. 1067), Harry J. Simons (Fort Peck No. 2157), Esther S. Overland (Fort Peck No. 2526), Frederick Alden (Crow No. 1058, patent No. 1138364, 80 acres in Sec. 34, T.6S., R.32E., issued March 17, 1953), Joseph Mountain Pocket (Crow No. 3399, 718.82 acres comprising Lots 1-4, S½N½, S½ of Sec. 5, N½N½ Sec. 8, T.2S., R.38E.), and John Takes Gun (Allotments 490 and 3572/3573). Patents were issued pursuant to authority delegated by Secretarial Order No. 2252 (September 9, 1946) and Commissioner of Indian Affairs Order No. 551 (March 29, 1951). The Bureau of Land Management processed requests forwarded from the Bureau of Indian Affairs through the Billings Area Office under Area Director Paul L. Fickinger.

The Kronmiller contract documents allege that Kronmiller, retained at $3,600 per annum, had testified before Congress supporting Yellowtail Dam appropriations on behalf of the Hardin Chamber of Commerce—directly opposing the Crow Tribe's repeated refusal of $12 million for dam-related land purchases. The complaint further notes Kronmiller initiated taxation of Crow personal property while serving as Big Horn County Attorney. The BIA approved the contract September 16, 1953, with conflict-of-interest safeguards. These documents collectively reveal how fee patents converted trust allotments to alienable status—often driven by allottees' immediate cash needs for supervised land sales—while the Kronmiller episode illustrates structural conflicts undermining tribal legal representation during the Termination era.

summaries.file_name: Murray fee patent applications and all or nothing termination 1954.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1953–1954: Crow Allottee Fee Patent Applications and Termination Policy Concerns
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1953–1954

This folder from the James E. Murray Papers (Series I, Box 273) at the University of Montana's Mansfield Library documents fee patent applications by Crow Indian allottees processed through Senator Murray's office during 1953–1954. The documents include correspondence between Murray's office, the Bureau of Indian Affairs (Billings Area Office under Area Director Paul L. Fickinger), the Bureau of Land Management, and individual Crow allottees or their representatives, along with Western Union telegrams tracking patent issuance.

Specific fee patents documented include: DeWitt A. Dillon (Allotment 3175, Patents 1144248 and 1147813); Anna C. Dillon (Allotment 3176, Patent 1147776); Charles Dillon Jr. (Allotment 3177, Patent 1147775); Knows His Coos (Allotment 1646, Patent 1148213, 80.42 acres valued at $4,500); Walks Pretty (Allotment 1649, Patent 1148214); Wallace W. Pease (Allotments 68, 537, 828–831); Alex Medicine Horse (Allotment 3379); William A. Wall (Allotment 3211, 720 acres, Patent 1142148); Peter Whiteman (254.46 acres, Charges Plenty Whiteman deceased allottee); Cordelia Big Man (Allotment 938, Yellow All Over estate, 678.86 acres); and Nina Bill Rock (Allotment 1939). Property valuations included $4,500, $3,600, and $1,000 for individual allotments.

Patents were issued under Secretarial Order 2508 (January 11, 1949) and Commissioner's Order 551 (March 29, 1951), with irrigation liens reserved per the Acts of March 7, 1928, July 1, 1932, and June 4, 1920. Robert Yellowtail's March 1954 dictated letter protests Orme Lewis's December 15, 1953 order requiring allottees seeking partial fee patents to accept patents on all their lands—an "all or nothing" termination-era policy Yellowtail called "the height of foolishness." Murray expressed opposition to both the Flathead termination bill and this compulsory competency policy. The documents collectively demonstrate how fee patenting converted trust land to alienable status, removing federal protections and facilitating dispossession through sale, debt, and taxation.

summaries.file_name: Murray fee patent applications, land sales, Fort Peck.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1950–1959: Senator Murray Files on Fort Peck Reservation Land Sales and Fee Patents
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1950–1959

This collection from the James E. Murray papers (University of Montana Archives, Collection No. 91, Series I, Box 285) comprises correspondence, resolutions, memoranda, telegrams, and a newspaper clipping documenting land dispossession mechanisms on the Fort Peck Reservation involving the Assiniboine and Sioux Tribes. The documents fall into two major threads.

The first concerns supervised land sales of approximately 25,000 acres of allotted land. Resolution No. 297-5-9 (received March 10, 1959) lists specific tracts the Tribes wished to exclude from sale, identifying allottees by fee patent number, legal description, and acreage—including FP#1376 (May Growing Four Times, 320 acres), FP#2289 (Francis Welch, 320.37 acres), FP#3711 (Dorothy Jean Todd, 160 acres), and others. A May 4, 1959 Solicitor's memorandum (Franklin C. Salisbury) concluded there was no statutory authority for the Tribes to purchase land with tribal funds and hold it in trust, citing the Act of June 29, 1954 (68 Stat. 329) and the Act of May 19, 1947 (61 Stat. 102), effectively blocking tribal land consolidation while individual allotment sales proceeded.

The second thread details Bessie Brown (Allottee #2791), whose patent-in-fee application was denied by Area Director Paul Fickinger, forcing supervised sale of her NE/4, Section 23, T33N, R53E (160 acres) to Merlin Anderson for $2,300, with mineral rights reserved only for her lifetime or 51 years. Resolution No. 8-52 protested Fickinger's policy of limiting mineral reservations. Commissioner D.S. Myer's October 24, 1952 letter eventually reversed this policy but only after significant alienation had occurred. Additional documents detail BIA control over Sibley and Sarah Firemoon's oil lease monies (approximately $40,000 in bonuses), illustrating paternalistic fund management that restricted individual access to their own resources.

summaries.file_name: Murray fee patent applications, loan on trust patent 1956.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1955–1956: Senator Murray Casework on Crow Allottee Fee Patent and Trust Land Loan Applications
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1955–1956

This collection from the James E. Murray Papers (Collection 91, Series 1) at the University of Montana's Mansfield Library documents Senator Murray's constituent casework involving multiple Crow Reservation allottees seeking fee patents and loans on trust land during 1955–1956. The documents reveal the Bureau of Indian Affairs' "all-or-none" policy requiring applicants to include all solely-owned trust lands in fee patent applications, a mechanism that effectively discouraged individual allottees from converting portions of their holdings.

Key cases include: William Bends, sole heir of Yellow Blackbird (Crow Allottee No. 958), who applied March 20, 1956 for a fee patent covering 204.65 acres but was denied by Superintendent L.C. Lippert because he did not include all holdings; Becker Little Light (Crow Allottee No. 129), seeking a fee patent for 480 acres (S½N½, S½, Section 10, T9S, R36E, M.P.M.) while retaining other trust land for income; Peter Whiteman, heir of Charges Plenty Whiteman (Allottee No. 202), seeking a partial fee patent; Agnes Cummins McComas (née Lobdell), whose application was denied on competency grounds; Richard W. Bonko (Allottee No. 3087), whose application was approved July 31, 1956; and John L. Glenn, seeking a $5,000 mortgage on trust land from Orville Stovall Jr. to cancel an illegal farm lease, with land valued over $40,000 (allotments 2371 and 2544 referenced). Acting Commissioner W. Barton Greenwood's November 1, 1956 letter articulates the competency-based fee patent policy. The documents also reference the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), limiting individual acreage ownership on the Crow Reservation, and note that proposed amendatory legislation failed in Congress. These records demonstrate how bureaucratic gatekeeping—competency determinations, the all-or-none policy, and appraisal backlogs—constrained Crow allottees' control over their own land.

summaries.file_name: Murray fee patent, Stanton on jurisdiction at Lodge Grass and termination.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1957–1958: Senator Murray Correspondence on Crow Land Sales, Jurisdiction at Lodge Grass, and Termination
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1957–1958

This collection of correspondence from the papers of U.S. Senator James E. Murray (Montana) documents several interrelated issues concerning the Crow Indian Reservation in 1957–1958. The documents include letters between Murray, the Bureau of Indian Affairs, the Department of the Interior, private citizens, and local officials.

A central thread involves the attempted sale of Crow allotted lands. A May 5, 1958 letter from Assistant Secretary Roger Ernst to Murray confirms that the Crow Indian Agency superintendent had advertised numerous tracts of allotted land for competitive bidding, with bids to open May 13, 1958. At Murray's request, citing pending legislation S. 332, the sales were suspended. Thomas D. Campbell of Albuquerque, a large-scale lessee who claimed forty years of leasing history, lobbied Murray to halt the bid process, arguing it disadvantaged both Indian landowners and existing lessees, and that 95 percent of landowners had petitioned for oral auction procedures.

The case of Florence Bad Boy, Crow Allottee No. 631, illustrates the fee patent mechanism: her applications for patent in fee were twice denied (December 7, 1955 and September 20, 1956) based on her alleged lack of "business ability, knowledge, training and experience," despite her dependence on lease income from land farmed by the same white tenant for twenty years near Hardin. Similarly, Carl Crooked Arm's efforts to complete the sale of Iron Fork's allotment (Crow Allottee No. 1030) were stalled because heir Otto Crooked Arm died before consenting, invoking the Act of June 25, 1910 (36 Stat. 855).

A lengthy 1958 exchange between County Attorney Harold G. Stanton and BIA Special Officer John C. Corbett addresses criminal jurisdiction at Lodge Grass, with Stanton asserting Montana lacked jurisdiction over Indian juveniles within reservation boundaries, while Commissioner Glenn Emmons argued concurrent jurisdiction existed under 18 U.S.C. §§ 1151–1154 and Public Law 280 (67 Stat. 586). These documents collectively reveal how bureaucratic gatekeeping, jurisdictional ambiguity, and paternalistic competency determinations functioned as mechanisms of continued land dispossession and diminished tribal sovereignty.

summaries.display_title: 1950: Crow Indian Council Petition and Dispute Over Unit Grazing System
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1950–1950

This collection of correspondence, housed in the James E. Murray Papers (Collection No. 91, Series I, Box 272) at the University of Montana's Mansfield Library, documents a dispute over the unit grazing system on the Crow Indian Reservation in Montana during late 1950. The principal documents include a letter from Crow allottee Jasper Long of St. Xavier, Montana (October 7, 1950) to Senator Murray; a formal petition/resolution from the Crow Indian Council to the President of the United States; Senator Murray's forwarding letters to President Truman (November 20, 1950) and to BIA Commissioner Dillon S. Myer; a White House acknowledgment from Secretary Matthew J. Connelly (November 24, 1950); and a substantive BIA reply signed by Acting Commissioner H. Rex Lee (dated 1950).

The BIA letter invokes 25 CFR 71 (General Grazing Regulations) and references recent "Crow Competency legislation" that would place an estimated 85% of trust allotted lands in competent (fee patent) status. The Bureau justified continuing the unit permit system after a June 1950 Washington conference attended by Bureau representatives, stockmen lessees/permittees, and the Crow Tribal Council Chairman. Grazing bids ranged from $5.00 to $10.00 per head for cattle. Long's letter and the petition protest that Crow allottees were never consulted, that the Tribal Council Chairman was unauthorized to speak for allottees, that the unit system reduced competition and benefited large non-Indian stockmen, and that individual allottees should control leasing of their own lands. The documents demonstrate how federal administrative mechanisms—unit grazing permits, competency determinations, and BIA-managed conferences excluding allottees—effectively dispossessed individual Crow landowners of meaningful control over their grazing lands despite nominal trust protections.

summaries.file_name: Murray land sales and a little on Section 2 1960.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1941–1959: Senator Murray Files on Crow Reservation Land Sales and Allotment Act Amendments
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1941–1959

This collection of documents from the James E. Murray papers (Mansfield Library, University of Montana, Collection No. 91, Series I, Box 274) concerns Crow Reservation land sales, leasing policy, and proposed amendments to the Crow Allotment Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), as amended by the Act of March 15, 1948 (62 Stat. 80) and the Act of June 8, 1940 (54 Stat. 252).

The central legislative document is S. 51, introduced January 9, 1959, by Senators Murray, Mansfield, McGee, and O'Mahoney in the 86th Congress, which sought to repeal the acreage limitations in Section 2 of the 1920 Crow Act and validate all prior conveyances made in violation of those limitations. Jasper Long of Hardin, Montana, wrote Murray on February 3, 1959, warning that S. 51 conflicted with the Crow Allotment Act's provisions allowing tribal members to lease without superintendent approval, and urged preferential rights for Crow members in land transactions.

A January 15, 1959 letter from the Assistant Secretary of the Interior denied Murray's request to postpone a January 20 land sale at Crow Agency, citing no Indian opposition and a telegram from Crow Tribal Council Chairman Edward Whiteman supporting the sale. Murray's administrative assistant, Charles A. Murray, forwarded this denial to Joseph F. Meaglen of Billings, acknowledging that land sales had been delayed nearly a year but that Indian indifference undermined further postponement. Whiteman's January 14, 1949 telegram to Commissioner of Indian Affairs characterized opponents of the sale as non-Indian operators acting under the guise of the "Crow Leaseholders Association," asserting that competitive public bidding secured the highest prices. Earlier 1941 correspondence from attorney Fred G. Huntington concerned Alden and Reed oil leases on the Crow Reservation, with Murray's office confirming via Western Union that the superintendent held full authority over farming lease renewals. Collectively, these documents reveal how federal administrative discretion, tribal council politics, and non-Indian economic interests intersected to facilitate allotted land alienation despite legislative acreage safeguards.

summaries.file_name: Murray land sales, fee patent, resignation letter 1960.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1959–1960: Senator Murray Correspondence on Crow Reservation Land Sales, Fee Patents, and BIA Policy
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1959–1960

This folder from the James E. Murray papers (Collection No. 91, Series I, Box 274, Mansfield Library, University of Montana) contains correspondence between Senator Murray, the Department of the Interior, and Crow tribal members regarding Indian land sales, fee patents, and Bureau of Indian Affairs policy on the Crow Reservation.

The central documents include a May 7, 1959 Bureau of Indian Affairs policy letter from Associate Commissioner H. Rex Lee to Area Director Percy E. Melis in Billings, Montana, interpreting the Secretary of the Interior's authority under the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751) as amended by the Act of June 8, 1940 (54 Stat. 252). The letter addresses proposed sales of Crow allotments to Walter B. Green of Lodge Grass, George C. Lande Jr., and allotments belonging to Weien Pease Wolf, Orville Stovall Jr., and Florence Ileta Dailey. It establishes that transactions under the 1940 Act must transfer title in trust, overriding the Bureau's general policy requiring competent Indians to take title in fee simple. Lande acquired 160 acres in trust on August 4, 1959. A separate case involves William Bends, a 77-year-old Crow Indian (Allotment No. 1000), seeking a fee patent or supervised sale of 80 acres; the Superintendent conditioned approval on a programmed expenditure plan. Tribal member correspondence from Pryor, Montana (February 15, 1960) protests Bureau bidding policies that prevented members from matching non-member bids, citing one instance where a $10.00 underbid cost an Indian his land. Regulations at 25 CFR 121.13 and 121.18 governed these procedures. The documents collectively demonstrate how Bureau administrative requirements—programmed funds, fee patent denials, competitive bidding rules favoring non-Indians—functioned as mechanisms of land dispossession despite statutory frameworks ostensibly designed to consolidate Crow holdings.

summaries.file_name: Murray misc fee patent applications, Blackfeet lands.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1955–1960: Senator Murray Files on Land Tenure and Fee Patents at Blackfeet, Fort Belknap, and Crow Reservations
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1955–1960

This collection from the James E. Murray papers (Collection No. 91, University of Montana) comprises congressional bills, committee reports, tribal resolutions, correspondence between senators, attorneys, tribal officials, and Interior Department officials, and Western Union telegrams relating to land tenure and dispossession on three Montana reservations: Blackfeet, Fort Belknap, and Crow.

The core Blackfeet materials concern S. 1535 (introduced March 23, 1955, by Mansfield and Murray) and its companion H.R. 4606, enacted as Public Law 539 (84th Congress, signed May 22, 1956). The bill authorized fee or trust patents for twelve named Blackfeet Indians—including Wayne W. Welch (351.36 acres), Stephen Afterbuffalo (400 acres), Guy Williamson (313.66 acres), and others—who held exchange assignments totaling approximately 1,705 acres under Article VII, Section 6 of the tribal constitution. Attorney Arthur Lazarus Jr. (Riegelman, Strasser & Spiegelberg) drafted the bill per Tribal Resolutions 33-54 and 80-54, which reserved all mineral rights to the tribe. The Interior Department recommended competency-based issuance, granting fee patents only to those deemed able to manage affairs independently. Attorney Dan S. Welch (Cut Bank) characterized the exchange-assignment system as unjust enrichment of the tribe at individual allottees' expense.

S. 1745 authorized a fee patent for Nellie Ohlerking Archambeau Moran's 180-acre homestead (Allotment 645) on Fort Belknap, restricted under the Act of March 3, 1921 (41 Stat. 1355, 1358). The patent was mailed April 20, 1956.

S. 3698 (introduced April 23, 1956) repealed acreage limitations in Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), as amended June 8, 1940 (54 Stat. 252), which capped non-Indian purchases at 640 agricultural or 1,280 grazing acres. The Bureau discovered that over 35 years approximately 300,000 acres had been conveyed in violation, with individual holdings reaching 30,000 acres. The bill validated all prior conveyances. The Crow Reservation Association, a non-Indian landowner group, lobbied for passage. Crow Tribal Council Resolutions 77 and 77A (April 14, 1956) supported repeal. S. 3321 (1960) addressed fractionated heirship lands on the Crow Reservation, authorizing sale or lease of undivided interests with tribal purchase preferences.

Extensive 1957–1958 correspondence documents Blackfeet Tribal Council Resolution 51-57 protesting supervised land sales, predicting that within five years insufficient land would remain for Indian grazing units. Assistant Secretary Roger Ernst refused a moratorium, insisting individual property rights were paramount. The Association on American Indian Affairs and Senator Murray both called for a moratorium. On Fort Peck, approximately 80,000 acres were sold in 1957 alone. These documents collectively demonstrate how fee patenting, supervised sales, competency determinations, exchange-assignment mechanisms, and repeal of protective acreage limitations operated as interlocking legal instruments of Indian land dispossession across multiple Montana reservations during the termination era.

summaries.display_title: 1939–1942: Senator Murray Correspondence on Crow Reservation Petitions and WPA Administration
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1939–1942

This collection from the James E. Murray Papers (Collection 91, Box 271, University of Montana Archives) comprises correspondence, petitions, and telegrams involving Crow Reservation residents, Senator James E. Murray, the Bureau of Indian Affairs, and WPA administrators. The documents are heavily degraded, with many pages nearly illegible due to poor OCR of handwritten materials.

The most substantive legible documents include a petition from Crow tribal members at St. Xavier, Montana, addressed to the Secretary of the Interior, Senator Burton K. Wheeler, Senator Murray, and Representative James F. O'Connor, requesting expedited payment of M. H. Tschrigi lease rentals. The petitioners describe themselves as "destitute, and on a verge of starvation," noting no CCC-ID or WPA work available and that they had "pawned our possessions of value" while lease payments were withheld because other lessees failed to post required bonds—demonstrating how bureaucratic procedures governing grazing leases on allotted lands directly caused economic deprivation.

A July 1939 exchange between Murray, Minnie Williams (President, Crow Indian Women's Federation), and WPA State Administrator Joseph E. Parker reveals that Superintendent Robert Yellowtail cancelled WPA certifications for women workers, closing a sewing project employing 10 Crow women at $44.00 per 130-hour work period. An April 1939 letter from Assistant Commissioner William Zimmerman Jr. addresses Williams's objections to a Crow delegation traveling to Washington, noting concerns about peyote use among delegates. Yellowtail himself wrote Murray on February 13, 1939, requesting the Senator serve as witness against charges filed by Charles Guinn, H. W. Bunston, Russell Whitebear, and Max Big Man. These documents collectively illustrate how federal administrative control over lease payments, employment programs, and tribal governance perpetuated economic dependency on the Crow Reservation.

summaries.display_title: 1929–1960: Senator Murray Files on Crow Land Dispossession and Allotment Leasing Policy
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1929–1960

This collection from the James E. Murray papers (Series I, Box 274, Mansfield Library, University of Montana, Collection No. 91) contains correspondence between U.S. Senator Murray, Bureau of Indian Affairs officials, Crow tribal members, and non-Indian leaseholder interests during early 1960. The documents address two distinct cases of Crow Indian land and property dispossession.

The first involves Mary Ann Bear Below Horn of Lodge Grass, Montana, a 66-year-old Crow member, who wrote Murray on February 24, 1960, disputing a $101.85 claim (plus 6% interest from December 10, 1956) filed by Henry Hochhalter, formerly doing business as Hanks Standard Service of Hardin, in Yellowstone County District Court (Thirteenth Judicial District). The Horns acknowledged owing only $60 total but faced inflated claims including court costs. Crow Agency Superintendent Hobbes reported on April 6, 1960, that the court had already recorded findings, leaving the family with no apparent remedy.

The second case concerns Aline Bull In Sight's effort to quiet title to Lots 4, 5, and 6, Block 3, Prando Addition, St. Xavier, Montana, on the Crow Reservation—property of Eagle Turns, a non-competent Crow Indian. In 1933, $400 from Eagle Turns' trust allotment sale purchased a warranty deed from Albert Thomas and Florence O. Thomas through Superintendent James I. Hyde. The land was sold for taxes on January 15, 1929, to Big Horn County; a tax deed issued in 1945 was conveyed to Olive Cox of Hardin. The Deputy Solicitor's March 31, 1960 memorandum concluded the tax deed was invalid for failure to provide statutory notice and that Eagle Turns' title was restricted Indian land. The matter was referred to the Department of Justice Lands Division for quiet title action, though Washington had been unresponsive since June 1958. Heirs identified included John Bullinsight Jr., Elizabeth Bullinsight, Mary Bullinsight (1/6 each), and Fred Home Bear and Naomi Foolish Bear (1/4 each).

Separately, the Crow Leaseholders Association, chaired by Jess J. Blankenship of Hardin, requested attorney J.F. Meglen monitor all Indian legislation, citing S. 55 (amending the 1920 Allotment Act), S. 51, and H.R. 6508 (minerals and oil/gas on Crow lands). These documents collectively demonstrate how bureaucratic delay, exploitative commercial claims, and defective tax sale procedures dispossessed Crow individuals of property and resources.

summaries.file_name: Murray patent in fee 1948 and one competency letter.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1949: Crow Allottee Jeanette Pearl Burns Fee Patent Application Correspondence
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1949–1949

This document comprises correspondence from the James E. Murray manuscript collection (Collection No. 91, Series I, Box 272) at the University of Montana's Mansfield Library. The materials relate to Jeanette Pearl Burns's effort to obtain a patent in fee for a portion of her allotment on the Crow Reservation, Montana, comprising 299.08 acres. The central letter, dated April 13, 1949, from Assistant Commissioner John H. Provinse to Senator James E. Murray (file reference 7625-49), acknowledges Murray's April 5 letter and enclosures regarding Burns's desire to sell part of her allotment. H.R. 3886, introduced by Congressman D'Ewart, would have authorized the Secretary of the Interior to issue the fee patent. The Bureau asked Superintendent Lippert at Crow Agency to furnish a report on the land before rendering a decision on the bill.

An earlier letter (reference 278049, dated March 21, 1949) from the Commissioner to Minnie F. Burns at Crow Agency reveals that Superintendent Lippert had denied the fee patent request on January 26, stating the land lay in an area predominantly in trust status and that fee patenting would interfere with administration of surrounding trust lands. The Bureau instead suggested Jeanette sell to another Crow Indian. A separate letter from attorney A.F. Lamey (April 11, 1949) to Murray praises Murray's staff assistance to Crow Tribal Council representatives, including Francis LaForge and Yellowtail, regarding competency procedures. Together, these documents illustrate how administrative gatekeeping—trust-status arguments and competency determinations—controlled individual allottees' ability to alienate or monetize their land, effectively perpetuating federal control over Crow allotments.

summaries.display_title: 1950: Crow Allottee Edna Birdintheground Allery Fee Patent Application Correspondence
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1950–1950

This document consists of correspondence from the James E. Murray manuscript collection (Collection No. 91, Series I, Box 272) held at the Mansfield Library, University of Montana Archives. The exchange involves Mrs. Edna Frances Birdintheground Allery of Malta, Montana, and U.S. Senator James E. Murray regarding her effort to obtain a patent in fee on her original allotment, likely on the Crow Reservation or another Montana reservation.

In her letter of December 20, 1950, Allery explains that she wishes to dispose of her original allotment—described as grazing land unsuitable for farming—in order to purchase a Chippewa Indian allotment near Belcourt, North Dakota, where she could build a home and engage in farming and cattle raising. She reports consulting with Mr. Fickenger, Area Director at Billings, Montana, and Mr. Belzel, Superintendent of Belcourt Consolidated Agency, who informed her that federal regulations did not permit direct exchange of allotments between different reservations. L. C. Lippert, Superintendent of the Crow Indian Agency, advised her that congressional legislation would be needed to secure the patent in fee.

Murray's reply of December 27, 1950, suggests she first apply for a patent in fee through the Indian Bureau; if rejected, a bill could then be introduced in Congress, following usual procedure.

The document illustrates how restrictive federal trust mechanisms forced individual allottees into complex bureaucratic and legislative processes simply to control their own land, effectively facilitating dispossession by making alienation contingent on federal approval. Much of the OCR text on pages 2–6 is severely degraded and illegible.

summaries.display_title: 1951: Felix Cohen and Senator Murray Correspondence on Crow Allottee Fee Patent Applications
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1951–1951

This document collection from the James E. Murray papers (Series 11, 82nd Congress) comprises legislative bills, committee correspondence, Department of Interior reports, and attorney correspondence relating to patent-in-fee applications for Crow Reservation allotments in Montana. On May 26, 1951, attorney Felix S. Cohen wrote Senator Murray on behalf of two Crow Indian allottees—Mrs. Ursula Rutherford Ollinger and Mrs. Mary Rutherford Spearson, both residing on the Blackfeet Reservation at Heart Butte, Montana—requesting legislation to convert their restricted trust allotments to fee simple patents. S. 1567 authorized a patent in fee for Ollinger's Allotment No. 3500, containing 1,016.21 acres in Township 2 South, Range 31 East, Montana principal meridian. S. 1568 covered Spearson's Allotment No. 3499, containing 1,041.92 acres in the same township and range. Both bills were introduced May 31, 1951, by Senator O'Mahoney for Murray, passed the House August 21, 1951, and received favorable Interior Department reports dated July 6, 1951, signed by Assistant Secretary Dale E. Doty. The Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751) restricted Crow allottees from selling more than 320 acres of homestead land, necessitating congressional action. The Interior Department confirmed both women were "qualified to conduct her affairs without governmental supervision" and that their lands were "located in an area of the reservation not used by Indians." Crow Superintendent Lorenz C. Lippert endorsed the applications. Cohen framed the dispositions as beneficial—providing cash for the sisters, relieving federal management burdens, and making lands taxable by Montana—illustrating how fee patenting served as a primary mechanism for removing Crow lands from trust status and tribal use.

summaries.display_title: 1955–1956: Senator Murray Files on Crow Allottee Fee Patent Applications and Supervised Land Sales
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1955–1956

This folder from the James E. Murray papers (Collection 91, Series I, Box 273, University of Montana Archives) contains correspondence between U.S. Senator James E. Murray of Montana, the Bureau of Indian Affairs, and Crow tribal members regarding patent in fee applications and supervised land sales on the Crow Reservation during 1955–1956.

The central case involves Francis Stewart, Jr., Crow allottee No. 3556 (also referenced as No. 3552), who sought either a supervised sale or patent in fee for 399.80 acres of allotted land in Sections 34–35, T7S, R37E, and Section 2, T8S, R37E, M.P.M. Stewart's June 1955 supervised sale application languished at Crow Agency; his February 13, 1956 patent in fee application was denied by Superintendent Lorenz C. Lippert, who held authority delegated under the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751). That statute declared invalid sales to persons owning more than 640 acres of agricultural land or 1,280 acres of rangeland on the reservation. Acting Commissioner W. Barton Greenwood's May 11, 1956 letter and Acting Area Director M. A. Johnson's May 18, 1956 letter detail the competency determination process required for fee patents. Additional cases include Harriet Walks Over Ice (allotment No. 3601), whose patent was issued March 12, 1956 (Patent No. 1157934); Raymond Howe (allotments 328 and 3285), receiving patents No. 1157599 and 1157639; and Richard Watt Bonko (allottee No. 3087), whose application covered 1,038.92 acres but was delayed pending physical appraisal. A clipped Billings Gazette article documents the 1956 Crow Tribal Council election and opposition to proposed legislation authorizing real estate mortgages on allotments, which members feared would dispossess Indians of their remaining land base. The file also includes BIA Form 5-853a, a reference letter for educational loans containing questions critics deemed demeaning to Indians. Together, these documents reveal how bureaucratic delays, competency determinations, and restrictive statutory frameworks functioned as mechanisms of land dispossession on the Crow Reservation.

summaries.display_title: 1957–1958: Senator Murray Correspondence on Crow Reservation Fee Patent Applications and Land Sales
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1957–1958

This collection of correspondence from the James E. Murray papers (Collection No. 91, Series I, Box 274, Mansfield Library, University of Montana) documents patent in fee applications and land sales involving Crow Reservation allottees in 1957–1958. The documents consist of letters between Senator Murray, the Bureau of Indian Affairs, the Department of the Interior, and individual Crow tribal members.

Two principal cases are documented. First, Beady Anderson Bad Boy sought Senator Murray's help regarding the stalled sale of his Allotment 1552 (80 acres) to Callie T. Kennedy of Sheridan, Wyoming. The sale, conducted at the December 11, 1957 Crow Indian Land Sale, was held up because Kennedy's association with the Paddis-Kennedy Cattle Company raised potential violations of the acreage limitations under Section 2 of the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751). Assistant Secretary Roger Ernst informed Murray on October 15, 1958, that the sale could proceed if Kennedy executed an affidavit prescribed by the Field Solicitor.

Second, William Bends (Crow Allottee 1000) applied for patent in fee on 160 inherited acres within Allotment 957, located approximately two miles southwest of Crow Agency. Bends had previously received fee patents in September 1956 (204 acres) and July 1957 (402 acres). Area Director Percy E. Melis noted Bends was a "borderline case" due to age and health. Fee Patent No. 1181752 was ultimately issued July 11, 1958. A third case involves Albert Iron (Allotment 1859), whose patent in fee application was converted to a supervised sale after the agency cited his limited education, lack of business training, and serious heart condition, with the sale held pending a moratorium on Indian land sales. These records demonstrate how bureaucratic delays, competency determinations, and the 1920 Act's acreage limitations functioned as mechanisms controlling—and often facilitating—Crow land alienation.

summaries.file_name: Murray patent in fee applications and James Stanton letter re patents in fee.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1951–1952: Senator Murray Files on Crow Allottee Fee Patent Applications and BIA Policy
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1951–1952

This collection from the James E. Murray papers (University of Montana, Collection No. 91, Series I, Box 272) documents correspondence between U.S. Senator James E. Murray, the Bureau of Indian Affairs, the Bureau of Land Management, and Crow Reservation allottees regarding patent in fee applications. The primary case involves William Bends, holder of Crow Allotment No. 1000, who sought a patent in fee for 80 acres described as Lot 3, SE/4 NW/4, Sec. 1, T. 4S., R. 34E., M.P.M., Montana. Murray wrote to Bends on multiple occasions between August 1951 and April 1952, tracking the application through bureaucratic delays. Area Director Paul L. Fickinger approved the application under Secretarial Order No. 2252 (September 9, 1946; 11 F.R. 10296) and Commissioner's Order No. 551 (March 29, 1951; 16 F.R. 65), reserving minerals for the Crow Tribe per the Crow Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751). A parallel case involved Francis LaForge, Allotment No. 1256, for 80 acres in Sec. 13, T. 6S., R. 34E. Bends also sought supervised sale of approximately 280 acres, which would exceed the statutory half-allotment sale restriction requiring legislation. Critically, James W. Stanton, Secretary-Manager of the Reservation Ranchers and Farmers Cooperative Association (132 non-Indian grass lessees), wrote Murray on January 11, 1951, alarmed by rumors of wholesale restriction removal. The BIA responded that patents in fee were issued only upon individual application after competency determination, protecting existing leases. The documents reveal the administrative machinery converting trust land to alienable fee land, facilitating dispossession parcel by parcel.

summaries.file_name: Murray patent in fee applications, Cohen as Blackfeet lawyer.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1949–1953: Senator Murray Bills and Correspondence on Crow Fee Patent Applications and Blackfeet Land Issues
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1949–1953

This collection from the James E. Murray papers (University of Montana Archives, Collection 91) comprises congressional bills, correspondence, Department of Interior reports, and a Western Union telegram relating to Crow Reservation patent-in-fee applications and Blackfeet tribal land issues during the 82nd and 83rd Congresses.

Three patent-in-fee bills were introduced by Senator Murray on January 7, 1953: S. 161 for Wesley Bear Ground (Crow allottee No. 1243, 160 acres of irrigable land); S. 163 for Olive Old Bull Comes In A Day (daughter of deceased allottee No. 1520, 333.13 acres of grazing land appraised at $3,300); and S. 165 for Amy Old Bull (granddaughter of allottee No. 1520, 280 acres of grazing land). The Interior Department favorably reported S. 161, noting Bear Ground was competent and that Section 13 of the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751) otherwise prohibited alienation of more than half of irrigable Crow allotments. However, Interior issued adverse reports on S. 163 and S. 165, deeming both women incompetent—Old Bull Comes In A Day earned only $850 annually, and Amy Old Bull $298—recommending supervised sales instead. A June 1952 letter from rancher H.H. Jones of Kirby, Montana, reveals a non-Indian buyer waiting to purchase these lands immediately upon patent issuance, illustrating how fee patents facilitated transfer to white ranchers. Separately, an August 1949 telegram documents the Blackfeet Tribal Council engaging Felix S. Cohen as attorney, and a March 1950 Cohen letter advocates S. 331 to place 9,037 acres of submarginal lands in trust for the Blackfeet, purchased under the National Industrial Recovery Act (48 Stat. 200) and transferred by Executive Order 7868 (April 15, 1938). Interior favorably reported this bill in June 1951. Together, these documents demonstrate the dual mechanisms of dispossession—fee patents converting trust land to alienable status for individual Crow allottees while simultaneously requiring legislative action to secure promised lands for the Blackfeet.

summaries.file_name: Murray removing Hardin from reservation to sell beer.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1935: Federal Effort to Remove Hardin, Montana from Crow Reservation and Enforce Indian Liquor Laws
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1935–1935

This document consists of correspondence and telegrams from the James E. Murray papers (Collection 91, Series 11, 74th–75th Congress) at the University of Montana's Mansfield Library, concerning federal efforts to close the Montana State Liquor Store and shut down beer vendors in Hardin, Montana, on grounds that the city lay within the boundaries of the Crow Indian Reservation and was therefore subject to federal Indian liquor laws.

The key letter, dated December 28, 1935, was written by Big Horn County Attorney Charles C. Guinn (with deputy Dan W. Maddox) to Elmer C. Anderson, Secretary-Treasurer of the Sheridan Brewing Company in Sheridan, Wyoming. Guinn argued that Hardin, incorporated in 1907 with approximately 1,500 residents and no Indian residents, sat on land purchased by the Lincoln Townsite Company from heirs of deceased Indians with Interior Department approval. He contended that all lands between Hardin and the reservation's west and north boundaries had been alienated to white owners, placing the "real boundary line" of the Crow Reservation wholly outside the city. Guinn urged amendment of the Act of June 27, 1934 (48 Stat. 1245, paragraph 254, Title 25 U.S.C.A.), which exempted former Indian lands outside existing reservations from special Indian liquor laws only where land was no longer held under trust or restricted patents.

Two telegrams from Montana Attorney General Raymond T. Nagle to Senator Murray describe Indian Department agent Gordon E. Johnson threatening seizure of liquor stock and arrest if the store was not removed by Friday, December 6, 1935. Nagle requested Murray intervene with the Indian Department to allow time for investigation. The documents reveal how townsite purchases and surrounding allotment alienation were leveraged to argue that dispossessed Crow lands should no longer carry federal Indian country protections, effectively using prior land dispossession as justification for further erosion of tribal sovereignty and jurisdictional authority over the reservation.

summaries.display_title: 1952–1953: Northern Cheyenne Agency Coal Mine Operations and Administrative Records
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1952–1953

This document collection comprises administrative records from the Northern Cheyenne Agency at Lame Deer, Montana, relating to the operation of the Northern Cheyenne Agency Coal Mine (also known as the Alderson Gulch Coal Mine) during 1952–1953. The core document is Contract No. 14-20-257-1, executed in 1952 between L. C. Sharp (operator, party of the first part) and Capt. L. Pearson, Superintendent of the Northern Cheyenne Agency (party of the second part), for coal mining operations on allotted and tribal subsurface lands. The mine encompassed specific parcels: the SE¼ of Section 25, Section 36, and E½NE¼NE¼ of Section 36, all in Township 2 South, Range 41 East, Montana Principal Meridian, with subsurface title held by the Northern Cheyenne Tribe.

A July 8, 1953 letter from L.B. Picotte, Administrative Assistant, to the U.S. Geological Survey in Billings reports total output from September 8, 1952 through June 30, 1953: Sharp mined 2,755½ tons under the contract, paid at tiered rates—281 tons at $2.00/ton, 45 tons at $1.75/ton, and 2,429½ tons at $4.50/ton. The agency remitted $551.10 less $17.50 (for coal delivered to tribal facilities) as royalty to the tribe. A separate Purchase Order No. 52-202 dated September 17, 1952, charged against appropriation 147452.1 (Deposited Proceeds of Labor, Crow Indians, Montana), transferred 8 tons of lump coal at $5.00/ton ($40.00) to the Northern Cheyenne Agency. The advertisement, approved August 22, 1952 by Area Director Paul L. Fickinger, solicited bids with stipulations including Indian hiring preference, U.S. Geological Survey regulatory compliance, surety bonding, Montana Workmen's Compensation compliance, Fair Employment Practices clauses, and a five-year contract term (September 1, 1952–August 31, 1957) with minimum 500-ton monthly production. These records illustrate how federal administrative mechanisms controlled tribal mineral resources, with the BIA managing extraction, setting prices, and directing minimal royalty payments to the tribe while cross-charging Crow Indian deposited proceeds for Northern Cheyenne operations.

summaries.display_title: 1953–1956: BIA Files on Land Sales, Leasing, Mineral Rights, and Credit Policy at Crow and Northern Cheyenne Reservations
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1953–1956

This folder contains a heterogeneous collection of Bureau of Indian Affairs documents primarily concerning land sales, leasing, mineral rights, and credit policy on the Crow and Northern Cheyenne Reservations in Montana, administered through the Billings Area Office. The core documents include a handwritten appraisal report for approximately 162 acres across three Northern Cheyenne allotments (Nos. 602, 603, 604) in Township 2 South, Range 41 East, near the Lame Deer townsite, with land classified into multiple categories (bottomland, dryland, residential/industrial sites) and valued at approximately $39,627 total. An August 17, 1955 telegram from Acting Area Director M. A. Johnson to the Commissioner requests authority for a 15-day advertising period to sell these allotments—owned by "non-competent Northern Cheyenne Indians"—to a sawmill operator seeking plant expansion, who employed 15-30 Indians and intended to bid on 10 million feet of reservation timber. Washington authorized the abbreviated sale period via teletype on August 24, 1955, instructing discretion on acreage sold and requiring the tribe be given opportunity to meet the high bid.

Additional documents include a Crow Reservation land sale advertisement listing 31 allotments totaling thousands of acres with mineral rights (coal, oil, gas) reserved to the Crow Tribe under competent leases; extensive Federal Register amendments to 25 CFR Parts 171, 186, 189, 192, and 195 governing leasing, mineral royalties, and rights-of-way; Bureau Order 551 amendments delegating authority over mineral leases; a detailed Commissioner's letter on revolving credit fund policy acknowledging that colonists were settled on 100% credit with inadequate planning; and a Solicitor's decision (Hansen/Dosdall appeal) holding that superintendents lacked authority to grant existing lessees preference rights to meet high bids after the 1948 regulatory revision eliminated that provision. Together these documents demonstrate systematic mechanisms—abbreviated advertising periods, appraisals at "highest and best use" for commercial development, sales of non-competent Indians' trust lands without meaningful consent, and mineral reservations benefiting tribal entities while surface rights were alienated—that facilitated dispossession of individual Indian landholders on both reservations.

summaries.display_title: 1956: Northern Cheyenne Agency File on Electric Line Relocation Along Ashland North Road
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1956–1956

This document is a multi-part administrative file from the Northern Cheyenne Agency (Bureau of Indian Affairs Field Service) at Lame Deer, Montana, concerning the relocation of electric distribution line poles along the Ashland North Road, Project No. 2, on or near the Northern Cheyenne Reservation. The principal letter, dated December 13, 1956, is from Superintendent Carl L. Pearson to the Area Director at the Billings Area Office, addressing procedural questions raised by Area Road Engineer Van Alstyne about a pole-moving agreement with the Tongue River Electric Cooperative (REA), managed by Eugene Morgan at Ashland. The agreement, dated the "1st day of" an unspecified month, obligated the Bureau to pay $928.23 (against an approved work order of $1,000, Work Order No. 257-6-143) for relocating poles Nos. 11-29 through 11-35, pole No. 11-47-R2, and transmission poles Nos. 271 and 272 outside the 50-foot line of the 100-foot road right-of-way. Road construction had proceeded before the agreement was formally signed, creating administrative complications involving the Comptroller General. The file includes certified invoices (No. 9619), detailed material and labor cost breakdowns ($411.11 in labor, equipment charges at $2.00/hour for 120 hours), and an itemized inventory of poles, insulators, hardware, and conductor used and retired. The document illustrates how federal road projects on reservation lands required securing rights-of-way that displaced existing infrastructure, with the BIA controlling both the approval process and payment mechanisms—demonstrating the administrative apparatus through which reservation lands were physically altered for federal transportation priorities, with delays attributed partly to former cooperative manager Orville Gentry's departure with relevant papers.

summaries.display_title: 1955–1956: BIA Billings Area Office Administrative Directives for Montana Agencies
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1955–1956
