This collection consists of internal Bureau of Indian Affairs (BIA) administrative memoranda and procedural directives originating from the Billings Area Office (804 North 29th Street, Billings, Montana) and the BIA Washington, D.C. central office. The documents span from April 14, 1955 to November 23, 1956 and address bureaucratic operations across multiple agencies including Blackfeet, Crow, Fort Belknap, Fort Peck, Northern Cheyenne, and Wind River.

The most substantive document regarding land dispossession is a November 15, 1956 memorandum from the Office of the Area Director to superintendents at six agencies concerning applications for fee patents. It instructs agencies to report whether lands being patented are under approved oil and gas leases, and to provide sufficient notice to lessees of forthcoming patent issuance. This reveals the active mechanism by which trust land was converted to fee simple status—a key instrument of dispossession, as fee-patented land became alienable and taxable, frequently resulting in loss of Indian ownership. The memorandum's concern with coordinating patent issuance with oil and gas lease rental dates suggests the volume and routine nature of this conversion process.

Additional documents include an April 1955 memorandum from the Acting Chief, Branch of Realty, requesting preliminary oil and gas and mining development reports (number of leases approved, total acreage leased, bonus, royalty, and rental income), and a blank Form 5-110c "Appraisal Accomplishment Report" tracking patents in fee, sales, exchanges, partitions, leases, rights-of-way, and easements by number, acreage, and valuation. The obsolete forms list references Branch of Realty forms including 5-110 (Petition for Sale of Land by Original Allottees), 5-110e (Report on Cash Sale of Allotted Indian Land When Patent in Fee Is to Be Issued to Purchaser), and 5-110j (Petition for Sale of Inherited Indian Land)—documenting the bureaucratic infrastructure facilitating systematic alienation of allotted Indian lands. OCR quality is poor throughout, with significant character degradation on several pages.

summaries.display_title: 1950–1956: BIA Files on Turtle Mountain Chippewa Public Domain Allotments in Carter County, Montana
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1950–1956

This folder contains Bureau of Indian Affairs inter-agency correspondence, ownership statements, land inspection reports, fee patent applications, petitions for sale, a deed, and mining lease reports concerning five Turtle Mountain Chippewa public domain allotments located in Carter County, Montana (Townships 3S and 4S, Range 58E, M.P.M.), administered by the Northern Cheyenne Agency at Lame Deer. The allotments were made under the Act of April 21, 1904 (33 Stat. 189–194), with trust patents issued April 8, 1916. The five tracts belong to Alexander Emil Gourneau (MC-026695, 160 acres, NW/4 Sec. 22), Louis Decoteau (MC-026507, 160 acres, NE/4 Sec. 8), Eliza Peltier (MC-026534, 160 acres, NE/4 Sec. 20), Alexander Decoteau Jr. (MC-026515, 160 acres, NW/4 Sec. 11), and Pierre Nadeau (MC-026540, 40 acres, SE/4 NE/4 Sec. 22). Superintendent Carl L. Pearson's September 1953 inspection report appraised these grazing lands at $2.00–$3.25 per acre, noting alkali soils, sparse forage, and chronic trespass by surrounding ranchers—specifically Ben Brownfield of Ridgeway and a Major of the same area—who fenced and used the allotments without leases or at nominal rents (ten cents per acre). Pearson recommended advertised supervised sales with retention of one-half sub-surface mineral rights, concluding that distance and rancher control made administration nearly impossible. Fee patents were processed for Florence Peltier Vooke (MC-026746) and St. Ann Gourneau Albert (MC-026696) in 1953. Pierre Nadeau, age 61, 1/2 blood, filed a petition for sale of his 40 acres on May 4, 1955, requesting unrestricted proceeds. The documents collectively demonstrate how geographic isolation, surrounding non-Indian land consolidation, and inadequate BIA enforcement rendered allotments economically useless to their Indian owners, effectively compelling alienation through fee patents or supervised sales.

summaries.display_title: 1953–1956: BIA Billings Area Office Appraisal Policy and Land Conveyance Procedures
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1953–1956

This collection comprises Bureau of Indian Affairs memoranda, policy directives, appraisal forms, and sample appraisal reports originating from the Billings Area Office (804 North 29th Street, Billings, Montana) and the Washington, D.C. central office. The documents establish the administrative framework through which Indian trust lands were appraised and conveyed during the mid-1950s termination era.

A key memorandum dated August 10, 1956, from the Area Realty Officer to the Superintendent of the Northern Cheyenne Agency, approves appraisal reports for six Fisher family allotments (Calleaus Fisher, Allot. #328; Langburn Fisher, #325; Kitty Belle Fisher, #323; Eugene Fisher, #322; Eugene Fisher Jr., #324; James Allen Fisher, #326), signed by Britton Clair. A June 1956 staff report by soil scientist John A. Elrod documents soil survey and land capability classification of "the Old Fisher Ranch" meadows, with data prepared for the Agency Realty Office to facilitate land transactions. The report was approved by Clyde W. Hobbs and Area Director I.A. Johnson.

The collection's bulk consists of the January 4, 1955 Area Director memorandum to all superintendents mandating new appraisal procedures following Secretary of the Interior criticism, citing Indian Affairs Manual Release No. 240 (approved June 29, 1955), Section 205.01, 25 CFR Part IV. A hypothetical appraisal of Crow Allotment No. 490 (John B. Doe/Heir of Looks At The Bear, S½ Section 17, T.1S., R.28E., 320 acres of dry range land) demonstrates the methodology, valuing Class VI land at $15/acre and Class VII at $12/acre, with comparable sales including Crow allottee #1815 (Old Alligator heirs, 600 acres at $13.15/acre to John Herren, July 1954) and allottee #349 (James Robinson heirs, 650.68 acres at $12/acre to Donald P. Blake, February 1954)—both with minerals reserved to the Crow Tribe. An irrigation statement form references the Act of March 1, 1928 (45 Stat. 210, 25 USC 387). Area Director approval authority was capped at $25,000; transactions exceeding that required Commissioner approval. The documents collectively reveal a bureaucratic apparatus that standardized and expedited alienation of Indian trust lands through systematic appraisal, advertising for public sale, and fee patent conveyance, while the appraisal standards themselves acknowledged that land values were being depressed by permit/lease structures controlled by non-Indian operators with stock water access.

summaries.display_title: 1953–1956: BIA Northern Cheyenne Agency Files on Mineral Permits, Land Status, and Appraisals
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1953–1956

This collection comprises Bureau of Indian Affairs administrative correspondence and reporting forms related to mineral permits, land status reporting, and appraisal requests for the Northern Cheyenne Reservation at Lame Deer, Montana. The principal author is Superintendent Carl L. Pearson, corresponding with Paul L. Fickinger (Area Director) and Britton Clair (Area Realty Officer) at the Billings Area Office, as well as J. R. Lerwill, Regional Mining Supervisor for the U.S. Geological Survey.

The documents reveal a coal mining agreement between the Northern Cheyenne tribe and an unspecified party covering 10 acres (SE¼NW¼ SE¼, Sec. 1, T.35, R.41E), producing 2,690 tons during the 1953–54 season at a royalty of $0.20 per ton, yielding $538.00 in total revenue, with an annual rental of $50.00. Oil and gas leasing activity was nonexistent during this period.

A May 1955 memorandum from Clair rejects the agency's annual land report (Form 5-153) as "unacceptable," noting a total reservation acreage of 433,298 acres. The correction identifies that 120 acres of Allotment #1163, for which a fee patent was issued to Nelson Tallbull on May 7, 1954, was improperly included in fiscal year 1955 disposals. The memorandum also notes unaccounted-for public domain allotments originally patented through the Miles City General Land Office, and references prior dispositions totaling 959.08 acres within and 113.34 acres outside the reservation. These records demonstrate the bureaucratic mechanisms—fee patents, certificates of competency, supervised sales, and removal of restrictions—through which individually allotted lands were systematically transferred out of trust status and BIA jurisdiction.

summaries.display_title: 1954–1956: BIA Billings Area Office Staffing Rosters and Appraisal Reporting Forms
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1954–1956

This collection comprises Bureau of Indian Affairs administrative documents from the Billings Area Office relating to the Northern Cheyenne Reservation and other Montana agencies. The materials include BIA Form 5-110c instructions and blank forms for quarterly appraisal accomplishment reports (August 1956); staffing rosters for the Billings Area Office, Crow Indian Agency, Blackfeet Agency, Flathead Agency, Fort Belknap Agency, Northern Cheyenne Agency, and Fort Peck Agency listing personnel by name, GS grade, and position in the Branch of Realty's Land Management section (code 1861); and operational memoranda on oil, gas, and mining lease reporting.

A critical document is the June 11, 1954 legal opinion from the law firm Wilkinson, Boyden, Cragun & Marks to Northern Cheyenne Tribal President Eugene Fisher, analyzing tribal authority to prevent allotment sales. It cites the Act of June 3, 1926 (44 Stat. 691), which reserved subsurface minerals to the tribe for 50 years, and the General Allotment Act (February 8, 1887, 24 Stat. 388), along with 25 C.F.R. Sections 241.2 and 241.4 governing fee patents. The opinion confirmed the tribe could recommend against fee patents but could not legally block them.

Resolution 93 of the Northern Cheyenne Tribal Council, adopted July 1954 by 13 affirmative votes, petitioned the Commissioner of Indian Affairs to investigate the consequences of land alienation and delay approval of fee patents and advertised sales pending investigation. The resolution noted the reservation had remained a solid block with no alienated land except the 90-acre Lame Deer townsite. A July 20, 1956 memorandum corrected acreage figures: tribal acreage of 214,769.17 acres (less 5,455.08 leased, yielding 209,314.09 net), and total allotted acreage of 229,249.57 (including 840.00 Turtle Mountain allotments). Coal lease data showed 120 acres leased, $1,205.00 in royalties, and $120.00 annual rental, with 5,625 tons produced. These documents collectively illustrate the bureaucratic machinery—appraisals, fee patents, supervised sales, and mineral leasing—through which allotted Indian land was systematically moved toward alienation, and the Northern Cheyenne Tribe's organized but legally constrained resistance to that process.

summaries.display_title: 1952–1956: BIA Billings Area Office Administrative Records on Land, Irrigation, and Grazing
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1952–1956

This collection comprises Bureau of Indian Affairs (BIA) internal memoranda, policy directives, manual releases, press releases, and committee reports from the Billings Area Office and Washington headquarters, primarily addressing fee patent issuance, land sales, rights-of-way, irrigation assessments, conservation operations, and grazing permit administration on Indian reservations. The OCR quality is poor throughout, with significant text degradation on several pages.

The documents reveal the administrative machinery of Indian land dispossession during the termination era. A July 13, 1955 press release announced "liberalized" fee patent policy removing trust restrictions from competent Indians' lands upon application, reversing a prior policy that had blocked fee patents when land lay within timber or grazing management units. Commissioner Glenn L. Emmons's June 13, 1955 letter to Billings Area Director John M. Cooper discusses the "all-or-none" policy requiring competent Indians to accept fee patents on all their trust holdings simultaneously—designed to stop "piece-meal" patenting by Indians who purchased trust lands at appraised value and later obtained fee patents to sell at profit. The letter references a February 29, 1952 policy statement and a June 17, 1953 Assistant Secretary memorandum.

A January 4, 1956 memorandum directs compliance with the Act of March 7, 1928 (45 Stat. 210) and 25 CFR 151, requiring collection of unpaid irrigation construction charges as first liens against irrigable Indian lands before patent or sale. Manual Release No. 242 (August 2, 1955) revised sale procedures under multiple statutes including the acts of May 27, 1902 (32 Stat. 275), March 1, 1907 (34 Stat. 1018), and June 25, 1910 (36 Stat. 855), as amended by April 30, 1934 (48 Stat. 647). Aberdeen Area committee reports detail approximately 6.5 million acres of Indian lands across North and South Dakota reservations, recommending conservation plans tied to grazing permits with 20% annual rental investment requirements and amortization schedules—effectively conditioning Indian land use on lessee-driven improvements. The permit regulation committee recommended restricting grazing allocations to Indians of 1/4 blood quantum or more and declaring Indian women married to white men ineligible, explicitly acknowledging that non-Indian husbands were the actual beneficiaries of Indian land subsidies. These documents collectively demonstrate how fee patenting, irrigation liens, blood quantum criteria, and administrative procedures systematically facilitated the transfer of Indian trust lands to non-Indian ownership.

summaries.display_title: 1920–1955: BIA Policy Documents on Indian Land Dispossession, Northern Plains Reservations
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1955

This folder contains approximately 50 documents—memoranda, circular letters, policy statements, form templates, manual transmittal sheets, and correspondence—generated by the Bureau of Indian Affairs (BIA) Billings Area Office, the Commissioner of Indian Affairs, and the Office of the Secretary of the Interior, spanning from a 1920 trust patent reference to June 1955 manual revisions. The collection documents the administrative machinery of Indian land dispossession on Northern Cheyenne, Fort Peck, Crow, Wind River, Blackfeet, Flathead, and Fort Belknap reservations.

Central to the file is the evolution of the "all-or-none" fee patent policy: Area Director Paul L. Fickinger required competent Indians to apply for patents in fee covering all individually owned trust lands simultaneously, rather than piecemeal. Commissioner Glenn L. Emmons's May 16, 1955 memorandum liberalized this further, explicitly subordinating tribal timber and grazing unit interests to individual fee patent rights, citing 25 U.S.C. § 349 (Act of May 8, 1906, 34 Stat. 182). Specific cases include Dorothy MacDonald Crowley (Fort Peck allottee No. 3055, 320 acres), Elizabeth Youpee Manning, Eva Mae Smith (320 acres), Dolly Akers, and Agnes Bad Wound (approximately 960 acres valued at $7,250). The Solicitor's opinion M-36197 (December 31, 1953) addressed tax exemption as a vested property right under termination-era legislation.

Revised 25 CFR Part 241 (published 20 F.R. 617, January 28, 1955) governed sales, appraisals within six months, 10% deposits, deferred payment terms, and mineral reservations. Circular Letter AO-218 prescribed standardized advertising, 30-day bid periods, and sale fee collection of ½ of 1%. Form 5-462b required irrigation repayment contracts under the Act of February 14, 1920 (41 Stat. 408). The August 11, 1954 letter from Acting Commissioner W. Barton Greenwood terminated further land purchases in trust using Section 5 IRA gratuity funds (48 Stat. 984), citing $29,805.92 in unobligated Billings Area balances—effectively halting tribal land consolidation while accelerating fee patenting. OCR quality is poor throughout, particularly on pages 1, 6–7, 10–11, and 82–85, rendering some names and figures partially illegible. Collectively, these documents demonstrate that BIA systematically converted trust land to fee status through competency determinations, advertised sales, and policy directives that prioritized alienability over tribal land retention.

summaries.display_title: 1953–1956: BIA Records on Grazing Leases, Mineral Permits, and Uranium Royalties, Northern Cheyenne Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1953–1956

This archival folder contains Bureau of Indian Affairs inter-office transmittals, legal correspondence, grazing lease contracts, mineral prospecting permit forms, mining lease templates, uranium royalty schedules, and BIA press releases, all relating to the Northern Cheyenne Reservation in Montana. The documents span from July 1953 form dates through 1956 administrative actions.

A key document is a February 4, 1955 letter from the law firm Wilkinson, Boyden, Cragun & Barker (Washington, D.C.) to J.C. Bell of Riverton, Wyoming, responding to Bell's January 31, 1955 inquiry about flying the Northern Cheyenne Reservation for uranium prospecting at 10¢ per acre. The letter references approximately 430,000 acres, noting some are individual allotments. An October 4, 1955 letter from attorney Carl S. Hawkins to Superintendent Carl L. Pearson reveals the tribe's general counsel contract expired May 26, 1955, with prior vouchers unpaid due to inadequate tribal funds—leaving the tribe without legal representation during critical mineral development negotiations.

Pasture leases (Lease No. 1036) dated October 5, 1956, lease 175 acres in Sections 1 and 12, T4S-R38E, to Joseph E. Hope of Kirby, Montana, at $0.50/acre ($87.50 total) for six months. The lessors include heirs of Edward Bearquiver (Estate Probate #66563-35)—allotments 44, 46, 47, 48, and 49—with fractional interests (ranging from 1/36 to 12/36) distributed among Minnie F. Bearquiver, Claude Bearquiver, James Bearquiver, and others. An Advice of Allotment dated July 14, 1955, allocates $20,000 for tribal purchase of "key tracts" in the Tongue River Reservation under Resolution No. 15(55). The Commissioner's August 31, 1955 memorandum establishes nonexclusive uranium prospecting permits on tribal lands under the Act of May 11, 1938 (52 Stat. 347; 25 U.S.C. 396(a-f)) and 25 CFR 186, with leases limited to 40-acre parcels and graduated royalty schedules (10–25%). A revised January 1956 royalty schedule increased rates (12.5–25%). BIA press releases from June 1956 removed the 960-acre limitation on uranium leasing and raised annual rental to $1/acre. These documents collectively demonstrate how federal administrative mechanisms—fractionated heirship, BIA-controlled leasing at minimal rents, and mineral development frameworks favoring outside operators—facilitated ongoing dispossession of Northern Cheyenne land and resources, compounded by the tribe's inability to retain legal counsel.

summaries.display_title: 1951–1958: BIA Billings Area Office Land Transaction Summaries, Montana and Wyoming Reservations
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1951–1958

This document collection comprises Bureau of Indian Affairs administrative records from the Billings Area Office, including land transaction summaries, inter-office transmittals, procedural memoranda, and staffing reports related to multiple Montana and Wyoming reservations. The core document is the "Billings Area Summary of Land Transactions — Calendar Year 1953," transmitted January 13, 1954, from the Billings Area Office to Superintendent Carl L. Pearson at the Northern Cheyenne Agency, Lame Deer, Montana.

The 1953 summary documents the removal of 222,418.17 acres from trust status across seven reservations, with an estimated total value of $4,851,303.91. The Crow Reservation alone lost 68,103.16 acres (252 transactions), valued at $1,251,731.44, including 53,734.99 acres through fee patents to Indian landowners and 14,368.17 acres through supervised sales to purchasers at $264,082.32. Fort Peck lost 85,895.45 acres ($2,411,411.89); Blackfeet lost 52,700.83 acres ($953,331.33). An additional 61,423.40 acres changed hands through restricted conveyances, including 40,994.52 acres in tribal purchases. Warranty deeds were issued under the Act of March 1, 1907 (34 Stat. 1015–1018). The Northern Cheyenne narrative describes chronic understaffing—one permanent forestry employee handling all land work—and details the tribe's Land Service Enterprise, which employed a clerk (Henry Scalpcane, GS-3 equivalent) funded by tribal money. Forms referenced include CFR 171.7, 171.8 leases, and Forms 5-152, 5-153, 5-183, and 5-110a. The documents collectively demonstrate systematic, large-scale conversion of Indian trust land to fee status, facilitating alienation to non-Indian purchasers. OCR quality is extremely poor across most pages, rendering many pages entirely illegible.

summaries.display_title: 1954–1955: BIA Records on Uranium and Mineral Leasing of Indian Trust Lands, Billings Area
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1954–1955

This folder contains a collection of Bureau of Indian Affairs administrative documents—circular letters, prospecting permit forms, lease procedures, regulatory amendments, appraisal reports, and inter-office correspondence—primarily generated by the Billings Area Office and the BIA Central Office in Washington, D.C., concerning the leasing of Indian trust lands for uranium and associated mineral extraction, as well as a land appraisal for industrial and residential development near Lame Deer, Montana.

The earliest document is a September 8, 1954 notice of sale of prospecting permits for three units of tribal land on the Rocky Boy's Reservation (Chippewa Cree Tribe), Fort Belknap Consolidated Agency, with a minimum bid of $50 per unit and performance bonds of $2,500–$5,000. The prospecting permit template grants one-year terms with renewal rights, acreage caps of 640–960 acres per lessee, annual rentals of $0.25–$1.00 per acre, minimum development expenditures of $5.00 per acre, and a sliding royalty schedule on uranium ore ranging from 5% to 15% of mine value per dry ton. An amendment to 25 CFR 186.2, signed by Secretary Douglas McKay on January 14, 1955, permitted negotiation of tribal mineral leases (other than oil and gas) without advertising. A January 3, 1955 letter authorized a Wind River uranium lease to Alfred Ward, a Shoshone Indian, under negotiated terms. Commissioner Glenn L. Emmons's May 31, 1955 letter outlined three tiers of prospecting permits—nonexclusive surface examination, preferential permits with lease rights on up to 40 acres, and competitively bid preferential permits at 50 cents per acre renewal bonus.

The appraisal report covers Allotments 602, 603, and 604 (Stanley, Grace, and Milton Littlewhiteman) totaling 162.1875 acres in Section 34, T2S, R41E, Rosebud County, Montana. Industrial site land was valued at $40–$125+ per acre, with a comparable 40-acre Ashland sawmill site purchased for $5,000 ($125/acre). These documents collectively demonstrate how federal administrative mechanisms—CFR amendments, negotiated leasing, royalty schedules, and supervised sales of allotted land—facilitated non-Indian mineral extraction and commercial development on Indian trust lands while constraining tribal control over resource disposition.

summaries.display_title: 1955–1956: BIA Administrative Records on Land Policy and Regulations, Northern Cheyenne Agency
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1955–1956

This folder contains a heterogeneous collection of Bureau of Indian Affairs administrative documents from the Billings Area Office and Northern Cheyenne Agency, Lame Deer, Montana, dating primarily to 1956. Document types include memoranda, Solicitor's opinions, regulatory amendments, policy statements, and form templates. Key authors include Superintendent Carl L. Pearson (Northern Cheyenne), Acting Area Director Reinholt Brust, Acting Commissioner W. Barton Greenwood, and Associate Solicitor W. H. Flanery.

Several documents directly address land dispossession mechanisms. A June 6, 1956 Solicitor's opinion (M-100505) interprets oil and gas lease Form 5-157 rental provisions as contractual "obligations," holding that late payment does not automatically terminate leases but subjects them to cancellation at the discretion of the Secretary and Tribal Council under Section 6. A June 22, 1956 memorandum confirms that restrictions against alienation are "removed by operation of law" when non-Indians inherit trust allotments, and that patents in fee should be issued to non-Indian sole heirs as "good policy." The Roland W. Harper/Blackfeet Allottee No. 1195 case illustrates this: 800 acres of trust land inherited from Baptiste Westwolf (Allottee No. 1107) were subject to a $3,282.07 Montana Department of Public Welfare lien (reduced to $2,110.38), with the patent withheld pending settlement. A Solicitor's Opinion (M-36111, June 2, 1952) established the Secretary's implied authority to allow "just claims" against deceased Indian estates under the 1910 act (36 Stat. 855), enabling state welfare debt collection from restricted Indian property. Amended 25 CFR 241 regulations formalized fee patent issuance, land sales with 30-day advertising, and restriction removal procedures. The comprehensive leasing regulations (25 CFR 171) set maximum lease terms, bonding requirements, and authorized superintendents to lease lands of incompetent, minor, and absent Indians without owner consent. Solicitor's Opinion M-36318 (December 15, 1955) confirmed the 1955 general leasing act (69 Stat. 540) applied to the Crow Reservation alongside existing Crow-specific statutes (41 Stat. 751, 44 Stat. 658, 44 Stat. 1365), expanding leasing authority. Additional documents address mineral prospecting permits on the Crow Reservation (39 permits on Unit No. 1, with 21 applications for 40-acre tracts), highway rights-of-way procedures under 25 CFR 256, and Hatch Act political activity restrictions for agency employees. OCR quality is poor on many pages, rendering some text fragmentary.

summaries.display_title: 1948–1955: BIA Administrative Records on Land Sales, Appraisals, and Policy, Northern Cheyenne Agency
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1948–1955

This folder contains a heterogeneous collection of Bureau of Indian Affairs administrative documents—federal regulations, memoranda, conference agendas, policy circulars, appraisal reports, land sale notices, and legal opinions—originating primarily from the Billings Area Office and the Northern Cheyenne Agency at Lame Deer, Montana, with some Central Office (Washington, D.C.) materials. Key authors include Commissioner Glenn L. Emmons, Area Director Paul L. Fickinger, Assistant Commissioner P. J. Utz, Superintendent Carl L. Pearson, and Acting Solicitor W. H. Flannery. Secretary of the Interior Douglas McKay signed several regulatory amendments.

The documents collectively establish the administrative machinery of Indian land dispossession during the termination era. Amended 25 CFR Part 241 (December 5, 1955, 20 F.R. 9175) governed patents in fee, competency certificates, and supervised sales, requiring appraisal at fair market value before issuance. A December 30, 1953 memorandum from Commissioner Emmons, transmitting Assistant Secretary Orme Lewis's June 17, 1953 policy directive, mandated that competent Indians applying for fee patents must include all trust lands they owned—a coercive mechanism pushing total removal from trust status. Bureau Order 551 delegated broad authority over leases, mortgages, and mineral permits to area directors and superintendents.

Specific land sales on the Northern Cheyenne Reservation (May 4, 1954 advertisement) listed 18 tracts from named allottees—including Rufus Wallowing (Allotment 1253, 160 acres), Victor Littlechief estate (Allotment 569, 160 acres), and others—with minerals reserved to the Northern Cheyenne Tribe per the Act of June 3, 1926 (44 Stat. 690). Sale fees were 2½ percent of purchase price, minimum $20.00. Petitions from Milton Littlewhiteman Jr. (Allotment 604, 160 acres) and another allottee (Allotment 624, 160 acres) sought supervised sales of grazing land. An appraisal report for Allotments 602, 603, and 604 (162.1875 acres near Lame Deer) valued industrial-site land at up to $254.28 per acre, reflecting a sawmill operation established in 1955 on State Highway 8.

A May 20, 1954 Portland Area Office memorandum opinion argued that 42 Stat. 995 (25 U.S.C. 392) authorized the Secretary to approve mortgages on restricted Indian land, countering title companies' refusals. The Solicitor's office confirmed that mortgage approval did not extend trust periods and that fee patents could issue subject to existing mortgages. A February 27, 1953 letter to Fickinger specifically discussed Joe Medicine Crow's $1,280 FHA loan on Crow Reservation trust land, with a 25-year repayment schedule, rejecting the Area Director's proposal to require fee patents before loan approval. The $96,015 increase in realty personnel funding for fiscal year 1956 and the $41,000 General Trustee Service allocation reveal the bureaucratic infrastructure supporting land transactions. Public Law 255 (84th Congress, August 9, 1955) authorized long-term leasing of restricted Indian lands for up to 25 years. Uranium prospecting permit forms and advertising notices for tribal mineral lands document resource extraction mechanisms under the Act of May 11, 1938 (52 Stat. 347).

These documents demonstrate how fee patenting, supervised sales, competency determinations, mortgage approvals, mineral leasing, and rights-of-way grants operated as interlocking legal mechanisms to transfer Indian trust land into unrestricted status, systematically diminishing the Northern Cheyenne and other reservations' land bases during the federal termination policy period.

summaries.display_title: 1953: Senator Mansfield Correspondence with BIA and Crow Tribal Members on Land Transactions
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1953–1953

This collection comprises correspondence between Senator Mike Mansfield (Montana, first Senate term 1953–1958), the Bureau of Indian Affairs, and Crow tribal members during fiscal year 1953. The core documents include: a July 24, 1953 letter from BIA Billings Area Director Paul L. Fickinger to Mansfield tabulating land transactions; a May 28, 1953 letter from Acting Commissioner M. Barton Greenwood responding to concerns raised by Allen Old Horn of Crow Agency; a March 23, 1953 memorandum from the Crow Indian Women's Federated Club (signed by President Susan Old Horn, Secretary-Treasurer Minnie R. Williams, and others); and an April 10, 1953 BIA letter regarding Crow tribal fund expenditures under the Act of June 20, 1936 (49 Stat. 1543).

The Fickinger letter provides devastating quantitative evidence of dispossession: during fiscal year 1953 alone, 410 fee patents were approved to Indians covering 91,000 acres, and 188 patents were issued to non-Indian purchasers under supervised sales totaling 36,661 acres, with 333 inter-Indian transactions covering 52,000 acres. In total, over 128,000 acres of Indian trust land were placed on state tax rolls in Montana and Wyoming. The Crow Reservation alone accounted for 137 fee patents (17,130.83 acres) and significant supervised sales. The Greenwood letter addresses the Act of June 14, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), noting that competent Indians could lease lands without federal supervision for up to five years, but criticizing the practice of collecting rentals five years in advance at heavily discounted rates—a practice the Bureau acknowledged disadvantaged Indian landowners yet claimed it could not control. The Women's Club memorandum protests Senator Butler's Bill S.335 compelling sale of deceased allotments without heir consent, Senator Murray's tribal funds bill, and 25-year lease proposals, arguing these measures would render Crow families "homeless and landless."

summaries.display_title: 1963–1964: Legislative History of S. 914, Crow Tribe Compensation for 107th Meridian Survey Error
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1963–1964

This file documents the legislative history of S. 914, introduced February 25, 1963, by Senator Lee Metcalf (with Senator Mike Mansfield) in the 88th Congress, to compensate the Crow Tribe of Indians, Montana, for approximately 29,760 acres of reservation land erroneously excluded by a faulty government survey of the 107th meridian. The 1889–1891 survey placed the eastern boundary of the Crow Reservation roughly nine-tenths of a mile west of the true 107th degree longitude over a 53-mile stretch, resulting in a strip of tribal land being treated as public domain or incorporated into the Northern Cheyenne Reservation. Of the total acreage, 15,360 acres were absorbed into the Northern Cheyenne Reservation (10,840 allotted to Northern Cheyenne individuals, 4,520 administered as Northern Cheyenne tribal land), and 14,400 acres were treated as public domain (14,080 patented to private parties, 320 acres remaining vacant).

The Department of Justice (Deputy Attorney General Nicholas Katzenbach, March 31, 1964) and the Bureau of the Budget both recommended against enactment, arguing the Crow Tribe's failure to file under the Indian Claims Commission Act (25 U.S.C. § 70k) within the five-year limitation barred the claim, and that the bill contravened the 1946 Act's purpose of eliminating special jurisdictional legislation. Justice further noted the Tribe had already recovered $10,242,984.70 in Docket No. 54 for ceded lands and may have been overpaid by $532,817.42 due to a subsequently overruled discounting methodology. The Department of the Interior recommended a substitute bill ratifying the erroneous survey as a taking, with compensation at 1891 land values plus four percent simple interest, rather than present-day value plus rental. Attorney Bert Kronmiller of Hardin, Montana, and Crow Tribal Council Resolution No. 63-25A supported the bill, citing treaty rights under the 1868 Treaty (15 Stat. 649) and Supreme Court precedent (*Shoshone v. United States*, 299 U.S. 476). The document powerfully illustrates how a government surveying error dispossessed the Crow Tribe of nearly 30,000 acres, with bureaucratic and legal mechanisms—statutes of limitations, claims commission procedures, and inter-agency opposition—subsequently deployed to minimize or deny restitution.

summaries.display_title: 1946–1949: Mansfield Papers on Crow Reservation Land Leasing, Tribal Governance, and Competency Determinations
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1946–1949

This folder from the Mike Mansfield Papers (Mss 65, University of Montana) contains correspondence, petitions, and resolutions concerning Crow Reservation land leasing, tribal governance disputes, and Indian competency determinations during 1946–1949. Key correspondents include attorney Kenneth R. L. Simmons (Crow Tribal Attorney, Billings), attorney A. F. Lamey (Billings), Superintendent L. C. Lippert of the Crow-Northern Cheyenne Agency, Secretary of the Interior J. A. Krug, and members of Montana's congressional delegation including Mansfield and Senator James E. Murray.

A June 3, 1947 tribal council resolution (47 for, 2 against) demanded congressional investigation of the Crow leasing and permit system, asserting that the unit grazing permit system prevented individual Indian landholders from participating in current high livestock profits. The resolution specifically endorsed S. 823, H.R. 2356 (sale of deceased allottee interests to the tribe), and H.R. 3200 (leasing of trust and restricted lands). The Big Horn County Chamber of Commerce noted that of 26,000 acres in the Big Horn Valley, 75% was Indian-owned, of which 94% was heirship land limited to five-year leases, discouraging improvements.

Secretary Krug's August 4, 1947 letter referenced the Crow tribe's $30,000,000 claim for 30,530,764 acres ceded under the 1868 Treaty (15 Stat. 649), for which approximately $1,500,000 was received. The Court of Claims (81 Ct. Cls. 238) found no fraud, per the jurisdictional act of July 3, 1926 (44 Stat. 807). Krug suggested pursuing claims before the Indian Claims Commission under the August 13, 1946 act. A January 1949 letter from Lamey to Murray detailed disputes between Matt Tschirgi and the "Pumpkin Rollers" small farmers' association over grazing permits encompassing several thousand acres, which was reportedly delaying competency determinations for Crow Indians. The documents collectively demonstrate how federal leasing regulations, heirship fractionation, and bureaucratic delays in competency proceedings systematically constrained Crow economic autonomy over their own lands.

summaries.display_title: 1976: Mansfield and Metcalf Correspondence on Enforcing Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act of 1920
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1976

This document consists of congressional correspondence between U.S. Senators Mike Mansfield (Majority Leader) and Lee Metcalf of Montana and the Department of Justice regarding proposed litigation to enforce Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act of 1920, which limits the amount of land on the Crow Indian Reservation that may be owned by any one person. The materials are from the Mansfield papers (Collection 6, Container 551L), covering his fourth Senate term (1971–1977).

On April 28, 1976, Mansfield and Metcalf wrote to Attorney General Edward H. Levi noting that the Department of the Interior had recommended on February 20, 1976, that the Justice Department initiate litigation to enforce Section 2's prohibitions against excess non-Indian landholdings on the Crow Reservation. The senators emphasized that the problem had been recognized for over a decade, that they had consulted with the Crow Tribe repeatedly, and that Attorney General Ramsey Clark had corresponded with Interior about the issue as early as 1967. Peter R. Taft, Assistant Attorney General for the Land and Natural Resources Division, replied on May 11, 1976, stating the case had been assigned to the Indian Resources Section and that the proposed litigation would "challenge title to tens of thousands of acres of lands involving hundreds of separate legal transactions." Taft anticipated the Department would determine its course of action by early summer 1976.

The correspondence proves that illegal or excessive non-Indian land accumulation on the Crow Reservation was a known, long-standing problem spanning at least from 1920 to 1976, that federal enforcement of protective statutory provisions was chronically delayed, and that bureaucratic inertia within the Justice Department effectively perpetuated dispossession despite clear statutory prohibitions. Dan Israel, copied on the letter, likely served as counsel to the Crow Tribe.

summaries.display_title: 1962: S. 3678 — Bill to Ratify Crow Reservation Land Conveyances Violating Section 2 Acreage Limits
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1962

This document collection centers on S. 3678, a bill introduced August 27, 1962, by Senators Lee Metcalf and Mike Mansfield (both Montana) to ratify certain conveyances of land on the Crow Indian Reservation that violated acreage limitations in Section 2 of the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), as amended by the Act of June 8, 1940 (54 Stat. 252). Section 2 prohibited conveyances to any person or corporation already owning 640 acres of agricultural or 1,280 acres of grazing land within the reservation, with violations punishable by a $5,000 fine or six months imprisonment. The bill would validate all such conveyances made prior to October 18, 1957, while preserving mineral rights under P.L. 86-283.

An accompanying letter from the Assistant Secretary of the Interior to Senate President Lyndon Johnson details systematic enforcement failures: between 1946 and 1955, 21 supervised sales covering 517 tracts occurred without any compliance checks. The BIA Area Director claimed Washington officials told him the limitations were inoperative. The Department of Justice declined criminal prosecution for pre-1955 violations and had not acted on three civil referrals submitted April 6, 1960. The Crow Tribe initially supported ratification (resolution of April 14, 1956) but later withdrew support and opposed the legislation. Attorney J.F. Meglen of Billings represented leaseholder interests and lobbied Mansfield and Assistant Secretary John Carver for resolution. Former BIA counsel Harlow Pease publicly stated he had warned officials of violations but was ignored. The Interior Department also suggested Congress consider repealing the acreage limitations entirely, arguing they depressed land prices for Indian sellers—effectively advocating removal of the sole statutory protection against land consolidation by non-Indian ranchers. Prior bills (S. 332, S. 55) in the 85th and 86th Congresses failed. The document proves institutional complicity in dispossession through deliberate non-enforcement of protective statutes.

summaries.display_title: 1975: Crow Tribe Effort to Compel Federal Test Case Enforcing Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1975–1975

This collection of correspondence from Senator Mike Mansfield's papers (Collection 65, Series X, Container 490) documents the Crow Tribe's 1975 effort to compel the United States to bring a test case enforcing Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act of 1920, which prohibited any non-Indian from acquiring more than 1,920 acres of land within the Crow Reservation boundaries. The key participants include Senator Mansfield, Senator Lee Metcalf, Crow Tribal Chairman Patrick Stands Over Bull, Daniel H. Israel of the Native American Rights Fund (Boulder, Colorado), and Solicitor Kent Frizzell and Deputy Solicitor David E. Lindgren at the Department of the Interior.

The April 21, 1975 letter from Mansfield and Metcalf to Frizzell provides critical specifics: the proposed defendant held approximately 67,000 acres on the Crow Reservation, of which at least 30,000 were acquired directly from Indians when the non-Indian purchaser already exceeded the 1,920-acre limit, and approximately 37,000 acres were acquired through intermediate non-violating purchasers. Even if completely divested, the defendant would still control 157,000 acres of Crow land under perpetual leases. The legal question centered on whether "directly or indirectly" imposed an absolute acreage ceiling regardless of acquisition route. The Interior Department's responses from May through October 1975 repeatedly deferred action, claiming the matter was under research. The documents demonstrate fifty years of federal non-enforcement of statutory protections against excessive non-Indian land accumulation on the Crow Reservation, revealing how bureaucratic delay functioned as a mechanism of ongoing dispossession.

summaries.display_title: 1951–1954: Mansfield Papers on Fee Patent Legislation for Blackfeet and Crow Indian Allottees
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1951–1954

This archival file from Senator Mike Mansfield's papers (Collection 65, Series VIII, 83rd Congress) documents multiple private bills and administrative proceedings for fee patents on the Blackfeet and Crow Indian Reservations in Montana. The documents include correspondence between Mansfield, constituents, the Bureau of Indian Affairs, the Department of the Interior, and the Senate Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs.

Three principal cases are documented. S. 680 sought a fee patent for Charles I. Chattin's 89.57-acre homestead allotment No. 2806 on the Blackfeet Reservation, restricted under the Act of June 30, 1919 (41 Stat. 3, 16). Chattin's letters reveal he needed the patent to leverage his land for business debts of approximately $4,000, claiming his business was worth $12,000. S. 681 sought a fee patent for Grace Kills A Boy (Pretty Paint), a full-blood Crow Indian, for approximately 1,040 acres in T4S R30E. The Interior Department issued an unfavorable report, noting she leased her land at 6¢ per acre versus the supervised rate of 17¢, and that her husband Perry Pretty Paint had sold 1,080 acres of fee-patented land—worth roughly $10,000—with only a new automobile to show. S. 714 sought a patent for Louis W. Milliken's 520 acres on the Crow Reservation, valued at $4,000 and leased at $100 annually; the Department recommended favorably given his 1/8th blood quantum and $2,500 annual income.

The file critically documents the transition from private congressional bills to administrative fee-patenting under Public Laws 48 and 49 (83rd Congress), which repealed homestead restrictions under the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 756, §13) for Crow allottees and the 1919 Act for Blackfeet allottees. Senator Butler's committee explicitly refused further private bills, directing applicants to the Department. The Crow Agency reported processing approximately 200 fee patent applications following repeal. S. 1301 for Lucy Yarlott Othermedicine ratified an erroneous prior sale of 480 acres exceeding the 320-acre homestead sale limit. These records demonstrate how federal competency determinations, paternalistic assessments of "prudence and wisdom," and eventual blanket legislative removal of trust protections facilitated systematic land alienation from tribal members.

summaries.file_name: Mansfield Crow fee patents James Stanton and Grover Cisel 1953.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1951–1953: Mansfield Papers on Fee Patent Applications for Crow Indian Allottees
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1951–1953

This collection comprises legislative bills, correspondence, telegrams, and agency records from the files of U.S. Senator Mike Mansfield (D-Montana, first term 1953–1958) relating to fee patent applications for Crow Indian allottees. The documents center on three individuals: John George Scott (Allotment No. 1765, approximately 645.76 acres, patent number 1140835), Guy Bulltail (Allotment No. 3109, 160 acres, NE¼ Section 28, T.4S., R.27E., M.P.M.), and Iva Bulltail (Allotment No. 1932, 120 acres, N½NW¼ Sec. 28 and NE¼NE¼ Sec. 29, T.4S., R.27E.). Mansfield introduced S. 1483 (March 27, 1953), S. 1563 (April 2, 1953), and S. 1707 (April 21, 1953) as private bills because Section 13 of the Crow Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751, 756) prohibited allottees from selling more than 320 acres of homestead land without congressional authorization. Attorney James W. Stanton of Hardin and lawyer Grover C. Cisel of Billings facilitated these requests. The Department of the Interior reported favorably on S. 1483, noting Scott was one-fourth degree Indian blood and had not resided on the reservation since 1922. Scott's fee patent was approved by the Billings Area Office on August 19, 1953, and issued September 21, 1953. Public Law 49 (H.R. 1244), passed during the 83rd Congress, subsequently authorized Crow allottees to apply directly for fee patents, eliminating the need for private legislation—rendering the Bulltail bills unnecessary. These records demonstrate how restrictive federal statutes forced individual Indians into lengthy bureaucratic and legislative processes to alienate their own allotted lands, facilitating dispossession through fee patenting.

summaries.file_name: Mansfield Crow misc 1953-59 some Crow Act 1.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1955–1959: Mansfield Papers on Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act and Reservation Land Sales
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1955–1959

This folder from Senator Mike Mansfield's papers (Collection 65, Container 42) documents the political and legal controversy surrounding Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), which imposed acreage limitations on land purchases within the Crow Reservation—640 acres of agricultural land and 1,280 acres of grazing land per individual purchaser. The documents include correspondence among Mansfield, Senator James E. Murray, Representative Lee Metcalf, Interior Department officials, Bureau of Indian Affairs Area Director Percy E. Melis (Billings), and Crow tribal members Perry Pretty Paint and Minnie R. Williams.

A key May 5, 1958 letter from the Interior Department confirms that at the Montana delegation's request, the Superintendent's advertised sale of allotted Crow lands—with bids to open May 13, 1958—was suspended pending congressional action on S. 332, which sought to repeal the acreage limitations and validate prior illegal conveyances. A newspaper clipping details that land sales had been halted since August 1955 after Commissioner Glenn L. Emmons ordered an investigation revealing widespread violations of the 1920 acreage caps. When sales resumed, 58 allotted tracts totaling 9,942 acres were offered on July 9. Crow delegate John L. Glenn denounced S. 332 as legalizing illegal transfers that enriched non-Indian ranchers, calling it a taking of private Indian property without compensation. A petition signed by 438 tribal members sought amendments to S. Bill 55 to extend trust periods, repeal acreage restrictions, and vest mineral rights—including oil and gas—in allottees. The documents collectively demonstrate how BIA-administered land sales systematically transferred allotted Crow lands to non-Indian purchasers in violation of existing federal law, and how legislative remedies were designed to ratify rather than reverse that dispossession.

summaries.file_name: Mansfield Crow misc 1953-59 some Crow Act.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1955–1959: Mansfield Correspondence and Petitions on Crow Reservation Land Dispossession and Section 2
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1955–1959

This collection comprises correspondence, petitions, newspaper clippings, and federal agency letters from the files of Senator Mike Mansfield (Montana, first term 1953–1958) concerning land dispossession on the Crow Reservation. The central legal issue is Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), which imposed acreage limitations on land purchases: no individual could own more than 640 acres of agricultural land or 1,280 acres of grazing land within reservation boundaries. Bureau of Indian Affairs investigations ordered by Commissioner Glenn L. Emmons revealed widespread violations of these limits, prompting a hold on land sales from approximately August 1955 until July 1957, when 58 allotted tracts totaling 9,942 acres were offered for bid at Crow Agency.

Senate Bill S. 332 (also referenced as S. 55) sought to repeal Section 2's acreage restrictions and validate prior illegal conveyances. A May 5, 1958 letter from the Department of the Interior to Mansfield confirmed suspension of further Crow land sales pending congressional action on S. 332. A petition organized by Perry Pretty Paint and signed by 438 Crow tribal members requested a special council meeting to consider amendments to S. Bill 55, including repeal of acreage limits and reversion of mineral rights—including oil and gas—to the tribe upon trust expiration. Crow delegate John L. Glenn opposed the repeal, arguing it would reward illegal purchases and constitute taking private Indian property without compensation for private enrichment. Minnie R. Williams raised the exclusion of Crow women from tribal delegate positions. Field Solicitor A.E. Bielefeld's interpretation of acreage caps is detailed. The documents collectively demonstrate how federal administrative mechanisms facilitated alienation of allotted Crow lands while legislative efforts sought retroactive legalization of illegal transfers.

summaries.display_title: 1952–1953: Mansfield Correspondence on Re-Leasing Crow Allotment Lands After Campbell Farming Corporation Lease
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1952–1953

This collection comprises correspondence between U.S. Senator Mike Mansfield (Montana), Bureau of Indian Affairs officials, and Montana constituents regarding the re-leasing of non-competent Indian allotment lands on the Crow Reservation previously held under a single large lease by the Campbell Farming Corporation of Hardin, Montana, owned largely by Thomas D. Campbell. The central BIA letter, dated January 16, 1953, from the Commissioner of Indian Affairs to Mansfield, explains that the Campbell lease block was divided into five units for competitive bidding at Mansfield's urging. High bids on Unit 1 came from Melvin Seitz, Waldo Lieger, and Glen Seitz of Billings; Ralph Metcalfe submitted high bids on Units 2 and 3; Campbell was the sole bidder on Unit 4; and Ervin H. Schnadt's conditional bid on Unit 5 was disqualified for falling below the 33⅓% minimum crop share. However, a regulatory provision (citing 25 CFR 171.18) permitted the previous lessee to match high bids, enabling Campbell to reclaim all five units. Lease terms included a 10% lessor's crop share, $1.10 per acre for grass land, and lessor-paid hail insurance at $11.00 per acre. Constituent letters from Ken Byerly, attorney Bill Jennings, and J. Ernest Weinberg of Lodge Grass protested Campbell's 35-year monopoly, arguing it suppressed Indian wages, prevented individual allottees from controlling their lands, and delayed emancipation from BIA trusteeship. The BIA acknowledged that many Indian lands were unsurveyed, unfenced, and intermingled with competent and fee lands, complicating breakup. The documents demonstrate how federal leasing regulations—particularly the incumbent-matching provision—functioned as a structural mechanism perpetuating large-scale non-Indian control over trust allotments despite nominal competitive bidding, effectively dispossessing Crow allottees of meaningful economic benefit from their own lands.

summaries.file_name: Mansfield Crow note re Crow Act 1962.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1962–1966: Mansfield Office Notes and Correspondence on Section 2 of the Crow Act
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1962–1966

This document consists of fragmentary office notes and correspondence from the files of U.S. Senator Mike Mansfield (D-Montana), relating to Crow Indian land matters, specifically Section 2 of the Crow Act. The materials appear to date from approximately 1962–1966, during Mansfield's third Senate term, and were filed under "BIA / CROW / 1966" in his federal agencies collection (Collection 65, Inner No. 236).

The principal note concerns Clem Cormier of Billings, Montana, who was in Washington seeking an appointment with Senator Mansfield regarding Section 2 of the Crow Indian Land matter, which dealt with leasing procedures and grazing on the Crow Reservation, including reference to the Fraser Land and Load End Livestock. The note observes that the Senate had passed the relevant bill "several times" but that Congressman Haley refused to act on it in the House, indicating legislative obstruction of Crow land reform. Cormier believed he could resolve the issue and would speak only with Mansfield directly. The note also references attorney Joe Meglen, who had previously represented Cormier but with whom relations had soured, citing information from Vic Reinemer.

The OCR quality is poor, rendering portions of the text fragmentary and difficult to parse. Nevertheless, the document evidences the political bottleneck in Congress over Crow Reservation leasing and grazing land policy, the role of non-Indian intermediaries in shaping legislative outcomes, and the Bureau of Indian Affairs' involvement in administering Crow land use—all mechanisms through which dispossession was perpetuated or contested.

summaries.file_name: Mansfield Meglan letter on land sales 1967.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1967–1968: Attorney Meglen Letter to Mansfield on Crow Reservation Land Sales Complaints
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1967–1968

This document is a letter dated December 2, 1967, from J. F. Megle (or Meglen), an attorney-at-law based at 207 Behner Building, 2822 3rd Ave North, Billings, Montana, to Senator Mike Mansfield at the Senate Office Building in Washington, D.C. It is filed within Senator Mansfield's papers (Container No. 236) under "BIA CROW - Misc. 1968."

Despite severely degraded OCR quality, the letter's core substance is discernible. Megle reports complaints from multiple members of the Crow Tribe that land belonging to individual Crow Indian allottees is being sold under conditions that yield only approximately one-half of the appraised price. He notes the legal restriction that land of a Crow Indian allottee "can be sold only to the Tribe or to another Crow Indian," indicating the constrained market created by federal trust land regulations. He further relays allegations that lands purchased by the Tribe are then leased to relatives of tribal officials, suggesting insider dealing in both acquisition and leasing. Megle mentions a Crow individual named Francis (possibly Francis L. F[—]) as a potential informant but cautions that Crow members will not speak to Bureau of Indian Affairs officials and will deny their statements. He explicitly asks Mansfield not to forward the letter to the BIA, since he cannot substantiate the claims, but suggests the Senator might prompt Bureau officials to investigate independently. The document evidences a pattern of below-market forced sales of allotted trust land and alleged corruption in tribal land transactions, illustrating how restricted sale provisions and BIA oversight failures facilitated dispossession of individual Crow allottees during the late 1960s.

summaries.display_title: 1955–1963: Mansfield Papers on Crow Land Dispossession and Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1955–1963

This collection comprises correspondence between Senator Mike Mansfield's office and Crow tribal members, Bureau of Indian Affairs communications, Crow Tribal Council resolutions, newspaper clippings, and a detailed memorandum by Harlow Pease (dated July 4, 1962) documenting the systematic dispossession of Crow Indian land. The central legal mechanism is Section 2 (also referenced as Section 11) of the Crow Allotment Act of 1920, enacted through efforts of Senator Thomas J. Walsh, which prohibited any person from acquiring more than 640 acres of agricultural land or 1,280 acres of grazing land within the Crow Reservation, with violations punishable by a $5,000 fine or six months imprisonment. Pease's memorandum details how the BIA conspired with non-Indian ranchers to ignore these restrictions, enabling massive illegal accumulations—specifically M.H. Tschirgi (Antler Land Co.) holding over 40,000 acres and Campbell Farming Corporation over 25,000 acres. Approximately 70 percent of the reservation passed to non-Indian control. The BIA's Billings Area Director reported 191,751 acres (80 percent) under overlapping leases as of December 19, 1957. Between 300,000 and 500,000 acres were estimated held under legally void fee patents. Three validating bills introduced in Congress were defeated after the 1957 Haley Subcommittee hearings. Tribal Council Resolutions 63-22A, B, and C (February 2, 1963), passed unanimously, demanded Justice Department prosecution and condemned ongoing conspiracies between the BIA, illegal landholders, and congressional lobbyists. The documents collectively demonstrate a decades-long pattern of institutional complicity in dispossession, with 71 ranchers illegally possessing 248,000 acres and maintaining a $22,197.52 lobbying fund.

summaries.display_title: 1954: Northern Cheyenne Tribal Council Resolution and Correspondence to Halt Land Alienation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1954–1954

This collection of correspondence and tribal resolutions documents the Northern Cheyenne Tribe's effort to halt land alienation on their reservation in Montana during 1954. The core document is Tribal Council Resolution No. 93 (54), adopted July 2, 1954, by a vote of 13 in favor with 1 not voting, petitioning Commissioner of Indian Affairs Glenn L. Emmons to suspend fee patents and advertised land sales pending investigation of their impact on the reservation economy. Eugene Fisher, Tribal President, transmitted the resolution to Senator Mike Mansfield and others.

The Bureau of Indian Affairs responded with detailed statistics: the Northern Cheyenne Reservation comprised approximately 445,736 acres, of which 233,120 acres were held under 1,457 individual allotments. Only 1,310 acres had been removed from trust status through fee patents or sales. At the time, 87 petitions for sale were pending, with land valued at $5.00 to $10.00 per acre, meaning allottees could expect $800.00 to $1,600.00 per allotment. The reservation's carrying capacity was approximately 18,660 animal unit months, with 62 individual Indians owning roughly 3,000 head of cattle and the tribal steer enterprise holding 800 head. Minerals were reserved for tribal benefit until June 1976.

Associate Commissioner W.M. Rexle instructed the Area Director to review all pending applications and assist the tribe in developing a land exchange program whereby allotments within range units could be swapped for tribal lands of equal market value outside those units. The documents demonstrate the tension between individual allottees' vested rights to petition for sales under existing regulations and the tribe's collective interest in preventing checkerboard ownership that would undermine stock water control and rental values, illustrating the structural mechanisms of dispossession embedded in federal allotment policy even on a reservation that had remained remarkably intact.

summaries.file_name: Mansfield Repeal of Section 2 Crow Act 1959.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1959–1960: Legislative Effort to Repeal Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act and Crow Tribal Opposition
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1960

This collection documents the legislative effort to repeal Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), as amended by the Act of June 8, 1940 (54 Stat. 252), and the Crow Tribe's vigorous opposition to that effort. The central document is S. 55, introduced January 9, 1959, in the 86th Congress by Senators Murray, Mansfield, McGee, and O'Mahoney, with a companion bill H.R. 439 introduced by Congressman Keith Thomson. The bill proposed to repeal the first paragraph of Section 2, which limited the acreage any non-Crow person could acquire on the Crow Reservation, and to validate, ratify, and confirm all prior conveyances made in violation of those acreage limitations.

The Department of the Interior, in a report dated March 15, 1960, to Chairman James E. Murray of the Senate Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs, recommended against enactment, noting that "numerous violations" of Section 2 had occurred—some in sales supervised by the Department itself, with "no adequate explanation" available. The Commissioner of Indian Affairs had suspended all Crow land sales in late 1955 and referred flagrant violators to the Department of Justice for criminal and civil action. The Bureau of the Budget, through Assistant Director Phillip S. Hughes (March 7 and March 10, 1960), likewise declined to recommend enactment given the Crow Tribe's opposition (Resolution 191, May 2, 1959).

Crow Tribal Council Chairman Edward P. Whiteman's March 6, 1959 statement, published as "The Crow Indian Sentinel," argued that conveyances violating Section 2 were void ab initio, citing Ewert v. Bluejacket (259 U.S. 129) and Heckman v. U.S. (224 U.S. 413). Whiteman detailed how large non-Indian landowners had surrounded allotments, stifling competitive bidding and depressing prices for Indian land. He traced the pattern from the 1887 Dawes Act through the 1920 Crow Act, noting that Indian landholdings nationally had been reduced from 139,000,000 acres in 1887. The documents collectively prove systematic dispossession through administrative maladministration of acreage limitations, followed by a legislative attempt to retroactively legalize those violations—an attempt blocked only by tribal opposition and federal agency reluctance.

summaries.display_title: 1956–1958: S. 3698 and S. 332 — Bills to Repeal Section 2 Acreage Limits of the Crow Allotment Act
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1958

This collection documents the legislative history of S. 3698 (84th Congress) and S. 332 (85th Congress), bills introduced by Senators Murray and Mansfield to repeal the acreage limitation provisions of Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), as amended June 8, 1940 (54 Stat. 252). Section 2 prohibited the Secretary of the Interior from approving conveyances of Crow land to any non-Indian owning 640+ acres of agricultural land or 1,280+ acres of grazing land on the reservation, with post-purchase caps of 1,280 acres agricultural and 1,920 acres grazing. Violations carried penalties of up to $5,000 fine and six months imprisonment.

The Department of the Interior's supplemental report of March 11, 1958, to Subcommittee Chairman James Haley reveals that these limitations were systematically unenforced for 35 years. Between 1946 and 1955, twenty-one supervised sales covering 517 tracts proceeded without reference to statutory limitations. The Bureau's Branch of Realty chief in Washington allegedly told the Billings Area Director that compliance "was not necessary," directly contradicting a February 12, 1947 legal opinion by Fred B. Woodard requiring compliance verification. Commissioner Emmons halted sales only in late 1955. Over 200,000 acres were affected. The Crow Tribal Council initially passed Resolution No. 77 on April 14, 1956, requesting repeal, but rescinded it on April 20, 1957, with the River Crow faction demanding enforcement. The Department consequently withdrew its recommendation for enactment. Attorneys Burke, Hibbs & Sweeney of Billings and Bert Kronmiller of Hardin represented non-Indian landowners seeking validation. The documents prove decades of bureaucratic negligence facilitated massive unauthorized land accumulation by non-Indians, constituting systematic dispossession through administrative failure to enforce congressionally mandated protections.

summaries.file_name: Mansfield Stanton letter in state of Crow 1970.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1970: Mansfield Correspondence with Attorney Stanton on State of the Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1970–1970

This document is a brief exchange of correspondence between Senator Mike Mansfield (Montana) and Harold G. Stanton of the law firm Stanton & Hovland in Hardin, Montana, dated March 23, 1970 and March 13, 1970 respectively, along with an attached newspaper clipping from the Billings Gazette dated March 12, 1970. The materials are filed in Mansfield's Senate papers (Collection 65, Container 236) under "Indians - Crow - Misc 1970."

Stanton's letter to Secretary of the Interior Walter Hickel describes economic progress on the Crow Reservation, including a spur factory employing 100 people (mostly formerly unemployed Crow Indians), a 60-unit motel and recreation complex incorporating a Crow Indian Heritage Park, and the Tribe's role as Master Concessionaire at Big Horn Canyon National Recreation Area, projected to employ 300 additional tribal members. Stanton praises Crow Tribal Council Chairman Edison Real Bird as a competent leader pursuing economic self-determination rather than militancy. Mansfield's reply acknowledges the progress in governance, social welfare, and economic development.

The newspaper clipping reports a debate between Real Bird and Flathead advocate Vic Charlo. Real Bird emphasized creating "the philosophy of ownership" among Crow people and capitalizing on Indian assets, while Charlo questioned whether economic development that destroys land truly benefits Indians, citing timber cutting and pollution on the Flathead Reservation. Real Bird noted 128 Crow students in college, manufacturing plants, and carpet industry interests. While the documents celebrate progress, they implicitly reveal the underlying dispossession framework: Indian economic development remained contingent on federal approval, BIA structures, and non-Indian legal intermediaries like Stanton's firm operating from ceded lands in Hardin.

summaries.file_name: Mansfield Urban Bear Don’t Walk Stanton complaint 1972.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1971–1976: Sen. Mansfield Correspondence on Urban Bear Don't Walk's Complaint Against Stanton Law Firm
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1971–1976

This document is a correspondence file from Senator Mike Mansfield's papers (Collection 65, Series X: Federal Agencies, Container 3555) concerning a complaint by Urban Bear Don't Walk, a member of the Crow Tribal Executive Committee, against the law firm of Stanton, Hoveland & Torske of Hardin, Montana. The core document is Bear Don't Walk's November 21, 1971 letter to BIA Commissioner Louis R. Bruce, followed by intermediary correspondence through Mansfield's office into March 1972.

Bear Don't Walk alleges that Stanton holds three contracts with the Crow Tribe—a general contract, a water rights contract (relating to Big Horn River water claims), and a claims contract—and that the firm does not represent Crow interests. He charges that Stanton violated the general contract's prohibition against speculating "directly or indirectly in Crow Tribal or allotee lands" by owning stock in companies with holdings on the Crow Reservation. He further alleges conflicts of interest, citing ABA Canon EC 5-1, since Stanton represents white ranchers and farmers who lease Crow tribal land. Bear Don't Walk calls the $10,000 annual retainer "exhorbitant," noting the tribe must pay additional fees for any tribunal or administrative work. He specifically references violations of the 1920 Crow Act, Section 2, and names the Little Horn Land & Livestock Co. as holding "tremendous landholdings" on the reservation in violation of that statute, yet Stanton took no action against them. The BIA confirmed an approved contract with Stanton expiring August 15, 1975, and a pending contract with Wilkinson, Cragun & Barker. The documents reveal how attorney conflicts of interest and BIA administrative inertia facilitated ongoing land dispossession on the Crow Reservation. OCR quality on pages 2–3 is extremely poor, rendering those pages largely illegible.

summaries.display_title: 1953–1954: Sen. Mansfield Correspondence on William Bends Fee Patent Application, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1953–1954

This document is a collection of correspondence from the archival papers of U.S. Senator Mike Mansfield (Montana, first term 1953–1958), housed in Container No. 45, Series X: Federal Agencies. The records concern William Bends, identified as a Crow Indian and allottee No. 1000, who applied for a patent in fee for lands allotted to him on the Crow Reservation, Montana. The correspondence involves Senator Mansfield's office, the Bureau of Indian Affairs (BIA) Commissioner, and the Billings Area Office under Area Director Paul L. Fickinger.

The key document is the BIA Commissioner's letter of April 15, 1954, which explains that Bends was objecting to the Department of the Interior's policy of withholding approval of fee patent applications from competent Indians unless they included all lands in which they owned the entire interest. The Commissioner articulated the policy's purpose: "to enable the Federal Government to eliminate the responsibilities of management of trust lands owned by competent Indians whenever possible." A follow-up letter from Fickinger dated April 13, 1954, reports that the Crow Agency Superintendent discussed the matter with Bends, who agreed to have his land advertised for public sale at the next Crow Reservation land sale. An earlier letter from August 20, 1953, from Mansfield's administrative assistant James F. Sullivan, also references the case and mentions a second individual, Violette Clairmont McLeod.

This file demonstrates how BIA fee patent policy pressured individual Crow allottees toward land alienation by conditioning fee patents on inclusion of all holdings, effectively channeling trust land into public sale and facilitating dispossession under the guise of competency determinations.

summaries.display_title: 1975: Sen. Mansfield Files on Enforcement of Section 2, Crow Allotment Act of 1920
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1919–1975

This collection of correspondence, legal memoranda, and court documents from Senator Mike Mansfield's papers (Collection 65, Series X, Container 490) concerns enforcement and interpretation of Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), as amended June 8, 1940 (54 Stat. 252). Section 2 imposed acreage limitations prohibiting conveyance of Crow Indian land to any non-Indian who already owned 640 acres of agricultural or 1,280 acres of grazing land on the Crow Reservation, or whose acquisition would exceed 1,280 acres agricultural or 1,920 acres grazing. Violations rendered conveyances void and constituted a misdemeanor punishable by up to $5,000 fine and six months imprisonment.

The central legal dispute involved Homer Scott of Ranchester, Wyoming, who through companies including Flying V. Cattle Corp., Trans-West Trust Company, Padlock Ranch Corp., P.K. Ranch Corp., and Scott Land and Livestock Company allegedly amassed approximately 67,000 acres of Crow Reservation land in violation of Section 2. The Native American Rights Fund, represented by attorney Daniel H. Israel, urged Solicitor Kent Frizzell and Senators Mansfield and Metcalf to support a federal test case. Crow Tribal Chairman Pat Stands authorized NARF to initiate litigation. A separate matter involved attorney Harold G. Stanton representing Phoebe Wilson Dillon, a Crow Indian owning over 1,920 acres of trust land, seeking private relief legislation to sell large parcels to non-Indians. The BIA's Acting Deputy Director William L. Benjamin confirmed that removing acreage limitations required congressional legislation. The legal memorandum extensively argued that fifty years of non-enforcement enabled massive non-Indian land accumulation overlying valuable coal reserves, constituting systematic dispossession directly contrary to congressional intent.

summaries.display_title: 1953: Sen. Mansfield Correspondence on Fee Patent for Crow Allotment #2077 (Russell/Bullshows)
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1953–1953

This document consists of a brief exchange of correspondence from November–December 1953 between U.S. Senator Mike Mansfield (Montana, first term), attorney Harold G. Stanton of Hardin, Montana, and the Bureau of Land Management concerning the issuance of a fee patent on Crow Allotment #2077. The allotment belonged to George W. Russell (deceased), whose lands passed by will to William Russell (2/3 share) and Maxine Bullshows (1/3 share). The land is described as located in T. 2 S., R. 26 E., P.M., Section 36: Lots 1, 2, 3, 6, 7, NW¼SE¼, W½NE¼, totaling 280.81 acres. Stanton wrote Mansfield on November 18, 1953, requesting help expediting the fee patent, noting that the Billings Area Office of the Bureau of Indian Affairs had sent a request to the Bureau of Land Management on September 23, 1953. Mansfield forwarded the inquiry to BLM Administrator Edward Woozley on November 23. BLM responded that Patent No. N-41453 was executed on November 6, 1953, and had already been sent to the Bureau of Indian Affairs for notation and delivery, directing further inquiries to the Area Director in Billings. Mansfield relayed this information to Stanton on December 4, 1953. The OCR quality is poor, particularly on page 2, which is largely illegible. This exchange documents the conversion of trust-status Crow allotment land to fee simple ownership, a key legal mechanism of dispossession that removed federal trust protections and made the land alienable and taxable, often leading to subsequent loss by Indian owners.

summaries.display_title: 1953–1958: Sen. Mansfield Files on Fee Patent Applications, Crow and Blackfeet Reservations
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1953–1958

This document consists of archival folder covers and fragmentary pages from the papers of U.S. Senator Mike Mansfield of Montana, specifically from his first Senate term, housed in Collection 65, Container No. 44, Series X: Federal Agencies. The pages reference the Bureau of Indian Affairs for the period 1953–1956, with one page bearing a location of Browning, Montana (headquarters of the Blackfeet Agency), and a received stamp dated July 18, 1956. A file number partially visible reads "722.0," which in BIA filing systems typically pertains to land transactions or fee patent matters. The folder is labeled as relating to a Mansfield fee patent application from 1954.

The OCR quality is extremely poor and the document is highly fragmentary, consisting almost entirely of folder labels, partial file numbers, and date stamps rather than substantive correspondence or decisions. No specific allotment numbers, acreages, dollar amounts, individual Indian names, or statutory citations are legible in the extracted text. The designation "fee patent application" in the folder title indicates this file concerned the conversion of trust-status Indian allotments to fee simple ownership—a key legal mechanism of dispossession, as fee patents removed federal trust protections and exposed Indian landholders to taxation, debt, and alienation. The Browning, Montana reference and BIA connection suggest this involved Blackfeet Reservation lands, with Senator Mansfield's office apparently intervening or corresponding regarding a constituent's fee patent request during the termination era of federal Indian policy.

summaries.file_name: Mansfield leasing on Crow and Section 2 1960.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1960–1962: Sen. Mansfield Legislative Files on Crow Reservation Leasing Disputes and S. 2938
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1960–1962

This collection comprises legislative records, correspondence, field trip reports, and legal documents from the files of Senator Mike Mansfield (87th Congress) concerning leasing disputes on the Crow Indian Reservation in Montana. The central legislative document is S. 2938, introduced March 5, 1962, by Senators Lee Metcalf and Mansfield, to establish standard procedures for leasing noncompetent allotted farming and grazing lands on the Crow Reservation. The bill would require written applications to the Superintendent of the Crow Indian Agency at minimum appraised value during a thirty-day negotiation period, with existing lessees retaining the right to meet competing bids at oral auction.

Surrounding correspondence reveals persistent trespass and lease violations involving R. B. Fraser and associates (Civil No. 1804, U.S. District Court, Montana, Judge W. J. Jameson presiding), dating back approximately eighteen years. Clem Cormier of Billings documented cattle trespass on allotted lands in Townships 1S and 3S, Range 29E, with field reports from BIA Range Conservationist Leslie Milam and Land Operations Officer James O. Jackson identifying branded cattle on Indian-controlled land. Attorney J. F. Meglen represented the Crow Reservation Leaseholders Association. Field Solicitor A. E. Bielefeld (Department of Interior) concluded existing evidence was insufficient for contempt proceedings. Former Crow Tribal Council Chairman Edward P. Whiteman raised the issue of simultaneous lease cancellation and re-execution as early as April 1960, with the Solicitor's office still deliberating in December 1960.
