The documents collectively demonstrate how inadequate federal enforcement, bureaucratic delay, and procedural gaps in BIA leasing facilitated non-Indian operators' exploitation of allotted Crow lands. References to the Lands Title Act of 1920, Section 2, and Assistant Secretary John Carver's involvement further illustrate the entanglement of federal policy with ongoing dispossession.

summaries.file_name: Mansfield letter from Lodge Grass racist 1973.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1973–1974: Sen. Mansfield Correspondence on Crow Tribal Hunting Restrictions and Lodge Grass Tensions
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1973–1974

This file comprises correspondence between Senator Mike Mansfield (D-Montana), the Bureau of Indian Affairs, and Lyle Shick, a non-Indian resident and school superintendent of Lodge Grass, Montana, concerning tensions on the Crow Reservation. Shick's letter of October 16, 1973, addressed to Mansfield, Senator Lee Metcalf, and Representatives John Melcher and Richard Shoup, complained about the Crow Tribal Council resolution of October 13, 1973, which prohibited all hunting and fishing by non-Indians within the reservation's exterior boundaries. He also protested all-Indian rodeos at a federally funded arena at Crow Agency, proposed exclusion of non-Indian reporters from Council meetings, Indians' exemption from Montana state income tax (based on a Supreme Court decision from an Arizona case), and alleged lack of law enforcement against Indian offenders.

The BIA's Acting Director of Indian Services responded to Mansfield on March 8, 1974, confirming the Tribe's authority to regulate hunting and fishing on Indian lands, noting Montana would treat the reservation as a non-hunting area. The BIA detailed its investigation of specific incidents Shick cited, including an auto theft by two juvenile Indian suspects and a trespass/disorderly conduct case involving a 17-year-old youth and a teacher's wife, which the U.S. District Attorney declined to prosecute for assault. A preliminary BIA Billings Area Office acknowledgment dated November 29, 1973, and Mansfield's interim reply of October 30, 1973, complete the file. The documents illuminate how Crow tribal sovereignty assertions—particularly over hunting, fishing, and taxation—provoked non-Indian backlash, while the BIA defended tribal legal authority and federal jurisdictional frameworks governing reservation governance.

summaries.file_name: Mansfield misc Crow land sales, Section 2.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1959–1962: Sen. Mansfield Correspondence on Crow Reservation Land Sales and Section 2 Restrictions
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1959–1962

This collection comprises correspondence between U.S. Senator Mike Mansfield's office (particularly legislative assistant Ray Dockstader) and constituents and federal officials regarding Crow Reservation land policies during Mansfield's second Senate term (1959–1964). The documents are drawn from Mansfield's papers, Collection 65, Container 1337, Series X: Federal Agencies.

The central exchange involves Edward "Posey" Whiteman of Pryor, Montana, who wrote on February 22, 1961, requesting information about new BIA supervised land sale procedures. Mansfield forwarded the inquiry to Commissioner John O. Crow. Acting Commissioner H. Rex Lee responded on March 3, 1961, explaining that Section 121.14 of Title 25, Code of Federal Regulations (effective September 2, 1959) required written notice to the Crow Tribe 30 days before advertising trust land for competitive sale, giving the tribe first opportunity to purchase. If the owner insisted on competitive bidding, sealed bids would be measured against BIA appraisals, with the tribe given the right to match the high bid.

A parallel thread involves attorney J.F. Meglen of Billings, Montana, who in July 1962 requested copies of the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), Act of May 26, 1926 (44 Stat. 658), Act of March 3, 1927 (44 Stat. 1365), and Act of March 15, 1948 (P.L. 444, 80th Congress). Commissioner Crow's July 1962 reply also cited the 1868 Treaty (15 Stat. 619), reservation sales acts of 1882, 1891, and 1904, and statutes through 1937 eliminating lands from the reservation. A newspaper clipping notes that approximately 70 percent of the Crow Reservation was in non-Indian hands through ownership or leasing, with Roger Stops alleging violations of the 1920 Crow Allotment Act involving individuals owning up to 30,000 acres. OCR quality is poor throughout, particularly on pages 2 and 17.

summaries.file_name: Mansfield patent in fee applications including Stanton 1953.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1952–1953: Sen. Mansfield Correspondence on Patent-in-Fee Applications, Crow and Blackfeet Reservations
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1952–1953

This document is a collection of congressional correspondence from Senator Mike Mansfield's first-term files (Series X, Federal Agencies, Containers 38 and 43), consisting of letters, telegrams, and Bureau of Land Management and Bureau of Indian Affairs responses concerning patent-in-fee applications by Native American allottees on the Crow and Blackfeet Reservations. The correspondence dates primarily from 1952–1953 and involves Mansfield's office acting as intermediary to expedite fee patent issuance.

Specific cases include: William Russell (Crow Allotment #2077, 280.61 acres, T.2S., R.26E., patent No. 1141453 issued November 6, 1953); Cecelia Other Blackbird (Crow Allotment #1964, approximately 600 acres); Joseph Ten Bear (Crow Allotment #3581, 320 acres, T.7S., R.35E.); Thomas Medicine Horse (Crow Allotment #3382); Harriet Walks Over Ice; Josephine Grainger (patents Nos. 1140901–1140904 issued September 22, 1953); Hugh Plenty Hoops (Allotment 1246, patent No. 1141523); Teddy Ray Don't Walk (Allotments 3179 and 867, patent No. 1141524); Frederick Paul Knows Gun (Allotment 1486, patent No. 1141525); Edmund Littlelight (approximately 200 acres at $10/acre to buyer Karney Redman); Mary Walks LaForge; and Ned Old Bull (Crow Allotment #647). Attorney Wilbur Werner of Cut Bank identified eight Blackfeet allotments pending at the Bureau of Land Management, noting a mineral rights reservation error on Ora Cobell's Allotment #1979. Attorney Harold Stanton and County Attorney C.W. Jones of Hardin facilitated multiple requests. The documents reveal a systematic pipeline converting trust land to fee-simple title, enabling immediate sale to non-Indians, with congressional pressure accelerating the administrative process through the Billings Area Office and Bureau of Land Management in Washington.

summaries.file_name: Mansfield speck introducing S 3698 Crow Act Section 2 1956.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1956: Sen. Mansfield Statement Introducing S. 3698 to Amend Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1956

This document is a statement by Senator Mike Mansfield (D-Montana) before a Senate subcommittee on Indian Affairs, dated May 8, 1956, introducing S. 3698, co-sponsored with Senator Murray. The bill sought to amend the Act of June 4, 1920, by repealing paragraph 1 of Section 2 of that act—which governed allotment of lands of the Crow Tribe—and to validate certain prior conveyances on the Crow Indian Reservation in Montana.

Mansfield explains that Section 2 imposed 640- and 1,280-acre limitations on land transactions, originally intended to protect Crow Indians against large-scale land speculation. However, over the preceding 35 years (approximately 1920–1956), numerous purchases, fee patents, and Indian deeds had been approved and delivered without anyone—neither purchasers nor federal officials—recognizing or enforcing this acreage restriction. Large acreages had been accumulated by individual white landowners through "restricted fee" patents that reserved mineral rights. Mansfield argues that enforcing the dormant provision would destabilize titles across a large portion of the reservation, enable speculators to buy small tracts cheaply from former Indian owners and then coerce current landowners into selling at exploitative prices, and restrict competent Crow Indians' ability to sell to the purchaser of their choosing. The Crow Tribal Council approved the legislation, and Resolution No. 77, dated April 11, 1956, endorsed this approach. The bill validated prior conveyances only regarding acreage limitations, preserving Indians' rights to challenge conveyances on grounds of fraud or lack of consideration. The OCR quality is poor, particularly on pages 2–3, rendering some text illegible.

summaries.file_name: Mansfield submarginal land and a little on Crow Act Section 2.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1959–1960: Sen. Mansfield Legislative Files on Submarginal Land Transfer to Montana Indian Tribes
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1933–1960

This collection comprises legislative files from Senator Mike Mansfield's papers (Collection 65, Container 21, 86th Congress) concerning proposed federal legislation to transfer submarginal lands to Indian tribes. The central document is S. 54, introduced January 9, 1959, by Senator Murray (for himself and Mansfield, by request), to place certain lands in trust for tribes on the Fort Peck, Fort Belknap, and Blackfeet Reservations in Montana. These lands—approximately 346,370 acres across 18 tribes in nine states—were acquired by the federal government during the 1930s drought under Title III of the National Industrial Recovery Act (June 16, 1933, 48 Stat. 200), the Emergency Relief Appropriation Act of 1935 (49 Stat. 115), and related statutes. Specific Montana acreages and original costs are enumerated: Fort Peck, 85,338 acres ($412,302); Fort Belknap, 25,530 acres ($80,936); Blackfeet, 9,037 acres ($31,076). Total original cost exceeded $1,600,000, with present-day value "many times this figure." A mineral leasing special fund held $844,928.30 under the Act of August 7, 1947 (61 Stat. 913).

President Truman's 1948 veto of H.R. 3153 (returning approximately 245,000 acres placed under Interior jurisdiction by Executive Order in 1938–1939) is included. A February 1959 telegram from John Holds of the Crow Tribe's "independent group" at Crow Agency requests delay on S. 51 and S. 55. Senator Murray's January 1959 letter protests a Crow Agency land sale scheduled for January 20, 1959, arguing acreage limitations on non-Indian purchases suppressed competitive bidding, harming Indian sellers. Governor Aronson endorsed H.R. 8115 covering approximately 120,000 Montana acres ($533,000 original cost). The documents collectively demonstrate that despite decades of Indian use, full legal title remained with the United States, leaving tribes in perpetual insecurity while local interests sought tax-roll placement of these lands.

summaries.display_title: 1955–1956: Sen. Mansfield Files on Blackfeet Allottee Dispossession and Fee Patent Legislation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1939–1956

This collection comprises congressional bills, correspondence, tribal resolutions, and Bureau of Indian Affairs records relating to the dispossession of individual Blackfeet allottees through the exchange assignment system on the Blackfeet Indian Reservation in Montana. The central legislative vehicle is S. 575 (later superseded by S. 1535), introduced by Senator Mike Mansfield on January 21, 1955, directing the Secretary of the Interior to issue a fee patent to Lorraine Dennis Crawford Woods for approximately 351.36 acres—the former allotment of Stephen Afterbuffalo, Blackfeet Allottee No. 2592, located in the north half of Section 2, Township 36 North, Range 9 West, Montana Meridian.

The documents reveal a systematic mechanism of dispossession: when individual allottees exchanged lands, the Secretary of the Interior issued trust patents to the Blackfeet Tribe under Section 5 of the Indian Reorganization Act of June 18, 1934 (48 Stat. 984), while individual Indians received only revocable exchange assignments under Article VIII, Section 6 of the tribal constitution approved December 13, 1936. Commissioner Glenn L. Emmons confirmed there was "no authority of law" to reconvey these lands administratively, requiring special legislation. Woods had conveyed 320 acres plus an 80-acre homestead allotment to the Tribe on May 25, 1939, and February 3, 1939, for $100 and other consideration. Blackfeet Tribal Resolution No. 33-54 (September 2, 1954) identified twelve affected individuals and acknowledged the inequity. Attorney Dan S. Welch of Cut Bank and tribal counsel Arthur Lazarus Jr. disputed whether mineral rights should transfer, with the Tribe insisting on retaining subsurface rights per Resolution No. 80-54. The documents demonstrate how federal trust mechanisms converted individual Indian fee or trust title into tribal trust land, leaving allottees with precarious, revocable surface-only assignments—effectively dispossessing them without adequate compensation.

summaries.display_title: 1923–1931: BIA Central Classified Files — Missing Crow Agency Land Records
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1923–1931

This document consists of archival folder covers and file separators from Record Group 75, Records of the Bureau of Indian Affairs, Central Classified Files, 1907–1939. It is housed in Box No. 106 under the Crow agency designation, with two specific file numbers: 1601-1923-313 and 47831-1931-313. The classification number 313 in the BIA's decimal filing system corresponds to land-related matters, indicating these files originally pertained to Crow Reservation land transactions or administration. The finding aid references are PI-163 (Preliminary Inventory 163) and E-121 (Entry 121), with the notation HM 2001 likely referencing a later accessioning or microfilm project.

The document is overwhelmingly fragmentary. Pages 2 through 9 are either blank or contain garbled OCR artifacts—largely misread characters rendered as Japanese script—suggesting the original pages were either empty folder dividers, heavily deteriorated, or illegible to the scanning software. No substantive textual content survives in the digitized version.

What is most significant is the absence itself. The file wrappers exist but the records they should contain—Crow land administration files from 1923 and 1931—are missing. This gap in the documentary record is itself evidence relevant to Crow land dispossession, as missing BIA files make it difficult or impossible to reconstruct the specific decisions, allotment actions, or land transfers that occurred under federal authority during this period. The empty files demonstrate the archival silences that complicate efforts to document and redress Native land loss.

summaries.display_title: 1960: Sen. Mansfield Correspondence on Northern Cheyenne Land Development and School Construction
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1960–1960

This folder contains correspondence among Senator Mike Mansfield, Senator James Murray, Bureau of Indian Affairs officials, tribal leaders, and constituents regarding Northern Cheyenne land development, school construction at Busby, Montana, and the inadequacy of the Indian Revolving Credit Loan Fund. The documents span February through June 1960 and include letters, a Western Union telegram, and interagency communications.

Central to the collection is the Northern Cheyenne Tribal Council's Land Development Program, adopted by Resolution 9(60) on February 15, 1960, which sought a $550,000 loan from the revolving fund. Assistant Secretary Roger Ernst informed Mansfield that the fund was overcommitted by $1,353,797 as of February 29, 1960, making the loan impossible without new appropriations. A prior land enterprise loan of $50,000 had been approved July 2, 1959, supplemented by $39,244 in tribal funds. Mansfield and Murray introduced S. 3546 to increase the fund from $10,000,000 to $25,000,000; a companion House bill, H.R. 1150, proposed a $15,000,000 increase. Edward "Posey" Whiteman, Crow Tribal Council Chairman and Affiliated Tribes of Northwest Indians President, urged a $50,000,000 appropriation for tribal land purchases, including fractionated heirship lands and strategically located non-Indian holdings.

Regarding Busby school facilities, the Education Committee protested that $40,000 for rehabilitation was grossly inadequate for 350 students in five substandard classrooms, when $600,000 had been requested. John Woodenlegs reported that 8,894.05 acres on the Northern Cheyenne Reservation were available for tribal purchase, though patent-in-fee applications threatened to remove land from trust status. Roger Ernst's April 20, 1960 letter to Bryan Stevens acknowledged ongoing policy review regarding trust versus fee title for Indian land purchases. These documents collectively demonstrate how chronic underfunding of revolving credit, bureaucratic delays, and fee-patent mechanisms facilitated continued dispossession of tribal land bases across Montana reservations.

summaries.file_name: Murray Kronmiller letter re Section 2 legislation.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1956–1958: Attorney Kronmiller Letter to Senators Murray and Mansfield on Crow Tribal Council Resolution Regarding Section 2
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1956–1958

This document consists of a letter dated April 16, 1956, from attorney Bert W. Kronmiller, whose law offices were located in the Kronmiller Building in Hardin, Montana, addressed jointly to U.S. Senators James E. Murray and Mike Mansfield of Montana. The letter accompanies a resolution adopted by the Crow Indian Tribal Council on April 14, 1956, and requests that the senators introduce legislation in Congress based on that resolution. Kronmiller describes the matter as "very urgent" and offers to assist at any congressional hearing. The document is filed within Collection 65, Series VIII, among bills introduced by the 85th Congress, and is associated with S. 332, described as a bill "To amend Act re Allot-" — likely referencing allotment legislation affecting the Crow Reservation. The OCR quality is poor, particularly on page 2, rendering much of the contextual detail about S. 332 illegible. Pages 3 and 4 appear to be duplicate copies of the same letter.

The document demonstrates the legal and political mechanisms through which Crow tribal interests were mediated by non-Indian attorneys operating from towns adjacent to the reservation. Kronmiller's role as intermediary between the Tribal Council and federal legislators illustrates how allotment-related legislation — a primary vehicle of Native land dispossession — moved through channels that depended on outside legal counsel to navigate congressional processes, raising questions about the degree of tribal autonomy in shaping legislation directly affecting Crow lands.

summaries.display_title: 1987–1989: Litigation File, Crow Tribe of Montana v. United States — Section 2 Land Purchase Violations
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1989

This document is a composite litigation file from Civil Action No. 87-2155, *Crow Tribe of Montana v. United States of America, et al.*, filed in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia before Judge N.H. Johnson. It contains a Department of Interior Inspector General memorandum report (March 3, 1986) on purchases of Crow Indian Reservation lands, lists of Section 2 violators, Crow Tribal Council resolutions spanning 1948–1983, legal memoranda, and affidavits from Richard Real Bird (Crow Tribal Chairman) and Robert J. Swan.

The core issue is systematic violation of Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act of 1920 (41 Stat. 751), which prohibited conveyance of reservation land to any person, company, or corporation already owning 640+ acres of agricultural or 1,280+ acres of grazing land, with criminal penalties. The IG report found that from 1946 to 1955, BIA sold 111,315 acres without enforcing Section 2. A 1977 NARF study identified 56 potential violators collectively owning approximately 285,000 acres. The largest violator, Homer Scott, reportedly controlled close to 90,000 acres in fee and leases. Detailed violator lists for Big Horn and Yellowstone Counties name dozens of individuals and entities—including Campbell Farming Corporation (approximately 36,610 acres), Dan S. Scott et al. (39,890 acres), and numerous ranching operations exceeding statutory limits. The documents demonstrate that BIA never incorporated Section 2 restrictions into 25 CFR, repeatedly failed to forward test cases to the Justice Department, and that Interior officials actively obstructed enforcement, constituting a decades-long pattern of federal complicity in Crow land dispossession.

summaries.display_title: 1959–1960: House Records on Crow Tribe's Efforts to Preserve Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act (H.R. 439)
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1960

This collection from RG 233 (Records of the U.S. House of Representatives, 86th Congress) documents the Crow Tribe's fight to preserve Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), as amended June 8, 1940 (54 Stat. 252), which limited acreage non-Indians could own within the Crow Reservation. The central legislative item is H.R. 439, introduced January 7, 1959, by Representative Keith Thomson of Wyoming, with companion bill S. 55 by Senator James E. Murray, seeking to repeal Section 2 and validate all prior illegal conveyances.

Crow Tribal Council Chairman Edward P. Whiteman and the Council passed Resolution No. 191 on May 2, 1959 (306 to 5 votes), reaffirming opposition to repeal and demanding enforcement by Attorney General William P. Rogers and U.S. Attorney Krest Cyr. The record reveals that approximately 30 violators held roughly 235,000–250,000 acres of Crow allotted land illegally, with individual holdings 20 to 100 times the statutory limit. Violators formed a leaseholders' association with a $22,000 lobbying fund. Assistant Attorney General Malcolm R. Wilkey's May 28, 1959 letter declined criminal prosecution for pre-1955 violations. The Department of Interior's March 1960 report to Chairman Wayne Aspinall recommended against enactment, acknowledging decades of departmental complicity in non-enforcement. The Little Horn Indian Livestock Association's February 20, 1959 letter detailed monopolistic land-control tactics by large non-Indian operators. A January 1959 land sale showed 26,301.66 acres offered across 77 tracts, with acceptable bids on only 12,009.2 acres at $16.31 average per acre. These documents collectively prove systematic federal failure—by both Interior and Justice—to enforce protective legislation, enabling massive non-Indian land accumulation on the Crow Reservation over a 34-year period.

summaries.display_title: 1963: Senate Committee Correspondence on Crow Reservation Land Issues (Edward Whiteman Letter)
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1963–1963

This document consists of fragmentary correspondence from Record Group 46 (Records of the U.S. Senate), specifically from the Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs files (Box 13, SEN88A-F11). The OCR quality is very poor, rendering several pages nearly illegible.

The primary legible correspondence is a letter dated March 7, 1963, from Edward P. Whiteman of Pryor, Montana—a location on the Crow Reservation—to James H. Gamble, Professional Staff Member of the Senate Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs. Whiteman requests that Gamble follow up on information enclosed in a separate letter to Senator Clinton Anderson, and asks for copies of all Indian bills then in the legislative hopper along with committee and agency reports. Whiteman references political dynamics "on the tribal level" as a factor in a potential visit to Washington. Gamble's reply of March 12, 1963, confirms receipt and encloses copies of introduced Indian bills, noting no committee reports had yet been received.

A separate letter dated January 15, 1963, from Dr. Jack D. Forbes of San Fernando Valley State College's History Department to Senator Anderson proposes creating an "Indo-American University" to serve indigenous communities, linking it to Peace Corps and Alliance for Progress objectives.

While the documents do not contain specific acreages, dollar amounts, or direct legal mechanisms of dispossession, they demonstrate Crow tribal members' active engagement with the federal legislative process during a critical period of Indian policy reform, seeking information about pending legislation that could affect reservation lands and governance. The poor OCR obscures potentially significant content on several pages.

summaries.display_title: 1944–1945: Senate Records on Crow Reservation Grazing Privilege Dispute, Big Horn County
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1944–1945

This file from RG 46, U.S. Senate Records (Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs, Indian Affairs Investigating Subcommittee, Sen83A-F9), documents a dispute over grazing privileges on the Crow Reservation in Big Horn County, Montana, during 1944–1945. The core documents include a letter from the Secretary of the Interior to Senator Burton K. Wheeler (dated approximately July 6, 1945), a January 20, 1945 letter from Assistant Commissioner J.C. McCaskill to Wheeler, a December 15, 1944 letter from the Reservation Ranchers and Farmers Cooperative Association of Hardin, Montana to Secretary Harold L. Ickes, and a December 27, 1944 letter from Harry Whiteman, Secretary of the Crow Tribal Council, to Commissioner John Collier.

The central conflict involved the method of awarding five-year grazing permits—sealed bids with preference rights for existing permittees versus public auction. The incorporated cooperative association, comprising 130 stockholders (white farmers and ranchers residing on the reservation), reported $43,258.30 cash on hand plus $9,085.00 in notes and anticipated a $95,000.00 fund. They specifically referenced Unit 45 (Flatiron), situated between the Big Horn and Little Horn rivers. Superintendent Robert Yellowtail favored sealed bids; the association accused him of excluding white representatives from a December 7, 1944 Tribal Council meeting where only 49 of approximately 125 attendees voted for the sealed-bid resolution. Whiteman challenged the meeting's legitimacy. The Interior Department investigated coercion allegations regarding agency staff canvassing allottees for powers of attorney and found them unsubstantiated. Superintendent Warren L. O'Hara replaced Yellowtail. Bids were opened June 25, 1945. The documents reveal how competing white ranching interests leveraged Senate oversight to challenge Crow self-governance over tribal grasslands, while the cooperative's pooled capital threatened to consolidate non-Indian control over reservation grazing resources.

summaries.display_title: 1963: Senate Records on S. 1757 — Validating Prior Section 2 Violations on Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1963

This file documents S. 1757, introduced June 24, 1963, by Senators Mike Mansfield and Lee Metcalf (Montana), to validate, ratify, and confirm conveyances of Crow Reservation land made prior to October 18, 1957, that violated the acreage limitations in Section 2 of the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), as amended by the Act of June 8, 1940 (54 Stat. 252). Section 2 prohibited the Secretary of the Interior from approving land sales to any person already owning 640 acres of agricultural or 1,280 acres of grazing land on the reservation, and capped acquisitions at 1,280 agricultural or 1,920 grazing acres, with violations punishable by fines up to $5,000 or six months' imprisonment.

The Department of the Interior's June 14, 1963, letter to the Senate President details systematic enforcement failures: between 1946 and 1955, 21 supervised sales covering 517 tracts proceeded without any statutory compliance check, with the Area Director claiming Washington officials told him the limitations were inoperative. The total number of violations remained unknown because private fee-patent sales were unsupervised. The Department of Justice declined criminal prosecution for pre-1955 violations and took no civil action despite referral of three specific cases on April 6, 1960. The Crow Tribal Council, through Secretary Arlis Whiteman and Chairman John B. Cummins, and the National Congress of American Indians (President Walter Wetzel) strongly opposed the bill, requesting postponement and opportunity to testify. The Senate Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs reported S. 1757 favorably (Senate Report No. 680) on December 4, 1963, and it passed the Senate December 6. The bill retroactively legalized illegal land transfers, extinguishing Crow rights to challenge them and foreclosing tribal land repurchase efforts, while preserving mineral rights from conveyance.

summaries.display_title: 1957–1958: Senate Committee Files on S. 332 — Proposed Amendment to Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1958

This document is a legislative file from the Senate Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs (RG 46) concerning S. 332 of the 85th Congress, a bill to amend the Crow Allotment Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), as amended by the Act of June 8, 1940 (54 Stat. 252). The bill was introduced on January 7, 1957, by Senators Murray, Mansfield, O'Mahoney, and Barrett, at the request of the Crow Tribe pursuant to a Tribal Council resolution of April 14, 1956, signed by Chairman William Wall and Secretary Phillip Beaumont.

The 1920 Act's Section 2 prohibited conveyances of Crow Reservation land to any person owning 640+ acres of agricultural land or 1,280+ acres of grazing land, and capped acquisitions at 1,280 acres agricultural or 1,920 acres grazing, with violations punishable by $5,000 fine and/or six months imprisonment. The Department of Interior's investigation, reported by Assistant Secretary Hatfield Chilson on March 11, 1957, revealed that this provision went unenforced for 35 years (1921–1956), with "substantial" violations—both through direct fee patent sales and Bureau of Indian Affairs-supervised transactions. The Commissioner of Indian Affairs suspended all Crow Reservation land sales only upon discovering the violations. S. 332 proposed repealing the acreage limitations and retroactively validating all prior illegal conveyances, while preserving mineral interests for Indian allottees. The Bureau of the Budget (Robert E. Merriam) concurred. The bill passed the Senate on March 25, 1957. This file demonstrates systematic land accumulation by non-Indian ranchers through decades of administrative negligence, with Congress ultimately ratifying the dispossession rather than unwinding it.

summaries.display_title: 1959–1960: Senate Committee Files on S. 55 — Proposed Repeal of Section 2 Acreage Limits, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1959–1960

This document is a legislative file from Record Group 46 (U.S. Senate), specifically from the Senate Committee on Interior and Insular Affairs (Box 60, S6A-E19), concerning S. 55 of the 86th Congress, 1st Session. The bill, introduced on January 9, 1959, by Senators Murray, Mansfield, McGee, and O'Mahoney, sought to amend the Crow Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), as amended by the Act of June 8, 1940 (54 Stat. 252), by repealing acreage limitations in Section 2 that restricted how many acres on the Crow Reservation any one person could purchase or own, and by ratifying all prior conveyances made in violation of those limitations.

The Department of the Interior's report (dated circa March 1960) reveals that "numerous violations" of the acreage limitations had occurred, some in sales supervised by the Department itself, with "no adequate explanation." The Commissioner of Indian Affairs suspended all Crow land sales in late 1955 upon discovering the violations. The Department requested the Department of Justice pursue both criminal and civil actions against violators, with the BIA gathering information on "the most flagrant violators." The Crow Tribe initially requested the bill (as S. 332, 85th Congress) but reversed its position, opposing enactment via Resolution 191 (May 2, 1959), signed by over 400 tribal members who petitioned Senator O'Mahoney. Both the Interior Department and the Bureau of the Budget recommended against enactment. The OCR quality is very poor, particularly on pages 1–2, rendering much text illegible. The file powerfully documents how federal administrative failures facilitated illegal land accumulation by non-Indians on the Crow Reservation, and how the legislative process was used in an attempt to retroactively legitimize dispossession over tribal objections.

summaries.display_title: c. 1907: General Land Office Archival Box Labels for Crow Reservation Records
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1907–undated

This document appears to be a series of archival box labels or folder covers from Record Group 49 (Records of the Bureau of Land Management / General Land Office) relating to Indian reserves, specifically the Crow Reservation. The OCR quality is extremely poor, rendering much of the text illegible, but key identifying information can be discerned across the three pages.

The pages identify materials from "Unidentified Divisions" of the General Land Office, cataloged under RG 49, with references to "Records Relating [to] Reserves" and "Indian" records. The designation "Crow" (rendered once as "Craw" by the OCR) confirms these are Crow Reservation records. A date of "1907-" appears on at least two pages, suggesting the records begin around 1907, a period of intense allotment and surplus land activity on the Crow Reservation following the 1904 Crow Act. Box No. 20 is specifically identified on one page.

Despite the fragmentary nature of the text, the document's provenance within RG 49 — the General Land Office records now housed under Bureau of Land Management administrative history — is significant. The General Land Office was the federal agency responsible for processing land patents, surveying allotments, and opening "surplus" reservation lands to non-Indian settlement. That these Crow-specific records exist within RG 49 rather than RG 75 (Bureau of Indian Affairs) demonstrates the direct role of the land disposal bureaucracy in Crow dispossession, particularly during the post-1907 period when surplus Crow lands were being transferred to white homesteaders. No specific acreages, dollar amounts, or allotment numbers are legible.

summaries.display_title: 1958–1962: Department of Justice Index Cards and Routing Sheets for Crow Reservation Litigation and Correspondence
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1958–1962

This document consists of Department of Justice (RG 60) index cards and file routing sheets tracking correspondence, memoranda, and litigation records related to the Crow Indian Reservation in Montana. The records involve multiple overlapping matters: the major case *Crow Tribe of Indians of Montana v. United States*, Civil No. 214, in U.S. District Court for the District of Montana before Judge William J. Jameson; quiet title actions concerning the heirs of Eagle Turns, deceased, regarding Lots 3, 5, and 6 of Block 3, Prando Addition to St. Xavier, Montana (file 90-2-11-6755); and enforcement of Section 2 of the Crow Allotment Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), as amended in 1940.

Civil No. 214 involved a $7.5 million claim against the United States for fixing the power site value of Crow Reservation lands related to the Yellowtail Dam, Yellowtail Unit, Missouri River Basin Project. The litigation required coordination among the U.S. Attorney in Butte (Krest Cyr), the Department of Interior Solicitor's office in Billings, the Federal Power Commission's Chicago Regional Office, and appraisers from Black & Veatch in Kansas City. A $12,000 Form 25B was approved for expert witnesses.

Separately, Crow Tribal Council Chairman Edward P. Whiteman and the Council sought civil proceedings to set aside land conveyances made in violation of Section 2's acreage limitations—particularly conveyances during the mid-1950s where non-Indians acquired restricted Crow lands exceeding the 640-acre agricultural land cap. The Solicitor's office acknowledged numerous violations but questioned whether criminal prosecution was warranted, favoring civil action instead. A related case involved a permanent injunction against a person named Fraser for grazing violations on the reservation (Civil No. 1804, granted November 21, 1957). OCR quality is poor throughout, rendering portions fragmentary.

summaries.file_name: Supt Ann Narr and Stat Rep Crow 1910-1928.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1910–1928: BIA Superintendents' Annual Narrative and Statistical Reports, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1910–1928

This microfilm publication (M1011, Roll 30) reproduces the Superintendents' Annual Narrative and Statistical Reports for the Crow Reservation in Montana, spanning 1910–1928, from the Bureau of Indian Affairs (Record Group 75, National Archives). The reports were submitted by successive superintendents including Winfield W. Scott, Evan W. Estep, and others, documenting reservation administration, land status, health, education, and industrial conditions.

The documents provide extensive evidence of land dispossession through multiple mechanisms. Allotments totaling 2,439 (later expanded to approximately 3,454) covering 446,226–455,540 acres were made under the Acts of April 11, 1882, February 8, 1887 (24 Stat. 388), and May 8, 1906 (34 Stat. 182). Superintendent Estep's 1915–1917 reports catalog dozens of fee patents issued to individual Crow allottees, documenting that approximately 90–95% of patented land was sold, with proceeds overwhelmingly squandered—often within days or weeks. Specific cases include Joseph Cooper (allottee 1736), whose $4,500 was spent in the "shortest time of any allottee," and George H. Pease (allottee 1316), whose 319.93 acres sold for $10,000 with proceeds "mostly gone." Surplus tribal lands—approximately 1.75 million unallotted acres—were leased to stockmen generating roughly $158,000–$194,650 annually, funding irrigation ($1 million system), annuities ($20–$50 per capita), and administration. The reservation was diminished through cession, and repeated Congressional bills sought further opening to white settlement. The tribal herd (ID brand) reached 17,000–18,000 head worth approximately $1 million. Statistical reports detail 949 allotments under lease, 352,250 acres in grazing leases, and extensive inherited land sales at prices often below true value. OCR quality is poor on many early pages, rendering some text fragmentary.

summaries.display_title: 1933–1934: Senate Subcommittee Hearings on Conditions of Montana Indian Reservations
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1933–1934

This document is Part 31 of the published hearings of the Senate Subcommittee on Indian Affairs, conducted pursuant to S. Res. 79 and related resolutions, chaired by Senator Burton K. Wheeler of Montana. Hearings were held at Poplar (Fort Peck, October 17, 1933), Harlem (Fort Belknap, October 18, 1933), Browning (Blackfeet, October 19, 1933), and other Montana locations. Assistant Commissioner William Zimmerman Jr. accompanied the subcommittee.

At Fort Peck (2,090,000 acres total, 49% already in white ownership), Superintendent C.L. Walker testified that approximately 112,000 acres of farm land and 500,000 acres of grazing land were leased to whites at 5–8 cents per acre, with 110,000 acres of homestead entries still unpaid after 23 years. The reservation had 2,400 Sioux/Assiniboin Indians, 620 heads of families, and 570 Turtle Mountain Chippewa allotments. Reimbursable debts totaled $92,251.48 from 429 debtors; a $50,000 lump sum advanced in 1928 had seen only $5,000 repaid. Witnesses described the Fry Cattle Company grazing Indian allotments without consent, then forcing lease signatures at 5 cents per acre. Between 150–200 forced fee patents were issued by competency commissions; 14 were later canceled. Individual Indian money balances stood at $46,838.86; tribal funds at $11,654.28.

At Fort Belknap (650,000 acres, 1,300 Indians), approximately 400,000 allotted acres were leased for grazing at 8 cents per acre, yielding $35,000 annually. Reimbursable indebtedness was $99,650.52 from 365 debtors. Approximately $107,000 in tribal funds had been spent on irrigation construction, $72,000 lost operating a tribal herd, and $16,000 spent on dourine horse destruction—totaling roughly $200,000 the tribe sought reimbursed. Only two fee patents had been issued. Indian witnesses repeatedly testified they were "oversupervised," that extension agents and foresters hindered rather than helped, and that the permit/block leasing system stripped Indians of control over their own land.

At Blackfeet, the irrigation project had cost $1,914,828 in construction on 21,600 assessable acres, yet only about 10,000 acres were cultivated, with Indians farming just 227 acres. Operation and maintenance cost $30,000 annually against $10,000 in collections. Only 15 Indian families used their 40-acre irrigated allotments. Project engineer Paul Anspaugh and white settlers testified that abandonment would displace approximately 80 families and render thousands of acres of Indian grazing land valueless by eliminating water sources fed by the canal system.

The Rocky Boy Reservation (55,040 acres, 670 Indians, unallotted) leased 30,000 acres of mountain grazing at 10 cents per acre ($3,500 annually). Reimbursable agreements totaled $36,293.63 individually and $31,285.57 tribally. Superintendent Wooldridge reported 770 cattle, 506 horses, and a sawmill operation that had generated $12,052.52 in repayments against $18,711.34 in reimbursable advances.

The document comprehensively demonstrates multiple mechanisms of land dispossession: forced fee patents by competency commissions; homestead openings where payments were never collected; leasing systems (particularly the permit/block system) that stripped Indian allottees of bargaining power and allowed cattlemen like Fry & Co. to graze land without consent at minimal rates; irrigation projects that burdened Indian land with construction liens while benefiting white lessees; and reimbursable loan systems that created perpetual indebtedness. Witnesses consistently testified that civil-service rules excluded Indians from agency employment, that extension personnel were ineffective, and that the fundamental structure of federal supervision prevented Indian self-sufficiency despite adequate land and resource bases

summaries.file_name: YT Dam - RYT and Posey Whiteman Talk on Yellowtail Dam 1957.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1957: Yellowtail, Whiteman, and McDonald Talks on Yellowtail Dam at University of Montana
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1957–1957

This document is a transcription of tape-recorded talks by Robert Yellowtail (former Crow Reservation Superintendent and Stanford graduate), Edward Posey Whiteman (Crow Tribal Council Chairman), and Walter McDonald (Inter Tribal Policy Board Chairman) at the Institute of Indian Affairs, University of Montana, Missoula, approximately spring 1957. Recorded by anthropologist Carling Malouf, the talks address the proposed Yellowtail Dam on the Crow Reservation in Bighorn County, Montana.

Yellowtail details a 45-year struggle beginning around 1911–1912 over the dam site on the Big Horn River. The Crow Tribe was divided: one faction accepted $5,000,000 under Senate Joint Resolution 12 (85th Congress), introduced by Senators Murray, Mansfield, Barrett, O'Mahoney, Malone, and Anderson; another faction led by Yellowtail opposed any dam. The Secretary of the Interior characterized $5,000,000 as "totally extravagant," offering only $1,500,000 (later raised to $2,500,000). President Eisenhower vetoed the prior SJR 135 (84th Congress) on June 7, 1956. The Montana Power Company and Pacific Power and Light Company offered $1,142,000 annually for 50 years, saving the government $51,500,000 in construction costs. The dam's estimated cost was $98,000,000, with $10,850,000 budgeted for fiscal year 1957. The site encompassed approximately 5,697 acres. The Crow Reservation had shrunk from 38,530,000 acres to 1,730,000 acres through treaties of 1868, acts of 1882, 1891, 1904, and 1937. The Secretary directed the Justice Department to pursue condemnation under eminent domain. Yellowtail retained former Senator Burton K. Wheeler as counsel. The Tribal Council voted 417–267 to accept $5,000,000 via Resolution 105 on January 25, 1957. The document powerfully illustrates the dual-capacity conflict of the Secretary of the Interior—simultaneously guardian of Indian rights and head of the Bureau of Reclamation seeking tribal lands—and invokes the Lone Wolf decision (187 U.S., 1903), the Twin Cities Power Company case, and the Klamath and Sioux precedents to demonstrate systematic legal dispossession.

summaries.file_name: Yellowtail Dam - A Study In Indian Land thesis 1981.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1981: Undergraduate Thesis on Crow Land Dispossession and the Yellowtail Dam Controversy
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1743–1981

This is an undergraduate honors thesis by William M. Brooke, submitted March 24, 1981 to Carroll College's History Department, directed by Dr. Robert Swartout. It traces Crow-white relations from initial contact through the Yellowtail Dam controversy of the 1950s, arguing that the dam exemplifies systematic federal dispossession of Indian land through structural conflicts of interest within the Department of Interior.

The thesis documents the progressive reduction of Crow territory from 38,530,000 acres recognized by the 1851 Fort Laramie Treaty to approximately 1,730,000 acres by the mid-twentieth century. The 1868 Fort Laramie Treaty reduced holdings to 30 million acres at roughly five cents per acre. The 1882 Act of Congress opened lands west of Boulder Creek, shrinking the reservation from eight million to three million acres. The 1904 Dixon allotment bill opened one million additional acres. In 1894, the U.S. Court of Claims found $55,129 missing from Crow tribal funds under an unaccountable agent.

The dam controversy centered on the Bureau of Reclamation's 1951 offer of $1,500,000 for land, timber, and rights, which the Crows rejected. Solicitor Clarence Davis's 1954 reinterpretation of a 1946 act—which had prohibited construction without Crow consent—removed legal protections, contradicting Truman-era Solicitor Mastin White's 1949 opinion. Senator Murray leveraged his chairmanship of the Senate Interior Committee to compel Secretary McKay to file condemnation proceedings. President Eisenhower vetoed a $5,000,000 payment in 1956, citing Twin Cities Power Co. precedent. The Crows ultimately accepted $2,500,000 under S.J. Resolution 12, signed July 15, 1958, later receiving $2,000,000 in additional compensation, half consumed by attorneys' fees. The thesis demonstrates how overlapping jurisdiction of the BIA and Bureau of Reclamation within Interior systematically disadvantaged Indian landholders.

summaries.file_name: 1919 Crow 30377-19-312 forced fee Dec of Pol.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1919–1920: BIA Administrative File on Forced Fee Patents for Crow Allottees
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1919–1920

This document is a multi-part administrative file from the Bureau of Indian Affairs concerning the issuance of fee patents to Crow Reservation allottees under the Declaration of Policy (the 1917 policy authorizing forced fee patents for Indians deemed "competent"). On April 2, 1919, the Superintendent at Crow Agency, Montana, submitted to the Commissioner of Indian Affairs a list of Indians "of half blood or less" who had not yet received fee patents, certifying them as "mentally competent, able bodied and twenty one years of age or over." The superintendent made one exception: John Wallace, allottee #216, age 56, whom he deemed "entirely incompetent to save anything" and likely to "squander his property and be without any means of support in his old age."

The schedule names dozens of allottees with allotment numbers, legal land descriptions, and acreages on the Crow Reservation, including Alice Cashen (#2280, 160 acres), Alvin Morrison (#2348, 338.5 acres), Ben Gardner (#734, 167.89 acres), Charles Claussen (#2066), Alice Robinson (#551, 163.63 acres), Dexter Williamson (#1162, 320 acres), and many others. On June 24, 1919, the Department of the Interior approved the recommendation, and Assistant Secretary S. G. Hopkins referred the matter to the General Land Office. The General Land Office memorandum of September 15, 1919, noted complications including overlapping allotments (Josephine LaForge #1254 overlapping James LaForge #1265) and rights-of-way under the Acts of March 4, 1915, March 2, 1899, and March 3, 1901. A January 31, 1920 letter from Chief Clerk C. F. Hauke to Superintendent C. E. Asbury confirmed patents would issue shortly. A separate January 16, 1920 letter from Asbury noted that allottee Charles Wilson was "very desirous of having the land sold" and requested expedited patent issuance so Wilson could complete a sale to bidder L. R. Prater. The file demonstrates the systematic, bureaucratic mechanism by which blood quantum determinations triggered forced fee patenting, converting trust-protected Crow allotments into alienable land vulnerable to immediate sale and dispossession.

summaries.file_name: 1920 CCF Minnie Reed Williams forced fee.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1918–1920: Forced Fee Patent Case of Minnie Reed Williams, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1918–1920

This document is a letter dated April 2, 1920, from the Superintendent of the Crow Agency, Montana, to Congressman Snyder, Chairman of the House Indian Committee in Washington, D.C. It responds to allegations raised by Thomas Sion that Minnie Reed Williams and her brother Frank Reed had not been notified that patents in fee had been issued for certain inherited lands, and that steps were taken to sell the land without their knowledge.

The Superintendent reports that two estates were involved—those of Mrs. Edward Reed (the mother) and Mary Reed Shepherd (a sister)—with six heirs, all of half-Indian blood or less. Because all heirs had already received patents in fee for their own allotments and were deemed competent, the Superintendent recommended patents in fee be issued for the estate lands, removing them from Bureau of Indian Affairs supervision. The patents issued on October 24, 1918. The Superintendent states he sent notice to all heirs, including Frank Reed and Minnie Reed Williams, on November 13, 1918, followed by a second letter to Williams on January 9, 1919, and received her reply dated January 26, 1919, along with signed receipts from all heirs.

The letter dismisses the allegations as "entirely false" and characterizes mixed-blood Indians as strategically invoking ward status only when facing litigation or debt. This document illustrates the forced fee patent mechanism as a tool of dispossession: once patents issued, trust protections vanished, exposing allotted lands to sale, lease, and creditor attachment, regardless of whether heirs fully understood the consequences. The Superintendent's dismissive tone toward Indian complaints reveals institutional bias embedded in competency determinations.

summaries.display_title: 1920: Forced Fee Patenting of Richard Wallace's Crow Allotment
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1920

This file documents the forced fee patenting of Richard Wallace's Crow allotment (No. 460), consisting of the NE¼ and E½ of SW¼ of Section 14, and the W½ of NW¼ of Section 13, Township 7 South, Range 32 East of the Principal Meridian in Montana, totaling 320 acres. The file contains a letter from the Superintendent at Crow Agency dated January 22, 1920, a recommendation from the Commissioner of Indian Affairs (C.F. Hauke, Chief Clerk) to the Secretary of the Interior dated around July 13, 1920, and the Secretary's approval signed by Assistant Secretary S.G. Hopkins on July 16, 1920, directing the General Land Office to issue the fee patent.

The Superintendent's letter is remarkably candid about the coercive nature of the policy. He describes Wallace as a full-blood Indian, "about the most efficient Indian on the reservation," who sold approximately $11,000 worth of cattle that year and maintained good credit. Yet the Superintendent explicitly calls the fee patent recommendation "a sort of punishment for efficiency," noting that the most capable Indians resist fee patents because "taxes are painfully sure and regular." He acknowledges the Bureau's policy to "issue arbitrarily if necessary patents in fee to Indians, even of full blood, who have proven themselves thoroughly competent." This document is powerful evidence that competency-based fee patenting under the 1906 Burke Act functioned as a dispossession mechanism targeting the most economically successful allottees, exposing their land to taxation and potential tax-sale forfeiture regardless of the allottee's wishes.

summaries.display_title: 1927: Crow Agency Correspondence on Cancellation of Forced Fee Patents
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1927–1927

This two-part correspondence concerns implementation of the Act of February 26, 1927 (Public Law 653, 69th Congress) at the Crow Agency in Montana, dealing with the cancellation of fee patents issued to Crow Indians during the "policy patent" era. The Superintendent of Crow Agency wrote to the Commissioner of Indian Affairs on March 2, 1927, reporting that he was distributing information about the new law through District Farmers. He noted that most fee patents had been issued seven or eight years earlier (approximately 1919–1920, during the forced-fee patenting policy), and that in most cases the patented land had already been sold or mortgaged, limiting the act's applicability. The Superintendent raised critical practical questions: what recourse would Indians have to recover taxes already paid or land already sold at tax sales if patents were cancelled, and how county land records would be cleared of the now-void patents.

The Office's reply (partially legible due to poor OCR) confirmed that under the 1927 act, the Secretary of the Interior had discretion to cancel fee patents provided the patentee had not mortgaged or sold any of the land, with each case to be considered individually, including the tax liability question. The exchange documents the devastating consequences of the forced-fee patent policy, which removed trust protections from Indian allotments, exposing them to taxation, tax forfeiture sales, and alienation. By the Superintendent's own admission, most Crow allottees who received these patents had already lost their land through sale or mortgage, rendering the remedial legislation largely moot and illustrating the near-irreversible nature of the dispossession.

summaries.display_title: 1928: Correspondence on Crow Indian Wardship and Authority to Cancel Fee Patents
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1928–1928

This document consists of correspondence and internal memoranda within the Department of the Interior, Office of Indian Affairs, and the House Committee on Indian Affairs regarding the legal status of Crow Indian wardship and the authority of the Secretary of the Interior to cancel fee patents. Congressman Scott Leavitt (Montana), a member of the House Committee on Indian Affairs, forwarded a letter dated January 30, 1928, from Oliver Lion Shows, a Crow Indian of Pryor, Montana, raising questions about "non-competent Indians" and taxation. Leavitt followed up on February 21, 1928, having received no response. The Office of Indian Affairs acknowledged receipt on or around February 22, 1928, with a reply signed by Charles H. Burke, Commissioner of Indian Affairs, expressing regret for delays and promising answers.

The Solicitor's Office memorandum dated February 10, 1928, addresses the legal authority to cancel fee patents, disagreeing with paragraph 1 of a proposed letter. The Solicitor emphasized that cancellation required proof both that the Indian did not consent to patent issuance and that no formal application was made, citing an earlier opinion on the Secretary's cancellation authority. The Solicitor further noted that patents should not be canceled if liens of record existed against the land, even if the land had not been mortgaged or sold. Internal notes from Assistant Commissioner E. B. Meritt's office discuss the difficulty of defining "wardship" comprehensively, with Solicitor Patterson suggesting additions to the proposed letter. This file demonstrates the bureaucratic complexity surrounding fee patent cancellation—a key mechanism of Crow land dispossession—and the contested legal definitions of Indian wardship that determined whether allottees retained federal protections or were exposed to taxation and alienation of their lands.

summaries.display_title: 1924: Crow Tribe Petition and Protest to the President of the United States
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1924–1924

This document is a petition and protest from the Crow Tribe of Indians, Crow Reservation, Montana, addressed to the President of the United States, dated August 16, 1924. It was received by the Interior Department on August 23, 1924, and by the Indian Office on September 19, 1924, bearing file number 46942-24-302. The OCR quality is very poor, rendering significant portions nearly illegible, but the core substance is recoverable.

The petition, executed in open Council of the whole Crow Tribe (numbering approximately 1778 souls), demands the immediate removal of Superintendent C.H. Asbury from the reservation. The tribe invokes its newly conferred citizenship under the Indian Citizenship Act of June 2, 1924, asserting their rights as registered voters who intend to participate in the August 26 primaries and November election. They argue that existing treaties and statutes remain in full force despite citizenship, maintaining dual federal and state authority over reservation affairs.

The specific grievances against Asbury include: systematic antagonism toward tribal interests over seven years; mismanagement of the tribal cattle herd, which declined from an estimated 1,050–1,800 head under a prior Democratic Commissioner to near destruction, with most cattle dying in the hard winter of 1919 due to neglect; collusion with county officials—specifically Commissioner Matt Tschirgi, nephew of F.H. Henrich—to illegally gather approximately 1,600 head of Indian horses for tax collection purposes, with the actual intent of killing or selling them to hide buyers. The tribe obtained a court injunction sustained by the Thirteenth Judicial District to prevent this dispossession. The Council formally notified Asbury in writing that he was no longer desired and requested his departure within thirty days. The document demonstrates how BIA superintendents actively facilitated property dispossession through collusion with local county authorities, while tribal protests to Washington repeatedly fell on deaf ears.

summaries.display_title: 1957: Fee Simple Patent Conveying Angela Bull Horse Allotment to Bert Kearns
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1957–1957

This document is a federal fee simple patent (Patent Number 1140201) issued by the Bureau of Land Management in November 1957, conveying 280.00 acres of land from the allotment of Angela Bull Horse to Bert Kearns, identified as the purchaser. The land is located in Township 1 South, Range 27 East, Section 14, Principal Meridian, Montana, within the Crow Reservation (designated "255 Crow" in the administrative notation). The patent was issued pursuant to an order from the Bureau of Indian Affairs directing fee simple conveyance, with the BIA file number 14914-57.

The legal authority cited is the Act of June 17, 1948 (62 Stat. 476), which authorized the issuance of patents. Critically, the patent reserves to the United States, for the benefit of the Crow Tribe, all coal, oil, gas, and other mineral deposits under the Act of Congress of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751). This mineral reservation clause reflects the federal policy of severing surface and subsurface rights during allotment-era land transfers.

This document demonstrates a concrete instance of Crow allotment land passing out of Indian ownership into non-Indian hands through a BIA-authorized sale. The conversion of Angela Bull Horse's trust allotment to fee simple title for a non-Indian purchaser exemplifies the systematic dispossession mechanism by which the Crow Reservation land base was diminished—individual allotments were sold, often under BIA supervision, permanently alienating tribal land while retaining only subsurface mineral rights for the tribe.

summaries.file_name: CCF 1907-39 10322-28-313 Crow fear of forced fee patent.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1928–1930: Crow Tribe Efforts to Extend Trust Periods on Allotments and 1930 Executive Order
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1928–1930

This file from the Bureau of Indian Affairs Central Classified Files documents the Crow Tribe's effort to secure extensions of trust periods on allotments facing imminent expiration, and the federal administrative process that culminated in a presidential Executive Order signed March 12, 1930. The core correspondence runs between Superintendent Calvin H. Asbury at Crow Agency, Montana, and the Commissioner of Indian Affairs, beginning December 26, 1928. Asbury reported that Crow Indians were "quite concerned and fearful that they will be overlooked and they will get fee patents without wanting them."

The file concerns 343 allotments patented May 23, 1905, under Section 5 of the General Allotment Act of February 8, 1887 (24 Stat. 588–89), with trust periods expiring May 22, 1930, and a second group of 1,929 allotments patented in 1907 expiring in late 1932. Assistant Commissioner E.B. Meritt directed a ten-year trust extension on February 27, 1928. Of the 343 original allotments, 130 remained in trust by 1930; the allottees were deceased and lands held by heirs. Secretary Ray Lyman Wilbur recommended the Executive Order to President Hoover, noting only three estates had competent heirs.

Asbury's detailed allotment-by-allotment report recommended trust extensions for the vast majority, citing heirs' incompetence, minority status, fractionated interests (some with 30+ heirs holding shares as small as 1/1300), and land values as low as $2 per acre. He recommended fee patents for only a handful of allotments (#18, #51, #194, #267, #274, #324). A tribal council resolution from April 12, 1929, explicitly referenced the damage caused by Franklin K. Lane's forced fee patent program of 10,000 patents and cited the 1927 remedial statute authorizing cancellation of involuntary fee patents. The document powerfully illustrates how fractionated heirship, forced competency determinations, and bureaucratic delays created ongoing mechanisms of dispossession even when trust extensions were nominally available.

summaries.display_title: 1920–1961: Frank Terrel's Decades-Long Contest of Forced Fee Patent on Josephine Collins Terrel's Allotment
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1961

This file (CCF 1958-75, 16671-58-312) documents the decades-long struggle of Frank Terrel, a white man married to Crow allottee Josephine Collins Terrel (allotment No. 3137), to contest a forced fee patent issued on her 200-acre allotment on the Crow Reservation. The core document is fee patent No. 913157, issued July 26, 1923, covering the NW¼ and N½N½SW¼ of Section 14, T.3S., R.33E., Montana Meridian. The patent was issued under Section 1 of the Crow Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), after the Crow Enrollment and Competency Commission classified Josephine Collins as "competent." A schedule dated April 2, 1923, signed by Special Allotting Agent Charles E. Roblin and Superintendent C. H. Asbury, certified that 111 competent allottees, including allottee 3137, had applied in writing for fee patents.

Superintendent Robert Yellowtail reported in September 1944 that the agency found no record that the allottee ever applied for the patent, that the patent remained undelivered in agency files, and that the land had been taken by Big Horn County via tax deed in 1937. Despite this, the Commissioner's office (Director of Lands, September 4, 1944) and subsequent officials—including Acting Commissioner W. Barton Greenwood and Commissioner Glenn L. Emmons—consistently refused cancellation, citing the presumption of regularity in official action (Dickson v. Luck Land Co., 242 U.S. 371; Nofire v. United States, 164 U.S. 657) and holding that title passed upon issuance regardless of delivery (United States v. Schurz, 102 U.S. 378). The Act of February 26, 1927 (44 Stat. 1247), authorizing cancellation of fee patents issued without consent, was deemed inapplicable because records showed a written application. Congressional inquiries from Senator Mike Mansfield and Representative Wesley D'Ewart were answered with the same position. The file reveals a classic dispossession mechanism: competency classification, forced fee patenting, tax forfeiture, and bureaucratic refusal to remedy the loss despite the allottee's persistent refusal to accept the patent and credible allegations of forgery.

summaries.display_title: 1920–1951: Dispossession of Josephine Collins Terrel Through Forced Fee Patent, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1920–1951

This file documents the prolonged dispossession of Josephine Collins Terrel (also Josephine Collins), Crow allottee No. 3137, through a forced fee patent on the Crow Reservation, Montana. The central record is fee patent No. 913157, issued July 26, 1923, covering 200 acres (NW¼, N½ N½ SW¼, Sec. 14, T. 3 S., R. 33 E., P.M., Montana) of her 640-acre allotment. The Bureau of Indian Affairs maintained that Collins applied in writing for the patent under Section 1 of the Crow Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), along with 110 other "competent" allottees, with the application approved by the Assistant Secretary on June 7, 1923. The allottee consistently denied making any such application. In an August 17, 1944 affidavit from Reed Point, Montana, she swore she never requested a fee patent and did not want one.

The patent was never delivered to or accepted by Collins; it remained in the Crow Agency files with no indication of recording. Despite this, Big Horn County sold the 200 acres for delinquent taxes, taking a tax deed in 1937 in the name of Big Horn County c/o M.H. Tschirgi et al. The BIA, in letters from Director of Lands M.M. Critchfield (September 4, 1944), Assistant Commissioner E.J. Utz (March 1951), and others, repeatedly refused cancellation, citing *United States v. Schurz* (102 U.S. 878) to argue that title passed upon issuance regardless of delivery. They acknowledged the Act of February 26, 1927 (44 Stat. 1247) authorized cancellation of patents issued without consent, but deemed it inapplicable because records showed a written application. Senator Karl E. Mundt intervened in February 1951 on behalf of Frank Terrel. The file powerfully illustrates how the forced fee patent mechanism—combined with bureaucratic refusal to credit allottee testimony—facilitated alienation of Indian land to non-Indian interests through tax forfeiture.

summaries.display_title: 1919: Federal Trust Patent Issued to Heirs of Crow Allottee Crack of the Gun
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1919–1919

This document is a federal trust patent (Patent Number 670054) issued by the United States to the heirs of an individual Crow Indian identified as "Crack of the Gun, or Takuse," a member of the Crow Reservation in Montana. The patent was signed by President Woodrow Wilson and bears the seal of the General Land Office. Based on the reference to the 143rd year of independence and the month of March, the document dates to March 14, 1919. The allotment schedule number appears to be 9617918.1.0, with additional file references 41008-R and 826909.

The patent allots the east half of the northwest quarter of Section 7, Township 3 South, Range 27 East of the Montana Meridian, Montana, comprising eighty acres. The land is held in trust by the United States for a period of twenty-five years for the sole use and benefit of the allottee's heirs, after which a fee patent would be conveyed. The document specifies that if the allottee died before the trust period expired—as evidently occurred here, since the patent issues to heirs—the Secretary of the Interior would ascertain legal heirs and either issue fee patent or sell the land for their benefit. A right of way for government-constructed ditches or canals is reserved from the allotment.

This patent demonstrates the standard Dawes-era allotment mechanism applied on the Crow Reservation, converting communal tribal land into individualized parcels subject to federal trust restrictions, ultimately facilitating alienation and dispossession upon fee patenting.

summaries.file_name: Fee Patent 457501, Firebird on Stanton list.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1915: Fee Simple Patent Conveying Crow Allotment Land to Herbert A. Shaw
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1915–1915

This document is a federal land patent (Patent Number 457501) issued under the authority of President Woodrow Wilson on February 12, 1915, granting 115.98 acres to Herbert A. Shaw. The patent was recorded at the Billings, Montana, land office (Billings 01547) and conveyed Lots two and three and the north-west quarter of the northwest quarter of Section 22, Township 1 North, Range 33 East of the Montana Meridian, Montana. The legal basis cited is the Homestead Act of May 20, 1862 ("To Secure Homesteads to Actual Settlers on the Public Domain") and its supplemental acts. The patent grants the land to Shaw and his heirs and assigns forever, subject to vested water rights and a reserved right of way for ditches or canals constructed by federal authority.

The document's significance lies in its connection to the Crow Reservation context indicated by the archival collection and the notation "Firebird on Stanton list" in the file name, suggesting this patent may relate to lands opened from the Crow Reservation for homesteading by non-Indians. Range 33 East of the Montana Meridian falls within or near historical Crow Reservation boundaries. The issuance of a homestead patent on such land demonstrates the mechanism by which federal policy converted reservation territory into fee-simple holdings for white settlers, directly facilitating Native land dispossession through the legal apparatus of the General Land Office. The OCR quality is moderate, with some garbled text but key details remain legible.

summaries.display_title: 1914: Fee Simple Patent Conveying Crow Allotment of Gun That Sings to Carl W. Gross
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1914–1914

This document is a federal fee simple patent (Patent Number 425890, General Land Office record 443298, file 69482-14) issued to Carl W. Gross for the southeast quarter of the southwest quarter of Section 22, Township 8 South, Range 35 East of the Montana Meridian, Montana, comprising forty acres. The patent was signed by President Woodrow Wilson on the thirteenth day of August 1914, in the 139th year of American independence. It was executed by M. K. Bulock (or Buleck) as Acting Secretary, and recorded by an Acting Recorder of the General Land Office. The document specifies that the patent was issued pursuant to an Order of the Secretary of the Interior deposited in the General Land Office directing issuance of a fee simple patent. The grant conveys full fee simple title to Gross and his heirs and assigns forever, with a reservation for rights of way for ditches or canals constructed by federal authority.

The location within Township 8 South, Range 35 East of the Montana Meridian places this parcel within or near the Crow Reservation in south-central Montana. The conversion from trust or restricted status to fee simple patent was a key mechanism of Native land dispossession, as fee simple title removed federal trust protections, making the land alienable and subject to taxation, sale, and seizure. This patent demonstrates the administrative process by which reservation lands passed out of Indian control through Secretarial orders authorizing fee patents, likely under the General Allotment (Dawes) Act of 1887 or subsequent legislation. The OCR quality is moderate, with some garbled text in the formulaic patent language.

summaries.display_title: 1914: Fee Simple Patent Conveying Crow Allotment of Gun That Sings to A Two Bars Cattle Co.
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1914–1914

This document is a federal fee simple patent (Patent Number 444745) issued under the authority of President Woodrow Wilson on November 27, 1914, granting land from the allotment of a Crow individual named Gun That Sings to the A Two Bars Cattle Co. The patent conveys the northeast quarter of the southwest quarter of Section 26, Township 8 South, Range 35 East of the Montana Meridian, Montana, comprising forty acres. The transaction is classified as a sale (noted as "Sale" with reference number 466680 and file number 74456-14), executed pursuant to an Order of the Secretary of the Interior deposited in the General Land Office directing issuance of fee simple title to the purchasing cattle company.

The legal mechanism at work is the allotment-to-fee-patent process, whereby trust-protected Indian allotment land was converted to fee simple status and sold to non-Indian purchasers. The patent grants the land with full rights, privileges, and appurtenances to the claimant and its successors and assigns forever, reserving only a right of way for federally constructed ditches or canals. This document is direct evidence of how Crow Reservation allotments were systematically transferred to white-owned ranching enterprises. The involvement of a cattle company as purchaser illustrates the role of the livestock industry in dispossessing individual Crow allottees of their land base during the early twentieth century, facilitated by federal administrative action at the highest levels of government.

summaries.display_title: 1955: Fee Simple Patent Conveying Jessie Cloud Allotment to Ed Kopac
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1955–1955

This document is a federal fee simple patent (Patent Number 1155055) issued on October 18, 1955, by the Bureau of Land Management, conveying 84.53 acres of land formerly allotted to Jessie Cloud, a Crow tribal member (allotment reference "249 Crow"), to Ed Kopac, identified as the purchaser. The land is located in Township 3 South, Range 32 East, Principal Meridian, Montana, specifically Section 23, Lot 1 (or partial lot in the NE¼NE¼) and Section 24, Lot 5, within or near the Crow Reservation. The patent was issued under authority of the Act of June 17, 1948 (62 Stat. 476), which authorized the Bureau of Land Management to issue patents on direction from the Bureau of Indian Affairs. The BIA reference number 12861-55 confirms the administrative chain: the BIA ordered the fee patent, and BLM executed it, transferring the land out of trust status and into fee simple ownership by a non-Indian purchaser.

This document exemplifies a key mechanism of Crow Reservation land dispossession: the conversion of trust allotments to fee patents followed by sale to non-Indian buyers. The allotment of Jessie Cloud was removed from federal trust protection and permanently alienated from tribal ownership. The relatively small acreage of 84.53 acres is consistent with fractional allotment parcels on the Crow Reservation. No purchase price is recorded on the patent itself. The document is partially legible due to OCR limitations but the essential details—patentee, acreage, legal description, date, and statutory authority—are discernible.

summaries.display_title: 1913: Fee Simple Patent Conveying Crow Allotment Land to T. P. Walter
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1913–1913

This document is a federal fee simple patent (Patent Number 217659, Record Number 332293) issued under the authority of President Woodrow Wilson on May 9, 1913, conveying land to T. P. Walter. The patent transfers 74.30 acres comprising the northeast quarter of the northeast quarter and Lot 1 of Section 7, Township 2 North, Range 28 East of the Montana Meridian, Montana—land situated within or adjacent to the Crow Reservation. The conveyance was initiated by an Order of the Secretary of the Interior deposited in the General Land Office, indicating an administrative sale process (noted as "Sale" on the form, reference 97153-11). The patent contains detailed metes-and-bounds exceptions for rights-of-way for ditches or canals, with specific strip widths (10, 15, and 25 feet) reserved for irrigation infrastructure constructed by federal authority, reflecting the reclamation-era policy of embedding water infrastructure easements into land patents.

This document evidences the conversion of formerly reserved or trust land into fee simple private ownership, a key mechanism of Native land dispossession during the allotment and surplus-land era. The 1913 date places it squarely within the period of accelerated fee patenting under the Burke Act (1906) and related policies that facilitated transfer of reservation lands to non-Indian purchasers. The patent's location in Range 28 East of the Montana Meridian corresponds to Crow Reservation geography, confirming that this parcel was alienated from the tribal land base through federally sanctioned sale. Some OCR degradation is present but the substantive content is largely legible.

summaries.file_name: Stanton Collection Series 4 Summary of Forced Fee patents.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1903–1959: Summary of Forced Fee Patent Cases on the Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1903–1959

This document is a multi-page tabular summary titled "Summary of Forced Fee Patent Cases on the Crow Reservation," cataloged as Stanton Collection Series 4, with case file numbers SL C52 202 165 through SL C52 202 712. It systematically records over 500 individual forced fee patent cases, each entry listing the allottee's name, allotment number, case number, land description with legal subdivision and acreage, type of case (designated "FP" for fee patent or "LS" for land sale/deed), fee patent number, and date issued. The document is undated and unsigned but appears to be a researcher's or advocate's compilation drawn from Bureau of Indian Affairs records.

The allottees are overwhelmingly Crow (Apsáalooke) individuals bearing traditional names—Takes Enemy, River Crow, Buffalow Calf, Kills Six Men, Woman Chief, Digs Well Known, Medicine Snake, and hundreds more—alongside individuals with English-language names such as William C. Pickett, Joseph Martinez, and Albert Anderson. Acreages range from as small as 4.53 acres (Strikes The Man, allotment 943) to 1,040 acres (Gerald Cecil Powers, allotment 3462; Charles Edward Yarlott, allotment 3658). Fee patent dates span from 1903 (allotment 1666, deed dated 4/28/03) through 1959 (allotment 3, deed 7/22/59). Many patents were issued in concentrated batches—notably July 26, 1923, when dozens of fee patents numbered in the 913,000 series were issued simultaneously, suggesting an administrative campaign of forced patenting.

The legal mechanism documented is the forced fee patent process authorized under the Burke Act of 1906 (amending the Dawes General Allotment Act of 1887), which allowed the Secretary of the Interior to issue fee simple patents to allottees deemed "competent," thereby removing trust protections and subjecting the land to taxation and alienation.
