The materials appear to have been assembled circa October 1984 as an advocacy document urging enforcement of Section 2, referencing also the June 8, 1940 amendment (54 Stat. 252). The OCR quality is poor, but substantive content is largely recoverable.

The document lists approximately 25 Crow Tribal Council resolutions passed between February 22, 1948 and October 13, 1984, including Resolution Nos. 63-22A, 63-22B, 65-12, 65-14, 68-03, 74-10, 75-24, 79-05, 79-34, 82-22, 82-29, and 83-33, all directing attorneys or federal agencies to enforce Section 2 or investigate violations. The compiler notes that despite 36 years of tribal action, neither the Interior Department, BIA, nor Justice Department followed through. Homer Scott is singled out as owning close to 90,000 acres in fee on the reservation. Detailed violator lists for Big Horn County and Yellowstone County name dozens of individuals and entities with specific acreages: Scott, Dan S. et al. (39,889.97 acres combined entries); Burke/Peterson/Leading Cattle Co. (41,172.2 acres); Campbell Farming Corp.; Consolidation Coal Co./Conoco; and many others holding between 2,000 and 36,000+ acres. The document explicitly connects non-enforcement to loss of tribal jurisdiction and sovereignty, citing the 1981 Big Horn River case.

summaries.file_name: Stanton Collection Series 4 Summary of Forced Fee patents.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1906–1959: Tabular Summary of Forced Fee Patent Cases on the Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1906–1959

This document is a comprehensive tabular summary of forced fee patent cases on the Crow Reservation in Montana, cataloged as Stanton Collection Series 4 (case numbers SL C52 202 165 through SL C52 202 712). It systematically records over 500 individual cases of land alienation from Crow allottees, listing each case by allotment number, allottee name, land description with legal subdivisions, acreage, case type (FP for fee patent or LS for land sale/deed), fee patent number, and date issued.

The cases span from approximately 1906 through 1959, with heavy concentrations in 1909–1916 and the early-to-mid 1920s—periods corresponding to the Burke Act (1906) and the forced fee patenting policy under Commissioner Cato Sells' "Declaration of Policy" (1917). Many patents were issued without allottee consent, as indicated by the "forced fee" designation. Acreages range from as small as 4.53 acres (allotment 943, Strikes The Man) to 1,040 acres (allotments in the 3000s series), with most parcels between 40 and 320 acres. Named allottees include Takes Enemy, River Crow, Miller Big Medicine, the Pease family (George, Sarah, Benjamine, Oliver, Ashabah, Leatus), the Piegan family, the Scott family, and hundreds of others bearing traditional Crow names.

The document constitutes a systematic administrative record proving the scale and bureaucratic mechanism of Crow land dispossession through forced fee patenting, which removed trust protections and exposed allotments to taxation, debt, and sale, resulting in massive land loss across the reservation.

summaries.file_name: Stanton Collection Series 5 individual cases part 1.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1952–1960: BIA Correspondence and Fee Patent Applications for Individual Crow and Northern Cheyenne Allottees
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1952–1960

This collection comprises Bureau of Indian Affairs correspondence, fee patent applications, lease agreements, congressional bills, chattel mortgages, promissory notes, and related financial documents pertaining to individual Crow and Northern Cheyenne allottees' attempts to obtain patents in fee or otherwise dispose of their trust lands. The OCR quality is poor throughout, with significant character corruption.

The core cases involve George Takes Gun (Allotment #47), who in 1957 obtained a fee patent on 80 acres and sold it to the Campbell Farming Corporation at $23.00 per acre, then was denied a patent in fee on his remaining 200 acres by both the Crow Agency Superintendent and the Billings Area Office in 1959–1960; Amy Old Bull (Allotment #927), whose individual patent bill S. 3298 (82nd Congress) and S. 165 (83rd Congress) failed, leading to Public Law 49 (67 Stat. 42, June 4, 1953) repealing homestead restrictions on Crow allotments, after which her fee patent application was still denied and she received only $2,940.40 from a supervised sale; Alex Medicine Horse Sr. (Allotments #3397, #3379, #307, #2602), denied a fee patent in 1955 for negotiating leases at 11¢/acre below market rates, who received a patent on 40 acres (SW¼SW¼, Sec. 24, T.2S., R.34E.) in 1954 and subsequently incurred debts including a $500 bank note, $150 chattel mortgages, and $3,230 in funeral expenses after a 1956 railroad crossing accident killing four family members; Florence Bad Boy Old Dwarf (Allotment #631, 1,794.36 acres denied); Frances Gardner Old Dwarf (Allotment #3222, 1,040.86 acres denied after $10,000 in prior land sale proceeds); Genevieve Big Shoulder Bravo (Allotment #3067, 1,040 acres denied); Guy Bulltail (Allotment #1378, denied 1958); and Frank Edward Lobdell (Allotment #3366, 560 acres). The documents reveal a systematic paternalistic regime in which BIA officials denied fee patents based on subjective competency determinations—citing insufficient education, poor lease negotiation, lack of employment initiative, or past spending patterns—while simultaneously recommending supervised sales that transferred land out of Indian ownership with proceeds programmed by the Superintendent. Leases to non-Indian entities like Reservation Ranchers and Farmers Cooperative Association at rates as low as 18¢/acre further demonstrate the extractive economics underlying trust administration. The legal framework invoked includes the Act of May 8, 1906 (34 Stat. 182), the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), and Secretarial Order No. 2508, all administered to maintain federal control while facilitating land alienation on terms unfavorable to allottees.

summaries.file_name: Stanton Collection Series 5 individual cases part 2.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1950–1959: BIA Fee Patent Applications and Land Transactions for Individual Crow Allottees
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1950–1959

This collection comprises Bureau of Indian Affairs fee patent applications, land classifications, correspondence, deeds, mortgages, financial ledgers, and related legal documents pertaining to individual Crow Indian allottees on the Crow Reservation in Montana. The documents involve multiple cases processed through the Crow Indian Agency (Superintendent L.C. Lippert) and the Billings Area Office.

Key cases include Jacob Stands Over Bull (Allotment Nos. 2113 and 3257), who received a fee patent in August 1954 for 160 acres (NW¼ Sec. 20, T.6S., R.28E.) under the Act of May 8, 1906 (34 Stat. 182), and subsequently conveyed this land by warranty deed to Harold G. Stanton for $1.00 in May 1955. Stands Over Bull also executed a mortgage to Stanton for $2,485.00 on the Schroeder ranch property. His wife Lena Lion Shows Stands Over Bull's application for a fee patent on 720 acres (Allotment No. 3342) was denied by the Area Director in January 1955, citing prior dissipation of nearly $4,000 from recent land sales. Joseph Mountain Pocket #2's applications for fee patents on 952 acres (Allotment No. 3400) and 320 acres (Allotment No. 3399) were denied by both Superintendent Lippert (November 8, 1954) and the Billings Area Office (May 27, 1955), citing failure to build a home as promised and inability to manage business affairs. John E. Schroeder received a fee patent (No. 1165153, October 1956) for 400 acres (T.9S., R.33E.), appraised at $14,500, which Stanton marketed for sale at $15,800–$20,000. Hannah Comes From Above's Allotment No. 1386 (640 acres, Sec. 15, T.7S., R.33E.) received a land use capability classification in May 1955. The documents collectively demonstrate how fee patents under the 1906 Burke Act, combined with attorney involvement and debt structures, facilitated rapid alienation of Crow allotment lands from Indian ownership. Mineral rights were reserved to the Crow Tribe per the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751). The OCR quality is poor throughout, rendering many handwritten entries partially illegible.

summaries.file_name: Stanton Collection Series 5 individual files part 3.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1953–1963: Fee Patents, Deeds, and Land Transactions for Crow Reservation Allottees (Stanton Law Firm Files)
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1953–1963

This collection comprises fee patent issuances, warranty deeds, contracts for deed, escrow agreements, assignment documents, and correspondence involving multiple Crow Reservation allottees and their land transactions, processed through the Bureau of Indian Affairs Billings Area Office and the law firm Stanton, Wilson & Hovland of Hardin, Montana.

Key transactions include: Henrietta Pretty On Top's contract for deed (October 13, 1954) to purchase Lot 6, Block 6, Hill's Addition, Lodge Grass, Montana, from Frank A. and Cora J. Sharp for $550.00 at $20.00/month, with $260.00 paid by March 1957 and $290.00 remaining. The Sharps later assigned this contract to the Montana State Department of Public Welfare. A 1963 Crow Family Plan memorandum authorized $1,600.00 from Pretty On Top's agency funds for a deed in the names of John J., Harold Jackson, and Dale Dean Prettyontop.

Joseph Ten Bear received Fee Patent No. 1149250 (January 19, 1955) for 720 acres in Secs. 15 and 22, T.6S., R.30E., valued at $5,760.00, with minerals reserved for the Crow Tribe under the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751). He immediately conveyed this land via warranty deed to Richard B. Hovland for $1.00 plus "other good and valuable consideration," who reconveyed to Harvey W. Willcutt for the same nominal sum—a pattern suggesting below-market dispossession.

May LaForge (also May Sun Goes Slow) received Patent No. 1142598 for 440.12 acres (Secs. 5-6, T.9S., R.38E.), valued at $3,840.00, and entered a contract for deed with P.K. Ranch, Incorporated for $4,401.20. A separate contingent fee contract with Stanton entitled the attorney to 33⅓–50% of any recovery from a disputed $2,500.00 income assignment to George Cooley on the Old Beaver Allotment.

The Kitty Belle Fisher case documents a prolonged dispute (1956–1960) over whether a Montana Public Assistance lien of $1,043.00 attached to her NW¼, Sec. 35, T.3S., R.42E., sold to Johnny Peters in February 1958. Attorney Thomas Mahan ultimately ruled the lien unenforceable because the fee patent's statutory protection under 24 Stat. 388 and 34 Stat. 182, 325 barred pre-patent debts, and Fisher was off assistance before the sale.

Kenneth Phillip Brien received Fee Patent No. 1176737 (November 25, 1957) for 160 acres (NE¼, Sec. 29, T.4N., R.36E.) and was immediately solicited by Stanton to sell to Rich Hope for $500.00. LeRoy Riggles received Northern Cheyenne patents (Nos. 1185792 and 1185810, August 1958) for land in Sec. 35, T.3S., R.38E., and Stanton promptly wrote offering to purchase. These documents collectively demonstrate a systematic pattern: BIA competency determinations triggered fee patents under authority delegated by Secretarial Order No. 2508 (14 F.R. 258-260) and Commissioner's Order No. 551, after which the attorney facilitating the patents or holding escrow immediately brokered sales at nominal or below-market prices, with mineral rights permanently reserved to the Crow Tribe, effectively separating allottees from both surface and subsurface wealth.

summaries.file_name: Stanton Collection Series 5 individual files part 5.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1954–1960: Fee Patents and Land Transactions of Perry Pretty Paint and Philip White Clay, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1954–1960

This collection comprises financial ledgers, fee patents, mortgages, contracts for deed, legal correspondence, tax receipts, court summons, and satisfaction of mortgage documents primarily concerning two Crow tribal members—Perry Pretty Paint (and his wife Sarah Bull Chief Pretty Paint) and Philip White Clay (and his wife Joyce/Doris White Clay)—and their interactions with attorney Harold G. Stanton of Stanton, Wilson & Hovland in Hardin, Montana.

Six fee simple patents were issued to Perry Pretty Paint on June 1, 1956, covering Crow allotments #3468, #2257 (Sits Down Far Away, 40 acres), #1087 (Woman On Top Of The Mountain, 80 acres), #1019 (Brings Horses, 20 acres), #139 (Henry Strong, 160 acres), and #1086 (Pretty Paint, deceased), aggregating over 700 acres across T.1S.–T.2S., R.34E.–R.37E. These patents were issued under Secretarial Order No. 2508 (1949) and Commissioner's Order No. 551 (1951), invoking the Dawes Act (24 Stat. 388) and amendments. Philip White Clay received a patent for 120 acres (Allotment #840, Percy Blackhair) on January 12, 1955, with irrigation liens reserved under the Acts of March 7, 1928 and June 4, 1920.

Upon receiving fee patents, both families rapidly accumulated debt. Perry Pretty Paint's ledger with Hardin Auto Company shows purchases of multiple vehicles (1954 F250 pickups, 1956 and 1957 Country Sedans) totaling $9,690.61 by October 1960. Philip White Clay mortgaged his NE¼NE¼, Sec. 25, T.2S., R.34E. to Stanton for $1,300 at 7% interest in August 1955; Stanton assigned this mortgage to Shirasago Farming Corporation for $1,300 in July 1956. Shirasago also held a seven-year lease on White Clay's land and executed a $285 mortgage and a contract for deed at $1,806.60 for a portion of the same quarter-section. White Clay sold approximately 35 acres to Shirasago. Perry Pretty Paint sold 80 acres of minerals and conveyed surface land to Scott Land & Livestock Company. Tax receipts show delinquent property taxes for 1955–1957 on the NE¼NE¼ in Big Horn County. The documents collectively demonstrate the systematic mechanism of dispossession: BIA-approved competency determinations led to fee patents, which immediately exposed allottees to debt, vehicle financing, mortgages, tax obligations, and land sales to non-Indian operators—all facilitated through a single attorney who simultaneously represented the Indian landowners and held mortgages on their property.

summaries.file_name: Stanton Collection Series 5 individual files part 6.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1953–1958: Crow Allotment Land Sales, Patent-in-Fee Applications, and Attorney Harold Stanton's Role
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1953–1958

This collection comprises correspondence, legal instruments, BIA records, probate orders, patent-in-fee applications, warranty deeds, mortgage deeds, lease assignments, and a lengthy legal brief on champerty and maintenance, all relating to Crow Reservation allotments in Big Horn County, Montana. Attorney Harold G. Stanton of Hardin, Montana, is the central figure, acting as intermediary in land transactions involving multiple Crow allottees.

Key transactions include: Sam Horn and Mary Ann Bear Below Horn (Allotments #2000 and #3280, T.4S., R.27E., Sec. 22-24) assigned $400 in crop-share lease income to Stanton on June 10, 1955, and later mortgaged all crop shares to Adolph Schaak for $1,000 at 6% (November 28, 1956). A November 29, 1954 letter from Mary Ann Bear Below alleges David Haun promised her a washing machine and pickup release in exchange for declining a sale of Nicholas Bear Tail land. Maxine Bull Shows (Allotment #3498, 849.09 acres, T.4S. R.25E. and T.6S. R.28E.) received a patent in fee in 1958 and immediately executed a $3,266.69 mortgage deed at 8% to Robert H. Wilson, with future advances up to $4,000. Myrtle Woodtick (Allotment #3645, 320 acres, T.6S. R.27E. Sec. 25 S½) received Patent No. 1175893 in October 1957; Stanton then sold her land to Richard R. Hovland for nominal consideration, reserving Crow Tribe minerals per the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751). Perry Howe (Allotment #2, 600 acres) received a patent in fee in September 1954 (land valued at $9,000); Stanton purchased the SE¼NE¼, Sec. 19, T.3S. R.33E. and resold it to Conrad Schanaman for $2,500, retaining a 20/40ths mineral interest. The Cecelia Other Blackbird Birdhat probate (Allottee #1964, estate appraised at $25,243.58) distributed fractional heirship interests across twelve heirs. Sampson Birdhat's patent-in-fee application (Allottee #2120, 360 acres) and Cecelia Other Blackbird's application (360 acres) detail modest incomes and dependents. The Superintendent initially denied Myrtle Woodtick's 640-acre patent application, noting she had already received over $7,000 from prior land sales.

The legal brief, prepared for the "Crow Indian Association," analyzes champerty and maintenance doctrines under Montana law (Sections 13-801, 93-2107, 93-2108 R.C.M. 1947), exploring whether non-Indian purchasers acquiring allotment land from Indians for purposes of litigation could face legal challenge. The documents collectively demonstrate a systematic pattern: BIA issuance of fee patents removed trust protections; allottees immediately encumbered or sold land through Stanton's office at prices well below appraised values; crop-share assignments and mortgages extracted income streams; and the legal infrastructure—including the Act of June 4, 1920, the Dawes Act (24 Stat. 388), and delegated Secretarial Orders No. 2508 and No. 551—facilitated rapid alienation of Crow trust lands into non-Indian ownership.

summaries.file_name: Stanton Collection Series 5 individual files part 7.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1953–1958: Conversion of Crow Trust Allotments to Fee Simple Patents and Sale to Non-Indians
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1953–1958

This collection documents the conversion of Crow Reservation trust allotments to fee simple patents and their subsequent sale to non-Indian purchasers, primarily handled by attorney Harold G. Stanton of Stanton, Wilson & Hovland, Hardin, Montana. Two principal transactions are documented in detail.

First, Susie Spotted Bullchief (Allotment No. 315, Crow) received a fee patent (Patent No. 1178865) dated January 22, 1958, for 600 acres across multiple sections in T. 4 S., R. 31 E., T. 7 S., R. 31 E., and T. 4 S., R. 32 E., issued pursuant to the Act of June 17, 1948 (62 Stat. 476), with minerals reserved for the Crow Tribe under the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751) on 400 of those acres. The Billings Area Office (Percy E. Melis, Area Director) processed the application under Secretarial Order No. 2508. Within days, Stanton arranged the sale via warranty deed to Richard B. Hovland for a nominal $1.00 consideration, recorded in Big Horn County Book 455, Page 187. Stanton simultaneously paid Bullchief's hospital bill ($69.55) and a $17.00 medical debt from the transaction proceeds.

Second, Paul Nomee (heir of Nellie Stewart, Allotment No. 1232, Crow) received a fee patent for 80 acres (S½SE¼, Sec. 22, T. 5 S., R. 34 E.) in July 1957, with all minerals reserved for the Crow Tribe. The land was appraised at $7–$10 per acre. Nomee conveyed it by warranty deed to Hovland on August 2, 1957, again for $1.00.

A legal memorandum by Robert S. Jones of Longan & Jones analyzes champerty and maintenance doctrines under Montana Code §67-1506 R.C.M. 1947, strategizing how to defend third-party conveyances of Indian land against possessory challenges. Philip Mountain Bull's 160-acre fee patent (Allotment No. 3683/2596, NW¼ Sec. 35, T. 3 S., R. 26 E.) was solicited for bids at approximately $2,000, with Stanton actively marketing it to multiple non-Indian buyers. These documents collectively demonstrate a systematic pattern: BIA approval of fee patents, immediate attorney-facilitated sales at minimal consideration, and legal maneuvering to secure non-Indian title against challenge.

summaries.file_name: Stanton Collection Series 5 individual files part 8.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1955–1957: BIA Fee Patent Applications for Pearl Big Shoulder Gardner and Shirley Oldhorn, Crow Reservation
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1955–1957

This collection of documents from the Stanton Collection traces the Bureau of Indian Affairs' handling of patent-in-fee applications and lease arrangements involving Crow Reservation allottees, primarily Pearl Big Shoulder Gardner and Shirley Oldhorn. The OCR quality is poor throughout, with many pages fragmentary or nearly illegible.

The central case involves Pearl Big Shoulder Gardner of Lodge Grass, Montana, whose application for a patent in fee covering her allotment No. 3068, comprising 1,040 acres on the Crow Reservation, was denied at every administrative level. Superintendent L.C. Lippert denied the application on June 27, 1955, noting her lands were under competent leases for five-year periods and that she was dependent on lease income. The Billings Area Director affirmed the denial on December 15, 1955, citing that her husband had already received a patent in fee on his allotted lands, sold them, and spent the proceeds without permanently improving the family's standard of living. The Commissioner of Indian Affairs affirmed again on February 21, 1956 (Realty 231-56), noting the husband's lack of steady employment. Harold G. Stanton, acting as Gardner's representative, corresponded with lessee Reuben Muhlbeir regarding assignment of lease income to Big Horn County State Bank and payment on the Invulate te Medicine Top allotments.

By contrast, Shirley Oldhorn's patent-in-fee application for allotment No. 3427 (SW¼, Sec. 33, T. 2 S., R. 38 E., 160 acres) was approved. Area Director Percy E. Melis requested issuance on August 9, 1957, reserving minerals for the Crow Tribe under the Act of June 4, 1920. Oldhorn immediately mortgaged the property to Harold G. Stanton for $1,075.00 at 7% interest on September 11, 1957, recorded in Book 40, Page 415, Big Horn County. The BIA's own inspection valued similar grazing land at $6.00–$9.00 per acre, meaning the $1,075 mortgage on 160 acres represented near-full encumbrance. These documents demonstrate the paternalistic denial mechanism used to keep Indian land in trust while simultaneously showing how fee patents, once granted, facilitated rapid alienation through mortgage debt to local non-Indian creditors—including Stanton himself, who served as both advocate and mortgagee.

summaries.file_name: Stanton Collection Series 5 individual files part 9.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1953–1964: Crow Allotment Dispossession Through Fee Patents, Including Amy Old Bull Congressional Cases
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1953–1964

This collection documents the systematic dispossession of Crow Reservation allotment lands through the fee patent process, involving multiple allottees and federal officials. The core documents trace Amy Old Bull (Crow allottee No. 927/4927) through years of congressional and administrative proceedings. Senator James Murray introduced S. 165 (83rd Congress) on her behalf in January 1953, but the Secretary of Interior recommended against it. Congressman Wesley D'Ewart (Montana, 2nd District) instead secured passage of H.R. 1244, signed June 4, 1953, as Public Law 49 (67 Stat. 42), amending Section 13 of the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), removing restrictions on sale or fee patenting of homestead, irrigable, or agricultural land on the Crow Reservation. Despite this legislative victory, Area Director J.M. Cooper denied Old Bull's fee patent application on November 10, 1954, declaring her incompetent and ordering supervised sale instead. She had previously received only $2,940.40 from a supervised land sale. Her allotment totaled approximately 900 acres valued at $7,500.

Anna Lionshows (allottee No. 1891) received fee patent No. 1173614 for 159.95 acres (Sec. 1, T.5S., R.28E.) on July 25, 1957, then immediately conveyed the land by warranty deed to Richard B. Hovland for $1.00 plus "other consideration." Becker Little Light (allottee No. 129) received a fee patent for 240 acres (Sec. 10, T.9S., R.36E.) and mortgaged it to Harold G. Stanton for $1,000 on November 8, 1957, the same day Stanton solicited a buyer. Genevieve Marie Conger (allottee No. 3141, heir of White Arm, allottee No. 1495) received patents for 1,000 and 240 acres in August 1956, then conveyed lands to non-Indians including Hovland and Jay Fitzgerald. Francis Rock (allottee No. 349) sought to sell 400 acres but was told to apply for fee patent instead. Ethel Plenty Hawk Blaine (allottee No. 1271, 286.80 acres) was recommended as competent by the Crow Agency superintendent but overruled by the Billings Area Director, who substituted supervised sale. Attorney Harold G. Stanton of Hardin, Montana appears repeatedly as intermediary, mortgagee, and purchaser, illustrating how fee patents facilitated rapid transfer of Indian lands to non-Indian ownership through legal mechanisms authorized by the Act of May 8, 1906 (34 Stat. 182), the Crow Allotment Act of June 4, 1920, and Secretarial Order No. 2508 (1949). Mineral rights were consistently reserved to the Crow Tribe under the 1920 Act.

summaries.file_name: Stanton Collection Series 5 individual files part4.pdf
summaries.display_title: 1954–1963: Crow Allotment Fee Patents and Land Sales Facilitated by Stanton, Wilson & Hovland
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1954–1963

This collection comprises correspondence, legal instruments, fee patent requests, land sale documents, and financial ledgers from the law offices of Stanton, Wilson & Hovland in Hardin, Montana, relating to Crow Reservation allotments and their conversion to fee simple ownership. The documents center on attorney Harold G. Stanton's role as intermediary in facilitating fee patents, land sales, and debt collection involving Crow tribal members in Big Horn County, Montana.

Key transactions include: fee patent applications for Michael Bull Chief covering allotments No. 584 (Paul Hill estate, 243.14 acres), No. 995 (John Birdwellknown estate, 761.88 acres), No. 1781 (Bull Chief estate, 40 acres), and No. 994 (Bird Wellknown estate, 748.40 acres total), all approved August 6, 1956 by Acting Area Director M. A. Johnson under Secretarial Order No. 2508 (14 F.R. 258-260) and Commissioner's Order No. 551 (16 F.R. 65). Perry Howe received a fee patent (No. 1147132) for 600 acres across allotment No. 1557, appraised at $9,000, issued October 18, 1954. Stanton subsequently purchased Howe's SE¼NE¼, Sec. 19, T.3S., R.33E. and resold it to Conrad Schanaman for $2,500 with a 20/40ths mineral reservation.

Mary Other Blackbird Woodtick's fee patent application for allotment No. 3432 (1,040 acres of grazing land) was denied at all three administrative levels—Superintendent, Area Director, and Commissioner—on competency grounds, citing below-market lease rates (62 cents/acre). Assignment documents show Martin Spotted Horse's crop-share income from Competent Indian Lease No. 010731 assigned to Hardin Auto Company ($1,550.23) and to Stanton himself ($268.70), illustrating how debt obligations captured Indian lease income. Henrietta Pretty On Top Denny's property (Lot 6, Block 6, Hill's Addition, Lodge Grass) was sold via contract for deed for $1,500 with Stanton as escrow agent, later handled through Crow Family Plan funds. Olive Record Venne offered 160 acres (SE¼, Sec. 22, T.5N., R.36E.) at $5.00/acre ($800). All patents reserved minerals for the Crow Tribe under the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751), with irrigation liens under the Act of March 7, 1928 (45 Stat. 200-210). The documents collectively demonstrate how fee patenting, attorney intermediation, debt instruments, and competency determinations operated as mechanisms of systematic land transfer from Crow allottees to non-Indian ownership.

summaries.display_title: 1953–1959: Conversion of Crow and Northern Cheyenne Trust Allotments to Fee Simple Patents (Stanton Collection)
summaries.summary:
DATE RANGE: 1953–1959

This collection comprises correspondence, billing statements, Western Union telegrams, Bureau of Land Management letters, and Bureau of Indian Affairs sale notices documenting the systematic conversion of Crow and Northern Cheyenne trust allotments into fee simple patents—a key mechanism of Indian land dispossession. The central figures are Harold G. Stanton of the law firm Stanton, Wilson & Hovland in Hardin, Montana, and Thelma S. Williams, a Washington, D.C.-based legal researcher retained at $35.00 per month to conduct daily surveillance of Bureau of Land Management patent records and telegraph Stanton immediately upon issuance of fee patents.

The documents reveal a deliberate, industrialized process: Williams monitored patent issuances and wired details—allotment numbers, patentee names, acreages, patent numbers, and legal descriptions—enabling Stanton to act swiftly on newly fee-patented Indian land. Specific patents documented include No. 1185998 (Crow Allotment 3498, Duke Russell heirs, 849.09 acres, September 3, 1958), No. 1186467 (Crow Allotment 1659, Frances Doyle Harx, 320 acres), No. 1188204 (Northern Cheyenne Allotment 167, Frank Bolson, 170.09 acres), and No. 1186782 (Northern Cheyenne Allotment 1320, Everett Whitewolf, 160 acres). Minerals were consistently reserved for tribal benefit under the Act of June 4, 1920 (41 Stat. 751). Allotment 1922 (Corn Woman) was flagged as a "key patent" reflecting a Bureau of Indian Affairs policy change. Sale notices cite acreage caps from Section 2 of the 1920 Act (640 agricultural/1,280 grazing acres) and irrigation lien provisions under 25 CFR 154. OCR quality is extremely poor on handwritten Hotel Washington stationery pages, rendering several pages nearly illegible.

The Crows usually leave the

After the trucks were loaded, 
the safari wound its way some 
ten miles back to camp, where 
the meat was hung to cool. Buf-
falo "meat has no odor, and it 
glazes over quickly in the dry 
air. No flies were around, and 
butchering was a clean job so 
that the meat looked no differ-
ent than beef quarters. ’
Pretty-On-Top told us that his

She makes pemmican in the old 
way—pounds up the dried meat, 
mixes it with wild fruit, and
tire and as heavy. No matter

Henry was a whiz at butcher-
ing. He dove into the abdominal 
cavity of the bull we skinned like 
a boy into the old swimming 
hole. As the animals are usually 
not bled, the interior holds about 
a washtub of gore. Henry fished 
around in it with much indiffer-
ence. and it needed darned little 
imagination to picture what the 
oldtime buffalo hunts may have 
been like before the Indian got 
his white schooling.
In bygone years the Redman 
gulped down little delicacies 
taken from the entrails (which 
the OPA was so fond of calling, 
edible offal) during the butcher-
ing. His tastes have become more 
refined under civilization so that 
he now cooks his tid-bits.
We did our job and were given 
three fine hides for our share, 
and all the liver. hearts and 
tongues we could carry away.

at good prices. Many f them 
had their own ranches!
Yellowtail and the Crow lead-
ers were smart. They picked an 
almost inaccessible area, be-
tween the Big Horn and Black
away,” he told Frank Linderman, 
the historian, “the hearts of my 
people fell to the ground and 
they could not lift them again.”
Perhaps old Plenty Coups is 
smiling now, for Be-sha has come 
back and his people are happy.
It all goes back to 1935 when 
Robert Yellowtail, a young full-
blood Crow, the first Indian to be 
appointed superintendent of his 
own reservation, used tribal funds 
to purchase seventy-five head of 
buffalo as a foundation herd.
At the time of purchase there 
was much shaking of: heads by 
the whites, with knowing grins 
and the skeptical predictions that 
the Crows would eat this small 
number of buffalo in a single 
season.
They Weren't Hungry
It might have happened, had 
the Crows been hungry. They 
were reasonably prosperous, how-
ever. Their fine grass lands were 
leased by stockmen at fair graz-
ing fees, and from time to time 
parcels of their, reservation, 
which originally embraced more
coyotes howl, and as the fire-
light* flickers on copper faces, it 
is not difficult to picture the days 
when the Redman ruled the buf-
falo country.
Times have changed, however, 
in more ways than one. One eve-
ning the talk turned to the tan-
ning of buffalo hides—and we 
asked Francis LaForge if he could 
get a Crow woman to tan the 
hides we had. He shook his head 
and grinned.
“Indian women,” he said, “are 
getting to be just like white 
women. They are now civilized—i 
too damned lazy to work!” 1

it* Then you get an ax put your 
foot on the carcass and chop it 
into four quarters.
Many books tell of how the 
lone frontiersman shot his buf- 
falo, skinned and dressed him.

who nodded in agreement.
Two of the hunters were busy 
cleaning rifles. Other blanketed 
figures half - drowsed in the 
warmth of the blaze. Far off, 
on the other side of the canyon; 
a coyote wailed and was an-
swered by the yapping of several 
of his kin. back of our camp.
One of the Indians said some-
thing in the soft accents of the 
Crows. "What did he say?" I 
asked. “He says/’ spoke up a 
wrinkled old man across the fire, 
“he says, our brother, the coyote, 
waits for his share. He has 
waited long and is hungry. He 
will eat well tomorrow. Every- 
one will eat welL The Crows will 
have much meat."
It was the eve of the great an-
nual Crow Indian buffalo hunt— 
in this year of 1948. Everyone 
was waiting impatiently for the 
dawn when the Crows would 
once more hunt the buffalo on 
the storied Big Horn as had their 
forefathers. It would be a great 
day!
Picked Beautiful Country
The Crow—or more properly, 
the Absaroka—Indians have lived 
in southern Montana, along the 
Yellowstone, Big Horn, Little Big 
Horn and Stillwater rivers for 
more than a century.
Legend has it that “Aleek- 
chea-ahoosh” (Plenty Coups), 
last of the tribal chieftains, who 
went to his fathers in 1932, se-
lected this million-and-a-half- 
acre empire of lush grasses, great 
plains, mountains, forests and 
clear running streams as the 
final home for his people, which 
was permanently established by 
treaty with the United States.

INTRODUCTION
1
NATIONAL ARCHIVES MICROFILM PUBLICATIONS 
____ 
(
NATIONAL ARCHIVES MICROFILM PUBLICATIONS
The Bureau of Indian Affairs, which was established within 
the War Department on March 11, 1824, and transferred in 1849 to 
the newly created Department of the Interior, administers the 
business of the U.S. Government relating to the American Indian. 
During the 19th century the Bureau had two principal types of 
field installations: superintendencies and agencies. Super- 
intendents had general responsibility for Indian affairs in 
a geographical area, usually a territory but sometimes a larger 
area. Agents were directly responsible for the affairs of one 
or more tribes and usually lived in the tribal areas. In 1879 
the first nonreservation Indian boarding school was established 
at Carlisle, Pa., and other schools were established elsewhere. 
In 1893 an act (27 Stat. 614) authorized the Commissioner of 
Indian Affairs, with the approval of the Secretary of the 
Interior, to assign the duties of Indian agent to school super-
intendents. This action was needed to eliminate political 
patronage because superintendents were now under Civil Service 
regulations whereas Indian agents remained political appointees. 
All agents were gradually replaced by superintendents who were 
not necessarily in charge of any school.
On the 174 rolls of this microfilm publication are reproduced 
the annual narrative reports, 1910-38, and the annual statistical 
reports, 1920-35, submitted to the Bureau of Indian Affairs 
(officially the Office of Indian Affairs before 1947). These 
reports were prepared by superintendents of the Bureau’s field 
jurisdictions and maintained by the Bureau in segregated files. 
Also included are annual narrative and statistical reports that 
were filed in the Bureau’s central classified files during the 
period 1907-9.
In 1910 the narrative and statistical data were submitted 
as one report. Beginning in 1911, however, the annual reports 
consisted of two separate parts, narrative and statistical, that 
were filed separately by the Statistics Section of the Bureau of 
Indian Affairs. For the purposes of this microfilm publication, 
the reports have been consolidated. The file of statistical 
reports (not including the 1907-9 statistical reports in the 
central classified files) begins with 1920 because those for 
the years 1911-19 inclusive were destroyed with congressional 
authorization in 1932. The narrative report was not required 
after 1934, but certain jurisdictions, fewer in number each 
year, continued to submit them voluntarily. Most of the 
statistical reports submitted after 1935, along with selected 
sections of narrative reports, constitute another series of 
records of the Statistics Section. These records, due to their 
fragmentary nature, have not been included in this publication.

--- Page 4 ---
2
|
NATIONAL ARCHIVES MICROFILM PUBLICATIONS
NATIONAL ARCHIVES MICROFILM PUBLICATIONS
In 1909 a Statistics Section was established in the Office 
of the Chief Clerk of the Indian Office. Thereafter, all 
annual reports of the field jurisdictions were made in accordance 
with directions prepared by the Statistics Section, which was 
responsible for compiling the data submitted. The Statistics 
Section also processed census rolls, reports of births and deaths, 
health reports, and other kinds of reports and statistical data, 
and it was responsible for compiling information for the Annual 
Report of the Cormissioner of Indian Affairs.
Each year circulars were sent to agents and superintendents 
giving instructions for preparing the annual reports. The annual 
narrative reports, 1910-38, document the operations and accomplish-
ments at the agencies, schools, hospitals, and other field 
jurisdictions. Some narrative reports may include, in addition 
to the superintendent's report, separate reports by the agency 
physician, field matron, or supervisor of farmers. Many super- 
intendents provided detailed historical sketches of their juris- 
dictions. A few ambitious superintendents took time to illustrate 
with drawings and poetry the activities and lifestyle of Indians 
in their jurisdictions. Photographs, maps, school publications, 
and news clippings or pages from newspapers were sometimes 
submitted with the reports and are filmed in this publication. 
The reports relate to law and order, health, land ownership, 
population, industries, forestry, allotments, land sales, and 
other subjects.
From 1837 to 1906 the annual reports of the superintendents, 
agents, and officers in charge of schools, agencies, or other 
field jurisdictions of the Bureau were printed as as appendix 
to the annual report of the Commissioner. Publication of the 
annual reports of the agents and superintendents as part of the 
Annual Report of the Commissioner was discontinued in 1907 at 
the insistence of the Department of the Interior. Arrangements 
were made to have the reports printed (but not published) by 
students in pamphlet form at various Indian schools where 
printing was taught. A number of complete sets of the annual 
reports for 1907, 1908, and possibly 1909 were made up and 
distributed within the service. There are no copies of these 
printed reports in the National Archives of the United States, 
but some typed copies of narrative and statistical reports 
for 1907, statistical reports for 1908, and a set of narrative 
reports for Indian Territory, 1908-9, are included in the 
correspondence files of the Bureau. They are reproduced on 
roll 1 of this microfilm publication.
The superintendents' annual statistical reports, 1920-35, 
were prepared on forms enclosed with the circulars of instructions 
to ensure uniformity. As the years passed the forms became 
more elaborate and the instructions more exacting. The reports 
contain separate sections on population, school enrollment 
showing tribal affiliation, health, agriculture, irrigation, 
and livestock. In 1933 the forms were revised slightly to

--- Page 5 ---

NATIONAL ARCHIVES MICROFILM PUBLICATIONS
1910-38 are arranged alphabetically by

The reports for 
of agency or other
provide statistical information on population and vital statistics, 
law and order, individual Indian income, land tenure, and other 
matters.
the narrative and statistical reports for each jurisdiction are 
interfiled. The arrangement of the sections of individual 
statistical reports varies from jurisdiction to jurisdiction and 
sometimes from year to year within a jurisdiction. Although 
the sections are numbered, the arrangement usually does not 
follow the numerical sequence. In some cases all sections 
within a report relating to a single tribe under a particular 
jurisdiction are grouped together; in other instances similarly 
numbered sections are grouped together regardless of the tribe 
involved.
Also reproduced with the reports are some transmittal letters, 
draft notes, and other correspondence. Interfiled are some nota- 
tions and correction slips, 1920-21, originally filed as a separate 
series of records. Included for fiscal year 1928 are weekly 
census reports and monthly medical reports that in other years 
were usually filed among the records of the Health Division. 
Sometimes either the words "Inapplicable to this agency" or "Not 
applicable" were typed on the report forms, or the forms were 
left blank except for the name of the reservation and jurisdiction 
typed in the heading.
The term "jurisdiction" as used by the Bureau of Indian 
Affairs refers to an administrative field unit whether it is an 
individual agency, school, hospital, or reservation composed 
of several agencies. The size of a jurisdiction varied greatly; 
some administered the affairs of several small tribes, while 
others had charge of a single tribe. Some tribes that were 
divided into widely scattered bands came under several different 
jurisdictions simultaneously. Smaller jurisdictions sometimes 
were consolidated into larger ones, and agencies and schools 
frequently were transferred from one jurisdiction to another. 
Not all Indian schools were separate jurisdictions; usually 
only nonreservation schools were recognized as independent 
jurisdictions. A jurisdiction identified as a school may have 
functioned also as an agency because school superintendents, 
as noted previously, often had agency duties assigned to them.
For the most part, the reports for the period 1907-9 that 
are reproduced on roll 1 of this microfilm publication are 
arranged chronologically and by type of report, thereunder 
alphabetically by the name of agency or other jurisdiction. 
No attempt has been made to interfile the narrative and statistical 
reports. The reports for Indian Territory (Five Civilized Tribes) 
are arranged chronologically, but their format does not permit

--- Page 6 ---

NATIONAL ARCHIVES MICROFILM PUBLICATIONS
NATIONAL ARCHIVES MICROFILM PUBLICATIONS
The records reproduced in this microfilm publication were 
arranged for filming by Samella T. Anderson, who also wrote 
the introduction.
The records reproduced in this microfilm publication and 
other series of records mentioned in this introduction are a 
part of the Records of the Bureau of Indian Affairs, Record 
Group 75. Another major series of the Statistics Section consists 
of Indian census rolls and supplements that contain vital 
statistics about American Indians. They have been reproduced 
as National Archives Microfilm Publication M595, Indian Census 
Rolls, 1885-1940.
The appendix provides a guide to frequent jurisdictional 
changes and other pertinent facts mentioned in the reports. It 
also shows the time period covered by the reports for each 
jurisdiction. As indicated in the appendix, some reports are 
missing. Letters often were filed stating that reports were 
not received from the superintendents.

extraction_samples.display_title: Charles J. Kappler Collection — Finding Aid
extraction_samples.chunk:
--- Page 1 ---
Archives: Charles J. Kappler Collection
Accession Number: 3719941
Donor: Julia Kappler
Processor: Jon Ille
Access Restrictions on Use: Due to the fragility of the collection, the Archivist must approve all use prior to allowing a 
patron to make copies, scan, or photographs.    
Preferred Citation Method:  Charles J. Kappler Collection, Little Big Horn College Library and Archives, Crow 
Agency, MT, Series, Box, File
Physical Description: 
Linear Feet: 12
Comprehensive Dates: 1906-1968- Copies of older treaties and historical documents exist within the collection.  
Materials Included: Correspondence, Congressional Legislation, Congressional Testimony, Tribal Council Minutes, 
Memorandum, Petitions to Congress and Federal Courts, Treaty Records, Federal Court Decisions, Tribal Histories as 
related to Legal Issues, and maps.
Organization of Material: The collection is organized into series by Native Nation, personal material of Charles J. 
Kappler, or other court cases Charles J. Kappler served as counsel. Each series is organized chronologically while 
generally maintaining original order.  
Provenance:
The Charles J. Kappler Collection details the lawyer’s career over a forty year span 1906-1946.  After Kappler’s death 
in 1946, the material resided with his son Charles T. Kappler in Washington DC.  The Little Big Horn College 
Archives acquired the collection from his widow Julia in 1994.  
Scope and Content:
The collection covers the period from 1906 to 1946 and represents Kappler’s legal career in a wide range of cases.  
There is a small portion of material from after Kappler’s death in the collection.  The collection includes materials 
produced working as a lawyer for a wide range of indigenous people including the Shoshone, Flathead, Blackfeet, 
Osage, Omaha, Wichita, Three Affiliated Tribes (North Dakota), Uintah Ouray (Ute), Ojibwe (Chippewa), Iowa, 
Potawatomi and Crow.  Kappler also served as the lead lawyer in a claim by the state of California against the federal 
government regarding debt the state incurred during the Civil War.  Finally, the collection also includes personal 
correspondence with friends and family.  The overwhelming majority of the contents held is correspondence and 
related to restitution for land loss (claims) filed in the United States Court of Claims.  
Biographical and/or Historical Note:
Charles J. Kappler was born in 1868 to Anton and Suzanna Kappler in Washington DC.  He attended the local public 
schools in Washington DC and subsequently studied stenography and typing.  He eventually gained employment as 
secretary for Senator William M. Stewart (R-Nevada).  Stewart served as Chairman of the Senate Indian Affairs 
Committee, which brought Kappler into contact with issues facing Native communities for the first time.
During his tenure working for Senator Stewart, Kappler attended Georgetown University where he graduated with a 
LLB (Bachelors of Law) in 1896 and an LLM (Masters of Law) in 1897.  Kappler gained admittance to the 
Washington DC Bar Association in 1896, although he continued to work for Stewart until 1903.  In 1901 the 
Commissioner of Indian Affairs William A. Jones requested a compilation of laws, treaties, and executive orders.  
Stewart placed Kappler in charge of this endeavor, which culminated in the first volume of Kappler’s Indian Laws and 
Treaties in 1907.  Kappler completed four more volumes while in private legal practice with the fifth volume 
published in 1938.  After his death, two more volumes were published.   
Once Kappler entered private practice in 1903, he entered into a partnership with Charles Merillat who already had 
experience working as counsel for Native communities.  Kappler and Merrilat dissolved their partnership in 1913 and 

--- Page 2 ---
the two developed an at times acrimonious relationship, particularly in relation to the adjudication of Indian claims 
cases.  
Kappler primarily worked in Indian law, but he also specialized in mining, oil, and land law.  Over the course of his 
career, Kappler argued ninety one cases before federal courts. As a recognition for his service, the Crows adopted him 
in 1931.  In a letter to Anthropologist Robert Lowie, James Carpenter Chairman of the Crow Tribal Business 
Committee noted that between one hundred and two hundred people attended the adoption ceremony in Hardin, 
Montana (Lowie, The Crow Indians, 1935.)  Kappler continued to practice law until his death in 1946.  He was 
survived by his wife Katherine and his children Charles T. and Suzanne.   
Preservation and Processing Note:  
The Charles J. Kappler Collection is in an extremely fragile state due to the environmental conditions it was stored in 
for nearly fifty years.  The collection is brittle due to humidity and most pieces of paper are very dirty due to the 
method of storage.  The archivist has noted mold on many sheets of paper.  During the initial processing of the 
collection, the archivist attempted to humidify the paper.  However, this caused the dirt and mold to deteriorate the 
paper to an even greater extent.  Due to the desire to allow patrons to use the collection, the archivist and librarian 
decided to process the collection in its current state with strict protocols surrounding access.    
During the processing of the collection, the archivist took care to maintain original order as closely as possible to 
replicate the activities of Charles Kappler in his legal practice.  Therefore, correspondence may not run in a strict 
chronological order as Kappler grouped letters in relation to a particular legal activity.  For example, Crow material 
related to irrigation may have letters over the course of several months and they will be grouped together to reflect that 
activity.  Therefore, series are grouped by year followed by the file number for that year. 
Series One: Personal Correspondence, Family Records, Books and Scrapbooks
This series includes Kappler’s correspondence not related to his legal practice primarily with family and 
acquaintances.  The correspondence also emphasizes business transactions not related to his legal practice.  There is 
also a will the he wrote for his sister and itemized list of her possessions.  The series also has scrapbooks that 
document Charles J. and Charles T. activities including the elder Kappler’s adoption into the Crow Tribe in 1931 along 
with a personal copy of Kappler’s Indian Laws and Treaties, Vol. 5.

Series Two: California Civil War Debt Claim
During the Civil War, California incurred a debt due to the California Militia going into New Mexico Territory in order 
to engage a group of Confederates who left Texas and threatened settlers in the Rio Grande Valley.  The lack of federal 
troops in California necessitated increased use of the militia.  California never received compensation and over time 

--- Page 3 ---
the state claimed that with interest the federal government owed them nine million dollars.  Kappler worked as a 
lawyer for California on this case and actively lobbied members of congress to award the state compensation to no 
avail.  Eventually, California’s claim made its way through the federal courts, but the Supreme Court rejected the 
claim in 1953.  This series includes correspondence, House and Senate resolutions, and newspaper articles from the 
1930s and 1940s.

Series Three: Crow
The Crow Nation hired Charles Kappler and Charles Merrilat as their attorneys in 1911.  After the dissolution of their 
partnership, Kappler remained as the attorney for the Crows.  The bulk of the material in the series consists of 
correspondence, legislation, memorandum, petitions and several maps. There is a great deal of historical research 
separated from the files to allow researchers access. The series includes material related to land claims, irrigation, and 
cattle.

Series Ten:  Miscellaneous  
This series includes smaller cases that Kappler worked and did not generate enough material to warrant an individual 
series, but still provides useful documents.  The series includes work he performed for the following Native peoples: 
Chippewa, Uintah Ouray, Iowa, and Potawatomi.
The series also includes Kappler’s work with Metis Fred Demarce who unwittingly turned over land scrip that he 
inherited from the Red River Cree settlements in the 1850s.  Demarce held on to the scrip until land speculators told 
him he could apply for land and use the scrip to acquire land in Florida, which was experiencing a land boom in the 
1920s.  Demarce signed his scrip over to the speculators and never received land or compensation for the scrip.  
Kappler was able to get Demarce an eighteen hundred dollar judgement.
